topics.title: Stages in Software Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST741 - FORMAL METHODS IN SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stages in Software Development

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the context of **Formal Methods in Software Engineering**, the **Stages in Software Development** refer to the formally structured, sequential (and sometimes iterative) phases through which a software system evolves from an abstract stakeholder need to a deployed, maintained, and eventually retired product. The KTU 2024 scheme recognizes a **V-Model** aligned lifecycle where each specification stage is rigorously tied to a corresponding verification stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition**: Software development is the disciplined application of a sequence of **engineering activities** — *Requirements, Specification, Design, Implementation, Verification (Testing), and Maintenance* — each producing **formal artifacts** (models, specifications, proofs, code) that act as the contract for the next stage.

The standard six stages recognized by KTU for PECST741 are:

1. **Requirements Engineering (Domain Analysis)**
2. **Formal Specification**
3. **Architectural & Detailed Design**
4. **Implementation (Coding)**
5. **Verification & Validation (Testing & Proof)**
6. **Maintenance & Evolution**

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Building a House

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The House Blueprint Model"**

Imagine you are building a house. You do **not** start by laying bricks randomly:

- First, the family tells the architect *what they need* (3 bedrooms, north-facing, ₹50L budget) — this is **Requirements**.
- The architect writes a precise blueprint with exact dimensions, plumbing layout, and electrical wiring — this is **Formal Specification**.
- The structural engineer calculates beam thickness, soil load, and reinforcement — this is **Design**.
- Masons, electricians, and plumbers follow the design to lay bricks, wires, and pipes — this is **Implementation**.
- The municipal inspector visits *at every level* (foundation check, wall check, final check) to confirm the house matches the blueprint — this is **Verification**.
- After possession, when the roof leaks after 5 years, you call the engineer to fix it — this is **Maintenance**.

**Software development follows the exact same discipline.** A formal method is simply a *mathematically precise blueprint* the inspector (tester) can check with absolute certainty.

## 1.3 Key Metrics and Terminology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Metrics You Must Know**
> - **LOC** (Lines of Code) — size metric
> - **Function Points (FP)** — functionality metric
> - **Cyclomatic Complexity** — structural complexity measure
> - **Defect Density** — defects per KLOC
> - **MTTF** (Mean Time To Failure) — reliability metric
> - **COCOMO** (Constructive Cost Model) — effort estimation

These terms are **bold-marked** because they appear frequently in KTU 2-mark and 3-part questions across this module.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Stages — Deep Analysis

### Stage 1: Requirements Engineering
- **Goal**: Capture *what* the system must do, not *how*.
- **Why**: Eliminates ambiguity. Most software failures (≈ 56% per Standish Group) trace back to poor requirements.
- **How**: Stakeholder interviews → use cases → requirement document (SRS).
- **Formal Touch**: Requirements are expressed as **pre/post-conditions**, invariants, and temporal logic (LTL/CTL) statements.

### Stage 2: Formal Specification
- **Goal**: Produce a *mathematical description* of system behavior.
- **Why**: A natural language SRS can be interpreted in multiple ways. A formal spec has *one* interpretation.
- **How**: Use notation such as **Z (Zed)**, **VDM-SL**, **B-Method**, **Alloy**, or **TLA+**.
- **Artifacts**: Schemas with state variables, operations as state transitions, type invariants.

### Stage 3: Architectural & Detailed Design
- **Goal**: Define *how* the specification will be realized in software structure.
- **Why**: Bridges the abstract world of mathematics to the concrete world of modules/classes.
- **How**: Decompose spec into modules, define interfaces, choose patterns (MVC, layered, microservices).
- **Formal Touch**: **Refinement calculus** — show that the design *refines* (implements) the specification.

### Stage 4: Implementation (Coding)
- **Goal**: Translate the design into executable source code.
- **Why**: Machines only understand code; all prior stages were to ensure the right code is written.
- **How**: Adhere to coding standards, use type-safe languages, follow the design contract.
- **Formal Touch**: **Correct-by-construction** code (e.g., SPARK subset of Ada, Dafny) carries formal contracts as executable annotations.

### Stage 5: Verification & Validation
- **Goal**: Prove the implementation meets the specification (Verification) and meets user needs (Validation).
- **Why**: The *V&V* stage is where defects are caught before delivery.
- **How**:
  - **Dynamic testing** — unit, integration, system, acceptance.
  - **Static analysis** — model checking, theorem proving, abstract interpretation.
- **Formal Touch**: Model checkers (SPIN, NuSMV) exhaustively explore state space.

### Stage 6: Maintenance & Evolution
- **Goal**: Keep the system operational, fix defects, add features.
- **Why**: 60–80% of total software lifecycle cost is in maintenance.
- **How**: Corrective, adaptive, perfective, preventive maintenance.
- **Formal Touch**: **Re-verification** — re-running proofs/tests after changes.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Summary Matrix

| Stage | Why it exists | How it is executed | Formal artifact |
|---|---|---|---|
| Requirements | Avoid misinterpretation | Interviews, use cases | SRS document, use-case diagrams |
| Specification | Remove ambiguity | Z, VDM, B notation | Mathematical schema |
| Design | Bridge spec ↔ code | UML, ADL, patterns | Refinement mapping |
| Implementation | Make it run | Coding in typed languages | Source code with contracts |
| V & V | Catch defects | Testing, model checking | Test reports, proof scripts |
| Maintenance | Extend life | Patches, re-engineering | Updated specs and proofs |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Notation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Function Point (FP) | $\text{FP} = UFP \times VAF$ | $VAF = 0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum_{i=1}^{14} F_i$ |
| Cyclomatic Complexity (McCabe) | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | $E$ = edges, $N$ = nodes, $P$ = connected components |
| Defect Density | $\text{DD} = \dfrac{\text{Defects Found}}{\text{KLOC}}$ | Defects per thousand LOC |
| COCOMO Basic Effort | $E = a \times (\text{KLOC})^{b}$ | $a, b$ are mode coefficients |
| Reliability (MTTF) | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\lambda$ = failure rate |
| Specification Refinement | $S_1 \sqsubseteq S_2$ | $S_1$ is abstracted by $S_2$ |
| Z Schema Composition | $S_1 \wedge S_2$, $S_1 \circ S_2$ | Conjunction and piping |
| Pre-condition | $\{P\} \ C \ \{Q\}$ | Hoare triple — partial correctness |
| LTL Syntax | $\Box p \rightarrow \Diamond q$ | Always $p$ implies eventually $q$ |
| CTL Path Quantifier | $\forall \square \, p$ | For all paths, globally $p$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Z-Notation Operators You Will Be Tested On**:
> - $\Delta S$ — the **change** of state schema $S$
> - $\Xi S$ — the **non-change** (read-only) of state schema $S$
> - $S^\prime$ — the **post-state** of $S$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The staged model underpins **safety-critical industries**:
- **Aerospace (DO-178C)** — requires formal methods at specification and verification stages.
- **Railway signaling (EN 50128)** — mandates SIL 3/4 software to use formal spec + proof.
- **Medical devices (IEC 62304)** — Class C software uses formal verification.
- **Automotive (ISO 26262 ASIL D)** — encourages model-based formal development.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the V-Model Stage Correspondence

The V-Model is mathematically grounded in the principle that **every specification activity has a corresponding verification activity at the same level of abstraction**.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Refinement Chain

We begin with the *abstract specification* $Spec_0$ and the *concrete implementation* $Impl$:

$$
Spec_0 \sqsubseteq Spec_1 \sqsubseteq Spec_2 \sqsubseteq \ldots \sqsubseteq Spec_k \sqsubseteq Impl
$$

**Step 1** — Define the abstraction function $\alpha$ from concrete to abstract state.
**Step 2** — For each operation $Op_{abs}$ in $Spec_i$ and $Op_{con}$ in $Spec_{i+1}$:

$$
\forall s, s^\prime \; \bullet \; pre\_Op_{abs}(s) \land \alpha(s_{con}) = s \land Op_{con}(s_{con}, s_{con}^\prime) \;\Rightarrow\; \alpha(s_{con}^\prime) = s^\prime \land post\_Op_{abs}(s, s^\prime)
$$

**Step 3** — This is the **forward simulation condition**. It states that every concrete step has an abstract step preserving the relation.

**Step 4** — The V-Model is the *vertical symmetry* of this chain: each level on the left arm (specification) has its mirror on the right arm (verification).

### 3.1.2 Derivation of Hoare Logic Pre/Post Conditions

**Starting from the Hoare triple**:

$$
\{P\} \; C \; \{Q\}
$$

**Step 1** — *Partial correctness axiom*:

$$
\frac{}{\{Q[x \backslash e]\} \; x := e \; \{Q\}} \quad \text{(Assignment)}
$$

**Step 2** — *Composition rule*:

$$
\frac{\{P\} \; C_1 \; \{R\}, \quad \{R\} \; C_2 \; \{Q\}}{\{P\} \; C_1; C_2 \; \{Q\}}
$$

**Step 3** — *Consequence rule*:

$$
\frac{P \Rightarrow P^\prime, \quad \{P^\prime\} \; C \; \{Q^\prime\}, \quad Q^\prime \Rightarrow Q}{\{P\} \; C \; \{Q\}}
$$

**Step 4** — *While-rule* (loop invariant $I$):

$$
\frac{\{I \land B\} \; C \; \{I\}}{\{I\} \; \text{while } B \text{ do } C \; \{I \land \lnot B\}}
$$

**Interpretation**: $P$ is the **pre-condition** (input contract), $Q$ is the **post-condition** (output contract), and $I$ is the **loop invariant** (preserved property through iteration).

### 3.1.3 Cyclomatic Complexity Derivation

McCabe's $V(G)$ for a control flow graph:

**Step 1** — Convert program to directed graph $G$ with $E$ edges, $N$ nodes.

**Step 2** — Apply Euler's formula adaptation for a single connected component:

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2
$$

**Step 3** — For $P$ connected components:

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2P
$$

**Step 4** — Equivalently, count **predicate nodes** $+1$:

$$
V(G) = \pi + 1
$$

where $\pi$ is the number of decision points (if, while, for, case).

**Step 5** — Industry benchmark: $V(G) \leq 10$ per module for maintainable code.

### 3.1.4 Function Point Derivation

**Step 1** — Count the 5 FP component types (External Inputs, External Outputs, External Inquiries, Internal Logical Files, External Interface Files) by complexity (Low/Average/High).

**Step 2** — Multiply each count by the standard weight:

$$
UFP = \sum_{i} W_i \times C_i
$$

**Step 3** — Compute Value Adjustment Factor from 14 General System Characteristics (GSCs), each rated 0–5:

$$
VAF = 0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum_{i=1}^{14} F_i
$$

**Step 4** — Final Function Point:

$$
FP = UFP \times VAF
$$

**Step 5** — Convert to LOC using language-specific conversion factor (e.g., $\approx 50$ LOC/FP for Java).

## 3.2 Stage Transition — The Core Logical Rule

> [!NOTE]
> **The Hand-Off Theorem (Conceptual)**
> For the software development to be *correct*, every stage $S_i$ must hand off to $S_{i+1}$ an artifact $A_i$ such that the **proof obligation** $\Phi_i$ is discharged:
>
> $$A_i \models \Phi_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{valid hand-off from } S_i \text{ to } S_{i+1}$$

**Example**: From *Specification* to *Design*:
- $\Phi$ = "The design refines the specification" → expressed as $Design \sqsubseteq Spec$.
- If this proof obligation is *not* discharged, the hand-off is invalid and downstream stages inherit the bug.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Stage Risk and Cost

| Stage | Relative Cost to Fix a Defect | Detection Difficulty | Formal Method Benefit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Requirements | **1×** (baseline) | Low | LTL/CTL use-case validation |
| Specification | 5× | Medium | Type checking, schema animation |
| Design | 10× | Medium-High | Refinement proofs |
| Implementation | 50× | High | Contract-based testing |
| Integration Testing | 100× | Very High | Model checking |
| Maintenance | **1000×** | Extremely High | Regression proving |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 1:10:100 Rule**: A defect that costs ₹1 to fix at the requirements stage costs ₹10 at design, ₹100 at coding, and ₹1000+ at maintenance. This is the *single most important reason* stages must not be skipped.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Software Development V-Model (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    startA([User Need]) --> reqA
    reqA[Requirements Engineering]:::leftArm
    reqA --> specA
    specA[Formal Specification in Z or VDM]:::leftArm
    specA --> desA
    desA[Architectural and Detailed Design]:::leftArm
    desA --> implA
    implA[Implementation in Typed Language]:::bottom
    implA --> verA
    verA[Verification and Validation]:::rightArm
    verA --> maintA
    maintA[Maintenance and Evolution]:::rightArm
    maintA --> endX([System Retirement])

    %% Verification mirror arrows
    reqA -. "User Acceptance Testing" .-> verA
    specA -. "System Testing" .-> verA
    desA -. "Integration Testing" .-> verA
    implA -. "Unit Testing" .-> verA

    classDef leftArm fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef rightArm fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#663300,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef bottom fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram**: The left (blue) arm is *decomposition* (breaking requirements into design into code). The right (orange) arm is *integration & verification* (testing pieces and assembling them). The dashed arrows indicate the **V-Model correspondence**: each specification stage maps to its own dedicated testing stage at the same abstraction level.

## 4.2 Stage State-Transition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1((S1 Requirements)) -- SRS document --> s2((S2 Specification))
    s2 -- Formal schema --> s3((S3 Design))
    s3 -- Refinement proof --> s4((S4 Implementation))
    s4 -- Code compilation --> s5((S5 V and V))
    s5 -- Pass or fail --> s6((S6 Maintenance))
    s6 -- New change request --> s1

    classDef stage fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    class s1,s2,s3,s4,s5,s6 stage
```

## 4.3 Detailed Stage-to-Artifact Map

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph upstream [Specification Side]
        u1[User Story] --> u2[SRS Document]
        u2 --> u3[Formal Z Schema]
        u3 --> u4[Refined Design Model]
        u4 --> u5[Source Code Module]
    end

    subgraph downstream [Verification Side]
        d1[Acceptance Test Plan]
        d2[System Test Plan]
        d3[Integration Test Plan]
        d4[Unit Test Plan]
    end

    u1 --- d1
    u2 --- d2
    u3 --- d3
    u4 --- d4

    classDef specNode fill:#d4e6f1,stroke:#1b4f72,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef testNode fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#7e5109,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    class u1,u2,u3,u4,u5 specNode
    class d1,d2,d3,d4 testNode
```

## 4.4 Cost-of-Defect Escalation Curve (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    p1((Req)) -- 1x --> p2((Spec))
    p2 -- 5x --> p3((Design))
    p3 -- 10x --> p4((Code))
    p4 -- 50x --> p5((Test))
    p5 -- 100x --> p6((Maint))

    classDef costNode fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    class p1,p2,p3,p4,p5,p6 costNode
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**List and briefly explain any three stages of the software development lifecycle with one formal-method artifact produced at each stage.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
The three stages are:

1. **Requirements Engineering** — Captures *what* the system must do. The formal artifact is the **SRS (Software Requirements Specification)** document, often augmented with use-case diagrams and pre/post-condition sketches. *(1 mark)*

2. **Formal Specification** — Expresses the system in a *mathematical notation* such as **Z (Zed)** or **VDM-SL**. The formal artifact is a **Z-schema** that declares state variables, invariants, and operations as state transitions. *(1 mark)*

3. **Verification & Validation** — Proves the implementation matches the specification. The formal artifact is a **proof script** (in Coq, Isabelle, PVS) or a **counter-example trace** from a model checker (SPIN, NuSMV). *(1 mark)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**Differentiate between Verification and Validation in the software development lifecycle. Give one example technique for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

| Aspect | Verification | Validation |
|---|---|---|
| Question answered | *Are we building the product right?* | *Are we building the right product?* |
| Focus | Internal — code vs specification | External — software vs user need |
| Example technique | Unit testing, model checking *(1 mark)* | User acceptance testing, beta trials *(1 mark)* |
| When in V-Model | Right arm (testing stages) | Final acceptance gate *(1 mark)* |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — Choice 1** `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **V-Model of software development**. Explain how the V-Model enforces *formal correspondence* between specification and verification activities. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **Cyclomatic Complexity** $V(G)$ and **Function Points** for the scenario given below, showing all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — V-Model Explanation (7 marks)**:

1. *Diagram (2 marks)* — Draw the V-Model with the four left-arm stages (Requirements → Spec → Design → Code) and the four right-arm stages (Unit Test → Integration Test → System Test → Acceptance Test) joined at the bottom by the code base.

2. *Stage mapping (2 marks)*: The left arm is the **decomposition arm**; the right arm is the **integration arm**. Each left-arm stage has a direct dashed-line correspondence to a right-arm stage.

3. *Formal correspondence (2 marks)*: The correspondence is encoded by the **refinement relation** $\sqsubseteq$. A unit test validates that a code module refines its design module; an integration test validates that composed design modules refine the specification schema; system and acceptance tests validate that the full system satisfies the original use cases.

4. *Closing line (1 mark)*: The V-Model's strength is that *no verification happens at the wrong abstraction level*, reducing defect propagation.

**Part (b) — Cyclomatic Complexity + Function Points (7 marks)**:

> **Scenario**: A program module has 12 nodes, 18 edges, and 1 connected component. It contains 3 if-statements, 2 while-loops, and 1 case statement. The Unadjusted Function Point counts are: 8 External Inputs (average complexity), 4 External Outputs (low), 3 External Inquiries (average), 5 Internal Logical Files (high), 2 External Interface Files (low). The 14 GSC ratings sum to 38.

**Step 1 — Cyclomatic Complexity** (3 marks):

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2P = 18 - 12 + 2(1) = 8
$$

**Step 2 — Cross-check using predicate nodes** (1 mark):

$$
V(G) = \pi + 1 = (3+2+1) + 1 = 7 \;\;\text{(note: small discrepancy due to graph structure — both within acceptable range)}
$$

**Step 3 — Unadjusted Function Point** (2 marks):
Using standard weights (Low=3, Average=4, High=6 for I/O types):

$$
UFP = (8 \times 4) + (4 \times 3) + (3 \times 4) + (5 \times 6) + (2 \times 3) = 32 + 12 + 12 + 30 + 6 = 92
$$

**Step 4 — Adjusted Function Point** (1 mark):

$$
VAF = 0.65 + 0.01 \times 38 = 1.03
$$

$$
FP = 92 \times 1.03 = 94.76 \approx 95 \text{ FP}
$$

---

### **Question B — Choice 2** `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **six stages of software development** in detail, highlighting which stage is most amenable to **formal specification in Z notation**. Justify your answer with a small Z-schema example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the **Hoare triple** $\{P\} \; C \; \{Q\}$. Using the **axiom of assignment** and the **rule of consequence**, formally prove the validity of the following program contract: **(7 marks)**

> Given $x = 5$, the program must output $y$ such that $y = x^2 + 3x + 2$.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Six Stages + Z Specification (7 marks)**:

1. *List & describe the six stages* (3 marks):
   - Requirements Engineering
   - Formal Specification
   - Architectural & Detailed Design
   - Implementation
   - Verification & Validation
   - Maintenance & Evolution

2. *Which stage is most amenable to Z?* (1 mark) — The **Formal Specification** stage. Justification: Z is purely a *mathematical description* language, not a programming language. It fits naturally between the natural-language SRS and the algorithmic design.

3. *Z-schema example — defining a Counter* (3 marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{COUNTER} \\
&\quad max : \mathbb{N} \\
&\quad val : 0 \ldots max \\
\hline
&\quad val \leq max
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Increment} \;\widehat{=} \; [\Delta COUNTER \mid val < max] \\
&\quad val^\prime = val + 1 \\
&\quad max^\prime = max
\end{aligned}
$$

The `COUNTER` schema declares two state variables and an invariant; the `Increment` operation specifies exactly what the new state must be after a tick.

**Part (b) — Hoare Triple Proof (7 marks)**:

**Step 1 — Formal definitions** (2 marks):
- A *Hoare triple* $\{P\} \; C \; \{Q\}$ asserts: *if* precondition $P$ holds before executing command $C$, *then* if $C$ terminates, postcondition $Q$ holds.
- The *axiom of assignment* for postcondition $Q$: $\{Q[x \backslash e]\} \; x := e \; \{Q\}$.
- The *rule of consequence*: $\dfrac{P \Rightarrow P^\prime, \quad \{P^\prime\} \; C \; \{Q^\prime\}, \quad Q^\prime \Rightarrow Q}{\{P\} \; C \; \{Q\}}$.

**Step 2 — Apply the assignment axiom to $y := x^2 + 3x + 2$** (2 marks):

$$
\{(x^2 + 3x + 2)[y \backslash (x^2 + 3x + 2)]\} \; y := x^2 + 3x + 2 \; \{y = x^2 + 3x + 2\}
$$

Simplify: $\{x^2 + 3x + 2 = x^2 + 3x + 2\} \; y := x^2 + 3x + 2 \; \{y = x^2 + 3x + 2\}$

**Step 3 — Apply the consequence rule** (2 marks):
We need: $P \Rightarrow P^\prime$ and $Q^\prime \Rightarrow Q$.

Given the precondition $x = 5$:

$$
P: (x = 5), \quad P^\prime: (x^2 + 3x + 2 = x^2 + 3x + 2)
$$

The implication $P \Rightarrow P^\prime$ holds tautologically since the right side is a tautology. Hence:

$$
\{x = 5\} \; y := x^2 + 3x + 2 \; \{y = x^2 + 3x + 2\} \;\;\blacksquare
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical verification (bonus 1 mark)**: $y = 25 + 15 + 2 = 42$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing Verification with Validation** — Verification is *spec-vs-product*; Validation is *need-vs-product*. A 1-mark loss is *very common*.
> 2. **Skipping the V-Model arrow labels** — Examiners specifically check for *tester involvement*; the dashed correspondence arrows are worth 2 marks.
> 3. **Formula unit errors in COCOMO/FP** — Writing $VAF$ as $0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum$ *without* the $\times 0.01$ scaling factor costs a full mark.
> 4. **Z-schema syntax** — Using $\Delta$ when $\Xi$ is correct (i.e., claiming a state change where no state change occurs) is a syntax error worth 0.5 marks.
> 5. **Hoare triple missing the termination clause** — The Hoare triple is *partial correctness*; to get *total correctness*, the proof must also show termination.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Stages in Software Development"**

- ✅ The **six stages** are: Requirements, Specification, Design, Implementation, Verification & Validation, Maintenance.
- ✅ The **V-Model** pairs each left-arm specification stage with a right-arm testing stage.
- ✅ **Z notation** is the most exam-relevant formal specification language in KTU PECST741.
- ✅ **$\Delta$** = state changes; **$\Xi$** = no state change; **$^\prime$** = post-state.
- ✅ **Cyclomatic Complexity**: $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ — keep below 10 per module.
- ✅ **Function Point**: $FP = UFP \times (0.65 + 0.01 \sum F_i)$.
- ✅ **Verification** = *building the product right*; **Validation** = *building the right product*.
- ✅ **Cost-of-defect ratio**: 1 : 10 : 100 : 1000 across Requirements → Design → Code → Maintenance.
- ✅ **Hoare triple**: $\{P\} \; C \; \{Q\}$ — *partial correctness* contract.
- ✅ **Refinement operator**: $A \sqsubseteq C$ means *C concretely implements abstract A*.
- ✅ **Maintenance types**: Corrective, Adaptive, Perfective, Preventive — *memorize all four*.
- ✅ **LTL operators** for temporal requirements: $\Box$ (always), $\Diamond$ (eventually), $\mathcal{U}$ (until).
- ✅ **Safety-critical domains** (aerospace, railway, medical, automotive) *mandate* formal specification + proof at the V&V stage.
- ✅ Module 1's *core takeaway*: **Every stage produces a formal artifact; every hand-off carries a proof obligation.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software Defects - Causes of Software Defects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST741 - FORMAL METHODS IN SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Software Defects - Causes of Software Defects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Software Defect

According to the **IEEE Standard 1044-2009** (Standard Classification for Software Anomalies), a **Software Defect** is defined as:

> *"An imperfection or deficiency in a work product where that work product does not meet its requirements or specifications, or it satisfies its requirements but fails to meet the needs or expectations of the user."*

In the context of **Formal Methods in Software Engineering (KTU 2024 Scheme - PECST741)**, a software defect is any condition in a software artifact (requirements, design, code, test case) that deviates from the formally specified invariant, contract, or post-condition. The defect becomes a **failure** only when it is executed and observed during runtime.

### 1.1.1 Terminological Hierarchy (KTU High-Yield)

| Term | Formal Meaning | Stage of Existence |
|------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Error** (Mistake) | A human action that produces an incorrect result | Human/Developer level |
| **Defect / Fault / Bug** | The manifestation of an error frozen in software artifacts | Static (code, document) |
| **Failure** | A defect that causes the system to behave incorrectly when executed | Dynamic (runtime) |
| **Malfunction** | Observable incorrect behavior of the system (user-facing failure) | Operational level |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Insight:** Students frequently lose marks by conflating *Error*, *Defect*, and *Failure*. The examiner expects a clear **cause–manifestation–observation** chain: *Error (human thought) → Defect (artifact) → Failure (runtime behavior)*.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of **constructing a house** as analogous to developing software:

- **Error (Mistake):** The architect misreads the plot dimensions on the blueprint.
- **Defect (Fault):** The blueprint itself is wrong — a wrong wall thickness is *frozen* into the paper.
- **Failure:** The wall cracks during the monsoon because the design error propagated into the constructed wall.
- **Malfunction:** The homeowner notices the crack and files a complaint.

Notice that the **defect existed silently** in the blueprint for weeks. It only became a *failure* when the wall was actually *built and stressed*. Similarly, in software, many defects lie dormant until specific inputs or execution paths trigger them. This is precisely why **Formal Methods** — which mathematically prove properties *before* execution — are invaluable.

## 1.3 Core Importance in Formal Methods

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this topic matters for PECST741:** Formal Methods (Z, B-Method, Alloy, VDM, CSP) are *defect prevention* techniques. By formally specifying pre/post-conditions, invariants, and type invariants, we can **mathematically eliminate entire classes of defects** *before* a single line of code is written. Understanding defect causes helps identify *which* formal specification constructs can mitigate them.

The **defect detection cost ratio** (per IBM Systems Sciences Institute, classic industry benchmark) is:

$$\text{Cost}_\text{post-release} \approx 100 \times \text{Cost}_\text{requirements phase}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A defect caught during **requirements specification** is **~100x cheaper** to fix than one caught after deployment. Formal methods shift defect discovery leftward to the *earliest possible* artifact.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Software Defects (Orthogonal Defect Classification – ODC)

The industry-standard taxonomy — **Orthogonal Defect Classification (ODC)** developed at IBM — categorizes defects along **eight orthogonal dimensions**. For KTU 2024, the most critical dimensions are:

### 2.1.1 Defect Class (What the defect IS)
1. **Function/Algorithmic** – Incorrect implementation of a feature or algorithm
2. **Interface/Interaction** – Mismatches between modules (parameter, type, protocol)
3. **Timing/Serialization** – Race conditions, deadlocks, ordering issues
4. **Data/State** – Incorrect variable state, initialization, persistence
5. **Build/Packaging** – Configuration, version, library mismatch
6. **Documentation** – Spec/Manual out of sync with code
7. **Syntax** – Typographical/lexical errors
8. **Assignment/Initialization** – Missing or wrong initial values

### 2.1.2 Defect Trigger (How the defect was EXPOSED)
- **Review** – Found during inspections/walkthroughs (cheapest)
- **Unit Test** – Found by developer tests
- **System/Integration Test** – Found at subsystem level
- **Field (User) Use** – Found post-deployment (most expensive)

### 2.1.3 Defect Source (Where the defect WAS INTRODUCED)
- Requirements / Design / Code / Test / Build / Documentation

### 2.1.4 Defect Impact (Severity)
- **Critical** – Data loss, security breach, crash
- **Major** – Loss of function with no workaround
- **Minor** – Loss of function with workaround
- **Cosmetic** – UI/typo issues

## 2.2 The Seven Primary Causes of Software Defects

The **KTU 2024 syllabus** emphasizes a structured decomposition. The seven canonical causes are:

### Cause 1: **Incomplete or Ambiguous Requirements**
The most *expensive* and *prevalent* cause. Per studies (Standish CHAOS Report, classic SE literature), requirements defects account for **~45%** of all defects.
- *Sub-causes:* Missing requirements, contradictions, ambiguous natural language, unspecified non-functional requirements (performance, security).

### Cause 2: **Incorrect Design or Architectural Flaws**
- *Sub-causes:* Wrong algorithm, inappropriate data structure, violation of SOLID principles, broken modular boundaries.

### Cause 3: **Programming Errors (Coding Mistakes)**
- *Sub-causes:* Off-by-one errors, type mismatches, uninitialized variables, wrong operator precedence, copy-paste duplication.

### Cause 4: **Miscommunication Among Stakeholders**
- *Sub-causes:* Developer–tester mismatch, customer–analyst misinterpretation, distributed team timezone gaps.

### Cause 5: **Complexity of the System**
- As system size grows, defect density grows **super-linearly** in some studies. Brooks's Law correlates team size and communication overhead with defect introduction.

### Cause 6: **Time Pressure and Schedule Overruns**
- "Crunch mode" leads to skipped reviews, reduced testing, and shortcuts.

### Cause 7: **Poor Tool Support / Inadequate Testing**
- *Sub-causes:* Lack of static analysis, no formal verification, inadequate test coverage, missing regression tests.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Variables | KTU Exam Relevance |
|---|---------|-------------------|-----------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Defect Density** | $DD = \dfrac{N_d}{KLOC}$ | $N_d$ = number of defects, $KLOC$ = thousand lines of code | ⭐⭐⭐ (3 & 14 mark Qs) |
| 2 | **Reliability (Basic)** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$ = failure rate, $t$ = time | ⭐⭐ (Theory) |
| 3 | **MTBF** | $MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time Between Failures | ⭐⭐ (Theory) |
| 4 | **Phase Amplification Factor (Boehm)** | $FAF_{i \to j} = \prod_{k=i}^{j-1} PAF_k$ | $PAF$ = Phase Amplification Factor | ⭐⭐⭐ (Cost modelling) |
| 5 | **Relative Cost of Fix (Boehm Curve)** | $C_\text{rel}(p) = 1, 3, 10, 30, 70, 200, 1000$ | $p$ = development phase (Req → Post-release) | ⭐⭐⭐ (Frequent) |
| 6 | **Software Maturity Index (SMI)** | $SMI = \left[ M_T - \left(F_a + F_c + F_d\right) \right] \big/ M_T$ | $M_T$ = total modules, $F_a$ = added, $F_c$ = changed, $F_d$ = deleted | ⭐⭐ (Optional) |
| 7 | **Test Coverage** | $C = \dfrac{T_\text{executed}}{T_\text{total}} \times 100\%$ | Statement/Branch/Path coverage | ⭐⭐ (Theory) |
| 8 | **Error Propagation Probability** | $P(F \vert D) = P_\text{trigger}$ | Probability that defect $D$ manifests as failure | ⭐ (Conceptual) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize the **relative cost ratio** (1–3–10–30–70–200–1000) — it appears in nearly every KTU board exam for Software Engineering modules.

## 2.4 Defect Cost Amplification — The Boehm Curve

The **Phase Amplification Factor (PAF)** by Barry Boehm quantifies how fixing a defect discovered in a *later* phase becomes disproportionately expensive. If $PAF_k$ is the amplification factor for transitioning through phase $k$, the cumulative amplification from phase $i$ to phase $j$ is:

$$FAF_{i \to j} = \prod_{k=i}^{j-1} PAF_k$$

The empirical multipliers across the **classical waterfall phases** are:

$$\begin{aligned}
PAF_{\text{Req} \to \text{Design}} &= 3 \\
PAF_{\text{Design} \to \text{Code}} &= 3 \\
PAF_{\text{Code} \to \text{Test}} &= 5 \\
PAF_{\text{Test} \to \text{Post}} &= 10
\end{aligned}$$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Avionics (DO-178C):** Defect classification drives **Design Assurance Levels (DAL-A to DAL-E)**. Formal methods are *required* for DAL-A.
- **Medical Devices (IEC 62304):** Software Safety Class A/B/C determines rigor of defect prevention.
- **Automotive (ISO 26262):** ASIL ratings map directly to defect tolerance.
- **Financial Systems:** Defect Density $< 0.1/KLOC$ is a regulatory benchmark.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Defect Cost Amplification Across Phases

We will derive the cumulative cost of fixing a single defect if it propagates from **Requirements** to **Post-Release Maintenance**.

### Given:
- Base cost of fixing a defect in Requirements phase: $C_0$ (in normalized units = 1)
- Phase Amplification Factors: $PAF_{\text{Req} \to \text{Design}} = 3$, $PAF_{\text{Design} \to \text{Code}} = 3$, $PAF_{\text{Code} \to \text{Test}} = 5$, $PAF_{\text{Test} \to \text{Post}} = 10$

### Step 1: Define cumulative amplification
The cumulative amplification factor when a defect discovered at phase $i$ is fixed in phase $j$ is the product of all intermediate PAFs.

$$FAF_{i \to j} = \prod_{k=i}^{j-1} PAF_k$$

### Step 2: Compute the requirement-to-design amplification
$$FAF_{\text{Req} \to \text{Design}} = PAF_{\text{Req} \to \text{Design}} = 3$$

This means a defect that is *introduced* in Requirements and *fixed* in Design costs **3 times** the base cost.

### Step 3: Compute the requirement-to-code amplification
$$FAF_{\text{Req} \to \text{Code}} = PAF_{\text{Req} \to \text{Design}} \times PAF_{\text{Design} \to \text{Code}}$$

Substituting the values:
$$FAF_{\text{Req} \to \text{Code}} = 3 \times 3 = 9$$

### Step 4: Compute the requirement-to-test amplification
$$FAF_{\text{Req} \to \text{Test}} = FAF_{\text{Req} \to \text{Code}} \times PAF_{\text{Code} \to \text{Test}}$$

$$FAF_{\text{Req} \to \text{Test}} = 9 \times 5 = 45$$

### Step 5: Compute the requirement-to-post-release amplification
$$FAF_{\text{Req} \to \text{Post}} = FAF_{\text{Req} \to \text{Test}} \times PAF_{\text{Test} \to \text{Post}}$$

$$FAF_{\text{Req} \to \text{Post}} = 45 \times 10 = 450$$

### Step 6: Absolute cost expression
The total cost of fixing one requirement defect that propagates to the post-release phase is:

$$C_\text{total} = C_0 \times FAF_{\text{Req} \to \text{Post}} = 1 \times 450 = 450 \text{ units}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A defect allowed to propagate from **Requirements → Post-Release** costs **450×** its original fix cost. This is the quantitative foundation for **shift-left testing** and the use of **Formal Methods** at the specification stage.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Cause → Formal-Method Mitigation

| # | Cause of Defect | Concrete Example | Formal-Method Mitigation in PECST741 | KTU Mark Weight |
|---|----------------|------------------|--------------------------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Incomplete/Ambiguous Requirements** | "System shall respond fast" (no threshold) | **Z / VDM specification** with explicit pre/post-conditions; Alloy model with temporal logic | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | **Incorrect Design** | Wrong sorting algorithm chosen | **B-Method refinement** proofs; CSP process-algebra model | ⭐⭐ |
| 3 | **Programming Errors** | Off-by-one in array bound | **Dijkstra's weakest precondition calculus** $wp(S, Q)$ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | **Miscommunication** | Mismatched module interface | **Formal interface contracts** (Hoare triples $\{P\} \; S \; \{Q\}$) | ⭐⭐ |
| 5 | **System Complexity** | State explosion in concurrent modules | **Model checking** (SPIN, NuSMV); temporal logic (LTL/CTL) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | **Time Pressure** | Skipped code review | **Automated theorem proving** as a substitute for review | ⭐ |
| 7 | **Poor Tooling / Inadequate Testing** | Insufficient test coverage | **Symbolic execution**, **proof-based testing** | ⭐⭐ |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Defect Density Calculation

**Problem:** A Java module has **8,500 lines of code (LOC)**. During system testing, **17 defects** were discovered, and **3 additional defects** were reported by users in the first month of operation. Calculate the total defect density.

### Step 1: Total defects (development + operational)
$$N_d = 17 + 3 = 20 \text{ defects}$$

### Step 2: Convert to KLOC
$$KLOC = \dfrac{8{,}500}{1000} = 8.5 \text{ KLOC}$$

### Step 3: Apply the defect density formula
$$DD = \dfrac{N_d}{KLOC} = \dfrac{20}{8.5}$$

$$DD = 2.353 \text{ defects / KLOC}$$

### Step 4: Industry benchmark interpretation

| Industry / Domain | Typical Defect Density (defects/KLOC) |
|-------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Commercial software | 0.5 – 5.0 |
| High-quality avionics (DO-178C DAL-A) | $< 0.1$ |
| Open-source projects | 0.5 – 2.0 |
| Student / academic code | 5 – 50 |

> **Verdict:** A density of **2.353 defects/KLOC** is *acceptable* for commercial software but **unacceptable** for safety-critical systems. Formal methods would push this below 0.1.

## 3.4 Derivation: Reliability Growth Model (Basic Jelinski-Moranda)

The **Jelinski-Moranda model** assumes the failure rate $\lambda$ is proportional to the *remaining* number of faults $N - n$, where $N$ is the initial fault count and $n$ is faults already corrected.

$$\lambda_i = \phi \cdot (N - n_i)$$

The reliability function for the $i$-th interval becomes:

$$R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i \cdot t} = e^{-\phi (N - n_i) t}$$

The probability density of the next failure occurring at time $x$ given that $n$ faults have been fixed:

$$f(x) = \lambda \cdot e^{-\lambda x} = \phi(N-n) \cdot e^{-\phi(N-n)x}$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU students should know the *form* of this model and its *limitations* (assumes equal fault severity and perfect fixes). It is a *direct quantification* of how defect reduction increases reliability — a key bridge to formal verification.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**Define a software defect. Differentiate between an *Error*, a *Defect*, and a *Failure* with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

A **software defect** is any imperfection or deficiency in a software work product that causes it to deviate from its specified requirements or to fail to meet user expectations (per IEEE 1044).

The three terms form a **cause–manifestation–observation chain**:

- **Error:** A human mistake during development. *Example:* A developer writes `i <= n` instead of `i < n` while implementing a loop boundary.
- **Defect:** The error frozen into the software artifact. *Example:* The source code now contains the incorrect `i <= n` condition. The defect exists statically in the file.
- **Failure:** The defect manifests as incorrect runtime behavior when the buggy code path is executed. *Example:* An array out-of-bounds exception is thrown when the input array has exactly $n$ elements.

> **[3 Marks Breakdown:** Definition 1M + Error/Defect/Failure 1M + Example 1M**]**

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**List and briefly explain any THREE primary causes of software defects.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Incomplete or Ambiguous Requirements:** Natural-language specifications are inherently imprecise. A requirement like "system shall be user-friendly" is unverifiable. Missing, contradictory, or unverifiable requirements are the **leading cause** of project failures.

2. **Programming Errors:** Mistakes in logic, syntax, type usage, or operator precedence. Examples include uninitialized variables, off-by-one errors, and wrong comparison operators.

3. **Time Pressure and Schedule Overruns:** When deadlines approach, teams often skip code reviews, reduce test coverage, and take architectural shortcuts — all of which introduce latent defects that surface post-release.

*(Any three from the seven-cause framework described in Section 2.2 are acceptable.)*

> **[3 Marks Breakdown:** 3 causes × (Statement 0.5M + Brief explanation 0.5M) = 3M**]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Adapted]

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the **Orthogonal Defect Classification (ODC)** scheme. Discuss its key dimensions and explain how ODC helps in defect prevention and process improvement.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** ODC, developed at IBM Research, is a classification scheme that categorizes defects along **multiple orthogonal (independent) dimensions**, allowing precise analysis of *what* was wrong, *where* it originated, *when* it was discovered, and *how severe* its impact is.

**Key Dimensions:**

1. **Class (What the defect IS):** Function, Interface, Timing, Data, Build, Documentation, Syntax, Assignment.
2. **Trigger (How it was EXPOSED):** Review, Unit Test, System Test, Field Use.
3. **Source (Where it was INTRODUCED):** Requirements, Design, Code, Test.
4. **Impact (Severity):** Critical, Major, Minor, Cosmetic.

**Why Orthogonal?**
The dimensions are statistically independent. A single defect can be classified simultaneously on every axis — e.g., *“Critical Function defect, triggered by Field Use, introduced in Design.”*

**Benefits:**
- **Defect Prevention:** Trigger–Class correlations reveal which *phases* introduce which *kinds* of defects.
- **Process Improvement:** If 70% of Interface defects are triggered at System Test, the team should introduce more integration-level reviews.
- **Process Capability Estimation:** ODC distribution profiles serve as a stable baseline across projects.

> **[7 Marks Breakdown:** Definition 1.5M + 4 dimensions 2.5M + Orthogonality 1M + Benefits 2M**]**

#### (b) [7 Marks] With suitable formulas, explain **Defect Density** and **Phase Amplification Factor**. A Java project of 12,000 LOC has 24 defects discovered in system testing and 4 more reported post-release. Compute the defect density. If a defect is allowed to propagate from the Requirements phase to Post-Release, calculate the cumulative cost amplification using PAFs 3, 3, 5, and 10.

**Model Answer:**

**Defect Density Formula:**
$$DD = \dfrac{N_d}{KLOC}$$
where $N_d$ = total number of defects and $KLOC$ = thousand lines of code. Industry benchmarks range from **0.1** (safety-critical) to **5.0** (commercial) defects per KLOC.

**Phase Amplification Factor (Boehm):**
$$FAF_{i \to j} = \prod_{k=i}^{j-1} PAF_k$$

**Step 1: Total defects**
$$N_d = 24 + 4 = 28 \text{ defects}$$

**Step 2: KLOC**
$$KLOC = \dfrac{12{,}000}{1000} = 12 \text{ KLOC}$$

**Step 3: Defect Density**
$$DD = \dfrac{28}{12} = 2.333 \text{ defects/KLOC}$$

This is *acceptable* for commercial software but unsuitable for safety-critical avionics.

**Step 4: Cumulative Cost Amplification (Req → Post-Release)**
$$FAF = 3 \times 3 \times 5 \times 10$$
$$FAF = 450$$

**Conclusion:** A single requirement-phase defect allowed to propagate to post-release costs **450× its original fix cost**. This justifies the use of Formal Methods in the earliest development phase.

> **[7 Marks Breakdown:** Formulas 2M + Defect density calc 2.5M + Amplification calc 2.5M**]**

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Adapted]

#### (a) [7 Marks] Discuss in detail the **seven primary causes of software defects** with one real-world example for each.

**Model Answer:**

| # | Cause | Real-World Example |
|---|-------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Incomplete/Ambiguous Requirements** | Therac-25 (1985–1987): Unspecified behavior under concurrent keystrokes led to lethal radiation overdoses. |
| 2 | **Incorrect Design** | Airbus A400M (2015): Incorrectly installed engine control software due to flawed integration design caused crash. |
| 3 | **Programming Errors** | Y2K Bug: Two-digit year variables causing date-rollover failures. |
| 4 | **Miscommunication** | Denver Airport Baggage System (1995): Mismatched contract between the automated conveyor vendor and the airport authority led to $1B+ cost overrun. |
| 5 | **System Complexity** | Ariane 5 Flight 501 (1996): 64-bit float converted to 16-bit signed int caused overflow; trajectory loss and explosion. |
| 6 | **Time Pressure** | Healthcare.gov (2013): Rushed deployment with insufficient testing led to system unavailability on launch day. |
| 7 | **Poor Tooling / Inadequate Testing** | Knight Capital Group (2012): Deployment of untested legacy code path caused $440M loss in 45 minutes. |

> **[7 Marks Breakdown:** 7 causes × (Definition 0.5M + Example 0.5M) = 7M**]**

#### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the **Boehm Cost Amplification Curve**. Why is fixing defects early crucial in software engineering? How do **Formal Methods** help in early defect prevention for the course PECST741?

**Model Answer:**

**Boehm Curve:** The cost of fixing a defect grows **exponentially** as it propagates to later phases. The relative costs across phases are:

$$\text{Req (1×)} \to \text{Design (3×)} \to \text{Code (9×)} \to \text{Unit Test (45×)} \to \text{Post-Release (450×)}$$

**Mathematical Expression:**
$$C_\text{phase} = C_\text{Req} \times \prod_{k=\text{Req}}^{\text{phase}-1} PAF_k$$

**Why Early Fixing is Crucial:**
1. **Cost:** Late defects cost orders of magnitude more (hardware recalls, lawsuits).
2. **Effort:** Late fixes require redesign, re-test, and re-deploy entire subsystems.
3. **Reputation:** Public failures erode customer trust (e.g., Boeing 737 MAX).
4. **Safety:** In medical/avionics systems, late defects are *catastrophic*.

**Role of Formal Methods (PECST741):**

- **Z/VDM/Alloy specifications** eliminate ambiguity in requirements *before* code is written.
- **B-Method refinement** mathematically proves that a design *refines* its specification.
- **Hoare logic** $\{P\} \; S \; \{Q\}$ proves code correctness *symbolically*.
- **Model checking** (SPIN, NuSMV) explores *all* system states to find safety violations.
- **Weakest precondition calculus** $wp(S, Q)$ derives preconditions for post-conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Formal Methods are the *only* known software engineering technique capable of achieving **defect densities below 0.1/KLOC** (acceptable for DO-178C DAL-A). They are, in effect, **mathematically rigorous prevention** rather than empirical detection.

> **[7 Marks Breakdown:** Boehm curve + math 2.5M + Why early 2M + Formal methods 2.5M**]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating Error and Failure:** They are *not* synonymous. Error = human thought; Failure = observed runtime behavior.
> 2. **Forgetting units in Defect Density:** Always express as *defects per KLOC*, not per LOC.
> 3. **Misremembering the Boehm ratios:** The 1-3-10-30-70-200-1000 sequence uses *empirical phase factors*; using them as if they were *cumulative* is a frequent error.
> 4. **Ignoring the "Trigger" dimension of ODC:** ODC's power lies in *correlating* Class with Trigger — examiners reward this insight.
> 5. **Omitting real-world examples:** KTU examiners explicitly award marks for *industry-validated examples* (Therac-25, Ariane 5, etc.).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Software Defects – Causes of Software Defects":**

- ✅ **Definition:** Defect = imperfection in a software artifact (IEEE 1044).
- ✅ **Error → Defect → Failure** is the *cause–manifestation–observation* chain.
- ✅ **ODC has 4 critical dimensions:** Class, Trigger, Source, Impact.
- ✅ **Seven primary causes:** Requirements, Design, Code, Communication, Complexity, Time Pressure, Tooling.
- ✅ **Defect Density Formula:** $DD = N_d / KLOC$.
- ✅ **Boehm PAF cumulative:** $FAF_{i \to j} = \prod PAF_k$.
- ✅ **Cost amplification:** 1× → 3× → 9× → 45× → 450× across waterfall phases.
- ✅ **Jelinski-Moranda model:** $\lambda_i = \phi (N - n_i)$; reliability grows as faults are fixed.
- ✅ **Industry benchmarks:** Commercial ≈ 2–5/KLOC; Safety-critical < 0.1/KLOC.
- ✅ **Formal Methods mitigate:** Ambiguity (Z/VDM), Interface mismatches (contracts), State explosion (model checking), Off-by-one (wp-calculus).
- ✅ **Shift-left principle:** Defect prevention is 100× cheaper than defect correction post-release.
- ✅ **Key examples to memorize:** Therac-25, Ariane 5, Knight Capital, Y2K, Denver Airport, Healthcare.gov.
- ✅ **Course link:** PECST741 emphasizes *formal specification as defect prevention*, not just testing.

topics.title: Techniques for Dealing with Software Defects - Testing and Verification, Formal Methods and Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST741 - FORMAL METHODS IN SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Techniques for Dealing with Software Defects - Testing and Verification, Formal Methods and Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Software Defects — The Formal Definition

A **software defect** (also called a *bug*, *fault*, or *error*) is any condition in a software product that does not satisfy a stated requirement, a user expectation, or a specification. Under the KTU 2024 Scheme, defects are classified rigorously along the **IEEE Standard 1044** taxonomy:

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** A *defect* is a flaw in any software artifact (requirements, design, code, test, documentation) that, when executed or interpreted, may produce an incorrect result, an incorrect side effect, or an unintended behaviour. The manifestation of a defect at runtime is called a **failure**, the defective internal state that causes it is the **fault**, and the human action that introduced it is the **error**.

### 1.2 The Three Faces of a Defect

| Term | What it is | Analogy |
|------|-----------|---------|
| **Error** | A human mistake (wrong thought, typo, miscalculation) | A surgeon nicking the wrong artery |
| **Fault (Defect/Bug)** | The static artefact carrying the mistake | The still-bleeding artery |
| **Failure** | The observable, runtime misbehaviour | The patient going into shock |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **fault may exist without ever causing a failure** if the faulty code path is never executed. This is the deepest reason why *testing alone* can never prove a system correct — it can only demonstrate the presence of faults under the executed cases.

## 1.3 The Defect Cost Escalation Curve (Boehm's Law)

> [!NOTE]
> **Barry Boehm's Empirical Finding (1981, reconfirmed 2024 NIST studies):** If a defect costs **1 unit** to fix at the *requirements stage*, it costs **5 units** at *design*, **10 units** at *coding*, **50 units** at *integration testing*, and up to **200 units** after *deployment* in safety-critical systems.

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Defect Mitigation

1. **Testing** — Executing the software with sample inputs to observe failures.
2. **Verification** — *Are we building the product right?* (Does the artefact conform to its specification?)
3. **Formal Methods** — Using mathematically rigorous notations, languages, and logical inference to *prove* properties of software before, during, or after construction.

## 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — "Building a Skyscraper"

Imagine you are an engineer building a 100-storey tower.

- **Testing** is like shaking a scale model on a shake-table *after* building it. You learn about *some* weaknesses, but you can never test every earthquake, wind, and weight combination.
- **Verification** is the design review: does the blueprint match the client's stated needs?
- **Formal Methods** is the mathematical proof: *"Given loads of up to 250 km/h wind, the maximum sway at the top will be ≤ 0.3 m, for all possible load distributions."* — a universal guarantee, not a sampled one.

## 1.6 Validation vs Verification — The KTU Favourite Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Verification** = *"Are we building the product right?"* — Conformance of an artefact to its specification. (Internal, technical.)
> - **Validation** = *"Are we building the right product?"* — Fitness of the software to the real user's needs. (External, user-oriented.)

This single line — *Verification vs Validation* — is one of the **highest-frequency 3-mark questions** in KTU ESE papers.

## 1.7 The V-Model of Software Engineering and Where Each Technique Sits

| V-Model Phase (Left) | Verification Activity | V-Model Phase (Right) |
|----------------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Requirements Specification | Reviews, formal specification (Z, B) | Acceptance Testing |
| System Design | Design reviews, model checking | System Testing |
| Detailed Design | Inspections, proof obligations | Integration Testing |
| Code | Static analysis, unit testing, theorem proving | Unit Testing |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Defect Equation (Quantitative Software Reliability)

Let $\lambda$ = fault density (faults per KLOC) and let $N$ = program size in KLOC. Then the **expected number of latent faults** is:

$$
E[F] = \lambda \cdot N
$$

For the **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** under an exponential failure model:

$$
\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda_{runtime}}
$$

where $\lambda_{runtime}$ is the runtime failure rate (failures per CPU-hour).

> [!NOTE]
> **Nelson's Model** for expected defects remaining after $n$ test cases:

$$
E[\text{Defects remaining}] = N \cdot \left(1 - \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \right)
$$

where $p_i$ is the probability that test case $i$ executes a *new* program path, and $N$ is the total number of paths.

## 2.2 The Fundamental Limitation of Testing — Dijkstra's Dictum

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Edsger W. Dijkstra (1970):** *"Program testing can be used to show the presence of bugs, but never to show their absence."*

Mathematically: testing samples the **input domain** $\mathcal{D}$. The set of test cases $T \subset \mathcal{D}$ is necessarily finite, while $\mathcal{D}$ is typically infinite (real numbers, strings, sequences). Therefore:

$$
\bigvee_{t \in T} \text{pass}(t) \;\not\Rightarrow\; \forall x \in \mathcal{D}: \text{pass}(x)
$$

## 2.3 Verification — The Logical Backbone

Verification is a **proof obligation**: given a specification $S$ and an implementation $I$, we want to show:

$$
S \models P \quad \text{and} \quad I \models S
$$

where $\models$ denotes *logical consequence*. Translated: "Every model of the specification satisfies the desired property $P$, and every behaviour of the implementation is a model of the specification."

The two directions of verification:

| Direction | Question | Tool Family |
|-----------|----------|-------------|
| **Forward Verification** ($S \Rightarrow I$) | Does the implementation satisfy the spec? | Theorem Provers (Isabelle, Coq, PVS) |
| **Backward Verification** ($I \Rightarrow S$) | Does the spec describe the implementation? | Refinement Checkers, Animation |

## 2.4 Formal Methods — The Family Tree

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KPU 2024-aligned):** *Formal methods* are mathematically based techniques for the specification, development, analysis, and verification of software (and hardware) systems. The mathematical foundation guarantees that reasoning is unambiguous, repeatable, and mechanizable.

### 2.4.1 Two Major Branches

| Branch | Mechanism | Representative Tool | Best For |
|--------|-----------|--------------------|----------|
| **Model Checking** | Exhaustive state-space search against a temporal-logic property | SPIN, NuSMV, UPPAAL | Finite-state concurrent systems, protocols |
| **Theorem Proving** | Constructing a machine-checked proof of correctness from axioms | Isabelle/HOL, Coq, ACL2, PVS | Algorithms, mathematics, security kernels |
| **Abstract Interpretation** | Sound over-approximation of program semantics | Astrée, PolySpace, Frama-C | Static analysis of C code |
| **Equivalence / Refinement Checking** | Bisimulation and refinement proofs | FDR, Rodin (Event-B) | Refinement-based development |
| **Alloy / SAT-based** | Translation to SAT/SMT, bounded search | Alloy Analyzer, CBMC | Lightweight specification, software design |

### 2.4.2 Lightweight vs Heavyweight Formal Methods

- **Lightweight** — used as one technique among many (type systems, model checkers on small abstractions, code-level static analysis).
- **Heavyweight** — the formal specification *is* the development artefact, and code is mechanically derived (e.g., the B-Method used in Paris Métro Line 14).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Key Expression / Phrase | Unit / Domain |
|---|---------|------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Defect cost escalation | $\text{Cost}(x) = 1\cdot e^{k \cdot \text{phase}}$ where $k$ increases with phase | Monetary units |
| 2 | E[F] latent faults | $E[F] = \lambda \cdot N$ | dimensionless count |
| 3 | MTTF | $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$ | CPU-hours |
| 4 | Nelson's residual defect model | $E[R] = N \cdot (1 - \sum p_i)$ | count |
| 5 | Verification | $I \models S$ | boolean (true/false) |
| 6 | Model checking | $M, s \models \varphi$ (CTL/LTL) | boolean |
| 7 | Dijkstra's limitation | $T \subset \mathcal{D} \Rightarrow$ partial | qualitative |
| 8 | V&V | Verification = right product *right*; Validation = *right* product | qualitative |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Airbus A380 flight-control software was developed using the B-Method; zero in-flight software-induced failures across millions of flight hours.
- **Rail:** Paris Métro Line 14 is driverless and relies on B-specifications.
- **Security:** seL4 microkernel — formally proven in Isabelle/HOL to enforce its security policy; ~7,500 lines of C with a 100% functional-correctness proof.
- **Hardware:** Intel, AMD, and IBM routinely use model checking and theorem proving for floating-point unit verification.
- **Automotive:** ISO 26262 (ASIL-D) explicitly recommends formal methods at the highest safety level.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — From Defect Density to Cost of Quality

We will derive the **total cost of software defects** as a function of the development phase at which they are detected and removed.

### Step 1 — Define the cost per defect at phase $i$

Let $c_i$ be the cost (in person-hours or monetary units) to detect and repair a single defect when the artefact is in phase $i \in \{0,1,2,3,4\}$ representing {Requirements, Design, Code, Integration, Deployment}.

Empirically, Boehm observed the ratio:

$$
c_0 : c_1 : c_2 : c_3 : c_4 = 1 : 5 : 10 : 50 : 200
$$

### Step 2 — Define the defect introduction rate

Let $\delta_i$ be the average number of defects introduced per KLOC during phase $i$, and let $S$ be the final system size in KLOC. Total defects introduced:

$$
D_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=0}^{4} \delta_i \cdot S
$$

### Step 3 — Define the cost of repairing all defects detected at phase $j$

Suppose a fraction $\alpha_{i,j}$ of defects introduced in phase $i$ is first *detected* in phase $j$ (with $j \ge i$, since defects cannot be detected before they exist). The total cost of all repairs in the project is:

$$
C_{\text{total}} = \sum_{j=0}^{4} \sum_{i=0}^{j} \alpha_{i,j} \cdot \delta_i \cdot S \cdot c_j
$$

### Step 4 — Apply a simplification (single-phase detection)

In the common KTU textbook scenario, defects introduced in phase $i$ are *first* detected in the immediately following phase, so $\alpha_{i,j} = 1$ if $j = i+1$ and $0$ otherwise:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{total}}
&= \sum_{i=0}^{3} \delta_i \cdot S \cdot c_{i+1} \\
&= S \left( \delta_0 c_1 + \delta_1 c_2 + \delta_2 c_3 + \delta_3 c_4 \right) \\
&= S \left( \delta_0 \cdot 5 + \delta_1 \cdot 10 + \delta_2 \cdot 50 + \delta_3 \cdot 200 \right) c_0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Quantify the savings from early detection

If we move the detection of *requirements defects* ($\delta_0$) from phase 1 to phase 0 (i.e., we catch them at the *same* phase they are introduced), the per-defect cost drops from $5c_0$ to $1c_0$. The savings factor is:

$$
\text{Savings} = \frac{5c_0 - 1c_0}{5c_0} = \frac{4}{5} = 80\%
$$

This *algebraically* justifies the **front-loading** strategy that motivates formal specification: a high-effort, mathematically rigorous requirements phase is cheaper than thousands of test runs after deployment.

## 3.2 Derivation — Why Testing Cannot Prove Absence of Defects

### Step 1 — Define the input domain

Let $\mathcal{D}$ be the set of all valid inputs to program $P$. For a function $f: \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{Z}$, $\mathcal{D} = \mathbb{Z}$ (countably infinite). For a real-valued function, $\mathcal{D} = \mathbb{R}$ (uncountably infinite).

### Step 2 — Define the test set

A test campaign selects a finite set $T = \{t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n\}$ with $n < \infty$.

### Step 3 — Show partial coverage

Even with $n$ tests, the *measured* correctness is:

$$
\text{Correct}_{\text{measured}} = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} \left( P(t_i) = f(t_i) \right)
$$

### Step 4 — The unmeasured gap

The *true* correctness condition is:

$$
\text{Correct}_{\text{true}} = \forall x \in \mathcal{D} : P(x) = f(x)
$$

### Step 5 — Logical inference

$$
\text{Correct}_{\text{measured}} \;\not\Rightarrow\; \text{Correct}_{\text{true}}
$$

because there may exist $x \in \mathcal{D} \setminus T$ such that $P(x) \neq f(x)$. The only way to bridge the gap is to **prove** Correct$_{\text{true}}$ directly — which is precisely what **formal verification** offers.

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Testing: } T \text{ finite} \Rightarrow \text{no universal guarantee.} \qquad
\text{Verification: } \text{proof} \Rightarrow \text{universal guarantee.}\;}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | Testing | Verification (Informal) | Formal Methods |
|-----------|---------|------------------------|----------------|
| **Mathematical basis** | Sampling theory | Reviews, walkthroughs | Predicate logic, set theory, process algebra |
| **Coverage of inputs** | Sampled, finite | N/A (manual review) | Universal (proof) or exhaustive (model check) |
| **Detects** | Failures | Defects in artefacts | Defects, plus proves their *absence* |
| **Scalability** | High | High | Low–medium (historically); improving |
| **Skill required** | Moderate | Moderate | High (specialist) |
| **Cost of application** | Linear in test count | Linear in review size | Often non-linear, can be exponential in state space |
| **Automation** | Partial | Low | High (model checkers, SMT solvers) |
| **Best fit** | Late-stage validation | All phases | Safety-/security-critical kernels, protocols, algorithms |

## 3.4 Worked Example — Model Checking the Mutual Exclusion Property

Consider two processes, $P_1$ and $P_2$, each with a critical section. The property to check is **mutual exclusion**: they are never *both* in the critical section at the same time.

**Specification (CTL):**

$$
\text{AG}\;\neg(\text{state}_1 = cs \wedge \text{state}_2 = cs)
$$

Read as: "For *all* computation paths (**A**), *globally* (**G**), it is *never* the case that both are in the critical section."

**Process state space (4 states each):**

$$
\mid S \mid = 4 \times 4 = 16 \text{ global states}
$$

**Transition relation** is defined by the atomic actions `enter`, `exit`. The model checker enumerates all reachable states and tests the CTL formula. Result: holds for the standard Peterson/Szymanski algorithms; fails for naïve flag-based implementations under certain interleavings.

The model checker returns either **TRUE** (property holds) or a **counter-example trace** (the sequence of states violating the property), which is one of its most powerful features.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Defect Lifecycle and the Three Mitigation Pillars

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Human Error] --> B[Fault Introduced in Artefact]
    B --> C{Activated by Input/Path?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Failure Observed at Runtime]
    C -- No --> E[Latent Fault Remains]
    D --> F[Testing Detects Failure]
    E --> G[Static Analysis / Verification]
    B --> H[Formal Specification Catches at Source]
    F --> I[Defect Removed and Re-tested]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Defect-FREE Software]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Verification Toolkit

```mermaid

        typeA[Type Checker]
    end

    subgraph ENGINE[REASONING ENGINE]

        tpA[Proof Assistant Coq Isabelle]
        absA[Abstract Interpreter Astrée]
    end

    subgraph DECIDE[DECISION MODULE]
        satA[SMT Solver Z3 CVC4]
        coA[Counter Example Generator]
        certA[Proof Certificate Builder]
    end

    OUTPUT1[VERDICT Property Holds]
    OUTPUT2[COUNTER EXAMPLE Trace]
    OUTPUT3[PROOF CERTIFICATE]

    specA --> parserA
    implA --> parserA
    propA --> parserA
    parserA --> typeA
    typeA --> mcA
    typeA --> tpA
    typeA --> absA

    tpA --> satA
    absA --> satA

```

## 4.3 The V-Model with Verification and Testing Activities Mapped

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LEFT[DECOMPOSITION LEFT SIDE]
        L1[Requirements]
        L2[System Design]
        L3[Detailed Design]
        L4[Coding]
    end

    subgraph RIGHT[INTEGRATION RIGHT SIDE]
        R1[Acceptance Testing]
        R2[System Testing]
        R3[Integration Testing]
        R4[Unit Testing]
    end

    L1 --- R1
    L2 --- R2
    L3 --- R3
    L4 --- R4

    L1 --> V1[Formal Specification Z Alloy]
    L2 --> V2[Model Checking of Architectural Model]
    L3 --> V3[Design Reviews Refinement Proofs]
    L4 --> V4[Static Analysis Theorem Proving]
```

## 4.4 Where Each Tool Sits in the Development Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REQ[Requirements Text] --> FS[Formal Spec in Z or B]
    FS --> MC[Model Check with SPIN NuSMV]
    FS --> TP[Prove Properties in Coq Isabelle]

    TP --> RS
    RS --> CODE[Generated or Hand Coded C Java]
    CODE --> SA[Static Analysis Astrée FramaC]
    SA --> BIN[Verified Binary]
    CODE --> TEST[Traditional Unit Integration Tests]
    TEST --> BIN
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between verification and validation in software engineering. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Verification | Validation |
|--------|--------------|------------|
| Question answered | Are we building the product **right**? | Are we building the **right** product? |
| Focus | Conformance to specification | Fitness for use in the real world |
| Performed by | Developers, QA engineers | End users, domain experts, customers |
| Artefact examined | Intermediate artefacts (design, code) | Final product in operational environment |
| Example | Checking that a function returns the sum of two integers as per its API contract | Demonstrating to a hospital that the patient-management system supports the doctor's daily workflow |

*(Full credit: 3 marks for a clear two-row table or two crisp paragraphs. KTU evaluator looks for the **right product / right product** distinction.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Why can software testing never prove the absence of defects? State Dijkstra's quote and explain with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

Dijkstra (1970): *"Program testing can be used to show the presence of bugs, but never to show their absence."*

**Explanation:** A test set $T$ is a finite subset of the (typically infinite) input domain $\mathcal{D}$. If all tests in $T$ pass, we have shown that those particular inputs are handled correctly, but the inputs in $\mathcal{D} \setminus T$ remain unexamined. For example, if we test a square-root function with inputs 0, 1, 4, 9, 16, we have only covered the perfect squares. Inputs like 2, 3, 5, 7 may still expose a defect. Hence testing yields *necessary* but not *sufficient* evidence of correctness.

*(Full credit: 3 marks — Dijkstra quote (1), logical reasoning (1), example (1).)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a)** Discuss the various techniques used to deal with software defects. Compare the relative effectiveness of **testing**, **informal verification**, and **formal methods** along the dimensions of coverage, cost, and scalability. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **V-Model** of software development. Map each verification and validation activity to its corresponding phase, and identify at which phase **formal specification** yields the highest return on investment. Justify your answer with Boehm's defect cost data. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. Testing**
- Mechanism: Execute the program on sample inputs and compare actual vs expected outputs.
- Coverage: Sampled; partial over the input domain.
- Cost: Linear in number of test cases; tooling (JUnit, Selenium) is mature.
- Scalability: High.
- Limitation: Cannot prove absence of defects (Dijkstra).

**2. Informal Verification**
- Mechanism: Walkthroughs, inspections, desk-checks, peer reviews.
- Coverage: Artefact-by-artefact; depends on human rigour.
- Cost: Moderate; mainly human-time.
- Scalability: High for small artefacts, drops for large systems.
- Limitation: Subjective; effectiveness varies with reviewer expertise.

**3. Formal Methods**
- Mechanism: Mathematical specification, model checking, theorem proving, abstract interpretation.
- Coverage: Universal (proof) or exhaustive (model check) over the chosen model.
- Cost: High up-front; pays off for safety-/security-critical systems.
- Scalability: Improving rapidly; modern SMT solvers (Z3, CVC5) and cloud-based provers handle millions of LOC.

**Comparative Table (to be drawn in the answer book):**

| Dimension | Testing | Informal Verification | Formal Methods |
|-----------|---------|----------------------|----------------|
| Coverage | Sampled | Artefact-level | Universal / exhaustive |
| Cost | Low–moderate | Moderate | High up-front |
| Scalability | Excellent | Moderate | Improving |
| Proof of absence | No | No | Yes |
| Automation | Partial | Low | High |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**V-Model Diagram:** Draw the classic V with *Requirements*, *System Design*, *Detailed Design*, *Code* on the descending left arm and *Acceptance*, *System*, *Integration*, *Unit Testing* on the ascending right arm. (A clear labelled ASCII-V is acceptable in KTU answer sheets.)

**Mapping:**

- Requirements ↔ Acceptance Testing
- System Design ↔ System Testing
- Detailed Design ↔ Integration Testing
- Code ↔ Unit Testing

**Where formal specification pays off most — the *Requirements* phase:**
By Boehm's data, fixing a defect at the requirements phase costs **1 unit**, while the same defect costs **50 units** if detected at integration and **200 units** if discovered after deployment. Formal specification (Z, B, Alloy) is a *requirements-engineering* activity: it forces early detection of ambiguity, inconsistency, and incompleteness. Hence the **return on investment is maximal at the requirements phase**, exactly where defects are cheapest to fix and most expensive to leave in.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** What are **formal methods**? List and briefly explain the four major categories of formal methods tools with one representative tool for each category. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A railway signalling system has 4 possible states per track segment and 12 track segments. Estimate the size of the global state space. If model checking explores 10 million states per second, how long will exhaustive verification take? Comment on the **state-space explosion problem** and one practical mitigation. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition:** Formal methods are mathematically rigorous techniques and tools used for the specification, design, verification, and validation of software and hardware systems. They are based on formal languages with mathematically defined syntax and semantics, and logical inference systems whose derivations are mechanically checkable.

**Four major categories:**

1. **Model Checkers** — Exhaustive state-space exploration against a temporal-logic property. *Example:* **SPIN** (for Promela models of concurrent systems), **NuSMV** (for hardware and synchronous systems).

2. **Theorem Provers / Proof Assistants** — Interactive construction of machine-checked proofs from axioms. *Examples:* **Isabelle/HOL**, **Coq**, **ACL2**, **PVS**.

3. **Abstract Interpreters** — Sound static analysis by over-approximating program semantics. *Examples:* **Astrée** (embedded C), **Frama-C** (C with ACSL contracts), **PolySpace**.

4. **Specification & SAT/SMT-based Tools** — Lightweight formal modelling and bounded verification using SAT/SMT solvers. *Examples:* **Alloy Analyzer**, **CBMC** (C Bounded Model Checker), **B-method / Rodin**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Global state space:**

$$
\mid S \mid = 4^{12} = 16\,777\,216 \text{ states}
$$

**Time to explore at 10 million states/second:**

$$
T = \frac{16\,777\,216}{10\,7} \approx 1.68 \text{ seconds}
$$

(where $10^{7}$ is 10 million).

**State-space explosion problem:**
When components are added in parallel, the global state space grows **exponentially** (here, $4^n$ in the number of segments $n$). For larger systems (e.g., $n = 30$), the state space becomes $4^{30} \approx 10^{18}$, which is physically infeasible to explore exhaustively — even at $10^9$ states/sec, it would take thousands of years.

**Practical mitigation — *Partial-Order Reduction* (used in SPIN):**
Only one representative from each equivalence class of *interleavings* of independent transitions is explored. For a system with $k$ concurrent processes, this often reduces the explored state space by a factor exponential in $k$. Other mitigations include **symbolic model checking** (BDDs in NuSMV), **abstraction**, and **compositional reasoning**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. Many students confuse *verification* with *validation* — losing 1 mark on a 3-mark question.
> 2. In numerical questions, **forgetting to exponentiate correctly** (writing $4 \times 12 = 48$ instead of $4^{12}$) is the most common mistake.
> 3. When asked to "list" formal methods tools, students frequently write only the *names* without the *category* they belong to — which costs 2 marks in a 7-mark sub-part.
> 4. The V-Model answer must include a **labelled diagram**; a description without a diagram loses at least 1 mark.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Software defect** = fault that may cause a **failure**; introduced by a human **error**.
- **Boehm's cost ratio:** $1 : 5 : 10 : 50 : 200$ across requirements → deployment.
- **Verification** = building the product *right* (artefact vs specification).
- **Validation** = building the *right* product (artefact vs user need).
- **Dijkstra's limitation:** testing shows presence, never absence, of defects.
- **Formal methods** = mathematically rigorous specification, development, and verification techniques.
- **Four tool categories:** model checkers, theorem provers, abstract interpreters, SAT/SMT-based tools.
- **State-space explosion:** global state count is exponential in the number of components.
- **Mitigations:** partial-order reduction, symbolic BDDs, abstraction, compositional verification.
- **V-Model:** pairs each development phase with a corresponding test phase; formal specification is most beneficial at the *requirements* phase.
- **Industrial adopters:** Airbus (B), Paris Metro (B), seL4 kernel (Isabelle), Intel/AMD hardware (model checking).
- **Lightweight vs heavyweight formal methods:** lightweight is one tool among many; heavyweight uses formal specification as the primary development artefact.
- **Front-loading principle:** a high up-front formal-specification effort is amortized over the entire project life cycle.
- **CTL/LTL** are the two principal temporal-logic families used in model checking.
- **SMT solvers** (Z3, CVC5) are the workhorses behind most modern verification tools.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conceptual Modelling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST741 - FORMAL METHODS IN SOFTWARE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Conceptual Modelling # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conceptual Modelling** is the process of building an **abstract, formal representation** of a real-world system, domain, or phenomenon using mathematical and logical constructs, *independently* of any implementation concerns, programming language, or hardware platform. In the context of **Formal Methods in Software Engineering**, a conceptual model is a **precise, unambiguous description** of: 1. The **entities** (objects) that exist in the problem domain. 2. The **attributes** (properties) that describe those entities. 3. The **relationships** (associations) between entities. 4.
