The **constraints** (invariants, rules) that the system must always satisfy. 5. The **operations** (behaviour) that may change the state of the system. The output of conceptual modelling is typically a **formal specification** written in a mathematical notation such as **Z (Zed)**, **VDM (Vienna Development Method)**, **B-Method**, **OBJ**, **Alloy**, or a graphical semi-formal language like **UML Class Diagrams**, **Entity-Relationship (ER) Diagrams**, or **Object-Role Models (ORM)**. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PECST741 Module 2):** Conceptual modelling sits at the *front-end* of the formal specification pipeline. The model produced here is later **refined** into a concrete design, then **implemented** in code. The "conceptual" qualifier means the model captures *what* the system does, not *how* it does it. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Architect's Blueprint" Imagine you want to build a new **airport terminal** in Kerala. | Real-World Stage | Engineering Equivalent | | :--- | :--- | | Architect's 2D drawings showing walls, doors, corridors, dimensions, fire exits | **Conceptual Model** (entities, relationships, constraints) | | Structural engineer's load calculations and steel reinforcement plan | **Formal Specification** in Z / VDM (mathematical detail) | | Construction by masons, electricians, plumbers | **Implementation** in Java / C++ / Python | | The finished, painted, furnished terminal | **Deployed Software System** | Before a single brick is laid, the architect produces a **conceptual model** of the terminal. This model: * Shows **rooms** (entities). * Labels each room with **purpose and capacity** (attributes). * Shows **corridors connecting rooms** (relationships). * Enforces rules like *"every fire exit must be ≤ 30 m from any point"* (constraints). > **Crucially, the blueprint does NOT say:** *which brand of cement to use, what colour the walls will be, or which subcontractor builds Block B.* Those are **implementation** concerns. **Software conceptual modelling is exactly the same discipline — applied to a software system's domain.** --- ## 1.3 Why Conceptual Modelling? (The Engineering "Why") > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** A common 3-mark question asks *"Why do we need conceptual modelling before writing specifications?"*. Memorise these three reasons verbatim. 1. **Abstraction** — Hides accidental complexity, allowing engineers to reason about correctness *before* coding begins. 2. **Communication** — Provides a shared, unambiguous vocabulary between **domain experts** (e.g., bank managers) and **software engineers**. 3. **Verification** — Mathematical models can be **proved** (using theorem provers) or **checked** (using model checkers) to satisfy safety, security, and correctness properties. --- ## 1.4 Position in the Formal Methods Lifecycle The conceptual model is the **first formal artefact** produced in the typical formal methods workflow: $$\text{Domain Analysis} \;\longrightarrow\; \boxed{\text{Conceptual Model}} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Formal Specification} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Refinement} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Implementation} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Verification} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Validation}$$ # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Pillars of a Conceptual Model Every conceptual model — whether expressed in Z, VDM, UML, or ORM — is built from the same four logical pillars. Understanding these is essential for any KTU Module 2 question. ### Pillar 1 — **Entities (Objects / Instances)** Entities are the "things" that exist in the domain. * **Example:** `Student`, `Course`, `Library`, `BankAccount`. * Formally, an entity is a member of a **set**: $\text{Student} \in \mathbb{S}$ where $\mathbb{S}$ is the universe of all students. ### Pillar 2 — **Attributes (Properties / Fields)** Attributes characterise entities. * **Example:** A `Student` has a `name`, `rollNo`, `cgpa`. * Formally, an attribute is a **function** mapping an entity to a value: $$\text{cgpa} : \text{Student} \rightarrow \mathbb{R} \quad \text{where} \; 0.0 \leq \text{cgpa}(s) \leq 10.0$$ ### Pillar 3 — **Relationships (Associations)** Relationships connect entities. * **Example:** A `Student` *enrols in* a `Course`. * Formally, a relationship is a **Cartesian product** of sets: $$\text{Enrolment} \subseteq \text{Student} \times \text{Course}$$ ### Pillar 4 — **Constraints (Invariants)** Constraints are conditions that must *always* hold. * **Example:** *"No student may enrol in more than 6 courses in a semester."* * Formally, a constraint is a **predicate** over the model: $$\forall s : \text{Student} \;\bullet\; \vert \{ c : \text{Course} \mid (s, c) \in \text{Enrolment} \} \vert \leq 6$$ > [!NOTE] > The symbol $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes the **cardinality** (size) of a set. It is rendered using `\vert` in LaTeX to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. --- ## 2.2 Mathematical Foundations Used in Conceptual Modelling Conceptual modelling rests on three classical mathematical pillars. Every KTU Module 2 question is built on these. ### (a) Set Theory (Naïve or Zermelo-Fraenkel) Set theory provides the vocabulary for collections of entities. | Symbol | LaTeX | Meaning | Example in Modelling | | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- | | $x \in S$ | `\in` | Membership | `rahul ∈ Student` | | $S \subseteq T$ | `\subseteq` | Subset | `Enrolled ⊆ Student` | | $S \cup T$ | `\cup` | Union | All students OR all staff | | $S \cap T$ | `\cap` | Intersection | Students who are also staff | | $S \setminus T$ | `\setminus` | Set difference | Students who are not staff | | $S \times T$ | `\times` | Cartesian product | `Student × Course` | | $\mathcal{P}(S)$ | `\mathcal{P}` | Power set | All possible groups of students | | $\vert S \vert$ | `\vert S \vert` | Cardinality | Number of students | ### (b) First-Order Predicate Logic (FOPL) FOPL provides the language for stating **facts** and **rules** about entities. | Logical Symbol | LaTeX | Read As | Use in Modelling | | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- | | $\forall$ | `\forall` | "For all" | "For every student..." | | $\exists$ | `\exists` | "There exists" | "Some student exists such that..." | | $\neg$ | `\neg` | NOT | "is not enrolled" | | $\wedge$ | `\wedge` | AND | Combine conditions | | $\vee$ | `\vee` | OR | Alternative cases | | $\rightarrow$ | `\rightarrow` | IMPLIES | "If enrolled, then has rollNo" | | $\leftrightarrow$ | `\leftrightarrow` | IFF | Equivalent statements | ### (c) Relations and Functions Relations describe how entities link; functions describe deterministic mappings. | Concept | Formal Definition | Modelling Use | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Binary relation** $R$ | $R \subseteq A \times B$ | `Enrols : Student ↔ Course` | | **Function** $f$ | $f : A \rightarrow B$ | `rollNo : Student → ℕ` (each student has exactly one roll number) | | **Partial function** | $f : A \nrightarrow B$ | `guardian : Student ⇸ Person` (some students have no guardian in DB) | | **Total function** | $f : A \rightarrow B$ | Defined for every input | | **Injection** | $f : A \nrightarrow B$ injective | `rollNo` uniquely identifies a student | | **Sequence** | $\text{seq } A$ | Ordered list, e.g., timetable | --- ## 2.3 The Z Notation — A Working Example **Z** is the formal language most commonly referenced in KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2. A Z schema has two parts: a **declaration** (top) and a **predicate** (bottom). $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Student} \;\hat{=}\; \left[\, \text{name} : \text{STRING};\;\; \text{rollNo} : \mathbb{N};\;\; \text{cgpa} : \mathbb{R} \;\mid\; \text{cgpa} \geq 0 \;\wedge\; \text{cgpa} \leq 10 \,\right] \\ &\text{Enrolment} \;\hat{=}\; \left[\, \text{student} : \text{Student};\;\; \text{course} : \text{Course} \;\mid\; \text{student.cgpa} \geq 5.0 \,\right] \end{aligned} $$ Reading the schema: * The **declaration part** (above the bar) introduces the variables and their types. * The **predicate part** (below the bar) lists the constraints. --- ## 2.4 Properties of a "Good" Conceptual Model | Property | Definition | Why It Matters (KTU) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Correctness** | Faithfully represents the real domain | Avoids costly re-specification | | **Completeness** | Captures all relevant entities, relations, constraints | No missing edge-cases | | **Consistency** | No internal contradictions | Enables formal proof | | **Unambiguity** | Exactly one interpretation | The *raison d'être* of formal methods | | **Minimality** | No redundant entities or relations | Easier to maintain | | **Abstraction** | Independent of implementation | Survives technology change | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** Conceptual models are used in **safety-critical systems** (avionics, railway signalling, medical devices) where the cost of a software defect is measured in human lives. Standards like **DO-178C** (aviation) and **IEC 61508** (industrial control) mandate formal conceptual modelling. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 The Five-Stage Construction of a Conceptual Model (Detailed) This is the **most important derivation** for KTU Module 2 — examiners routinely award 7 marks for this procedure. ### Stage 1 — Domain Identification Identify the **universe of discourse** (the slice of the real world the system will handle). > *Example (KTU Lab Library System):* The domain is the management of **books**, **members**, and **loans** in a college library. Formally, define a top-level set: $$ \text{Domain} = \{ \text{Book},\; \text{Member},\; \text{Loan},\; \text{Author},\; \text{Category} \} $$ --- ### Stage 2 — Entity (Type) Declaration For each item in the domain, declare a **basic type** (an unstructured set of identifiers). $$ \begin{aligned} &[\text{BOOK},\; \text{MEMBER},\; \text{CATEGORY}] \\ &\text{Author} : \mathbb{P}_1\; \text{BOOK} \quad \text{(non-empty set of authors)} \end{aligned} $$ Explanation: $\mathbb{P}_1$ denotes the set of *non-empty* subsets (so the library always has at least one author known). --- ### Stage 3 — Attribute (State Schema) Declaration Define a **state schema** — a named cluster of related variables and their constraints. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{LibraryState} \;\hat{=}\; [\; &\text{stock} : \text{BOOK} \nrightarrow \mathbb{N}; \\ &\text{loans} : \text{MEMBER} \leftrightarrow \text{BOOK}; \\ &\text{catalogue} : \text{BOOK} \rightarrow \text{CATEGORY} \mid \\ &\forall b : \text{BOOK} \;\bullet\; b \in \operatorname{dom}\, \text{catalogue} \;\wedge\; \text{stock}(b) \geq 0 \;] \end{aligned} $$ Reading the constraints: * Every book in the system must appear in the catalogue: $\forall b : \text{BOOK} \;\bullet\; b \in \operatorname{dom}\, \text{catalogue}$. * Stock count is never negative: $\text{stock}(b) \geq 0$. --- ### Stage 4 — Relationship (Association) Derivation Derive higher-level relations from the basic types and state. > **Goal:** "A member is *eligible* to borrow a book if they have fewer than 3 active loans." $$ \begin{aligned} \text{EligibleToBorrow} : \text{MEMBER} \leftrightarrow \text{BOOK} \;\;\hat{=}\;\; \{ (m, b) \mid & \; m \in \text{MEMBER} \;\wedge\; b \in \text{BOOK} \;\wedge \\ & \vert \{ b' : \text{BOOK} \mid (m, b') \in \text{loans} \} \vert < 3 \;\wedge \\ & \text{stock}(b) > 0 \;\} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > This is a **derived relationship**. It is *defined* (with the `==` symbol) rather than *declared* (with the `:` symbol), because it can be computed from existing state. --- ### Stage 5 — Operation (Behavioural Schema) Derivation Define the **operations** that transform the state. Each operation schema has three parts: * **Δ (Delta) declaration** — which state variables change. * **Inputs** — new information from the outside world. * **Predicate** — pre-conditions (inputs satisfy) and post-conditions (state after). **Operation: Issue a loan** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{IssueLoan} \;\hat{=}\; [\; &\Delta \text{LibraryState}; \\ &m? : \text{MEMBER};\;\; b? : \text{BOOK} \mid \\ &(m?, b?) \in \text{EligibleToBorrow} \;\wedge \\ &\text{stock}' = \text{stock} \oplus \{ b? \mapsto \text{stock}(b?) - 1 \} \;\wedge \\ &\text{loans}' = \text{loans} \cup \{ (m?, b?) \} \;] \end{aligned} $$ **Line-by-line analysis:** | Schema Line | Meaning | | :--- | :--- | | $\Delta \text{LibraryState}$ | The state variables `stock` and `loans` may change; unprimed = before, primed = after | | $m? : \text{MEMBER}$ | `m?` is an input parameter (the borrowing member) | | $b? : \text{BOOK}$ | `b?` is an input parameter (the requested book) | | $(m?, b?) \in \text{EligibleToBorrow}$ | **Pre-condition:** member is eligible to borrow this book | | $\text{stock}' = \text{stock} \oplus \{ b? \mapsto \text{stock}(b?) - 1 \}$ | **Post-condition:** stock of `b?` is decremented by 1 (the $\oplus$ operator overrides one entry of the function) | | $\text{loans}' = \text{loans} \cup \{ (m?, b?) \}$ | **Post-condition:** the new loan pair is added to the loans relation | --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Engineering Concepts to Formal Constructs | Engineering Concept (Plain English) | Formal Construct (Z / VDM / Logic) | LaTeX Notation | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | "A set of items exists" | Set declaration | $A : \mathbb{P}\,T$ | | "Every element satisfies a rule" | Universal quantification | $\forall x : T \;\bullet\; P(x)$ | | "At least one element satisfies a rule" | Existential quantification | $\exists x : T \;\bullet\; P(x)$ | | "Two things are linked" | Pair / relation membership | $(a, b) \in R$ | | "Each input produces exactly one output" | Function | $f : A \rightarrow B$ | | "The output may not exist" | Partial function | $f : A \nrightarrow B$ | | "An attribute has a default value" | Schema with predicate | `field = default` | | "State may change" | $\Delta$-schema | $\Delta \text{State}$ | | "State does not change" | $\Xi$-schema | $\Xi \text{State}$ | | "After the operation, this holds" | Primed variable in predicate | $\text{field}' = \ldots$ | | "Count of items" | Cardinality | $\vert S \vert$ | | "Ordered list" | Sequence type | $\text{seq}\; T$ | | "Multi-set / duplicates allowed" | Bag type | $\text{bag}\; T$ | --- ## 3.3 Worked Example — Constraint Simplification **Problem (typical KTU Part B):** "In a hostel, each room is assigned to **at most one student**, and each student is assigned to **exactly one room**. Express these constraints formally." **Step 1 — Declare basic types.** $$ [\text{ROOM}, \text{STUDENT}] $$ **Step 2 — Declare the allocation relation.** $$ \text{Alloc} : \text{STUDENT} \nrightarrow \text{ROOM} $$ The $\nrightarrow$ (partial function) already encodes *"each student is assigned to at most one room"* because a function can map each input to at most one output. **Step 3 — Encode the additional constraint that the assignment is total (every student gets a room).** $$ \forall s : \text{STUDENT} \;\bullet\; s \in \operatorname{dom}\, \text{Alloc} $$ **Step 4 — Encode "at most one student per room" (injectivity).** $$ \forall s_1, s_2 : \text{STUDENT};\;\; r : \text{ROOM} \mid (s_1, r) \in \text{Alloc} \;\wedge\; (s_2, r) \in \text{Alloc} \;\bullet\; s_1 = s_2 $$ **Step 5 — Combined final predicate in the state schema.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{HostelState} \;\hat{=}\; [\; &\text{Alloc} : \text{STUDENT} \nrightarrow \text{ROOM} \mid \\ &\operatorname{dom}\, \text{Alloc} = \text{STUDENT} \;\wedge \\ &\forall s_1, s_2 : \text{STUDENT};\;\; r : \text{ROOM} \mid (s_1, r) \in \text{Alloc} \;\wedge\; (s_2, r) \in \text{Alloc} \;\bullet\; s_1 = s_2 \;] \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > **Pro tip for KTU marks:** The single character $\nrightarrow$ (partial function arrow) is worth **2 marks** on its own when correctly used to express "at most one." Don't waste an entire $\forall$ clause on a constraint that the function type already provides for free. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Conceptual Modelling Pipeline (Block-Level Architecture) The following block diagram shows how the conceptual model fits into the broader formal methods workflow, with the data/control flow between stages. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Domain
Expert Input] --> nodeB[Stage 1
Domain
Identification] nodeB --> nodeC[Stage 2
Entity
Declaration] nodeC --> nodeD[Stage 3
State Schema
and Attributes] nodeD --> nodeE[Stage 4
Relationship
Derivation] nodeE --> nodeF[Stage 5
Operation
Schema] nodeF --> nodeG{Formal
Verification} nodeG -->|Pass| nodeH[Refined
Design Model] nodeG -->|Fail| nodeC nodeH --> nodeI[Implementation
in Target Language] nodeI --> nodeJ[Deployed
System] nodeA -.feedback.-> nodeC ``` **Reading the diagram:** * The top row (`A → B → C → D → E → F`) is the **left-to-right forward construction** of the conceptual model. * Node `G` is the **formal verification gate** (proof obligations discharged using a theorem prover or model checker). * The `G → C` backward arrow represents the **re-specification loop** when verification fails. * The dashed feedback loop from `A` to `C` represents **domain-expert consultation** when ambiguities surface during modelling. --- ## 4.2 Entity-Relationship Schematic for the Library Example A graphical ER view of the same library domain expressed in Section 3.1: ```mermaid erDiagram MEMBER ||--o{ LOAN : "raises" BOOK ||--o{ LOAN : "is lent in" BOOK }o--|| CATEGORY : "belongs to" BOOK }o--o{ AUTHOR : "written by" MEMBER { string memberID string name date joinDate } BOOK { string isbn string title int stock } CATEGORY { string catCode string catName } AUTHOR { string authorID string authorName } LOAN { date issueDate date dueDate bool returned } ``` **Reading the cardinality notation:** * `||` — exactly one (mandatory, unique). * `o{` — zero or many (optional, multiple). * `}o` — one or many (mandatory, multiple). * `o|` — zero or one (optional, unique). For example, `MEMBER ||--o{ LOAN` means **one member raises zero-or-many loans**, while `BOOK ||--o{ LOAN` means **one book appears in zero-or-many loans** (an unused book has zero loans, a popular book has many). --- ## 4.3 Conceptual Model Refinement Pyramid This diagram captures the **abstraction hierarchy** of formal specification languages, with conceptual modelling at the base. ```mermaid graph TD nodeP[Executable Code
Java / C++ / Python] nodeO[Refined Design
Z with Implementation Notes] nodeN[Formal Specification
Z / VDM / B Schemas] nodeM[Conceptual Model
Domain Entities and Constraints] nodeK[Informal Requirements
Plain English Use Cases] nodeK --> nodeM nodeM --> nodeN nodeN --> nodeO nodeO --> nodeP style nodeM fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#d79b00,stroke-width:3px ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The highlighted node (`nodeM` — Conceptual Model) is **our topic**. It sits *above* informal requirements but *below* the full formal specification. The conceptual model is **almost formal** — it uses the same mathematical vocabulary as a full Z specification, but stops short of declaring every implementation detail (e.g., concrete data structures, algorithms, error-handling routines). --- ## 4.4 The Three Layers of a Conceptual Model ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph L1[Static Layer - What exists] nodeA1[Entity Sets] nodeA2[Attributes] nodeA3[Structural Relationships] end subgraph L2[Constraint Layer - What must hold] nodeB1[Invariants] nodeB2[Domain Restrictions] nodeB3[Cardinality Rules] end subgraph L3[Behavioural Layer - What can change] nodeC1[Operations / Transitions] nodeC2[Pre-conditions] nodeC3[Post-conditions] end L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 ``` The three layers must be developed in this order: **define the static structure first, then the constraints that govern it, then the operations that evolve it.** Attempting to specify behaviour before pinning down the static structure is a common KTU pitfall (see Section 5 warning). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **"Differentiate between a conceptual model and a formal specification. Give one example of each."** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Conceptual Model | Formal Specification | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Purpose** | Captures *what* the system represents (domain semantics) | Captures *what* the system *must do* in mathematical detail | | **Level of detail** | Abstract — omits implementation | Detailed — fully precise, ready for refinement | | **Example** | A UML class diagram of a `Library` showing `Book`, `Member`, `Loan` entities and their associations | A Z schema with declared types, invariants, and pre/post-conditions for an `IssueLoan` operation | | **Audience** | Domain experts + software engineers | Software engineers + theorem provers | > **Mark split (typical KTU key):** 1 mark for the conceptual-model column, 1 mark for the formal-spec column, 1 mark for the example. Use a **table** — examiners reward visual structure. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **"List any four mathematical foundations used in conceptual modelling and state the role of each."** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer (each item = 0.75 marks):** 1. **Set Theory** — Provides the vocabulary for collections of entities (e.g., `Student`, `Book`). 2. **First-Order Predicate Logic (FOPL)** — Provides quantifiers ($\forall$, $\exists$) and connectives ($\wedge$, $\vee$, $\neg$, $\rightarrow$) to express constraints. 3. **Relations and Functions** — Describe associations between entities (`Enrols : Student ↔ Course`) and deterministic mappings (`rollNo : Student → ℕ`). 4. **Sequence / Bag types** — Model ordered or duplicate-allowed collections (e.g., transaction history). > **Examiner note:** A *fourth* valid foundation you may add is **Type Theory** (used in languages like Alloy and dependent-type systems). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) > **KTU Rule:** In the End Semester Examination, every Module 2 question offers an internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Answer exactly **one**. Each question has two sub-parts: **(a) for 7 marks** and **(b) for 7 marks**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### **Part (a) — 7 marks** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]** *"For a hospital appointment system, identify the domain entities, their attributes, and one significant relationship. Express the conceptual model using Z notation. State the type of each attribute."* #### **Model Solution — Part (a):** **Step 1 — Basic type declarations.** $$ [\text{PATIENT},\; \text{DOCTOR},\; \text{APPOINTMENT},\; \text{DEPARTMENT}] $$ **Step 2 — Entity schemas with attributes.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Patient} \;\hat{=}\;& [\; \text{pid} : \text{PATIENT};\;\; \text{name} : \text{STRING};\;\; \text{age} : \mathbb{N};\;\; \text{contact} : \mathbb{N} \mid \\ & \text{age} \geq 0 \;\wedge\; \text{age} \leq 130 \;] \\ \text{Doctor} \;\hat{=}\;& [\; \text{did} : \text{DOCTOR};\;\; \text{name} : \text{STRING};\;\; \text{specialisation} : \text{STRING} \mid \\ & \text{specialisation} \neq \langle \rangle \;] \\ \text{Appointment} \;\hat{=}\;& [\; \text{aid} : \text{APPOINTMENT};\;\; \text{date} : \mathbb{N};\;\; \text{slot} : \mathbb{N} \mid \\ & 1 \leq \text{date} \leq 31 \;\wedge\; 9 \leq \text{slot} \leq 17 \;] \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Relationship (a doctor may have many appointments, a patient may have many appointments — many-to-many).** $$ \text{HasAppt} : \text{PATIENT} \leftrightarrow \text{DOCTOR} $$ **Step 4 — State schema assembling the model.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{HospitalState} \;\hat{=}\;& [\; \text{appts} : \text{APPOINTMENT} \rightarrow (\text{PATIENT} \times \text{DOCTOR}); \\ & \text{dept} : \text{DOCTOR} \rightarrow \text{DEPARTMENT} \mid \\ & \forall a : \text{APPOINTMENT} \;\bullet\; a \in \operatorname{dom}\, \text{appts} \;] \end{aligned} $$ > **Mark allocation:** (i) Basic types — 1 mark, (ii) Two entity schemas — 2 marks, (iii) Relationship declaration — 1 mark, (iv) State schema with constraint — 2 marks, (v) Correct use of Z notation syntax — 1 mark. --- #### **Part (b) — 7 marks** *"Add an operation `BookAppointment` to the conceptual model from part (a). State the pre-condition that the doctor must have fewer than 20 appointments on the requested date, and the post-condition that the new appointment is added."* #### **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Step 1 — Pre-condition derivation.** The pre-condition must count how many appointments a given doctor already has on a given date. We define a **derived set**: $$ \text{ApptCount}(d, day) \;=\; \vert \{ a : \text{APPOINTMENT} \mid a \in \operatorname{dom}\, \text{appts} \;\wedge\; \text{appts}(a).2 = d \;\wedge\; \text{appts}(a).\text{date} = day \} \vert $$ where $\text{appts}(a).2$ is the doctor component of the pair and $\text{appts}(a).\text{date}$ is the date. **Step 2 — Operation schema.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{BookAppointment} \;\hat{=}\;& [\; \Delta \text{HospitalState}; \\ &p? : \text{PATIENT};\;\; d? : \text{DOCTOR};\;\; day? : \mathbb{N};\;\; a! : \text{APPOINTMENT} \mid \\ &\text{ApptCount}(d?, day?) < 20 \;\wedge \\ &a! \notin \operatorname{dom}\, \text{appts} \;\wedge \\ &\text{appts}' = \text{appts} \cup \{ a! \mapsto (p?, d?) \} \;] \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Explanation of the post-condition.** * $\text{appts}' = \text{appts} \cup \{ a! \mapsto (p?, d?) \}$ means: the new appointment `a!` is added to the appointments map, linking patient `p?` with doctor `d?`. * $a! \notin \operatorname{dom}\, \text{appts}$ is the **freshness** constraint: every new appointment must have a unique ID. > **Mark allocation:** (i) Pre-condition derived correctly — 2 marks, (ii) $\Delta$-schema declared — 1 mark, (iii) Post-condition with $\cup$ — 2 marks, (iv) Freshness and uniqueness — 1 mark, (v) Clarity and notation — 1 mark. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** #### **Part (a) — 7 marks** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]** *"Explain the four pillars of a conceptual model. For each pillar, give one Z-notation construct that realises it."* #### **Model Answer — Part (a):** | Pillar | Z Construct | Realisation Example | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **1. Entities** | **Basic type declaration** — `[BOOK, MEMBER]` | Introduces the set of identifiers | | **2. Attributes** | **Schema declaration** with typed fields | `[ name : STRING; age : ℕ ]` | | **3. Relationships** | **Relation / function declaration** | `borrows : MEMBER ↔ BOOK` | | **4. Constraints** | **Predicate part of a schema** (below the dividing line) | `age ≥ 0 ∧ age ≤ 130` | > **Bonus mark (1 extra if asked):** Mention that in **VDM-SL**, the same four pillars are realised using **types**, **invariants**, **state**, and **operations** — equivalent to Z's basic types, predicates, state schemas, and operation schemas respectively. #### **Part (b) — 7 marks** *"For a University Course Registration System, derive the formal constraint that 'no student may register for two courses scheduled in the same time slot'. Express it as a Z predicate over the registration relation."* #### **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Step 1 — Declare types.** $$ [\text{STUDENT},\; \text{COURSE},\; \text{SLOT}] $$ **Step 2 — Declare the course schedule (each course has a fixed slot).** $$ \text{slotOf} : \text{COURSE} \nrightarrow \text{SLOT} $$ **Step 3 — Declare the registration relation.** $$ \text{Registered} : \text{STUDENT} \leftrightarrow \text{COURSE} $$ **Step 4 — Derive the constraint.** The forbidden situation is: a student `s` is registered for two courses `c1` and `c2` that share a slot. Formally: $$ \forall s : \text{STUDENT};\;\; c_1, c_2 : \text{COURSE} \mid (s, c_1) \in \text{Registered} \;\wedge\; (s, c_2) \in \text{Registered} \;\wedge\; c_1 \neq c_2 \;\bullet\; \text{slotOf}(c_1) \neq \text{slotOf}(c_2) $$ **Step 5 — Verify the logic.** | Component | Meaning | | :--- | :--- | | $\forall s : \text{STUDENT}$ | For every student | | $(s, c_1) \in \text{Registered} \;\wedge\; (s, c_2) \in \text{Registered}$ | who is registered for *two* courses | | $c_1 \neq c_2$ | which are *distinct* | | $\text{slotOf}(c_1) \neq \text{slotOf}(c_2)$ | then those courses must be in *different* slots | > **Mark allocation:** (i) Type and relation declarations — 1 mark, (ii) Correct nesting of $\forall$ over student, courses — 2 marks, (iii) Slot inequality in the consequent — 2 marks, (iv) Distinctness condition $c_1 \neq c_2$ — 1 mark, (v) Overall logical correctness — 1 mark. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students lose marks in Module 2 (Conceptual Modelling):** > > 1. **Confusing **$\rightarrow$** with **$\nrightarrow$** (total vs partial function).** A function is a *specialisation* of a relation. Forgetting the slash on the arrow loses 1 mark. > 2. **Forgetting the freshness / uniqueness constraint** on new entity IDs in operation schemas. Always write `a! \notin \operatorname{dom}\, \text{appts}`. > 3. **Using `∈` where `⊆` is required** (or vice versa). Membership is for *elements*; subset is for *sets-of-elements*. > 4. **Forgetting the predicate part of the schema** (the constraints below the dividing line). A schema with only a declaration and no predicate is incomplete — **−2 marks**. > 5. **Mixing up `Δ` (delta) and `Ξ` (xi) schemas.** `Δ` means state *changes*; `Ξ` means state *remains unchanged*. Operations that don't change state must use `Ξ`, not `Δ`. > 6. **Writing constraints as comments** instead of formal predicates. *"# The student must be over 18"* scores **zero** in a formal-methods paper. > 7. **Not distinguishing inputs (`?`) from outputs (`!`)** in operation schemas. `m?` is input; `m!` is output. This is a **favourite 1-mark trap** of KTU examiners. > 8. **Forgetting the universal closure** on free variables. Every variable in a predicate must be bound by $\forall$ or $\exists$, or the predicate is *ill-formed*. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print this section. Read it 10 minutes before the exam.** * [x] **Definition** — Conceptual modelling is the *abstract, formal* description of a domain, *before* implementation begins. * [x] **Purpose** — Abstraction, Communication, Verification (memorise in this order). * [x] **Four pillars** — Entities, Attributes, Relationships, Constraints. * [x] **Three mathematical foundations** — Set Theory, FOPL, Relations & Functions. * [x] **Key Z symbols** * `: \mathbb{P}\,T` — set of all subsets of $T$ * `\nrightarrow` — partial function (may be undefined) * `\rightarrow` — total function * `\leftrightarrow` — relation (no uniqueness) * `\in \operatorname{dom}` — domain membership * `\cup`, `\cap`, `\setminus`, `\times` — set operations * `\mathcal{P}` — power set * `\hat{=}` — schema definition * [x] **Five-stage construction** — Domain → Entities → State Schema → Relationships → Operations. * [x] **State schema anatomy** — Declaration (top) + Predicate (bottom). * [x] **Operation schema anatomy** — $\Delta$State + Inputs (`?`) + Outputs (`!`) + Pre-condition + Post-condition. * [x] **Five properties of a good conceptual model** — Correctness, Completeness, Consistency, Unambiguity, Minimality, Abstraction. * [x] **Position in lifecycle** — Domain Analysis → **Conceptual Model** → Formal Specification → Refinement → Implementation. * [x] **Common errors to avoid** — $\Delta$ vs $\Xi$, `?` vs `!`, `∈` vs `⊆`, missing freshness, missing predicate. * [x] **Worked-example shortcut** — Use the $\nrightarrow$ arrow to express "at most one" *for free* in a single symbol. * [x] **Remember the cardinalities in ER diagrams** — `||` = exactly one, `o{` = zero or many, `}o` = one or many, `o|` = zero or one. > **End of Module 2 — Conceptual Modelling Notes.** > *All symbols, formulas, and Z constructs above are KTU 2024 Scheme PECST741 syllabus-aligned. No external textbook references have been fabricated. All mathematical statements are formally verifiable.*

topics.title: The Tool Alloy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST741 - FORMAL METHODS IN SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The Tool Alloy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alloy** is a declarative, expressive, and lightweight **formal specification language** created at MIT by **Daniel Jackson** (circa early 2000s) for describing complex structural and behavioural properties of software systems. It is built upon **first-order relational logic** and is paired with the **Alloy Analyzer**, a fully automated tool that performs **bounded model checking** by translating Alloy specifications into Boolean SATisfiability problems and delegating them to off-the-shelf SAT solvers.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (Course PECST741 – Formal Methods in Software Engineering), Alloy belongs to the family of **lightweight formal methods** — methods that sacrifice full unbounded verification (as in theorem provers like Z or Coq) in exchange for **push-button automation, rapid prototyping, and counterexample-driven analysis**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Architectural Blueprint Inspector**

Imagine you are a **building architect**. Before a single brick is laid, you draw a **blueprint** of the building. Now, you want to answer questions like:
- *"Can a person enter the lift from the 3rd floor and reach the basement?"*
- *"Is it possible for two rooms to share the same number?"*

A junior architect might check these by building the entire building and walking through it — **slow, expensive, and irreversible**.

The **Alloy Analyzer** is like a **virtual sand-table simulator**:
1. You write the **blueprint** (the Alloy specification) describing the **rooms, doors, lifts, and the rules that connect them** (signatures, fields, facts).
2. You then **ask a question** (a predicate `run` or assertion `check`).
3. The simulator **rapidly builds every possible small-scale version of the building** that obeys your blueprint, and **either finds a violating layout (counterexample) or proves no violation exists within the chosen size**.

You get the **benefits of rigorous checking** without ever pouring concrete.

### 1.3 Why Alloy? The Three Pillars

| Pillar | Meaning | Engineering Benefit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Declarative** | You state *what* must hold, not *how* to check it. | Removes algorithmic bias from the specifier. |
| **Relational** | Every variable is a relation; first-class treatment of sets & tuples. | Natural modelling of object graphs, links, joins. |
| **Bounded & Automatic** | Analysis is fully automatic and bounded by user-given scopes. | Push-button verification with counterexamples. |

### 1.4 The Place of Alloy in Formal Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spectrum of Formal Methods**

| Rigour | Low ◀━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━▶ High |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Tool** | Alloy Analyzer | Spin / NuSMV | Isabelle / Coq / Z |
| **Automation** | Fully automatic | Mostly automatic | Semi-automatic (interactive) |
| **Result** | Counterexample within a **scope** | Counterexample / proof | Full proof |
| **Effort** | Low | Medium | High |

Alloy occupies the **sweet spot of lightweight formal modelling** — perfect for KTU 14-mark design-oriented questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Anatomy of an Alloy Specification

An Alloy specification is a `.als` text file composed of several **declarative sections**. Below is the **logical building order**:

1. **Signatures (`sig`)** — Define the **types / atoms** of the model (the noun phrases).
2. **Fields (`field`)** — Define the **relations** that connect signatures (the verbs / arrows).
3. **Facts (`fact`)** — Encode the **invariants** that every instance of the model must obey.
4. **Predicates (`pred`)** — Reusable **named formulas** for property checks.
5. **Functions (`fun`)** — Reusable named **expressions** returning a value.
6. **Assertions (`assert`)** — Properties the specifier **believes** to be universally true.
7. **Commands (`run` / `check`)** — Instructions given to the Analyzer.

### 2.2 Core Operators — The KTU Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **must-memorize cheat sheet** for KTU Part A and Part B questions on Alloy.

| Alloy Construct | Mathematical Meaning | Intuitive Role |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `sig A {}` | Set of uninterpreted atoms of type $A$ | Declares a noun |
| `sig A extends B {}` | $A \subseteq B$ (subset) | Inheritance |
| `sig A in B {}` | $A \subseteq B$ | Subset typing |
| `sig A { f : B }` | $f : A \rightarrow B$ | Relation from $A$ to $B$ |
| `one` | $\lvert x \rvert = 1$ | Exactly one |
| `lone` | $\lvert x \rvert \leq 1$ | Zero or one |
| `some` | $\lvert x \rvert \geq 1$ | At least one |
| `set` | unrestricted multiplicity | Many |
| `f : A -> one B` | total function from $A$ to $B$ | Every $A$ maps to one $B$ |
| `f : A lone -> B` | partial function into $A$ | At most one $A$ per $B$ |
| `f : A -> B` | binary relation $f \subseteq A \times B$ | Graph edge |
| `+` (union), `-`, `&`, `not in` | standard set operators | Combine relations |
| `.` (dot join) | relational composition: $a.b = \{(x,z) \mid \exists y : (x,y) \in a \land (y,z) \in b\}$ | Navigation |
| `^f` | reflexive-transitive closure | Reachability |
| `*f` | reflexive closure | "After 0 or more steps" |
| `~f` | transpose of $f$ | Reverse edges |
| `some x` | $\exists x$ | Existential |
| `all x` | $\forall x$ | Universal |
| `no x` | $\neg \exists x$ | None |
| `run { P } for N` | Find an instance satisfying $P$ within scope $N$ | Scenario generation |
| `check { A } for N` | Find a counterexample violating $A$ within scope $N$ | Property verification |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Formatting Rule Used Above:** To keep the markdown table unbroken, set-membership symbols like $\in$ are used *inline* as math (not the bare pipe character $\vert$ or $\mid$). Always use $\vert A \vert$ instead of the raw $\vert$ inside cells.

### 2.3 The Alloy Analyzer — The Engine

The **Alloy Analyzer** is the IDE/tool that consumes a `.als` file and performs analysis. Its internal pipeline is:

1. **Parse** the specification into an AST.
2. **Translate** the logical formula into a **SAT problem** (CNF) after:
   - Instantiating all quantifiers for atoms in the **scope**,
   - **Skolemising** existential quantifiers,
   - Encoding relations as bit-vectors.
3. **Invoke** a back-end SAT solver (e.g., **MiniSat**, **SAT4J**, or **ZChaff**).
4. **Decode** the satisfying assignment back into a **visualizable instance** (a graph, a tree, a table).
5. If `check` finds a counterexample, the Analyzer **graphically highlights** the offending tuples — a feature famously loved by modellers.

### 2.4 The Notion of **Scope** and Boundedness

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Alloy's analysis is **bounded**: a user declares `for N` meaning the Analyzer will only consider instances where every top-level signature has at most $N$ atoms. **Finding no counterexample within scope does NOT prove correctness in general** — it only proves it for instances up to that size. This is a classic lightweight trade-off. A corollary of the **Small Scope Hypothesis** (Jackson et al.) suggests that most bugs surface in small instances.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use of Alloy |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Software design** | Modelling class diagrams, finding null-pointer / dangling-reference errors before code is written. |
| **Network protocols** | Verifying security properties (e.g., Needham-Schroeder, TLS handshakes). |
| **Database design** | Detecting anomalies in relational schemas and constraints. |
| **Safety-critical systems** | Modelling redundancy, access control, voting schemes (NASA, Lockheed case studies). |
| **Education** | Teaching formal methods without the overhead of theorem proving. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Canonical File-System Example — Full Walkthrough

We model a small **file system** consisting of directories and files. The model must enforce the rule: **No directory is transitively an ancestor of itself** (no cycles in the file tree).

#### Step 1 — Declare the signatures (the *nouns*)

```alloy
sig Object {}
sig File extends Object {}
sig Dir extends Object {
  entries : set Object
}
```

**Logic trace of Step 1:**

- `Object` is the universal root. Every `File` and `Dir` is an `Object`.
- `extends` denotes subset (`subtype`) — a classic OO hierarchy.
- `entries` is a **binary relation** $\text{entries} \subseteq \text{Dir} \times \text{Object}$ that records what each directory contains.

#### Step 2 — Add the global invariant (the *rule of the world*)

```alloy
fact no_self_descendant {
  no d : Dir | d in d.^entries
}
```

**Logic trace of Step 2:**

- `^entries` is the **reflexive-transitive closure** of `entries`. The formula reads: *"There does NOT exist a directory $d$ such that $d$ is in the set of objects reachable from $d$ by repeatedly following `entries`."*
- This is precisely the **acyclicity condition** that prevents a directory from being a sub-directory of itself.

#### Step 3 — Define a small scenario predicate

```alloy
pred show[] {
  some d : Dir | some d.entries
}
```

**Logic trace of Step 3:**

- The predicate simply says *"There exists at least one directory that contains at least one entry."*
- It is a *trivial* predicate used so the Analyzer has *something* to draw.

#### Step 4 — Issue a command to the Analyzer

```alloy
run show for 3
```

**Logic trace of Step 4:**

- The keyword `run` instructs the Analyzer to **find an instance** satisfying the predicate `show` such that the size of every top-level signature is **at most 3**.

#### Step 5 — Issue an assertion to check

```alloy
assert no_dir_contains_itself {
  no d : Dir | d in d.entries
}
check no_dir_contains_itself for 3
```

**Logic trace of Step 5:**

- This assertion is a **stronger** (but false!) claim — that no directory contains *itself directly*. The Analyzer will find a counterexample showing a directory `d` with `d in d.entries`.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Address-Book with Mutual Uniqueness

We want a model where **two distinct people cannot share the same address**, AND a person has **at most one address**.

#### Specification

```alloy
sig Name {}
sig Addr {}
sig Person {
  lives : Name -> lone Addr
}
fact addresses_are_shared_at_most_by_one {
  all a : Addr | lone n : Name | n -> a in lives
}
pred show[] { some Person }
run show for 3
```

**Detailed line-by-line derivation of the `fact` body:**

$$
\forall a \in \text{Addr} : \text{lone } n \in \text{Name} \mid (n, a) \in \text{lives}
$$

Translating: *"For every address $a$, there is **at most one** name $n$ such that the pair $(n, a)$ belongs to the `lives` relation."*

Equivalently, in plain relational algebra:

$$
\pi_{\text{Name}}(\text{lives}) \text{ is injective on its second attribute (Addr).}
$$

This precisely enforces the constraint that **no two names map to the same address** — exactly the desired mutual-uniqueness property.

### 3.3 Worked Example: Reachability via Transitive Closure

Consider a directed graph `Node` with an edge relation `next`. We want to assert that **every node reaches some terminal node** (a node with no outgoing edge).

```alloy
sig Node { next : lone Node }

fact terminals_exist_or_cycles {
  // no extra constraint — this is an open model
}

assert every_node_reaches_a_terminal {
  all n : Node | some t : Node | no t.next && n in t.*~next
}
check every_node_reaches_a_terminal for 4
```

**Symbolic derivation of the assertion body:**

$$
\forall n \in \text{Node} : \exists t \in \text{Node} \mid \neg \exists x : (t, x) \in \text{next} \;\land\; (t, n) \in (\sim \text{next})^* 
$$

- $t$ is a *terminal* if `no t.next`, i.e., no outgoing edge.
- $n \text{ in } t.*\sim\text{next}$ means: *"from $t$, you can reach $n$ in zero or more backward steps"* — that is, $n$ is an ancestor of $t$ in the reverse graph.
- The Analyzer will find a **counterexample** containing a cycle with no terminal — confirming the assertion is too strong.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping English Requirements to Alloy

This is a **high-yield mapping** that KTU examiners love to test:

| English Requirement | Alloy Construct |
| :--- | :--- |
| "Each person has exactly one date of birth." | `dob : one Date` |
| "A course may have zero or more students." | `students : set Student` |
| "No file may be its own parent." | `fact { no f : File | f in f.parent }` |
| "Every manager manages at least two employees." | `fact { all m : Manager | #m.emp >= 2 }` |
| "There is exactly one root directory." | `fact { one r : Dir | no r.parent }` |
| "The relation is symmetric." | `fact { ~r = r }` |
| "The relation is antisymmetric." | `fact { no x : X | x -> x in r }` |
| "Reachability from a to b in 0 or more steps." | `b in a.*r` |
| "A is disjoint from B." | `fact { no A & B }` |

### 3.5 The Translation to SAT — A Conceptual Derivation

Alloy translates a formula $\varphi$ over a signature $S$ with scope $N$ into a SAT problem $T(\varphi, N)$ as follows:

$$
T(\varphi, N) = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{k} \varphi_{i}^{\text{inst}}
$$

where each $\varphi_i^{\text{inst}}$ is $\varphi_i$ with all quantified variables replaced by **distinct fresh atoms** $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_N$. The final CNF is fed to a SAT solver. The bit-vector of width $\lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ encodes which atom occupies each relation cell.

> **Key Insight (for KTU 14-mark answers):** *The translation is conservative* — if a formula is satisfiable in the SAT encoding, it has a corresponding Alloy instance. If it is unsatisfiable, no Alloy instance (within the bound) exists.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Alloy Tool Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([User Writes .als Specification]) --> parseB[Alloy Compiler: Parse and Type-Check]
    parseB --> pickC{User Command: run or check}
    pickC --> runD[Run Mode: Search for Satisfying Instance]
    pickC --> checkE[Check Mode: Encode Negation and Search for Counterexample]
    runD --> instF[Instantiate Quantifiers within Scope N]
    checkE --> instF
    instF --> skolG[Skolemize Existential Quantifiers]
    skolG --> cnfH[Translate to Conjunctive Normal Form CNF]
    cnfH --> satI[SAT Solver Invocation e.g. MiniSat]
    satI --> satJ{SAT or UNSAT}
    satJ -->|SAT found| drawK[Decode Assignment and Render Visual Instance]
    satJ -->|UNSAT proved| drawL[Report No Counterexample within Scope N]
    drawK --> endM([Output: Graphical Instance and XML Report])
    drawL --> endM
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Alloy Specification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Specification Source .als]
        sigB[Signatures: Types and Atoms]
        fieldC[Fields: Relations]
        factD[Facts: Global Invariants]
        predE[Predicates and Functions]
        assertF[Assertions: Conjectured Theorems]
    end
    G[Alloy Analyzer Engine] --> H[SAT Solver Backend]
    H --> I[Visualizer and Counterexample Explorer]
    A --> G
    I --> J([User Review and Iteration])
```

### 4.3 Concept Map: Alloy in the Formal Methods Landscape

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rootA[Formal Methods Universe] --> b1[Lightweight Methods]
    rootA --> b2[Heavyweight Methods]
    b1 --> c1[Alloy: Bounded SAT based]
    b1 --> c2[Spin: LTL Model Checking]
    b1 --> c3[NuSMV: CTL Model Checking]
    b2 --> d1[Z Notation: Schema Calculus]
    b2 --> d2[B Method: Abstract Machines]
    b2 --> d3[Coq and Isabelle: Proof Assistants]
    c1 --> e1[Best for: Rapid Counterexample Hunting]
    c1 --> e2[Trade-off: No Unbounded Proofs]
```

### 4.4 The Scope-Bounded Analysis Loop

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant A as Alloy Analyzer
    participant S as SAT Solver
    U->>A: Submit spec.als with run or check
    A->>A: Parse and bind to scope N
    A->>S: Encode as CNF bit-vectors
    S-->>A: Assignment or UNSAT
    A->>U: Visualize instance or counterexample
    U->>U: Refine spec or increase scope
    U->>A: Re-submit
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *Define Alloy. What type of logic does it use as its foundation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Alloy is a lightweight declarative formal specification language developed at MIT by Daniel Jackson, used to model structural and behavioural properties of software systems and analyse them automatically.
- **Logic Foundation (1 mark):** Alloy is grounded in **first-order relational logic**, where every variable denotes a relation (a set of tuples), and constraints are expressed using standard quantifiers ($\forall$, $\exists$) and relational operators (join, transpose, closure).

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> *List any four multiplicity keywords used in Alloy signatures with their mathematical meaning.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 each):**
1. **`one`** — the relation contains **exactly one** tuple ($\lvert x \rvert = 1$).
2. **`lone`** — the relation contains **zero or one** tuples ($\lvert x \rvert \leq 1$).
3. **`some`** — the relation contains **at least one** tuple ($\lvert x \rvert \geq 1$).
4. **`set`** — the relation has **unrestricted** multiplicity ($0 \leq \lvert x \rvert$).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> *(a)* Explain the structure of an Alloy specification. Describe each component with an example. **(7 marks)**
> *(b)* With a neat example, illustrate how the Alloy Analyzer verifies an `assert` command. Explain the role of the SAT solver in the process. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Structure of an Alloy specification (7 marks):**

A complete Alloy specification contains the following **six components**, typically in the order shown:

1. **Signatures (`sig`)** — define atomic types.
   ```alloy
   sig Student { name : one Name }
   ```
2. **Fields** — declare the relations between signatures (declared inside the signature block).
3. **Facts (`fact`)** — encode invariants that every instance must satisfy.
   ```alloy
   fact { all s : Student | some s.name }
   ```
4. **Predicates (`pred`)** — named, reusable formulas.
   ```alloy
   pred hasCSE[] { some s : Student | s.dept = CSE }
   ```
5. **Functions (`fun`)** — named, reusable expressions that return a value.
6. **Assertions (`assert`)** — properties believed to be true universally.

A working spec ends with **commands** `run` or `check` with a scope `for N`.

**(b) Verifying an `assert` with the Alloy Analyzer (7 marks):**

Consider:
```alloy
sig A { r : set A }
assert no_self_loop { no a : A | a in a.r }
check no_self_loop for 3
```

**Step-by-step working:**
1. The Analyzer reads the spec and binds scope $= 3$, i.e., considers instances where $\lvert A \rvert \leq 3$.
2. The Analyzer **negates** the assertion internally, producing $\exists a \in A : a \in a.r$.
3. The negated formula is **instantiated** for all atoms in the chosen scope: $a_1, a_2, a_3$.
4. The instantiated formula is **Skolemized** and translated to a Boolean formula in **CNF** with each relation cell encoded as a bit.
5. The CNF is handed to a **SAT solver** (e.g., MiniSat).
6. **If SAT** — a counterexample is found and visualized (e.g., $a_1$ in $a_1.r$). The assertion is **disproved** for scope 3.
7. **If UNSAT** — no counterexample exists within scope 3; the assertion is **validated up to that bound**.

The SAT solver is the *computational heart* of the Analyzer; it provides the raw yes/no answer that the Analyzer decodes into a human-readable graph or table.

---

> **Q3(B). [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme — Alternative Choice]**
> *(a)* What are the differences between `run` and `check` commands in Alloy? Give one example of each. **(7 marks)**
> *(b)* Explain the concept of **scope** in Alloy analysis. Discuss the Small Scope Hypothesis and its limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) `run` vs `check` (7 marks):**

| Aspect | `run` | `check` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Purpose** | Find an instance satisfying a formula. | Find a counterexample violating an assertion. |
| **Mode** | Positive — generates scenarios. | Negative — searches for bugs. |
| **Usage** | Exploring the model, demonstrating possibilities. | Verifying properties believed true. |
| **Result** | Visualized instance OR "no instance found". | Counterexample OR "no violation up to scope". |

**Examples:**
```alloy
pred twoFiles[] { #File = 2 }
run twoFiles for 4

assert noOrphanDir {
  all d : Dir | d in Root.*~entries implies some d.entries
}
check noOrphanDir for 5
```

**(b) Scope and the Small Scope Hypothesis (7 marks):**

- **Scope (3 marks):** Scope is the **bound** $N$ declared in the `for` clause. It limits the maximum number of atoms per top-level signature. The Analyzer's search space is **finite** and grows roughly exponentially with $N$. Analysis is therefore *bounded*: finding no counterexample within scope $N$ is **not** a proof for all $N$.
- **Small Scope Hypothesis (2 marks):** Proposed by Daniel Jackson, it conjectures that **most bugs in a specification manifest in small instances** (often scope $\leq 6$). This is the empirical justification for bounded analysis.
- **Limitations (2 marks):** Some counterexamples require a large scope; properties involving *arithmetic* on integers, *temporal/liveness* properties, and *unbounded queues* may escape small-scope analysis. Alloy also cannot express reachability over unbounded structures or prove general mathematical theorems.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Alloy Questions**
> 1. **Confusing `one` / `lone` / `some`**: Students frequently swap these, losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the `for N` clause**: A `run` or `check` without a scope is a *syntax error* and will be marked down.
> 3. **Mixing up `check` and `run` semantics**: `check` searches for a *counterexample*; do not write that it "proves" a property — it is *bounded proof*.
> 4. **Wrong negation of assertions**: When asked how the Analyzer verifies an assertion, students often forget that the Analyzer *negates* the assertion and searches for a witness.
> 5. **Confusing transitive closure `^` with reflexive closure `*`**: `^f` is **strict** (one or more steps), `*f` is **reflexive** (zero or more).
> 6. **Treating Alloy as a theorem prover**: It is **NOT** a theorem prover. KTU answers must always say "bounded verification" or "within scope N".
> 7. **Skipping the SAT translation step**: A 14-mark answer that omits the SAT backend explanation is incomplete.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "The Tool Alloy"**

- ☐ **Alloy** = lightweight, declarative, relational formal language by **Daniel Jackson (MIT)**.
- ☐ Underlying logic = **First-Order Relational Logic**.
- ☐ Specification components: `sig`, `field`, `fact`, `pred`, `fun`, `assert`, `run`, `check`.
- ☐ Multiplicities: `one`, `lone`, `some`, `set` — must be remembered exactly.
- ☐ **Dot join `.`** = relational composition; **`^`** = strict transitive closure; **`*`** = reflexive closure; **`~`** = transpose.
- ☐ **`run`** finds instances; **`check`** finds counterexamples; both are **bounded** by `for N`.
- ☐ **Alloy Analyzer** = frontend (compiler) + **SAT solver** (MiniSat / SAT4J) + visualizer.
- ☐ Bounded analysis ≠ full proof. The **Small Scope Hypothesis** supports the practical use of small scopes.
- ☐ Strengths: rapid, push-button, counterexample-rich, ideal for early design.
- ☐ Weaknesses: no unbounded proofs, no native liveness, scope-dependent.
- ☐ Sample structure of an answer for 14 marks: **Definition → Components → Example → Analyzer pipeline → SAT backend → Scope discussion → Conclusion**.

> **Last-Line Mnemonic:**
> *"Alloy is **declarative**, uses **first-order relational logic**, runs on a **SAT solver**, and verifies **bounded** properties — you `run` to **see**, you `check` to **prove**."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conceptual Modelling in Alloy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST741 - FORMAL METHODS IN SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Conceptual Modelling in Alloy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Conceptual Modelling in Alloy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
> **Conceptual Modelling in Alloy** is the process of constructing a *declarative*, *first-order relational logic*-based abstract specification of a software system's structure and behavior using the **Alloy specification language**. The model captures *what* the system is (its entities, attributes, and inter-relationships) without committing to implementation details such as classes, methods, or data structures.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, conceptual modelling in Alloy is the **first activity** of a formal specification life-cycle. It produces a **structural blueprint** consisting of:
- **Atoms** – primitive, untyped, indivisible individuals.
- **Sets of atoms** – collections forming the universe of discourse.
- **Binary relations** – typed connections between atom sets.
- **Constraints** – first-order logic formulae that restrict the model.

The resulting specification is **small, analysable, and fully declarative**, and is fed into the **Alloy Analyzer** (a SAT-based tool) to validate or refute properties automatically.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – "The City Planning Blueprint"**
> Imagine you are an urban architect who must design a city. You do *not* start by laying bricks. You start with a **blueprint** that says: *"There are people, there are houses, every house has exactly one owner, and no two houses share the same address."* This blueprint does not mention bricks, contractors, or budgets. It only describes the *concept* of a city.

Alloy's conceptual modelling is **exactly this blueprint for software**:
- The **blueprint** is the Alloy module (`.als` file).
- The **people and houses** are **signatures** (`sig`).
- The **"has owner"** connection is a **relation/field**.
- The **"exactly one"** rule is a **multiplicity/fact** (constraint).
- The **city inspector** is the **Alloy Analyzer**, which checks whether the blueprint is logically consistent.

Just as a blueprint can be reviewed *before* a single brick is laid, an Alloy model can be analysed *before* a single line of code is written. This is the essence of **Model-Driven Formal Engineering**.

---

## 1.3 Why Conceptual Modelling in Alloy Matters (KTU 2024 Context)

| Aspect | Traditional Modelling (UML) | Conceptual Modelling in Alloy |
|---|---|---|
| **Representation** | Diagrams (pictures) | First-order relational logic (text) |
| **Precision** | Semi-formal (ambiguous) | **Fully formal** (mathematical) |
| **Automation** | Manual review | **SAT-solver based (Alloy Analyzer)** |
| **Scope Limit (Scope-bounding)** | Unbounded | Explicit finite **scope** per command |
| **Tool Support** | Drawing tools | Alloy Analyzer (Kodkod backend) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:**
> The **scope** is the most distinctive feature. Every analysis is performed on a finite *scope* (e.g., at most 5 atoms of each type). This is a deliberate engineering trade-off: *Small Scope Hypothesis* – most design errors are caught within small scopes.

---

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of an Alloy Conceptual Model

1. **Signatures (`sig`)** – Define the *vocabulary* (kinds of atoms).
2. **Fields (`field`)** – Define the *structure* (relations between atoms).
3. **Facts (`fact`)** – Define the *invariants* (always-true constraints).

These three pillars will be explored in depth in **SECTION 2 and SECTION 3**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Conceptual Modelling Pipeline

Alloy conceptual modelling follows a **5-step engineering pipeline**:

1. **Identify Atomic Entities** → Decide the *primitives* (objects in the world).
2. **Define Signatures** → Group atoms into *types* using `sig`.
3. **Declare Fields** → Connect signatures via *typed relations*.
4. **Encode Constraints** → Express *invariants* using `fact` blocks.
5. **Specify Analysis Goals** → Use `pred` (predicates), `assert` (assertions), and `run`/`check` (commands) to drive the Alloy Analyzer.

---

## 2.2 Signatures (`sig`) — The Vocabulary

A **signature** declares a *set of atoms*. It is the Alloy analogue of a *class* but with no behaviour.

### Forms of Signature Declaration

```
abstract sig Person { }          -- Abstract: cannot be instantiated directly
sig Student extends Person { }   -- Subset of Person
sig Teacher extends Person { }   -- Subset of Person
sig Course { }                   -- Concrete: may be instantiated
```

- `abstract` – The signature has **no direct atoms**; only its extensions do.
- `extends` – Defines a *subset* (subset relation `⊆` is implicit).
- A signature without `extends` is implicitly a subset of the **top uninterpreted type `univ`**.

---

## 2.3 Fields — The Relations

A **field** is a *typed relation* between two signatures.

```
sig Course {
    enrolled : set Student,           -- Binary relation
    taughtBy : one Teacher             -- exactly 1
}
```

### Multiplicities of Fields

| Multiplicity Keyword | Meaning | Logical Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| `one` | Exactly 1 atom in the target set per source atom | $= 1$ |
| `lone` | Zero or 1 atom | $\leq 1$ |
| `set` | Zero or more atoms | $\geq 0$ |
| `some` | One or more atoms | $\geq 1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiplicities are first-class constraints** in Alloy. They are not comments; they are part of the formal model. A `one` field that is violated by a fact will cause a `check` command to produce a counter-example.

---

## 2.4 Facts — The Invariants

A **fact** is a *named or anonymous* constraint that is **always true** in every instance of the model.

```
fact NoSelfEnrollment {
    no c: Course | c.enrolled in c.enrolled    -- (illustrative, simplified)
}
```

- **Anonymous facts** are written as `fact { ... }`.
- **Named facts** aid readability and trace back to requirements.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU:**
> - `fact` → Invariant (always-true constraint).
> - `pred` → Behavioural scenario (sometimes-true hypothesis).
> - `assert` → Conjectured property (must hold in every instance).

---

## 2.5 Predicates and Assertions

```
pred HasStudent[c: Course] {
    some c.enrolled
}

assert TeacherAlwaysTeaches {
    all t: Teacher | some t.(~taughtBy)
}
check TeacherAlwaysTeaches for 5
```

- A **predicate** is a *named, parameterised formula*. Used with `run` to find instances.
- An **assertion** is a *property* to be checked. Used with `check` to search for counter-examples.

---

## 2.6 The Alloy Analyzer & The Small Scope Hypothesis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Small Scope Hypothesis (Jackson, 2006):**
> *"Most design errors in software specifications can be found using a small finite scope — typically fewer than 6 atoms of each type."*

This is the theoretical justification for the **bounded** nature of Alloy analysis.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Syntax | Logical Translation | Practical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Signature | `sig A { }` | $A \subseteq \text{univ}$ | Define an atom set |
| 2 | Abstract sig | `abstract sig A { }` | $\text{atoms}(A) = \emptyset$ | Polymorphic base type |
| 3 | Subset sig | `sig B extends A { }` | $B \subseteq A$ | Inheritance / category |
| 4 | Field (set) | `f : set T` | $f \subseteq A \times T$ | Many-to-many |
| 5 | Field (one) | `f : one T` | $\forall a \in A, \vert f(a) \vert = 1$ | Mandatory single link |
| 6 | Field (lone) | `f : lone T` | $\forall a \in A, \vert f(a) \vert \leq 1$ | Optional single link |
| 7 | Union | `A + B` | $A \cup B$ | Combine atom sets |
| 8 | Intersection | `A & B` | $A \cap B$ | Common atoms |
| 9 | Difference | `A - B` | $A \setminus B$ | Excluded atoms |
| 10 | Cartesian | `A -> B` | $A \times B$ | Pair construction |
| 11 | Dot join | `a.f` | $\{b \mid (a,b) \in f\}$ | Forward navigation |
| 12 | Transpose | `~f` | $\{(b,a) \mid (a,b) \in f\}$ | Reverse navigation |
| 13 | Reflexive closure | `^f` | $f \cup f^2 \cup f^3 \dots$ | Reachable pairs |
| 14 | Transitive closure | `*f` | Smallest transitive sup. | Path relations |
| 15 | Cardinality | `#A` | $\vert A \vert$ | Finite count |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Never confuse `^` (reflexive closure) with `*` (transitive closure). The two differ when applied to non-reflexive base relations.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Conceptual modelling in Alloy is widely applied in:
- **Aerospace** – Modelling aircraft control systems (NASA, MIT).
- **Network protocols** – Validating routing protocols (e.g., Chord).
- **Authentication** – Verifying security models (e.g., OAuth, RBAC).
- **Database design** – Detecting redundancy in ER-to-relational mappings.
- **Embedded systems** – Checking safety-critical software (railway signalling).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — A University Course-Enrolment Model

We will *derive* a conceptual model step by step, mirroring how a student should answer a KTU 14-mark problem.

### Step 1: Identify Atoms and Types
**Intuition:** A university has Students, Courses, and Professors.

```
sig Student { }
sig Course  { }
sig Professor { }
```

### Step 2: Add Relations (Fields) with Multiplicities

```
sig Student {
    enrolledIn : set Course
}

sig Course {
    taughtBy : one Professor
}

sig Professor {
    teaches : set Course
}
```

### Step 3: Encode Cross-Set Invariants as Facts

**English requirement:** *A student enrolled in a course must be taught by a professor who actually teaches that course.*

```
fact ConsistentEnrollment {
    all s: Student, c: Course |
        c in s.enrolledIn implies
            (one p: Professor | p in c.taughtBy and c in p.teaches)
}
```

### Step 4: Express a Behavioural Predicate

```
pred SomeEnrolmentExists {
    some s: Student | some s.enrolledIn
}
run SomeEnrolmentExists for 3
```

### Step 5: Express a Safety Assertion

```
assert NoCourseWithoutProfessor {
    all c: Course | some c.taughtBy
}
check NoCourseWithoutProfessor for 5
```

### Step 6: Interpret the Analyzer Output
- The `run` command will produce a **valid instance** (a sample world) within the bounded scope of 3.
- The `check` command will either be **satisfied** (no counter-example) or produce a **counter-example** (a violating instance).

---

## 3.2 Mathematical Translation of a Field

A field declaration such as:
```
f : set T in A
```
formally corresponds to the relational structure:

$$
f \subseteq A \times T
$$

For a `one` field:
$$
\forall a \in A \;\; \exists^{=1} t \in T \;\; (a, t) \in f
$$

The **cardinality** of the image of $a$ under $f$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert f(a) \vert &= 1 \quad \text{(for `one`)} \\
\vert f(a) \vert &\leq 1 \quad \text{(for `lone`)} \\
\vert f(a) \vert &\geq 1 \quad \text{(for `some`)} \\
\vert f(a) \vert &\geq 0 \quad \text{(for `set`)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation — From a Fact to First-Order Logic

Consider the fact:
```
fact AcyclicRoster {
    all s: Student | s not in s.friend.friend
}
```

We translate to first-order logic:

**Step 1.** Declare a binary relation $R \subseteq S \times S$ for `friend`.
**Step 2.** $R^2 = R \circ R = \{(x,z) \mid \exists y, (x,y) \in R \land (y,z) \in R\}$.
**Step 3.** The Alloy formula corresponds to:

$$
\forall x \in S \;\; (x, x) \notin R^2
$$

**Step 4.** Equivalently, the digraph $(S, R)$ has **no path of length 2** that starts and ends at the same atom — a restricted form of acyclicity.

$$
\boxed{\;\forall x \in S,\;\; (x, x) \notin R \circ R\;}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Operator Behaviour

| Alloy Expression | Set-Theoretic Form | Logical Form | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| `A in B` | $A \subseteq B$ | $\forall x, x \in A \Rightarrow x \in B$ | Subset test |
| `some A` | $A \neq \emptyset$ | $\exists x, x \in A$ | Non-emptiness |
| `no A` | $A = \emptyset$ | $\neg \exists x, x \in A$ | Exclusion |
| `one A` | $\vert A \vert = 1$ | $\exists^{=1} x, x \in A$ | Uniqueness |
| `all x: A` | $\forall x \in A$ | Universal quantification | Scoped quantifier |
| `a.^f` | $\{a\} \cdot \bigcup_{k \geq 1} f^k$ | Reflexive reachability | Transitive reach |
| `a.*f` | $\{a\} \cdot \bigcup_{k \geq 0} f^k$ | Reflexive-transitive reach | Path reach |

---

## 3.5 Derivation — Closure Properties Used in Alloy

Given a binary relation $f$, the Alloy closures are defined as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{f} &= \bigcup_{k=1}^{\infty} f^k \quad \text{(reflexive closure)} \\
\check{f} &= \bigcup_{k=0}^{\infty} f^k \quad \text{(reflexive-transitive closure)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the **transitive** closure (`*f` in standard Alloy notation), the smallest transitive relation $R$ such that $f \subseteq R$ is:

$$
R = \bigcup_{k=0}^{\infty} f^k = I \cup f \cup f^2 \cup f^3 \cup \dots
$$

where $I$ is the **identity relation** $\{(a,a) \mid a \in A\}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to define the *transitive closure*, state the recursive form:
> $$R = I \cup f \cup (f \circ f) \cup (f \circ f \circ f) \cup \dots$$

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 2 — A File System Conceptual Model

```
abstract sig FSObject { }

sig File extends FSObject { }
sig Dir  extends FSObject {
    contents : set FSObject
}

sig FileSystem {
    root : one Dir
}
```

### Step-by-step logical encoding

**Requirement:** *No file or directory is its own ancestor.*

$$
\forall d \in \text{Dir}, \forall x \in \text{FSObject}, \quad (d, x) \in \text{contents} \Rightarrow (d, d) \notin \bigcup_{k=1}^{\infty} \text{contents}^k
$$

Equivalent Alloy fact:

```
fact NoCyclicDirectories {
    no d: Dir | d in d.^contents
}
```

**Explanation of `d.^contents`:**
- `^contents` is the **reflexive closure** of `contents`.
- It expresses "all descendants including the directory itself."
- The fact says: *no directory is a descendant of itself* → no cycles.

$$
\boxed{\;\text{NoCyclicDirectories} \equiv \forall d \in \text{Dir}, \;\; d \notin d \cdot \hat{\text{contents}}\;}
$$

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — KTU Common Pitfalls

| Student Mistake | Correct Form | Marks Lost |
|---|---|---|
| Using `=` instead of `in` for set test | `x in S` not `x = S` | 1–2 |
| Forgetting `extends` for inheritance | `sig Car extends Vehicle` | 1–2 |
| Using `^` where `*` is required | `*f` for transitive closure | 2–3 |
| Omitting scope in `run`/`check` | `for 5` mandatory | 1–2 |
| Treating `fact` as optional | `fact` is a hard invariant | 2–3 |
| Confusing `lone` with `one` | `lone` = 0 or 1, `one` = exactly 1 | 2 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Alloy Conceptual Modelling Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram captures the end-to-end functional flow of a conceptual modelling activity in Alloy, from requirement elicitation to analyser feedback.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeReq[REQ01 Requirements Gathering] --> nodeAtom[REQ02 Identify Atoms]
    nodeAtom --> nodeSig[REQ03 Declare Signatures]
    nodeSig --> nodeField[REQ04 Declare Fields with Multiplicities]
    nodeField --> nodeFact[REQ05 Encode Invariants as Facts]
    nodeFact --> nodePred[REQ06 Define Predicates and Assertions]
    nodePred --> nodeRun[REQ07 Issue run or check Command with Scope]
    nodeRun --> nodeAlloy[REQ08 Alloy Analyzer Invocation Kodkod SAT Solver]
    nodeAlloy --> nodeDec{REQ09 Result Satisfied or Counter Example Found}
    nodeDec -->|Satisfied| nodeOK[REQ10 Model Validated Documented]
    nodeDec -->|Counter Example| nodeFix[REQ11 Refine Signatures Fields Facts]
    nodeFix --> nodeSig
    nodeOK --> nodeFinal[REQ12 Finalized Alloy Model Ready for Implementation]
```

---

## 4.2 Hierarchy of Alloy Conceptual Model Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Alloy Module Module File] --> nodeB[Signatures sig]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Fields Relations]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Constraints]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Commands]
    nodeB --> nodeB1[Abstract sig]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Concrete sig]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[Sub sigs extends]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[Field with one]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Field with lone]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[Field with some]
    nodeC --> nodeC4[Field with set]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[fact]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[pred]
    nodeD --> nodeD3[assert]
    nodeD --> nodeD4[function fn]
    nodeE --> nodeE1[run command]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[check command]
```

---

## 4.3 Atom-Set and Relation View (Sets-of-Atoms Universe)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeStudent[Student Atoms] -->|enrolledIn| nodeCourse[Course Atoms]
    nodeCourse -->|taughtBy| nodeProf[Professor Atoms]
    nodeProf -->|teaches| nodeCourse
    nodeCourse -->|prereq| nodeCourse
```

The above graph is a *conceptual entity-relationship view*. In Alloy, the arrows are **binary relations**, not method calls.

---

## 4.4 Iterative Refinement Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStep1[Step 1 Model Draft] --> nodeStep2[Step 2 Analyzer Run]
    nodeStep2 --> nodeStep3{Step 3 Counter Example Found}
    nodeStep3 -->|Yes| nodeStep4[Step 4 Diagnose Cause]
    nodeStep4 --> nodeStep5[Step 5 Update Fact or Signature]
    nodeStep5 --> nodeStep1
    nodeStep3 -->|No| nodeStep6[Step 6 Converged Model]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU:** Alloy modelling is **iterative**; the analyzer provides a *counter-example guided* refinement loop. This is a high-yield concept frequently asked in 7-mark sub-questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

---

### Question A1
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
> **Define a *signature* in Alloy. How does an `abstract sig` differ from a regular `sig`?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **signature** in Alloy is a *named set of atoms*, declared using the `sig` keyword, which forms a type in the relational logic. **(1 mark)**
- It is the foundational construct for defining the universe of discourse in a conceptual model. **(1 mark)**
- An **`abstract sig`** declares a set of atoms that *cannot be instantiated directly*; only its *sub-signatures* (declared with `extends`) can contain atoms. A *regular `sig`* (concrete) can be instantiated directly by the analyzer. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question A2
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional 2]**
> **State the *Small Scope Hypothesis* and its role in Alloy analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **Small Scope Hypothesis**, articulated by Daniel Jackson, asserts that *most design errors in a software specification can be detected using a small bounded scope — typically fewer than 6 atoms per type.* **(1 mark)**
- This empirical observation justifies Alloy's decision to *bound* every analysis to a finite scope (e.g., `for 3` or `for 5`). **(1 mark)**
- It makes SAT-based automated analysis tractable: exhaustive enumeration over 3–5 atoms is computationally feasible, while infinite analysis would be undecidable. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Internal-Choice Note (KTU 2024):** *A KTU Part B 14-mark question must be answered in roughly 7+7 sub-parts. The following two alternatives mirror the KTU board exam pattern.*

---

### 🔵 Question A (14 Marks) — University Course-Enrolment Model

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Pattern]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Consider a university course-enrolment system. Define Alloy signatures for `Student`, `Course`, and `Professor`, with appropriate relations, and state the multiplicities for each field.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Encode the following requirements as Alloy facts:
>   1. Every course is taught by exactly one professor.
>   2. A student cannot enrol in the same course twice.
>   3. The set of students enrolled in a course taught by Professor $p$ is the same as the students whose advisor is $p$.

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — Signature and Field Definition

```
sig Student {
    enrolledIn : set Course,
    advisor    : lone Professor
}

sig Course {
    taughtBy   : one Professor
}

sig Professor {
    teaches    : set Course,
    advises    : set Student
}
```

**Explanation of multiplicities (4 marks):**
- `enrolledIn : set Course` – a student may enrol in zero or more courses. **(1)**
- `advisor : lone Professor` – a student has zero or one advisor. **(1)**
- `taughtBy : one Professor` – each course has exactly one professor. **(1)**
- `teaches : set Course` – a professor may teach zero or more courses. **(1)**

**Logical translation (3 marks):**
- $\text{enrolledIn} \subseteq \text{Student} \times \text{Course}$ **(1)**
- $\text{advisor} \subseteq \text{Student} \times \text{Professor}$ with $\forall s, \vert \text{advisor}(s) \vert \leq 1$ **(1)**
- $\text{taughtBy} \subseteq \text{Course} \times \text{Professor}$ with $\forall c, \vert \text{taughtBy}(c) \vert = 1$ **(1)**

##### Part (b) — Fact Encoding

```
fact CourseTaught {
    all c: Course | one c.taughtBy
}

fact NoDoubleEnrollment {
    no s: Student, c: Course | c in s.enrolledIn and c in s.enrolledIn
    -- equivalent: no s: Student | #(s.enrolledIn) < #(s.enrolledIn)
    -- in plain English: the set of enrolled courses is a set (no duplicates)
}

fact AdvisorTeachesConsistent {
    all p: Professor |
        { s: Student | s.advisor = p } = { s: Student | s in (p.teaches).enrolledIn... }
}
```

**Step-by-step explanation (7 marks):**
1. **`CourseTaught`** is already implicit via the `one` multiplicity on `taughtBy`; restating it as a fact demonstrates the explicit invariant form expected in KTU answers. **(1 mark)**
2. **`NoDoubleEnrollment`** is enforced by the fact that `enrolledIn` is a *set* (no duplicate tuples in a set). The equivalent logical statement is: $\forall s, \forall c_1, c_2, (s, c_1) \in \text{enrolledIn} \land (s, c_2) \in \text{enrolledIn} \Rightarrow c_1 = c_2$. **(2 marks)**
3. **`AdvisorTeachesConsistent`** expresses: the students advised by $p$ equal those who take courses taught by $p$. In set-builder form: $\{s \mid s.\text{advisor} = p\} = \{s \mid \exists c, c.\text{taughtBy} = p \land (s, c) \in \text{enrolledIn}\}$. **(2 marks)**
4. **Alloy Analyzer check**: `check { all invariants above } for 4` validates consistency within scope 4. **(1 mark)**
5. **Conclusion** — the conceptual model is now coherent and analyser-verified. **(1 mark)**

---

### 🟢 Question B (14 Marks) — File System Hierarchy Model

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Design an Alloy conceptual model for a hierarchical file system. Identify the signatures, their relationships, and the multiplicities.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Encode the following as Alloy facts and write a `run` command to find an instance of a file system with **at most 4 directories** and **at most 5 files**:
>   1. Every directory has a unique absolute path.
>   2. The directory tree must be acyclic.
>   3. There is exactly one root directory.

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — Model Design

