```
abstract sig FSNode {
    path : lone Path
}

sig File   extends FSNode { }
sig Dir    extends FSNode {
    parent : lone Dir,
    children : set FSNode
}

sig Path { }
```

**Step-by-step explanation (7 marks):**
1. **Abstract sig `FSNode`** creates a common base for `File` and `Dir`, ensuring polymorphic operations. **(1)**
2. **`File`** is a leaf node with no structural fields. **(1)**
3. **`Dir`** has `parent : lone Dir` (0 or 1 parent) and `children : set FSNode` (any number of children). **(2)**
4. **Multiplicities:** `parent` is `lone` (a directory may be a root with no parent); `children` is `set`. **(1)**
5. **Path** is a separate signature used to label each node with a unique absolute path. **(1)**
6. **Universe of discourse:** $U = \text{FSNode} \cup \text{Path}$, with $\text{File} \subset \text{FSNode}$ and $\text{Dir} \subset \text{FSNode}$. **(1)**

##### Part (b) — Fact Encoding and Run Command

```
fact UniquePaths {
    all n1, n2: FSNode | n1 != n2 implies n1.path != n2.path
}

fact AcyclicTree {
    no d: Dir | d in d.^parent
}

fact UniqueRoot {
    one r: Dir | no r.parent
}

run {} for 4 Dir, 5 File
```

**Step-by-step explanation (7 marks):**
1. **`UniquePaths`** – $\forall n_1 \neq n_2, n_1.\text{path} \neq n_2.\text{path}$. (Distinct atoms must have distinct path labels.) **(2 marks)**
2. **`AcyclicTree`** – $\forall d \in \text{Dir}, (d, d) \notin \hat{\text{parent}}$. The reflexive closure of `parent` is used; no directory is its own ancestor implies a DAG, and combined with `UniqueRoot` a tree. **(2 marks)**
3. **`UniqueRoot`** – $\exists^{=1} r \in \text{Dir}, \text{parent}(r) = \emptyset$. **(1 mark)**
4. **The `run` command** requests an instance: at most 4 directories and 5 files, satisfying all facts. **(1 mark)**
5. **Analyzer output** – a valid instance is produced; if any fact fails, a counter-example appears for the user to refine. **(1 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not** write `sig` blocks without specifying **multiplicities** on every field. Full-mark answers always include the multiplicity keyword (`one`, `lone`, `set`, `some`). Loss: 2–3 marks.
> - **Do not** forget the `for N` clause on `run`/`check` commands. Loss: 1–2 marks.
> - **Do not** mix `fact` and `pred`. `fact` is *always-true*; `pred` is *scenario-based*. Examiners will deduct 2 marks.
> - **Do not** confuse `extends` (subset) with `in` (subset of an existing field). They are different Alloy constructs.
> - Always show the **logical translation** of at least one field; examiners award bonus marks for mathematical rigour.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Conceptual Modelling in Alloy**

- **Signature (`sig`)** = a named set of atoms. The basic *type* construct.
- **`abstract sig`** = cannot be instantiated; only its extensions can.
- **`extends`** = declares a sub-signature (subset relation).
- **Field** = typed binary relation with a multiplicity.
- **Multiplicities** = `one` (exactly 1), `lone` (0 or 1), `some` (≥ 1), `set` (≥ 0).
- **`fact`** = hard invariant; always true in every instance.
- **`pred`** = named, parameterised formula; used with `run`.
- **`assert`** = property to be checked; used with `check`.
- **Scope (`for N`)** = finite bound for the analyser; key to *Small Scope Hypothesis*.
- **Alloy Analyzer** = SAT-based tool (built on Kodkod); produces instances or counter-examples.
- **Set operators**: `+` (union), `&` (intersection), `-` (difference), `->` (Cartesian product).
- **Relational operators**: `.` (dot join), `~` (transpose), `^` (reflexive closure), `*` (transitive closure), `:` (declarative type).
- **Logical operators**: `and`, `or`, `not`, `implies`, `iff`, `all`, `some`, `no`, `one`, `lone`.
- **Small Scope Hypothesis** = most design errors are caught with N ≤ 5.
- **Conceptual modelling** = structural, declarative, behaviour-free specification.
- **The `run` command** finds an instance satisfying its formula; **`check` finds a counter-example violating its assertion.**
- **Common scope patterns**: `for 3` (default), `for 5` (deep checks), `for 4 Dir, 5 File` (typed scopes).
- **Alloy is a *declarative* language** — you state *what*, not *how*.
- **Translation to FOL** is expected in KTU 14-mark answers.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Conceptual Modelling in Alloy**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysing Alloy Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST741 - FORMAL METHODS IN SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Analysing Alloy Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Analysing Alloy Models** refers to the systematic, automated process of evaluating an Alloy specification using the **Alloy Analyzer** (a.k.a. *Alloy4*) to determine whether the model is **satisfiable**, whether the **asserted invariants hold in every instance**, and whether the **operational predicates can be exhibited** as concrete scenarios. Analysis is the verification step of the Alloy methodology (Model → Analyse → Iterate).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** An *Alloy model* is a declarative description built from **signatures**, **relations**, **fields**, **facts**, **predicates**, **functions**, and **assertions** written in first-order relational logic with transitive closure. *Analysing* a model means mechanically exploring its logical consequences within a finite **scope** using a **SAT (Boolean Satisfiability) solver**.

The three canonical analytical commands recognized by the KTU syllabus are:

| Command | Purpose | KTU Tag |
|---|---|---|
| `run {pred}` | Find an **instance** satisfying the predicate | Model Realisation |
| `check {assert}` | Find a **counter-example** violating the assertion | Property Verification |
| `run {}` (empty) | Look for a **counter-model** of the whole spec | Inconsistency Check |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have written a **rule-book for a chess tournament** in English. The rule-book says things like *"every player has exactly one score"*, *"no two players share the same rank"*, and *"the winner's score is the maximum"*.

> **The Question:** Are the rules *self-consistent*? Is there *some* tournament where these rules can be played? Does the rule *“if Alice beats Bob, then Alice’s score is greater than Bob’s”* actually follow from the other rules?

Reading the rule-book manually is slow. You ask a **logic referee** (the Alloy Analyzer) to:
1. Try to **set up a concrete tournament** of, say, **3 players** that obeys every rule. If it succeeds, the rules are *consistent*. This is `run`.
2. Try to find **a counter-example tournament** that *breaks* a stated rule. If it fails, the rule is *safe*. This is `check`.
3. Show you the *smallest possible* tournament or the *smallest breaking scenario* so you can see the bug with your own eyes.

The referee does not read English — it translates the rule-book into Boolean formulas and asks a **SAT solver** to either satisfy them (`run`) or to satisfy the *negation* of one of them (`check`). The mechanical referee is **bounded** by a number you give it — the *scope* — because exhaustive reasoning about infinite tournaments is undecidable.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Hook — “CRV” = Consistency, Run, Verify.** The three outputs of analysing an Alloy model are: (i) **C**onsistency, (ii) **R**ealizable scenario, (iii) **V**iolation counter-example.

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

- **Scope:** The maximum number of atoms of each top-level signature that the SAT solver is allowed to use. Default = 3. KTU default cited value: **3**.
- **Instance:** A binding of every relation to actual atoms that satisfies every `fact` and the given predicate.
- **Witness:** A successfully returned instance of a `run` command.
- **Counter-example:** A successfully returned instance of a `check` command that violates the assertion.
- **Off-table atom:** An atom declared but not used in the instance (Arity: scope may produce unused atoms).
- **Bit-width:** The number of bits used to encode each atom; default = **4**, which means a *single* signature can hold up to $2^4 - 1 = 15$ atoms in scope 15.
- **Partial vs. Total Instance:** Alloy, by default, permits relations to be *partial* — not every atom needs to be in the relation’s image.
- **Skolem Variables:** Existential quantifiers are eliminated into fresh constants before SAT solving (a process called *Skolemization*).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Misconception:** “Alloy proves correctness for *all* systems.” — **FALSE.** Alloy analysis is **bounded model checking** (BMC). It is only complete up to the chosen *scope*. A bug free at scope 3 *may* appear at scope 7. KTU examiners deduct marks for this oversight.

## 1.4 The Closed-Loop of Analysis

The KTU 2024 syllabus frames analysis as a **reactive, iterative loop**, not a one-shot pass:

1. **Formulate** an Alloy model (signatures, facts, predicates, assertions).
2. **Choose a scope** and a **command**.
3. **Execute** the command through the Alloy Analyzer.
4. **Interpret** the returned instance / counter-example.
5. **Refine** the model if a violation, ambiguity, or unintended over-constraint is found.
6. **Re-analyse**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This *“small scope hypothesis”* — that most design errors manifest at very small scopes — is the philosophical pillar justifying Alloy’s bounded analysis. The hypothesis is empirical but is taught in PECST741 as the **practical justification** for using bounded SAT-based analysis on potentially infinite-state systems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Mathematics — From Alloy to CNF to SAT

The Alloy Analyzer performs analysis in three formal stages. Mastering these stages is the single most-tested topic on Analysing Alloy Models in KTU 2024.

### Stage A — *Translation to First-Order Relational Logic*
Every Alloy construct is a syntactic sugar over **first-order logic with transitive closure (FOL+TC)**. Example:

- `sig A { r: set B }`  →  $\forall a{:}A,\; b{:}B\;[\;r(a,b) \rightarrow b \in B\;]$
- `fact { all a:A | some a.r }` → $\forall a{:}A,\;\exists b{:}B\;[\;r(a,b)\;]$
- `assert Invariant { all a:A | P[a] }` → $\neg Invariant$ becomes the search target for `check`.

### Stage B — *Bounded Unrolling & Skolemization*
Because SAT solvers operate on **propositional** logic, the unbounded quantifiers are *bounded* by the chosen scope and then *eliminated*:

$$\exists x\,P(x) \;\;\xrightarrow{\text{Skolemize}}\;\; P(c) \quad (\text{fresh constant } c)$$

### Stage C — *Encoding to Boolean Formula (CNF)*
Each relation is encoded as a 2-D Boolean matrix (rows = source atoms, columns = target atoms), and a **bit-blasting** translation converts the first-order formulas into a Boolean **Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF)**. The CNF is fed to an off-the-shelf SAT solver such as **MiniSat** or **Sat4J**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick:** “Alloy uses a *theorem prover*” is **wrong** in the strict sense. It uses a **SAT solver**, hence it is *bounded but decidable* (as long as the scope is finite and the bit-width is finite). True theorem provers (Isabelle, Coq) are unbounded but undecidable.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The table below collects every numerical/legal formula and bound relevant to the *Analysing Alloy Models* topic. **No vertical pipes are used inside cells to preserve markdown table integrity.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Law | Engineering / Logical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Scope-to-Atom Count** | $N_s = 2^{b}$ where $b$ = bit-width | Max atoms per top-level sig at a given bit-width |
| 2 | **Default Bit-width** | $b = 4 \Rightarrow N_s = 2^4 = 16$ | Used unless overridden by `bitwidth` option |
| 3 | **Default Scope** | $\text{scope} = 3$ | The “small scope” the KTU syllabus emphasises |
| 4 | **State Space Size (unary rels)** | $2^{n \times n}$ for relation of arity 1 over $n$ atoms | Cardinality of the search space; **explodes** with scope |
| 5 | **Binary Relation Cardinality** | $2^{n^2}$ | Search space of a single binary field |
| 6 | **Predicate Truth = SAT Satisfiable** | $\text{SAT}(P) \iff \exists\, \sigma : \sigma \models P$ | Witness found by `run` |
| 7 | **Assertion Soundness = UNSAT of $\neg A$** | $\text{UNSAT}(\neg A) \iff A$ is valid in the scope | Counter-example *not* found by `check` |
| 8 | **Skolemization (existential)** | $\exists x\,\phi(x) \equiv \phi(c)$ | Removes quantifiers for SAT encoding |
| 9 | **Alloy Negation (De Morgan)** | $\neg \forall x\,\phi \equiv \exists x\,\neg\phi$ | Used to flip `check` into a SAT search |
| 10 | **Transitive Closure** | $r^{+} \;=\; r \;\cup\; r.r \;\cup\; r.r.r \;\cup\; \ldots$ | Terminated in Alloy via `^` operator; unrolled to depth $n$ |
| 11 | **No Instance (UNSAT)** | $\sigma = \varnothing$ returned by Analyzer | Model is *over-constrained* — a **bug** |
| 12 | **Counter-example Found (SAT)** | $\sigma \models \neg A$ | Assertion is *violated* — a **bug** |
| 13 | **Off-table Atom** | atom in scope but $\not\in \text{domain}(\sigma)$ | Allowed; reduces interpretation ambiguity |
| 14 | **MiniSat complexity** | NP-complete in worst case | Justifies *small* scope hypothesis |
| 15 | **Visualizer File Format** | `.als.xml` or `.als.instance` | Engine output for human inspection |

## 2.3 The Small Scope Hypothesis (KTU High-Yield Paragraph)

The hypothesis (Jackson, 2000) — taught in PECST741 as the *practical* reason Alloy can find industrial bugs despite its boundedness — is summarised below:

- **Statement:** If a model has a counter-example in *any* scope, it has one in some *small* scope (typically $\leq 6$).
- **Justification:** Most modelling bugs are *local* — they involve only a few interacting atoms.
- **Engineering utility:** Allows bounded SAT to substitute for unbounded theorem proving in *design-stage* verification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often state “Alloy verifies the *entire* system” or “Alloy is a *complete* proof system.” Both statements are **incorrect** and *will* cost marks. Use the precise phrase: *“Alloy performs **bounded exhaustive analysis** within the given scope, sound up to that scope.”*

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Flow Inside the Alloy Analyzer

1. **Lex & Parse** the `.als` source.
2. **Type-check** signatures and fields.
3. **Resolve** `extends`, `in`, `one`, `lone`, `set` qualifiers into FOL+TC.
4. **Skolemize** and **bound** quantifiers to the chosen scope.
5. **Translate** the FOL+TC formula into a Boolean formula (matrix encoding).
6. **Convert** to CNF.
7. **Hand** to the SAT solver.
8. If `run` → return any satisfying assignment as an instance.
9. If `check` → assert *negation* of target; if SAT, return counter-example; if UNSAT, report assertion holds.
10. **Render** the instance in the *Visualizer* for human interpretation.

> [!NOTE]
> This ten-step pipeline is examinable. In Module 2, KTU questions frequently test whether students can identify **where Skolemization happens** (Step 4) and **what an UNSAT result means** (Step 9).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Software design validation:** Catching design errors in *address-book*, *memory model*, *access control* specifications before code is written.
- **Protocol verification:** AllJoyn, OAuth, and TLS handshakes have been modelled in Alloy (industry case studies).
- **Database schema checking:** Alloy’s set-relational semantics are a near-perfect match for relational schema constraints.
- **Security policy audit:** Verifying *Bell-LaPadula*, *RBAC*, *Chinese-Wall* policies through `assert` commands.
- **Microservice contract testing:** Alloys “predicate describes an allowed message exchange” maps to API contract checks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Analysing a Tiny Model

Consider the canonical KTU-style Alloy snippet shown below:

```alloy
module tour/analysisDemo
sig Player { score: one Int }
fact ValidScore { all p:Player | p.score >= 0 and p.score <= 10 }
pred TwoPlayers { #Player = 2 }
pred HasHigh { some p:Player | p.score > 7 }
assert NoAllHigh { all p:Player | p.score <= 7 }
run TwoPlayers for 3
run HasHigh for 4
check NoAllHigh for 4
```

We shall analyse this model step by step.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Translation of `sig Player { score: one Int }`

The keyword `one` enforces that for every Player atom, *exactly one* Int is bound to `score`. Translated to FOL+TC:

$$\forall p{:}Player,\; \exists_1\, v{:}Int \;[\; score(p, v)\;]$$

The phrase $\exists_1$ is a *count quantifier* meaning “exists exactly one.” The Alloy Analyzer rewrites this internally as:

$$\forall p{:}Player,\; \big(\exists v{:}Int,\; score(p, v)\big) \;\land\; \big(\forall v_1, v_2{:}Int,\; score(p, v_1) \land score(p, v_2) \rightarrow v_1 = v_2\big)$$

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Translation of the `fact` Constraint

```alloy
fact ValidScore { all p:Player | p.score >= 0 and p.score <= 10 }
```

Becomes:

$$\forall p{:}Player,\; \big(score(p,v) \rightarrow v \geq 0 \big) \;\land\; \big(score(p,v) \rightarrow v \leq 10 \big)$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Translation of the Three Commands

- `run TwoPlayers for 3` instructs the analyzer to find an instance in which $\#Player = 2$ and the `ValidScore` fact holds.
- `run HasHigh for 4` looks for a player whose `score` strictly exceeds 7 within a universe of 4 Players.
- `check NoAllHigh for 4` searches for a counter-example: an instance where at least one player has a score greater than 7.

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Skolemization and Bounding

Suppose the scope is 3. The Analyzer allocates three fresh Skolem constants:

$$c_1, c_2, c_3 \quad :\quad Player$$

and three fresh Skolem constants for `score`’s value:

$$v_1, v_2, v_3 \quad :\quad Int$$

After Skolemization, the universal quantifiers remain, but the existential “exactly-one” quantifier is replaced by the explicit introduction of a single Skolem value per player:

$$\bigwedge_{i=1}^{3}\;\big(\,score(c_i, v_i) \,\land\, \forall w{:}Int,\; score(c_i, w) \rightarrow w = v_i\,\big)$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — CNF Encoding

A `Player` atom is encoded with **4 bits** (default), so each atom $c_i$ corresponds to a 4-bit vector. The relation $score$ becomes a $3 \times 16$ Boolean matrix. The integer value $v_i$ is also a 4-bit signed integer (range $-8$ to $7$ *or* $0$–$15$ depending on Alloy’s Int interpretation; the `Int` in Alloy is treated as a bit-vector).

The bound $0 \leq v_i \leq 10$ becomes a conjunction of *bitwise* constraints on the four bits of $v_i$.

### 3.1.6 Step 6 — SAT Call for `run HasHigh`

The conjunction of facts and the predicate $\exists p{:}Player,\; score(p, v) \land v > 7$ is handed to MiniSat. Suppose MiniSat returns:

$$\sigma = \{Player_0, Player_1, Player_2,\;\; score(Player_1) = 9\}$$

This is the **witness** displayed in the Visualizer.

### 3.1.7 Step 7 — SAT Call for `check NoAllHigh`

The analyzer negates the assertion: $\exists p{:}Player,\; score(p, v) \land v > 7$. Conjoined with `ValidScore`, the call is again satisfiable. The same instance above is returned as the **counter-example**. The KTU examiner’s marks: the assertion is *violated* and the model is **buggy** unless the user intended `score` to be unbounded.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Notice how *the same witness* serves as both the `run` output and the `check` counter-example. This is a recurring KTU question pattern.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Commands vs. Outcomes

The matrix below is the *single most-tested artefact* on this topic. Memorise it.

| Command | Solver Asks | Possible Outcome | KTU Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| `run P for n` | $\text{SAT}(F \land P)$ | **SAT** → witness shown | Predicate *realizable*; facts are consistent |
| `run P for n` | $\text{SAT}(F \land P)$ | **UNSAT** → no instance | Over-constrained; fix the model |
| `check A for n` | $\text{SAT}(F \land \neg A)$ | **SAT** → counter-example shown | Assertion *falsified*; **bug** in model |
| `check A for n` | $\text{SAT}(F \land \neg A)$ | **UNSAT** → none shown | Assertion *valid* **within scope** |
| `run {}` (no pred) | $\text{SAT}(F)$ | **SAT** → at least one model | Spec is consistent |
| `run {}` | $\text{SAT}(F)$ | **UNSAT** → none | Spec is *unsatisfiable* — a modelling error |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Reading Hint:** A *successful* `check` is **bad news** — it means the assertion is *wrong*. A *successful* `run` is *good news* — the predicate can happen. KTU students often invert this and lose marks.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Bound — Search Space

Suppose we have two signatures `A` and `B` and a single binary field `r: A -> lone B`. We want to know how large the SAT problem gets when we set the scope of `A` to 5 and `B` to 4.

- $A$ contributes $2^4 = 16$ distinct atoms *in principle* but bounded to 5 — 5 fresh Skolem atoms.
- $B$ contributes 4 atoms.
- The relation $r$ is a $5 \times 4 = 20$-cell matrix of Booleans.
- Number of satisfying assignments to the matrix: $2^{20} = 1\,048\,576$.
- With additional `Int` fields of bit-width 4, each cell becomes a 4-bit vector; the search space grows to $2^{4 \times 20} = 2^{80}$ — already intractable for naïve SAT.

This is *why* Alloy defaults to scope **3** and bit-width **4**. KTU 2024 may ask: *“Why does the Analyzer take exponentially longer as scope grows?”* The numerical derivation above is the expected answer.

## 3.4 Bound Selection Heuristic (KTU-Recommended)

When the Analyzer returns UNSAT and the user suspects a deeper bug, the KTU syllabus recommends the following sequence of *scope increments*:

1. `for 3` (default).
2. `for 4` — if the predicate is small.
3. `for 5` — only if prior scopes are still UNSAT.
4. **Stop at 6 or 7** and rewrite the model; the bug is structural, not size-dependent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory aid:** *“3, 4, 5 — give up alive.”* The bug almost always lives in the *facts*, not in the *scope*.

## 3.5 Case Study: Memory-Model Analysed in Alloy (Adapted for KTU)

```alloy
sig Location {}
sig Value {}
sig Memory { map: Location -> lone Value }
fact NoAlias { all m:Memory | all l:Location | one m.map[l] }
assert Consistent { all m:Memory, l:Location | some m.map[l] }
check Consistent for 3
```

**Analysis walkthrough:**

- `map: Location -> lone Value` is a partial function from locations to values; `lone` permits *zero* mappings.
- `NoAlias` forces the function to be *total* in the locations actually used.
- `Consistent` is violated the moment a `Memory` atom exists with an unused location. SAT solver returns:

$$\sigma = \{Memory_0,\; Location_0, Location_1,\; map(Memory_0, Location_0) = Value_0\}$$

The unused `Location_1` is the counter-example. KTU lesson: *partiality* of `lone` is often the bug source.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: The Alloy Analysis Pipeline

The flow below captures the full transformation chain from a textual Alloy specification to a Boolean satisfiability result.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Alloy source .als] --> B[Lexer and Parser]
    B --> C[Type Checker]
    C --> D[FOL plus TC Translator]
    D --> E[Scope Bounder]
    E --> F[Skolemizer]
    F --> G[Boolean Matrix Encoder]
    G --> H[CNF Converter]
    H --> I{SAT Solver}
    I -- SAT and run --> J[Witness Instance]
    I -- SAT and check --> K[Counter Example]
    I -- UNSAT and run --> L[No Instance Found]
    I -- UNSAT and check --> M[Assertion Holds in Scope]
    J --> N[Visualizer Renderer]
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Human Inspection and Model Refinement]
    O --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram for KTU exams:** the **only** decision point is the SAT solver. Everything *upstream* of the diamond is a deterministic translation; everything *downstream* is a deterministic visualisation. All intelligence lives in (a) the translation correctness and (b) the SAT solver’s heuristic.

## 4.2 State-Transition View: Re-Analysing After a Counter-Example

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Drafted
    Drafted --> RunCheck: run or check executed
    RunCheck --> CounterExampleFound: SAT and check
    RunCheck --> WitnessFound: SAT and run
    RunCheck --> NoInstance: UNSAT and run
    RunCheck --> AssertionValid: UNSAT and check
    CounterExampleFound --> RefineFact: fix facts
    CounterExampleFound --> RefineAssertion: weaken claim
    NoInstance --> RefineFact: relax facts
    WitnessFound --> Drafted: accept
    AssertionValid --> Drafted: accept
    RefineFact --> Drafted
    RefineAssertion --> Drafted
```

## 4.3 Scope-Incrementation Ladder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Scope 3 default] --> S2[Scope 4]
    S2 --> S3[Scope 5]
    S3 --> S4[Scope 6]
    S4 --> S5[Scope 7 structural fix likely needed]
    S5 --> S6[Model Rewrite]
```

## 4.4 Entity-Relationship of an Analysis Session

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Spec[Alloy Specification] --> FactBlock[Fact Block]
    Spec --> PredBlock[Predicate Block]
    Spec --> AssertBlock[Assertion Block]
    FactBlock --> Analyzer
    PredBlock --> Analyzer
    AssertBlock --> Analyzer
    Analyzer --> Instance
    Analyzer --> CounterEx
    Instance --> User[Developer Review]
    CounterEx --> User
    User --> Spec
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Visualizer Tip:** When asked to draw the analysis flow, students *must* include the feedback loop from the developer back to the specification. Marks are awarded for the loop, not the boxes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**“Differentiate between the `run` and `check` commands in the Alloy Analyzer. What is the role of the SAT solver in each case?”** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer (verbatim-ready):**

- The **`run`** command asks the Alloy Analyzer to find an **instance** (a *witness*) that satisfies a given predicate together with all the `fact`s in the model. Internally, it asks the SAT solver whether the conjunction $F \land P$ is *satisfiable*, where $F$ is the conjunction of facts and $P$ is the predicate body.
- The **`check`** command asks the Analyzer to find a *counter-example* to an assertion $A$. Internally, it negates the assertion and asks the SAT solver whether $F \land \neg A$ is satisfiable.
- If the SAT solver returns **SAT**, the corresponding `run` produces a witness and the corresponding `check` produces a counter-example. If **UNSAT**, `run` returns “no instance” (over-constrained model) and `check` declares the assertion *valid within the scope*.
- The **role of the SAT solver** is the *mechanical decision procedure* for Boolean satisfiability. Alloy is *not* a theorem prover; the SAT solver is what makes the bounded analysis decidable.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**“What is the small-scope hypothesis? Why does it justify bounded analysis in Alloy?”** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

- The **small-scope hypothesis**, introduced by Daniel Jackson (the creator of Alloy), states that *if a relational model has a counter-example in some scope, it almost always has one in some small scope (typically $\leq 6$).*
- This is an *empirical* claim, derived from years of industrial case studies, not a theorem.
- It justifies bounded analysis because: (i) the Alloy Analyzer is *bounded* — it can only explore finite scopes, and (ii) most design-level bugs are *local*, involving only a few interacting atoms, and therefore surface at small scopes.
- Without this hypothesis, bounded analysis would be of little practical value; with it, Alloy becomes a powerful *design-time* bug-finder rather than a *full* proof system.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Between A and B)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2 — Model Paper Pattern]*

**(a)** With a suitable diagram, describe the **internal architecture of the Alloy Analyzer**. List and explain the four most important translation stages. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider the Alloy specification below and answer the sub-questions.

```alloy
module library
sig Book { isbn: one Int, loans: set Borrower }
sig Borrower { id: one Int }
fact NoDuplicate { all b1, b2: Book | b1 != b2 implies b1.isbn != b2.isbn }
pred SomeOverdue { some b:Book | #b.loans > 2 }
assert AtMostTwo { all b:Book | #b.loans <= 2 }
run SomeOverdue for 4
check AtMostTwo for 4
```

- (i) Translate the `fact` `NoDuplicate` and the `assert` `AtMostTwo` into first-order logic. *(3 marks)*
- (ii) For scope = 4 and bit-width = 4, what is the maximum number of `Book` atoms and `Borrower` atoms? *(1 mark)*
- (iii) Predict the outcome of `run SomeOverdue for 4` and `check AtMostTwo for 4`. Justify each. *(3 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Internal Architecture of the Alloy Analyzer — 7 marks**

Refer to the **Block-Level Architecture** diagram in Section 4.1. The student must reproduce a *flowchart* with the following nodes: *Lexer/Parser → Type Checker → FOL+TC Translator → Scope Bounder → Skolemizer → Boolean Matrix Encoder → CNF Converter → SAT Solver → Visualizer*. Marks allocation:

- 2 marks for the **diagram** (must include all 8 nodes plus the SAT-solver decision diamond).
- 1 mark for naming the SAT solver (MiniSat / Sat4J).
- 1 mark each for explaining the **four most important stages** (suggested: FOL+TC translation, Skolemization, Boolean encoding, SAT solving). Total 4 marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Warning:** Many students confuse *parsing* with *type-checking*. Parsing produces a syntax tree; type-checking resolves signatures and infers relation arities. Drawing a *single box* for both stages costs **1 mark**.

**(b) Worked Solution — 7 marks**

**(i) Translation of `NoDuplicate` and `AtMostTwo` (3 marks):**

- `NoDuplicate`:
  $$\forall\, b_1, b_2{:}Book,\; b_1 \neq b_2 \;\rightarrow\; \neg (isbn(b_1, v) \land isbn(b_2, v))$$
  Equivalently: $b_1.isbn \neq b_2.isbn$ whenever $b_1 \neq b_2$.
- `AtMostTwo`:
  $$\forall\, b{:}Book,\; \#loans(b) \leq 2$$
  The `#loans(b)` notation is shorthand for the *cardinality* of the set of borrowers related to $b$, which the Alloy Analyzer unrolls as:
  $$\forall b{:}Book,\; \neg(\exists l_1, l_2, l_3{:}Borrower,\; distinct(l_1, l_2, l_3) \land \bigwedge_{i=1}^{3} loans(b, l_i))$$

**(ii) Maximum atoms (1 mark):**

- With **bit-width = 4**, a single signature can carry $2^4 = 16$ atoms. The scope of 4 limits `Book` and `Borrower` each to 4 atoms. Therefore:
  - $\#Book_{\max} = 4$
  - $\#Borrower_{\max} = 4$

**(iii) Predicted outcomes (3 marks):**

- `run SomeOverdue for 4` — The conjunction of `NoDuplicate` with $\#loans(b) > 2$ for some $b$ is satisfiable. **Outcome: SAT — a witness is found** showing a single book lent to (at least) three borrowers.
- `check AtMostTwo for 4` — The conjunction of `NoDuplicate` with the negation of `AtMostTwo` (i.e. $\exists b{:}Book, \#loans(b) \geq 3$) is satisfiable. **Outcome: SAT — a counter-example is found.** The assertion `AtMostTwo` is **violated** within scope 4.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2 — Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** Explain the *small-scope hypothesis* and the *bounded exhaustive analysis* approach used in Alloy. Discuss the *limitations* of bounded analysis. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For the Alloy specification below, perform a **manual translation to FOL+TC** and **predict the SAT/UNSAT outcome** for each command.

```alloy
sig Node { adj: set Node }
fact Acyclic { no n:Node | n in n.^adj }
pred TwoNodes { #Node = 2 }
assert ReachableForward { all n:Node | n in Node - n.^adj implies ... }
run TwoNodes for 3
check ReachableForward for 3
```

- (i) Translate the fact `Acyclic` into FOL+TC. *(2 marks)*
- (ii) Translate the predicate `TwoNodes`. *(1 mark)*
- (iii) State the **truth value** of `Acyclic` in the empty model (no nodes). Justify. *(1 mark)*
- (iv) Will the Analyzer return a counter-example for `check ReachableForward`? Justify with reference to the *small-scope hypothesis*. *(3 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Small-scope hypothesis and bounded exhaustive analysis (7 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** The small-scope hypothesis is the empirical observation that most design-level counter-examples in relational models are detectable within a scope of at most 6 atoms.
- **Why bounded analysis works (2 marks):** Because (i) SAT is decidable in finite scopes, (ii) bugs are *local*, (iii) designers only need to catch errors at the design stage, not prove deep theorems.
- **Process (2 marks):** The user fixes a scope $n$ and a bit-width $b$. The Analyzer exhaustively explores every relational binding consistent with the model up to $n$. For each assertion, it tries to find a counter-example; for each predicate, it tries to find a witness.
- **Limitations (2 marks):** (1) Cannot prove correctness for scopes $> n$; (2) counter-examples at higher scopes may be missed (false negatives); (3) choosing an inappropriate scope wastes time; (4) bit-width limitations cap integer ranges; (5) unbounded types like `seq` (sequences) must be explicitly bounded, else the encoding explodes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Warning:** Students who claim “Alloy is a *complete* proof system” or “Alloy verifies *all* possible executions” lose 1 mark per such phrase. The correct phrase is **“bounded exhaustive analysis within the given scope.”**

**(b) Manual Translation and Outcome Prediction (7 marks):**

**(i) Translation of `Acyclic` (2 marks):**

$$ \text{no } n{:}Node \mid n \in n.\hat{adj} $$

$$ \equiv \;\; \forall\, n{:}Node,\; \neg\,(n, n) \in \hat{adj} $$

$$ \equiv \;\; \forall\, n{:}Node,\; \forall\, k \geq 1,\; \neg\,(adj^k)(n, n) $$

The transitive closure $\hat{adj} = adj \cup adj.adj \cup \ldots$ is unrolled to depth $n = 3$ in the scope.

**(ii) Translation of `TwoNodes` (1 mark):**

$$ \#Node = 2 \;\;\equiv\;\; \exists\, a, b{:}Node,\; a \neq b \;\land\; \forall\, c{:}Node,\; c = a \lor c = b $$

**(iii) Truth in the empty model (1 mark):** *True* — the empty set vacuously satisfies `Acyclic` because there is no $n$ for which the body holds. The Analyzer would happily return an empty instance.

**(iv) Counter-example prediction (3 marks):** Given that `ReachableForward` involves the complement `Node - n.^adj`, the Analyzer searches for a node $n$ that is *not* reachable from itself. By the *small-scope hypothesis*, if a violation exists, it surfaces by scope 3. With two or more nodes and an `adj` field that does *not* form a complete graph, the predicate is almost certainly violated, hence the Analyzer returns a **counter-example** and the assertion is **falsified within scope 3**.

---

## 5.3 Examiner’s Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 KTU Mark-Deduction Triggers on “Analysing Alloy Models”**
> 1. **Calling Alloy a *theorem prover*** — it is a *bounded model checker* powered by SAT.
> 2. **Saying `check` is *successful* when a counter-example is found** — it is *unsuccessful* (i.e., the assertion is *wrong*). The wording matters.
> 3. **Ignoring the scope** — “the assertion is true” is wrong; “the assertion is true *within scope 3*” is correct.
> 4. **Confusing Skolemization with quantification** — Skolemization *replaces* existential quantifiers, not universal ones.
> 5. **Forgetting the bit-width** — the default is **4**, not 32. Integers are bit-vectors in Alloy.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Alloy Analyzer** = a bounded model checker for relational first-order logic with transitive closure.
- **Three commands**: `run` (find witness), `check` (find counter-example), `run {}` (check spec consistency).
- **Outcome meaning**: `run` returning *no instance* = over-constrained model (bug); `check` returning *no counter-example* = assertion holds *in the chosen scope*.
- **Underlying engine**: SAT solver (MiniSat / Sat4J) over Boolean matrices encoding the relational semantics.
- **Default scope = 3, default bit-width = 4.**
- **Small-scope hypothesis** justifies bounded analysis for design-time bug finding.
- **Alloy is *not* a theorem prover** — it is *sound up to scope*, not *complete*.
- **Translation chain**: Alloy → FOL+TC → Skolemized formula → Boolean matrix → CNF → SAT.
- **Visualizer** is a *post-processing* tool, not a verification tool; the SAT solver is the verifier.
- **Iteration loop**: Analyse → Visualise → Refine → Re-analyse is mandatory for non-trivial specs.
- **Skolemization** eliminates existentials, leaving universals to become a conjunction of ground instances.
- **Transitive closure** (`^`) is *unrolled* to the scope depth during translation.
- **Search space** grows as $2^{n^2}$ per binary relation — practical scopes are tiny.
- **Counter-example path** is the *primary* artefact examined by a KTU student during analysis.
- **Examiner-recommended sequence**: 3 → 4 → 5 → *rewrite the model*; the bug is usually in the `fact`s.
- **Partial relations** (`lone`, `set`) often produce counter-examples via *off-table* atoms.
- **Module 2’s high-yield keywords** for answer-writing: *bounded exhaustive, SAT, Skolem, scope, witness, counter-example, sound-but-not-complete, small-scope.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fixing Bugs in Modelling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST741 - FORMAL METHODS IN SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fixing Bugs in Modelling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Formal Methods in Software Engineering (PECST741)**, a **"bug in modelling"** is formally defined as a *defect, inconsistency, incompleteness, or anomaly* embedded within a formal specification that causes the model to diverge from the real-world requirements, the intended system behaviour, or from the rules of the underlying mathematical logic in which the model is expressed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> A *modelling bug* is any syntactic, type-theoretic, semantic, logical, or refinement-level error that appears when a software engineer translates informal requirements into a formal model (Z, VDM, B, Alloy, Event-B, etc.). The activity of *fixing bugs in modelling* encompasses the entire defect-management lifecycle applied to the model itself: **detection → diagnosis → repair → re-verification**.

These defects are categorised along three orthogonal axes in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

| Axis | Bug Type | Example |
|------|----------|---------|
| Syntactic | Notation misuse | Wrong Z schema-decorator usage (`Δ` instead of `Ξ`) |
| Semantic | Wrong domain/range | `total: ℕ` when it should be `ℕ₁` |
| Logical | Contradiction in invariants | $\text{inv1} \land \text{inv2} \equiv \text{false}$ |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of building a **scale architectural model** of a building before construction. If the model has the wrong number of floors, the wrong orientation of stairs, or a door in a load-bearing wall, the resulting skyscraper will collapse. We don't wait for the building to fall — we *inspect the model* under a magnifying glass, on a shake-table, with wind simulators, and with computer analysis.

A **formal software model is that architectural miniature**, written in mathematical notation, and **"fixing bugs in modelling"** is the disciplined act of *detecting and patching the miniature before pouring the concrete of actual code*. The model is small, cheap, and easy to inspect — but it is only useful if it is *correct*; otherwise, the bug propagates downwards into the implementation.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters for KTU Examiners

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Examiners at KTU 2024 scheme frequently award marks for *correctly classifying a bug*, *proposing a fix using schema calculus or refinement rules*, and *demonstrating proof obligations are satisfied post-fix*. Memorising tool names is **not** sufficient — examiners expect **logical justification** of the fix.

### Three Pillars of Bug-Fixing in Modelling (KTU 2024)

1. **Static Bug Detection** — Type-checking, domain-checking, and proof obligations computed *without executing* the model.
2. **Dynamic Bug Detection** — Animation (executing the model on sample inputs) and model checking (exhaustive state-space exploration).
3. **Bug Repair via Refinement** — Forward/backward simulation proofs, precondition weakening, and postcondition strengthening.

---

> **Visual Placeholder Anchor (continued in Section 4):** The end-to-end model-debugging pipeline is shown as a Mermaid process diagram in Section 4.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Modelling Bugs (KTU High-Yield Classification)

The KTU 2024 PECST741 syllabus organises modelling bugs into **five canonical categories**. Each category has a *signature* (how it is detected) and a *remedy* (how it is fixed).

### 2.1.1 Category A — Syntactic / Typing Bugs

These are caught by the **type-checker** of the formal-method tool. They include:

- Wrong number of arguments in a generic instantiation.
- Using a relation where a function is required.
- Mismatched decorations (e.g., `state'` instead of `state!`).

**Fix:** Re-write the schema according to the language's BNF grammar. No mathematical proof is needed; the parser rejects the model.

### 2.1.2 Category B — Domain (Initialisation) Bugs

The **initial state schema** has no inhabitants. Formally, if `Init ≜ [State | preInit]` is the initial state, then we require:

$$\text{dom}(\text{Init}) \neq \varnothing$$

A model whose initial state is *empty* cannot be executed — the system can never start.

**Fix:** Loosen the predicates of `Init` or tighten the state variables' types so that *at least one* state is reachable.

### 2.1.3 Category C — Stuck-Operation (Liveness) Bugs

An operation `Op` is *stuck* in state $s$ if its precondition is false in $s$, i.e., the system has reached a state from which it cannot proceed. The KTU proof obligation is:

$$\forall \text{State} \cdot \text{pre}\;Op \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{pre}\;Op$$

Equivalently, for every reachable state $s$:

$$s \in \text{reachable\_states} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{pre}\;Op(s) = \text{true}$$

**Fix:** *Weaken* the precondition (drop a conjunct) or *strengthen* the invariant to bring the state back into the precondition's domain.

### 2.1.4 Category D — Refinement / Simulation Bugs

When moving from an **abstract model** $A$ to a **concrete model** $C$, the retrieve relation `retrieve: A_State ↔ C_State` must satisfy the *forward simulation* obligations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Init}_C &\;\Rightarrow\; \exists A\_State \cdot \text{Init}_A \land \text{retrieve} \\
\text{pre}\;Op_A \land \text{retrieve} \land \text{Op}_C &\;\Rightarrow\; \exists A\_State' \cdot Op_A \land \text{retrieve}'
\end{aligned}
$$

A **refinement bug** occurs when these obligations are *not* discharged by the theorem prover.

**Fix:** Adjust the abstract postcondition (weaken it) or the concrete precondition (strengthen it), following the *postcondition-strengthening* and *precondition-weakening* rules:

$$
\text{pre}\;Op_A \;\Leftarrow\; \text{pre}\;Op_C \quad\text{and}\quad \text{post}\;Op_A \;\Rightarrow\; \text{post}\;Op_C
$$

### 2.1.5 Category E — Inconsistency with Requirements

The model is *internally consistent* (passes all proof obligations) but does **not** match the customer requirement. These bugs are the *hardest* — they are not caught by tools, only by **validation** (animation, review, walk-throughs).

**Fix:** Update the *requirements* document and re-derive the model. This is sometimes called the *replay-from-requirements* technique.

## 2.2 The Five-Step Bug-Fix Workflow

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus prescribes a **systematic workflow** for fixing modelling bugs. Memorise these five steps; they appear verbatim in past exam questions.

1. **Detect** — Run the type-checker, domain-checker, animator, and model-checker.
2. **Localise** — Read the counter-example or failing proof goal; identify the offending schema and predicate line.
3. **Diagnose** — Classify the bug into one of Categories A–E above.
4. **Repair** — Apply the appropriate fix: re-write, weaken pre, strengthen post, add invariant, or split operation.
5. **Re-verify** — Re-run the full proof-obligation suite. If a goal still fails, iterate from step 2.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | When Used |
|---|---------|---------------|-----------|
| 1 | Initial-state existence | $\text{dom}(\text{Init}) \neq \varnothing$ | Domain bug check |
| 2 | Operation non-stuckness | $\forall s \in \text{reachable} \cdot \text{pre}\;Op(s)$ | Liveness bug check |
| 3 | Type well-formedness | $\forall x \cdot x \in S \Rightarrow \text{type}(x) = T$ | Type-bug check |
| 4 | Forward simulation (data) | $\text{Init}_C \Rightarrow \exists a \cdot \text{Init}_A \land R(a,c)$ | Refinement check |
| 5 | Forward simulation (op) | $\text{pre}\;Op_A \land R(a,c) \land Op_C(c,c') \Rightarrow \exists a' \cdot Op_A(a,a') \land R(a',c')$ | Refinement check |
| 6 | Precondition weakening (valid) | $\text{pre}\;Op_{\text{new}} \Leftarrow \text{pre}\;Op_{\text{old}}$ | Fix stuck op |
| 7 | Postcondition strengthening (valid) | $\text{post}\;Op_{\text{new}} \Rightarrow \text{post}\;Op_{\text{old}}$ | Fix over-permissive op |
| 8 | Invariant strengthening (valid) | $I_{\text{new}} \Rightarrow I_{\text{old}}$ | Tighten reachable space |
| 9 | Schema composition | $S_1 \circ S_2 \;\widehat{=}\; \exists \text{mid} \cdot S_1 \land S_2[\text{mid}/\text{state}'\text{etc.}]$ | Build sequential ops |
| 10 | Domain restriction | $\text{dom}(R) = \{x \mid \exists y \cdot x \mapsto y \in R\}$ | Locate unreferenced elements |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In the table above, the logical symbol "$\Rightarrow$" is rendered via LaTeX, *not* the vertical pipe `|`. KTU examiners *do not* penalise LaTeX but *do* penalise Markdown-table breakage caused by unescaped pipes.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does industry care?** In safety-critical domains — avionics (DO-178C Level A), railway signalling (EN 50128 SIL 3/4), medical devices (IEC 62304 Class C), and nuclear reactor control (IEC 61508 SIL 4) — *fixing bugs in modelling* is **not optional**. A bug caught at the Z/VDM specification stage costs ≈ **1 unit**; the same bug caught at unit-test costs ≈ **10 units**; at system test ≈ **100 units**; in the field ≈ **10 000 units** (Boehm's cost-of-defect curve, applied to formal specs).

The **Paris Métro Line 14**, the **Shinkansen signalling system**, and the **Roissy CDG VAL shuttle** are famous case studies where formal Z and B models caught critical defects *before* any hardware was procured.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Proving a Stuck-Operation Bug and Fixing It

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

Consider a *Traffic Light Controller* with the following Z-style abstract model.

**State Schema:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TLC} \;\widehat{=}\;& [\text{colour} : \{\text{red}, \text{amber}, \text{green}\}; \\
&\text{time} : \mathbb{N}]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Initial State:**

$$\text{InitTLC} \;\widehat{=}\; [\text{TLC}' \mid \text{colour}' = \text{red} \land \text{time}' = 30]$$

**Operation `Tick` (advance by 1 second, decrementing timer):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tick} \;\widehat{=}\;& [\Delta \text{TLC} \mid \text{time} > 0 \;\land\; \text{time}' = \text{time} - 1 \;\land\; \text{colour}' = \text{colour}]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Operation `NextColour` (when timer reaches 0, rotate colour):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NextColour} \;\widehat{=}\;& [\Delta \text{TLC} \mid \text{time} = 0 \;\land \\
&\text{time}' = 30 \;\land\; \text{colour}' = \text{next}(\text{colour})]
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\text{next} : \text{colour} \rightarrow \text{colour}$ is defined cyclically.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Proof — Is `NextColour` Stuck?

> **Step 1.** Compute the precondition of `NextColour` in the *post-state* of `Tick`.

After one `Tick`, the new state has $\text{time}' = \text{time} - 1$. For the system to be able to call `NextColour` next, we need $\text{time}' = 0$.

Setting $\text{time} - 1 = 0$ gives $\text{time} = 1$.

So `NextColour` is callable *only* if the previous `Tick` reduced `time` from $1$ to $0$. The system starts with $\text{time} = 30$, and `Tick` decrements by $1$ each time. After $30$ ticks, $\text{time} = 0$, and `NextColour` *can* fire.

> **Step 2.** Check reachability of the trigger state $(\text{time} = 1)$.

We must show: $\text{InitTLC} \Rightarrow \text{pre}(\text{Tick}^{30})$ where $\text{Tick}^{30}$ is the 30-fold sequential composition of `Tick`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tick}^{30} &\;\equiv\; [\text{time} > 0 \;\land\; \text{time}' = \text{time} - 30] \\
\text{pre}(\text{Tick}^{30}) &\;\equiv\; \text{time} \geq 30
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the initial state has $\text{time} = 30 \geq 30$, the precondition is satisfied. So after $30$ ticks, the state is $(\text{time} = 0, \text{colour} = \text{red})$.

> **Step 3.** Verify that `NextColour` is applicable in $(\text{time} = 0, \text{colour} = \text{red})$.

Precondition of `NextColour`: $\text{time} = 0$. ✔ Satisfied. The operation is *not* stuck.

> **Step 4.** Now introduce a deliberate modelling bug: the engineer writes $\text{time}' = 29$ instead of $\text{time}' = 30$ in `InitTLC`.

Let $\text{InitTLC}_{\text{buggy}} \;\widehat{=}\; [\text{TLC}' \mid \text{colour}' = \text{red} \;\land\; \text{time}' = 29]$.

Repeating Step 2 with the buggy initialisation:

$$\text{time} = 29 \;\not\geq\; 30 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{pre}(\text{Tick}^{30}) = \text{false}$$

Therefore the state $(\text{time} = 0)$ is *unreachable*, and `NextColour` is **stuck forever** — a classic Category C bug.

> **Step 5.** The fix — initialisation bug (Category B, since initial state is also no longer *uniquely* populated but `time = 29` is still a valid state, so this is a Category C stuck-op bug induced by Category B initialisation).

**Fix Option 1 (Category B fix):** Restore the correct initialisation: $\text{time}' = 30$.

**Fix Option 2 (Category C fix — precondition weakening):** Loosen `Tick`'s precondition so that it works for any `time` (e.g., allow `time' = 0` if `time = 0` — i.e., make `Tick` a no-op at the boundary):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tick}_{\text{fixed}} \;\widehat{=}\;& [\Delta \text{TLC} \mid \text{time} \geq 0 \;\land\; \\
&\text{if } \text{time} = 0 \text{ then } \text{time}' = 0 \text{ else } \text{time}' = \text{time} - 1 \;\land\; \text{colour}' = \text{colour}]
\end{aligned}
$$

This violates the original intention (a stuck-at-zero clock is still a bug), so **Fix Option 1 is preferred** — illustrating that the KTU examiner rewards *correct classification before fix*.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Refinement Bug and Forward-Simulation Proof

### 3.2.1 Abstract Model $A$

A *counter* that can be incremented or decremented, but never below $0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Counter}_A &\;\widehat{=}\; [\text{val} : \mathbb{N}] \\
\text{InitCounter}_A &\;\widehat{=}\; [\text{Counter}_A' \mid \text{val}' = 0] \\
\text{Inc}_A &\;\widehat{=}\; [\Delta \text{Counter}_A \mid \text{val}' = \text{val} + 1] \\
\text{Dec}_A &\;\widehat{=}\; [\Delta \text{Counter}_A \mid \text{val} > 0 \;\land\; \text{val}' = \text{val} - 1]
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.2 Concrete Model $C$ (with deliberate bug)

The engineer introduces a *defensive* $\text{val} \geq 0$ check but forgets the strict inequality, allowing the concrete decrement when $\text{val} = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Counter}_C &\;\widehat{=}\; [\text{val} : \mathbb{N}] \\
\text{Dec}_C^{\text{buggy}} &\;\widehat{=}\; [\Delta \text{Counter}_C \mid \text{val} \geq 0 \;\land\; \text{val}' = \text{val} - 1]
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Forward Simulation Proof Obligation

Using the trivial retrieve relation $R(a, c) \;\widehat{=}\; a.\text{val} = c.\text{val}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Obligation} \;:\;& \text{pre}\; \text{Dec}_A \;\land\; R(a, c) \;\land\; \text{Dec}_C^{\text{buggy}}(c, c') \;\Rightarrow\; \exists a' \cdot \text{Dec}_A(a, a') \;\land\; R(a', c')
\end{aligned}
$$

**Substitute** the predicates:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LHS} &\;\equiv\; (a.\text{val} > 0) \;\land\; (a.\text{val} = c.\text{val}) \;\land\; (c.\text{val} \geq 0 \;\land\; c.\text{val}' = c.\text{val} - 1) \\
\text{RHS} &\;\equiv\; \exists a' \cdot (a'.\text{val} = a.\text{val} - 1) \;\land\; (a'.\text{val} = c.\text{val}')
\end{aligned}
$$

Instantiate $a' = (a.\text{val} - 1)$:

$$a'.\text{val} = a.\text{val} - 1 \;\land\; c.\text{val}' = c.\text{val} - 1 \;\land\; a.\text{val} = c.\text{val} \;\Rightarrow\; a'.\text{val} = c.\text{val}' \; \checkmark$$

So the simulation obligation *is* satisfied for $\text{val} > 0$. But what about the case $\text{val} = 0$?

In the abstract model, $\text{Dec}_A$ is *not enabled* when $\text{val} = 0$. In the concrete model, $\text{Dec}_C^{\text{buggy}}$ *is* enabled and produces $\text{val}' = -1$ — but wait, $\text{val} : \mathbb{N}$, so this gives a **type error** (Category A bug, but at runtime/execution level).

The **Category D refinement bug** is more subtle: the *retrieve* relation fails to capture the boundary. The fix is to **strengthen the concrete precondition** to match the abstract:

$$\text{Dec}_C^{\text{fixed}} \;\widehat{=}\; [\Delta \text{Counter}_C \mid \text{val} > 0 \;\land\; \text{val}' = \text{val} - 1]$$

Now the simulation is trivially preserved.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Bug → Detection Method → Fix

| Bug Category | Detection Method | Diagnostic Output | Repair Action | Re-Verification |
|--------------|------------------|-------------------|---------------|-----------------|
| A. Syntactic | Parser / Type-checker | Syntax error line number | Rewrite notation | Re-parse |
| B. Domain | Domain-checker | "No initial state" message | Loosen `Init` predicates | Re-run domain check |
| C. Stuck-op | Theorem prover | Undischarged goal: $\text{pre}\;Op = \text{false}$ | Weaken pre / Strengthen inv | Re-prove |
| D. Refinement | Forward-sim checker | Failed simulation goal | Adjust retrieve / pre / post | Re-check refine |
| E. Requirements | Animation / Review | Customer rejects demo | Re-derive from updated reqs | Re-validate |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The Five-Step Bug-Fix Workflow (Iterative Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Step 1: Build Formal Model"] --> A2["Step 2: Run Static Checks"]
    A2 --> A3["Step 3: Run Dynamic Checks"]
    A3 --> A4{"Any Counter Example or Failed Proof Goal?"}
    A4 -- Yes --> A5["Step 4A: Localise the Bug in Schema"]
    A5 --> A6["Step 4B: Classify Bug Type A to E"]
    A6 --> A7["Step 4C: Apply Repair Rule"]
    A7 --> A8["Step 4D: Re run Full Proof Obligation Suite"]
    A8 --> A4
    A4 -- No --> A9["Step 5: Model Accepted - Move to Refinement"]
    A9 --> A10["Step 6: Verify Refinement Obligations"]
    A10 --> A11{"Refinement Holds?"}
    A11 -- No --> A12["Step 7: Adjust Concrete Model"]
    A12 --> A10
    A11 -- Yes --> A13["Step 8: Generate Code from Concrete Model"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Model-Debugging Toolkit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1["Formal Specification File in Z or VDM"] --> B2["Lexical and Syntax Analyser"]
    B2 -- Token Stream --> B3["Type Checker Module"]
    B3 -- Typed AST --> B4["Domain Checker Module"]
    B4 -- Typed AST --> B5["Proof Obligation Generator"]
    B5 -- Goal Set --> B6["Automated Theorem Prover"]
    B6 -- Proof Status --> B7["Diagnosis Engine"]
    B5 -- Goal Set --> B8["Model Checker Backend"]
    B8 -- Counter Example Trace --> B7
    B1 --> B9["Animator Engine"]
    B9 -- Sample Trace --> B10["Requirements Validation UI"]
    B10 -- User Feedback --> B7
    B7 -- Bug Report --> B11["Repair Advisor Module"]
    B11 -- Suggested Fix --> B12["Model Editor"]
    B12 -- Updated Model --> B2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Hierarchical Taxonomy of Modelling Bugs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1["Modelling Bug Root"] --> C2["Category A: Syntactic and Typing"]
    C1 --> C3["Category B: Domain and Initialisation"]
    C1 --> C4["Category C: Stuck Operation Liveness"]
    C1 --> C5["Category D: Refinement and Simulation"]
    C1 --> C6["Category E: Requirements Mismatch"]
    C2 --> C21["A1: Wrong Decorator Usage"]
    C2 --> C22["A2: Type Mismatch in Expression"]
    C3 --> C31["B1: Empty Initial State"]
    C3 --> C32["B2: Unreachable Initial State"]
    C4 --> C41["C1: Precondition Too Strong"]
    C4 --> C42["C2: Invariant Contradicts Pre"]
    C5 --> C51["D1: Retrieve Relation Wrong"]
    C5 --> C52["D2: Concrete Pre Weaker than Abstract"]
    C5 --> C53["D3: Concrete Post Not Strong Enough"]
    C6 --> C61["E1: Misread Natural Language"]
    C6 --> C62["E2: Outdated Requirements Doc"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All Mermaid node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes (`A1`, `B7`, `C21`, etc.). Labels contain plain uppercase alphanumeric text — **no bold `**`, no italics `*`, no nested markdown tables inside double-quoted labels**, as mandated by the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 specification.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **"List any three categories of bugs that can occur in a formal software model. For each, give a one-line example."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

1. **Syntactic/Typing Bug** — Using `ΔState` (change decorator) where the operation should logically be `ΞState` (no change), causing an over-specification error. (1 mark)
2. **Domain/Initialisation Bug** — Defining `Init ≜ [x' : ℕ | x' < 0]`, which yields an empty schema and no initial state. (1 mark)
3. **Refinement Bug** — A concrete operation with a precondition *weaker* than the abstract, violating the *forward simulation* proof obligation. (1 mark)

> **Examiner's Tip:** Award 1 mark per correct category with a valid example. Generic answers like "model errors" without classification get 0.

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **"Differentiate between validation and verification in the context of fixing bugs in formal modelling."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Verification | Validation |
|--------|--------------|------------|
| **Goal** | *Are we building the model right?* | *Are we building the right model?* |
| **Method** | Type-checking, proof obligations, refinement checks | Animation, requirements walk-throughs, customer demos |
| **Catches** | Syntactic, semantic, refinement bugs (A–D) | Requirements-mismatch bugs (E) |
| **Tool** | Theorem prover, model checker | Animator, stakeholder review |

(3 marks — 1.5 for the contrast line, 1.5 for the bug-category mapping)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part-B question contains sub-parts (a) and (b) carrying **7 marks each**. Solutions must be complete and exhaustive.

---

### Question B — Choice 1 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

> **(a)** Consider a Z-style specification of a **stack of integers** with operations `Push`, `Pop`, and `NewStack`. The `Pop` operation is defined as:
>
> $$\text{Pop} \;\widehat{=}\; [\Delta \text{Stack} \mid \text{stack} \neq \langle\rangle \;\land\; \text{top} = \text{head}(\text{stack}) \;\land\; \text{stack}' = \text{tail}(\text{stack})]$$
>
> Show that `Pop` is *not* stuck in any reachable state. Identify the bug if the initial state were defined as `NewStack ≜ [Stack' | stack' = \langle\rangle \;\land\; top' = 999]`. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define reachability.** A state is reachable if it can be obtained from `NewStack` by a sequence of `Push`/`Pop` operations. The reachable set is:

$$\text{Reach} = \{\,s : \text{seq}\,\mathbb{Z} \mid \text{stack} = s \,\} \cup \{\,(\text{stack} = \langle\rangle, \text{top} = \text{undefined})\,\}$$

Since `NewStack` sets $\text{stack}' = \langle\rangle$, the empty sequence *is* a reachable state.

**Step 2 — Check the precondition of `Pop` on the empty state.** The precondition is $\text{stack} \neq \langle\rangle$. On the empty state, this is *false*. So `Pop` is *not* enabled immediately after `NewStack`.

**Step 3 — Check the precondition of `Pop` after a `Push`.** After one `Push(x)`, we have $\text{stack}' = \langle x \rangle \neq \langle\rangle$. Now `Pop` *is* enabled. ✔

**Step 4 — Conclude non-stuckness.** `Pop` is enabled in *every* reachable state that has a non-empty stack, and there exists a *bounded* sequence (`Push(x)`) that always takes the system to such a state. The liveness proof obligation is discharged.

**Step 5 — Identify the bug.** The buggy `NewStack` initialises `top' = 999` — a value that is *not* constrained by the model. This is a **Category B (Domain/Initialisation) bug**: a meaningless component enters the state. (7 marks — 2 for reachability, 2 for precondition check, 1 for conclusion, 2 for bug identification & classification)

---

> **(b)** With reference to the same stack specification, define the concept of **refinement** and show, using *forward simulation*, how a buggy concrete `Pop` that allows popping from an empty stack would be detected and fixed. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of Refinement.** A concrete model $C$ *refines* an abstract model $A$ if every observable behaviour of $C$ is also a behaviour of $A$. Formally, there exists a *retrieve relation* $R : \text{Stack}_A \leftrightarrow \text{Stack}_C$ such that the forward-simulation obligations hold.

**Step 2 — Define the retrieve relation.**

$$R(a, c) \;\widehat{=}\; a.\text{stack} = c.\text{stack} \;\land\; a.\text{top} = c.\text{top}$$

**Step 3 — State the buggy concrete operation.**

$$\text{Pop}_C^{\text{buggy}} \;\widehat{=}\; [\Delta \text{Stack}_C \mid \text{stack}' = \text{tail}(\text{stack})]$$

Note the *missing* precondition $\text{stack} \neq \langle\rangle$ — this is the bug.

**Step 4 — Formulate the forward-simulation obligation for `Pop`.**

$$
\text{Obligation} \;:\; \text{pre}\; \text{Pop}_A \;\land\; R(a, c) \;\land\; \text{Pop}_C^{\text{buggy}}(c, c') \;\Rightarrow\; \exists a' \cdot \text{Pop}_A(a, a') \;\land\; R(a', c')
$$

**Step 5 — Find a counter-example.** Take $a.\text{stack} = \langle\rangle$ and $c.\text{stack} = \langle\rangle$ (so $R$ holds).

- LHS: $\text{pre}\; \text{Pop}_A$ is $\text{stack} \neq \langle\rangle$, which is **false**. So the LHS of the implication is *false* — vacuously satisfied.

**Step 6 — The real bug emerges at the abstract-side reasoning.** In the abstract model, the system *should never* reach the state $(\text{stack} = \langle\rangle)$ and then call `Pop` — but if it does, `Pop_A` is stuck. In the concrete model, the buggy `Pop_C` *does* execute and produces $(\text{stack}' = \text{tail}(\langle\rangle) = \text{undefined})$ — a **runtime error**.

**Step 7 — The fix.** Restore the precondition:

$$\text{Pop}_C^{\text{fixed}} \;\widehat{=}\; [\Delta \text{Stack}_C \mid \text{stack} \neq \langle\rangle \;\land\; \text{stack}' = \text{tail}(\text{stack})]$$

Now the simulation is trivially preserved. (7 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for retrieve, 2 for buggy op, 1 for obligation, 1 for counter-example, 1 for fix)

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **(a)** Explain the **five-step workflow** for fixing bugs in formal modelling. For each step, state the input, the tool used (if any), and the expected output. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Step | Input | Tool | Output |
|------|-------|------|--------|
| 1. Detect | Formal model file | Type-checker, domain-checker, animator, model-checker | List of failed obligations / counter-examples |
| 2. Localise | Failed obligation | Goal-tracing in IDE | Schema and predicate line of defect |
| 3. Diagnose | Localised defect | Manual reasoning, classification chart | Bug category (A–E) |
| 4. Repair | Diagnosis | Schema calculus, rule application | Modified schema |
| 5. Re-verify | Modified model | Full PO suite | All obligations discharged OR new failure list |

(7 marks — 1 per step, with 2 additional marks for the input/output clarity)

---

> **(b)** A formal Z specification of a *bank account* has the schema:
>
> $$\text{Withdraw} \;\widehat{=}\; [\Delta \text{Account} \mid \text{balance} \geq 0 \;\land\; \text{balance}' = \text{balance} - \text{amount} \;\land\; \text{amount} > 0]$$
>
> Show that the system can deadlock when `balance = 0` and `amount > 0`. Propose and justify *two* distinct fixes using the *precondition-weakening* and *postcondition-strengthening* rules. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the precondition.** `pre Withdraw ≡ balance ≥ 0 ∧ amount > 0`. The pre is satisfied when `balance = 0` and `amount > 0`.

**Step 2 — Identify the bug.** The postcondition `balance' = balance − amount` requires `balance − amount ≥ 0` to satisfy the *invariant* `balance ≥ 0`. When `balance = 0` and `amount > 0`, the post-condition yields `balance' = −amount < 0`, **violating the invariant**. The model is internally inconsistent. (Category C, with a Category B invariant flavour.)

**Step 3 — Fix A: Precondition strengthening (most natural).** Strengthen the precondition to forbid overdraft:

$$\text{Withdraw}_{\text{fixA}} \;\widehat{=}\; [\Delta \text{Account} \mid \text{balance} \geq \text{amount} \;\land\; \text{amount} > 0 \;\land\; \text{balance}' = \text{balance} - \text{amount}]$$

This is a valid *strengthening* of the original pre `balance ≥ 0`. The operation is now never enabled when `balance = 0` and `amount > 0`. ✔

**Step 4 — Fix B: Postcondition strengthening with error handling.** Add an error branch:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Withdraw}_{\text{fixB}} \;\widehat{=}\;& [\Delta \text{Account} \mid \text{amount} > 0 \;\land\; \\
&( \text{balance} \geq \text{amount} \;\land\; \text{balance}' = \text{balance} - \text{amount} \;\land\; \text{status}' = \text{ok} \\
&\quad\lor\; \text{balance} < \text{amount} \;\land\; \text{balance}' = \text{balance} \;\land\; \text{status}' = \text{failed} )]
\end{aligned}
$$

This *strengthens* the original postcondition (which didn't specify `status`) and is therefore a valid refinement. ✔

**Step 5 — Why not precondition weakening?** The question asks for *precondition-weakening* as a fix option, but in this case, *weakening* would *increase* the set of states in which `Withdraw` is enabled — making the bug worse. Weakening is the wrong direction here. The correct tool is *precondition strengthening* (Fix A) or *postcondition strengthening* (Fix B). The student who mistakenly applies *weakening* should be marked down. (7 marks — 2 for bug identification, 2 for Fix A, 2 for Fix B, 1 for explicitly rejecting the wrong-direction fix)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks on "Fixing Bugs in Modelling" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Misclassifying the bug type** — saying "it's a syntax error" when it is actually a Category C stuck-operation bug. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks per misclassification.
> 2. **Applying the wrong-direction refinement rule** — *weakening* a precondition when *strengthening* was required (or vice-versa). This is the most common error and costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the re-verification step** — the KTU 2024 rubric explicitly awards **1 mark** for "re-running the proof obligations after the fix." Students who stop at "now it looks right" lose this mark.
> 4. **Forgetting to specify the retrieve relation** — when discussing refinement, omitting $R$ is treated as an incomplete answer.
> 5. **Confusing validation with verification** — saying "we use the theorem prover to find requirement bugs." Wrong tool for the wrong category. See Part A Question 2.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Module 2):**

- A *modelling bug* is a defect in the *formal specification itself*, not in the code that will be generated from it.
- There are **five canonical categories**: **A (Syntactic), B (Domain/Initialisation), C (Stuck Operation), D (Refinement), E (Requirements Mismatch)**.
- The **five-step workflow** is **Detect → Localise → Diagnose → Repair → Re-verify**. Memorise it verbatim.
- **Initialisation bug** check: $\text{dom}(\text{Init}) \neq \varnothing$.
- **Stuck-operation** check: $\forall s \in \text{reachable} \cdot \text{pre}\;Op(s) = \text{true}$.
- **Refinement** check: forward simulation with a *retrieve relation* $R$.
- **Valid refinement rules** — *precondition weakening* (more states can call op) and *postcondition strengthening* (fewer behaviours satisfy op) are *safe* directions; the reverse directions introduce bugs.
- **Verification** catches Categories A–D; **validation** catches Category E.
- **Common tools:** Z/EVES, ProofPower, Isabelle/Z, Rodin (Event-B), Alloy Analyzer, FDR, SPIN.
- **Cost-of-defect rationale:** catching a bug in the model is ≈ 10 000× cheaper than catching it in the field. This is the *business case* examiners expect you to articulate in 14-mark answers.
- **Diagrams to draw in exams:** the five-step cycle (Section 4, Diagram 1) and the toolkit architecture (Section 4, Diagram 2). Drawing them earns engagement marks even if your prose is incomplete.
- **Final mnemonic:** **"D-L-D-R-R"** = **D**etect, **L**ocalise, **D**iagnose, **R**epair, **R**e-verify.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: How Alloy Works? Showing that the Konigsberg Bridge Problem Has No Solution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST741 - FORMAL METHODS IN SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** How Alloy Works? Showing that the Konigsberg Bridge Problem Has No Solution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 2: How Alloy Works & Modeling the Konigsberg Bridge Problem

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Alloy? (Formal Definition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alloy** is a **declarative, lightweight formal specification language** developed at MIT by Daniel Jackson. It is built upon **first-order relational logic** augmented with **transitive closure**. Alloy is specifically engineered to express complex **structural constraints** and **behavioral properties** of software systems in a manner that is both mathematically rigorous and practically analyzable.

Unlike procedural programming languages that describe *how* to compute a result, Alloy describes *what* structural invariants must hold. The accompanying **Alloy Analyzer** performs **bounded model checking** by translating Alloy specifications into propositional satisfiability (SAT) problems, then delegating them to off-the-shelf SAT solvers.

### 1.2 The Konigsberg Bridge Problem (Historical Context)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Konigsberg Bridge Problem** (1736) is the foundational problem of **graph theory** and **topology**. It asks whether one can walk through the city of Konigsberg (now Kaliningrad) crossing each of its **7 bridges** exactly once and return to the starting point. **Leonhard Euler** proved in 1736 that **no such walk exists**, thereby founding modern graph theory.

The city had:
- **4 land masses** (vertices): North Bank, South Bank, Kneiphof (central island), and a smaller eastern island
- **7 bridges** (edges) connecting them

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Blueprint Inspector" 🎯

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** Think of Alloy as a **"hyper-precise building inspector"** for software. You hand it a **blueprint** (the model) and a **set of rules** (assertions/predicates). The inspector then *exhaustively* examines every possible way the building *could* be constructed within a given size and reports:
> - ✅ *"This blueprint is realizable as drawn"* (the model is satisfiable)
> - ❌ *"The blueprint has a structural flaw — here's the counterexample!"* (an assertion is violated)
>
> Just as an inspector does not need to physically build the building, Alloy does not need to execute the software — it *abstractly explores* all configurations up to a user-defined scope.

For the Konigsberg problem, the "blueprint" is the city layout, the "rule" is *"Can I walk across every bridge exactly once?"*, and the "inspector" (Alloy Analyzer) will return a counterexample *only if* such a walk exists. If it returns no counterexample within the scope, we have **formal evidence** (within the bounded universe) that no such walk exists.

### 1.4 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Signature (`sig`)** | Declares a set of atoms (e.g., `sig Land {}`) |
| **Field (`bridges`)** | A relation between signatures |
| **Predicate (`pred`)** | A named, parameterized constraint |
| **Assertion (`assert`)** | A property that *must* hold in all instances |
| **Fact** | A constraint assumed to always be true |
| **Scope** | Bounds on universe size (e.g., `for 4 Land, 7 Bridge`) |
| **SAT Solver** | Boolean satisfiability engine (e.g., MiniSat) used internally |
| **Bounded Model Checking** | Verification technique limited to a finite scope |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 How Alloy Works — The Operational Pipeline

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Alloy's operation is a **5-stage pipeline** that bridges human-readable specifications and machine-verifiable logic.

### Stage-by-Stage Breakdown

1. **Specification (Modeling)**
   - The user writes a model in `.als` text files using signatures, relations, facts, predicates, and assertions.
   - The model captures *what* the world (system) looks like and *what rules* it must obey.

2. **Translation to Relational Logic**
   - The Alloy Analyzer parses the `.als` file and converts all constructs into **first-order relational logic formulas** (with transitive closure).
   - Set operations like `+`, `&`, `-`, `in`, `=`, `<:`, `:>` are interpreted as logical quantifiers and Boolean connectives.

3. **Translation to Boolean Logic (CNF)**
   - The relational logic formulas are reduced to **propositional logic** by:
     - **Skolemization** (eliminating existential quantifiers using fresh constants)
     - **Reduction to SAT**: every atom in the bounded universe becomes a Boolean variable
     - Conversion to **Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF)** for the SAT solver

4. **Bounded Model Checking via SAT Solver**
   - The SAT solver (typically **MiniSat**, **Kodkod's** internal engine) searches for a satisfying assignment.
   - The search is *bounded* by the user-specified scope (e.g., `for 4 Land, 7 Bridge` means at most 4 Land atoms and 7 Bridge atoms).

5. **Result Reporting**
   - **Satisfiable (SAT)** → A counterexample (instance) is generated and rendered visually.
   - **Unsatisfiable (UNSAT)** → The assertion holds within the given scope (the model is "proven" correct up to that bound).

> [!NOTE]
> The **Small Scope Hypothesis** (a foundational assumption in Alloy) states that most bugs manifest in *small* instances. This is why bounded checking, despite not being absolute proof, is empirically powerful.

## 2.2 Core Architecture of the Alloy Analyzer

```text
+--------------------------+
|   .als Source File       |   ← User writes signatures, facts, assertions
+--------------------------+
            │
            ▼
+--------------------------+
|   Kodkod Model Finder    |   ← Translates FOL → Boolean SAT
+--------------------------+
            │
            ▼
+--------------------------+
|   SAT Solver (MiniSat)   |   ← Finds a satisfying assignment
+--------------------------+
            │
            ▼
+--------------------------+
|   Counterexample Renderer|   ← Visualizes the instance (graph, table)
+--------------------------+
```

## 2.3 Euler's Theorem on Graph Traversal (The Mathematical Foundation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> To understand the Konigsberg problem, students **must internalize Euler's Theorem** — this is the most-tested concept in graph-theoretic KTU questions on this topic.

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Graph Theory)

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering / Software Utility |
|---|---------|----------------|--------------------------------|
| 1 | **Degree of a vertex** | $\deg(v) = \text{number of edges incident to } v$ | Counting dependencies in a software module graph |
| 2 | **Handshaking Lemma** | $\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 2 \vert E \vert$ | Verifying graph integrity in network protocols |
| 3 | **Eulerian Path** | A trail that uses every edge **exactly once** | Optimal route planning, network packet traversal |
| 4 | **Eulerian Circuit** | An Eulerian path that **starts and ends** at the same vertex | Verifying cyclic dependencies in build systems |
| 5 | **Euler's Existence Theorem (Path)** | An undirected graph has an Eulerian path **iff** it is connected AND has **exactly 0 or 2** vertices of odd degree | Garbage collection, deadlock detection, mail delivery |
| 6 | **Euler's Existence Theorem (Circuit)** | An undirected graph has an Eulerian circuit **iff** it is connected AND **all** vertices have even degree | Round-trip testing in test automation |
| 7 | **Odd-Degree Vertex Count** | Number of odd-degree vertices is **always even** | Proves impossibility of certain configurations |
| 8 | **Konigsberg Result** | All 4 vertices have odd degree (3, 3, 5, 3) → **No Eulerian path/circuit** | Demonstrates necessity of formal verification |

## 2.4 Mapping the Konigsberg Problem to Alloy

The translation is as follows:

| Graph Theory Concept | Alloy Construct |
|----------------------|-----------------|
| Vertices (land masses) | `sig Land {}` |
| Edges (bridges) | `sig Bridge {}` |
| Incidence relation | `sig Land { bridges: set Bridge }` |
| Specific city layout | A `pred Konigsberg` with 4 named Land atoms |
| "Walk crossing each bridge once" | A predicate defining a path through the bridges |
| "No solution exists" | An `assert NoEulerianWalk` checked by the Analyzer |

### Real-World Software Engineering Utility

- **Software Architecture Verification**: Model module dependencies and check for circular references
- **Network Protocol Design**: Verify that routing graphs satisfy reachability properties
- **Access Control Modeling**: Model role hierarchies and verify security invariants
- **Database Schema Validation**: Verify referential integrity constraints
