# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Konigsberg Bridge Configuration — Formal Derivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following step-by-step derivation establishes the **degree count** of every vertex in the Konigsberg bridge graph. This is the **rigorous mathematical core** of the "no solution" proof.

### 3.1.1 Enumerating the Bridges

The original Konigsberg bridge configuration (as Euler described it) contains exactly **7 bridges**. Let us label them $b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_7$:

| Bridge Label | Connects |
|--------------|----------|
| $b_1$ | North Bank ↔ Kneiphof |
| $b_2$ | North Bank ↔ Kneiphof |
| $b_3$ | North Bank ↔ Island |
| $b_4$ | South Bank ↔ Kneiphof |
| $b_5$ | South Bank ↔ Kneiphof |
| $b_6$ | South Bank ↔ Island |
| $b_7$ | Kneiphof ↔ Island |

### 3.1.2 Computing the Degree of Each Vertex

By definition, the degree of a vertex is the number of edges incident to it.

$$
\deg(\text{North Bank}) = \vert\{b_1, b_2, b_3\}\vert = 3
$$

$$
\deg(\text{South Bank}) = \vert\{b_4, b_5, b_6\}\vert = 3
$$

$$
\deg(\text{Kneiphof}) = \vert\{b_1, b_2, b_4, b_5, b_7\}\vert = 5
$$

$$
\deg(\text{Island}) = \vert\{b_3, b_6, b_7\}\vert = 3
$$

### 3.1.3 Verification Using the Handshaking Lemma

The Handshaking Lemma states that the sum of all vertex degrees must equal **twice the number of edges** (since each edge contributes to the degree count of exactly two vertices).

$$
\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 2 \vert E \vert
$$

Substituting our computed values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) &= \deg(\text{North}) + \deg(\text{South}) + \deg(\text{Kneiphof}) + \deg(\text{Island}) \\
&= 3 + 3 + 5 + 3 \\
&= 14
\end{aligned}
$$

And the right-hand side:

$$
2 \vert E \vert = 2 \times 7 = 14
$$

Both sides are equal:

$$
14 = 14 \quad \checkmark
$$

This confirms the **arithmetic consistency** of our degree assignments. The Handshaking Lemma is also useful as a *sanity check* for Alloy-generated configurations.

### 3.1.4 Applying Euler's Existence Theorem

Euler's theorem states:

> An undirected connected graph admits an **Eulerian path** (a walk using every edge exactly once) **if and only if** it has **exactly 0 or 2 vertices of odd degree**.

In Konigsberg:

$$
\text{Number of odd-degree vertices} = \vert\{v \in V : \deg(v) \text{ is odd}\}\vert = \vert\{3, 3, 5, 3\}\vert = 4
$$

Since $4 \neq 0$ and $4 \neq 2$, the condition fails:

$$
\boxed{\therefore \text{The Konigsberg bridge graph admits NO Eulerian path.}}
$$

Note also that since there is more than 2 odd-degree vertices, there is **no Eulerian circuit** either (which requires 0 odd-degree vertices).

## 3.2 Modeling the Konigsberg Problem in Alloy — Full Exhaustive Walkthrough

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following is a **complete, executable Alloy specification** that models the Konigsberg bridge problem. This is the central artifact that students must be able to read, explain, and (in a KTU exam) annotate.

### 3.2.1 The Full Alloy Model

```alloy
module Konigsberg

-- ==================== SIGNATURES ====================
-- A Land mass (vertex in the graph)
sig Land {}

-- A Bridge (edge in the graph)
sig Bridge {
  ends: set Land   -- A bridge connects two land masses
}

-- ==================== FACTS ====================
-- The incidence relation: which bridges touch which land?
fact BridgeIncidence {
  -- A bridge must have exactly 2 distinct endpoints
  all b: Bridge | #(b.ends) = 2
}

-- ==================== THE KONIGSBERG CONFIGURATION ====================
pred Konigsberg() {
  -- Declare exactly 4 land masses and 7 bridges
  #Land = 4
  #Bridge = 7

  -- Each land mass must have a positive degree (touches at least one bridge)
  all l: Land | some l.~ends

  -- The specific topology of Konigsberg:
  -- North Bank (n) connects to Kneiphof via 2 bridges and to Island via 1 bridge
  -- South Bank (s) connects to Kneiphof via 2 bridges and to Island via 1 bridge
  -- Kneiphof (k) connects to North, South, and Island
  -- Island (i) connects to North, South, and Kneiphof
  some n, s, k, i: Land {
    n != s and n != k and n != i and s != k and s != i and k != i
    -- North Bank: 3 bridges (2 to Kneiphof, 1 to Island)
    -- South Bank: 3 bridges (2 to Kneiphof, 1 to Island)
    -- Kneiphof:    5 bridges (2 to North, 2 to South, 1 to Island)
    -- Island:      3 bridges (1 to North, 1 to South, 1 to Kneiphof)
  }
}

-- ==================== DEGREE DEFINITION ====================
fun degree[l: Land]: Int {
  #{b: Bridge | l in b.ends}
}

-- ==================== ASSERTION: NO EULERIAN WALK ====================
-- Euler's theorem: A connected graph has an Eulerian path iff
-- exactly 0 or 2 vertices have odd degree.
assert NoEulerianWalk {
  -- If the graph is connected and has the Konigsberg structure,
  -- then it CANNOT have 0 or 2 odd-degree vertices.
  -- Equivalently, all 4 vertices have odd degree.
  Konigsberg() implies {
    -- Count odd-degree vertices
    #{l: Land | (degree[l] % 2) = 1} != 0
    #{l: Land | (degree[l] % 2) = 1} != 2
  }
}

-- ==================== COMMAND ====================
-- Check the assertion for the bounded scope
check NoEulerianWalk for 4 Land, 7 Bridge
```

### 3.2.2 Detailed Line-by-Line Annotation

| Line / Block | Explanation |
|--------------|-------------|
| `module Konigsberg` | Names the module — every Alloy file is a module |
| `sig Land {}` | Declares a set `Land` of uninterpreted atoms (the vertices) |
| `sig Bridge { ends: set Land }` | Declares a set `Bridge` with a relation `ends` mapping each bridge to its two endpoint lands |
| `fact BridgeIncidence { ... }` | A **fact** is a constraint that must hold in every instance — bridges are 2-ended |
| `pred Konigsberg() { ... }` | A **predicate** that captures the specific city layout — 4 lands, 7 bridges, with degree distribution 3,3,5,3 |
| `fun degree[l: Land]: Int { ... }` | A **function** that returns the degree (cardinality of incident bridges) |
| `assert NoEulerianWalk { ... }` | The **assertion** to verify: Konigsberg has neither 0 nor 2 odd-degree vertices |
| `check NoEulerianWalk for 4 Land, 7 Bridge` | **Command** to the Analyzer: search for counterexamples within the bounded scope |

### 3.2.3 The Verification Argument

> [!TIP]
> When the Alloy Analyzer is invoked with `check NoEulerianWalk for 4 Land, 7 Bridge`, it:
> 1. Enumerates **all possible** configurations of 4 lands and 7 bridges satisfying the `Konigsberg` predicate.
> 2. For each configuration, it computes the degree of every land mass.
> 3. It searches for a configuration where the number of odd-degree vertices is **0 or 2**.
> 4. **It finds none.** The Analyzer reports: *"No counterexample found. Assertion may be valid."*
>
> This is *bounded* proof (not absolute proof), but combined with the Handshaking Lemma verification, it constitutes overwhelming evidence that the Konigsberg bridge problem has no solution.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Alloy Constructs vs. Konigsberg Concepts

| Alloy Construct | Formal Logic Meaning | Konigsberg Interpretation |
|-----------------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| `sig Land` | A unary predicate $L(x)$ | "x is a land mass" |
| `sig Bridge` | A unary predicate $B(x)$ | "x is a bridge" |
| `ends: set Land` | A binary relation $E(b, l)$ | "Bridge b ends at land l" |
| `all b: Bridge \| #(b.ends) = 2` | $\forall b \in B: \vert E(b) \vert = 2$ | Every bridge connects 2 lands |
| `degree[l]` | $\vert\{b : l \in E(b)\}\vert$ | Number of bridges touching land l |
| `(degree[l] % 2) = 1` | $d(l) \equiv 1 \pmod 2$ | Land l has odd degree |
| `#{l: Land \| (degree[l] % 2) = 1} != 2` | $\vert\{l : d(l) \text{ odd}\}\vert \neq 2$ | Not exactly 2 odd-degree vertices |
| `check ... for 4 Land, 7 Bridge` | Bounded domain $\vert L \vert \leq 4, \vert B \vert \leq 7$ | Search space bound |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*What is Alloy? Briefly explain the role of the Alloy Analyzer in formal specification.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Alloy is a **declarative formal specification language** developed at MIT by Daniel Jackson, based on **first-order relational logic** with transitive closure. It is used to model the structural constraints and behavioral properties of software systems.

The **Alloy Analyzer** is a companion tool that performs **bounded model checking**:
1. It parses the Alloy specification.
2. It translates the model and assertions into **Boolean SAT formulas** (via the Kodkod model finder).
3. It uses an off-the-shelf **SAT solver** to search for instances that satisfy or violate the assertions.
4. If a violating instance is found, it is returned as a **counterexample** and visualized.

The Analyzer thus automates the verification process, allowing designers to check whether a model satisfies critical invariants within a defined scope.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
*State Euler's theorem for the existence of an Eulerian path in an undirected graph.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Euler's Theorem (1736):** An undirected, connected graph $G = (V, E)$ contains an **Eulerian path** (a trail that traverses every edge exactly once) **if and only if**:

1. The graph is **connected** (ignoring isolated vertices), AND
2. The number of vertices with **odd degree** is either **0 or 2**.

- If **0** odd-degree vertices exist → an **Eulerian circuit** (closed path) exists.
- If exactly **2** odd-degree vertices exist → an **Eulerian path** exists, starting at one odd vertex and ending at the other.
- If **more than 2** odd-degree vertices exist → **no Eulerian path** exists.

**Application to Konigsberg:** The Konigsberg bridge graph has **4 odd-degree vertices** (3, 3, 5, 3), violating the condition, hence **no Eulerian walk is possible**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing "Alloy is a programming language" — it is a **declaration/specification** language. (−1 mark)
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **signatures** with **classes** in OOP — Alloy signatures are *sets of uninterpreted atoms*, not templates. (−1 mark)
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting that Alloy's analysis is **bounded**, not absolute. Always mention the scope. (−1 mark)
> - **Common Mistake 4:** In Konigsberg questions, students often miscount the degree of Kneiphof (forgetting the 2 bridges to each bank and the 1 to the island). Kneiphof's degree is **5**, not 3. (−1 mark)
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Failing to state that **Euler's theorem is necessary AND sufficient**, not merely necessary. (−1 mark)

---

### **Part B – Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the working of the Alloy Analyzer. Describe each stage from specification writing to result reporting. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Model the Konigsberg Bridge Problem in Alloy and demonstrate that the problem has no solution. State and apply Euler's theorem as part of your reasoning. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The Alloy Analyzer operates through the following **five stages**:

**Stage 1 — Specification Authoring:**
The user writes a `.als` file containing `sig` (signatures, declaring sets of atoms), `fact` (universally-true constraints), `pred` (named predicates), `fun` (functions), and `assert` (properties to verify). Each construct captures structural and behavioral aspects of the system under design.

**Stage 2 — Translation to First-Order Relational Logic:**
The Alloy parser converts the specification into a set of **first-order relational logic** formulas. Signatures become unary predicates; fields become binary relations; quantifiers `all`, `some`, `no`, `one` become $\forall, \exists, \neg\exists, \exists!$ respectively. Set operations like `+` (union), `&` (intersection), `-` (difference), `in` (membership) are interpreted as standard set-theoretic operations.

**Stage 3 — Skolemization and Reduction to SAT:**
The relational logic is converted to **propositional logic** by:
- **Skolemization**: existential quantifiers are eliminated using fresh Skolem constants.
- **Boolean encoding**: every atom in the bounded universe becomes a Boolean variable. A relation $R$ between two sets of size $m$ and $n$ becomes a $2^{m+n}$ bit-vector.
- **CNF conversion**: The Boolean formula is converted to **Conjunctive Normal Form** suitable for SAT solving.

**Stage 4 — SAT Solving:**
The CNF formula is fed to a **SAT solver** (typically MiniSat). The solver searches for a satisfying assignment. If found, a **counterexample** is constructed; if not, the assertion is **vacuously valid within the scope**.

**Stage 5 — Result Reporting and Visualization:**
If a counterexample is found, the Analyzer instantiates a concrete example and renders it visually — as a **graph**, a **table of relations**, or a **tree** — to help the user understand the failure. The user can iterate on the model based on this feedback.

> **The Small Scope Hypothesis** justifies this bounded approach: most specification errors manifest in small instances.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — State the Problem:**
The Konigsberg bridge graph has 4 vertices (North, South, Kneiphof, Island) and 7 edges (bridges). We must show no walk crosses each bridge exactly once.

**Step 2 — State Euler's Theorem:**
An undirected connected graph has an Eulerian path **iff** it has **exactly 0 or 2 vertices of odd degree**.

**Step 3 — Compute Degrees:**

$$
\deg(N) = 3, \quad \deg(S) = 3, \quad \deg(K) = 5, \quad \deg(I) = 3
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Theorem:**

$$
\text{Number of odd-degree vertices} = 4 \neq 0 \text{ and } 4 \neq 2
$$

Therefore, **no Eulerian path exists**.

**Step 5 — Encode in Alloy:**

```alloy
module Konigsberg
sig Land {}
sig Bridge { ends: set Land }
fact { all b: Bridge | #(b.ends) = 2 }
fun degree[l: Land]: Int { #{b: Bridge | l in b.ends} }
pred Konigsberg { #Land = 4 and #Bridge = 7 }
assert NoEulerianWalk {
  Konigsberg() implies (
    #{l: Land | (degree[l] % 2) = 1} != 0 and
    #{l: Land | (degree[l] % 2) = 1} != 2
  )
}
check NoEulerianWalk for 4 Land, 7 Bridge
```

**Step 6 — Interpret Result:**
The Analyzer searches all configurations within the scope. None satisfies the `Konigsberg()` predicate *and* has 0 or 2 odd-degree vertices. The assertion is **valid** (within the bound), proving that the Konigsberg problem has no solution.

---

### **Part B – Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of the Alloy Analyzer. How does it differ from a model checker like SPIN or NuSMV? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Verify Euler's theorem for the Konigsberg Bridge Problem by computing the degrees of all four land masses. Use the Handshaking Lemma to validate your computation. Explain why this proof is **bounded** but still compelling. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Architecture of the Alloy Analyzer:**

```
.als file → Parser → Kodkod Model Finder → SAT Solver → Result Renderer
```

1. **Parser**: Lexically and syntactically analyzes the `.als` source.
2. **Kodkod Model Finder**: Translates FOL+TC specifications into Boolean formulas. It is the *core* innovation of Alloy — it bridges high-level relational logic and low-level SAT.
3. **SAT Solver**: MiniSat/Kodkod's internal engine that finds a satisfying assignment (or proves UNSAT).
4. **Result Renderer**: Visualizes counterexamples as graphs, tables, or tree structures.

**Differences from SPIN / NuSMV:**

| Feature | Alloy | SPIN / NuSMV |
|---------|-------|--------------|
| **Primary Use** | Structural modeling, lightweight verification | Behavioral verification of concurrent systems |
| **Logic Base** | First-order relational logic + transitive closure | Linear Temporal Logic (LTL), Computation Tree Logic (CTL) |
| **Backend** | SAT solver (Kodkod/MiniSat) | Explicit state enumeration (SPIN) or BDDs/SAT (NuSMV) |
| **Input Style** | Declarative signatures and relations | Promela code (SPIN) or state machines (NuSMV) |
| **Verification Type** | Bounded model finding | Temporal property verification |
| **Best For** | Software designs, data structures | Protocols, reactive systems |

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Degree Computation (revisited with verification):**

| Vertex | Bridges Incident | Degree | Odd? |
|--------|------------------|--------|------|
| North Bank (N) | $b_1, b_2, b_3$ | $3$ | ✅ |
| South Bank (S) | $b_4, b_5, b_6$ | $3$ | ✅ |
| Kneiphof (K) | $b_1, b_2, b_4, b_5, b_7$ | $5$ | ✅ |
| Eastern Island (I) | $b_3, b_6, b_7$ | $3$ | ✅ |

**Handshaking Lemma Check:**

$$
\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 3 + 3 + 5 + 3 = 14
$$

$$
2 \vert E \vert = 2 \times 7 = 14
$$

Since $14 = 14$, the degree assignments are **internally consistent**.

**Euler's Theorem Application:**

$$
\text{Odd-degree vertex count} = 4 \not\in \{0, 2\}
$$

By Euler's theorem, the graph has **no Eulerian path**.

**Why this proof is bounded but compelling:**

The Alloy Analyzer checks the assertion within the **bounded scope** $\vert L \vert \leq 4, \vert B \vert \leq 7$. It does **not** constitute a mathematical proof for all possible universes. However, by the **Small Scope Hypothesis** and the fact that the degree counts are uniquely determined by the city layout, the bound is sufficient: no instance with $\leq 4$ lands and $\leq 7$ bridges can simultaneously satisfy the Konigsberg topology and have 0 or 2 odd-degree vertices. This is the kind of result that holds at *all* scopes once verified at the minimum.

---

### 🔑 Core Concepts

- ✅ **Alloy Analyzer** = bounded model checker (uses SAT via Kodkod + MiniSat)
- ✅ **Konigsberg problem** = 4 vertices, 7 bridges, **4 odd-degree** vertices
- ✅ **Euler's theorem** = Eulerian path exists ⟺ exactly **0 or 2** odd-degree vertices
- ✅ **Handshaking Lemma** = $\sum \deg(v) = 2 \vert E \vert$

### 🛠️ Key Alloy Syntax to Memorize
- `sig A { f: set B }` — signature with a field
- `fact { all x: A | ... }` — universally true constraint
- `pred P { ... }` — named predicate
- `fun f[x: A]: Int { ... }` — function returning an integer
- `assert A { ... }` — property to verify
- `check A for n A, m B` — run bounded verification

### 📐 Numerical Facts (Must Memorize)
- **Konigsberg degrees:** 3, 3, 5, 3 (all odd)
- **Number of bridges:** **7**
- **Number of land masses:** **4**
- **Sum of degrees:** **14** (equals $2 \times 7$)
- **Odd-degree vertex count:** **4** (so NO Eulerian path)

### ⚠️ Common Pitfalls
- ❌ Don't confuse Alloy's `sig` with OOP classes
- ❌ Don't claim Alloy provides *absolute* proof — it's **bounded**
- ❌ Don't forget to invoke the `check` command after writing `assert`
- ❌ Don't miscount Kneiphof's degree — it has **5** bridges
- ❌ Don't say "the Analyzer proves it" — say "the Analyzer **finds no counterexample within scope**"

### 🎯 KTU High-Weight Keywords (Use These in Answers)
- *First-order relational logic*
- *Bounded model checking*
- *SAT solver / Kodkod / MiniSat*
- *Small Scope Hypothesis*
- *Eulerian path / circuit*
- *Handshaking Lemma*
- *Counterexample visualization*

### 🧠 The "One-Liner" You Can Quote in Exams
> *"Alloy does not prove theorems — it **finds counterexamples** within a bounded scope. The absence of a counterexample, combined with theoretical analysis, constitutes a compelling bounded proof."*

### 📚 Historical Trivia (Bonus Marks!)
- The Konigsberg problem was solved by **Leonhard Euler** in **1736**
- The paper was titled *"Solutio problematis ad geometriam situs pertinentis"* (The solution of a problem relating to the geometry of position)
- **Daniel Jackson** created Alloy at **MIT** in the late 1990s
- Alloy is named as a play on "alloy of ideas" from formal methods
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Verifier for Concurrent C (VCC): A Hoare-Triple Based Tool for Verifying Concurrent C, Intraprocedure Verification of Programs, Ghost Statements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST741 - FORMAL METHODS IN SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Verifier for Concurrent C (VCC): A Hoare-Triple Based Tool for Verifying Concurrent C, Intraprocedure Verification of Programs, Ghost Statements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of VCC (Verifier for Concurrent C)

**VCC (Verifier for Concurrent C)** is a *sound*, *modular*, *permission-based* deductive verification tool for *low-level concurrent C programs*, developed at **Microsoft Research**. It is a *Hoare-style logical inference system* extended with **two-level ghost-state / ownership reasoning** to prove correctness of multithreaded C code annotated with contracts.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framing, VCC is studied as a **practical industrial realization of Hoare logic** applied to a mainstream imperative language (C), extended to handle:

1. **Hoare-triple based contract reasoning** (precondition / postcondition / invariants),
2. **Intraprocedural verification** — proving correctness of the body of a single procedure by generating Verification Conditions (VCs) over the control-flow graph,
3. **Ghost statements** — auxiliary, executable-in-logic-only statements that assist the prover but vanish at compile time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST741 / Module 3):**
> VCC is *the* canonical example of "applying Hoare logic to an industrial-strength language". For exam purposes, you must connect every VCC feature to a specific Hoare-logic construct (precondition, postcondition, invariant, weakest precondition, verification condition).

The mathematical backbone is the **Hoare triple**:

$$\{\,P\,\}\ \ C\ \ \{\,Q\,\}$$

where $P$ is the *precondition* (a first-order predicate over program state), $C$ is a C statement, and $Q$ is the *postcondition*. The triple is **valid** iff every terminating execution of $C$ starting in a state satisfying $P$ ends in a state satisfying $Q$ (partial correctness) — and does not abort (total correctness in VCC).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Building Inspector with a Blueprint"

Imagine a **building inspector** who must approve a multi-floor construction blueprint (your C program) before workers (the compiler / CPU) actually build it:

- The **blueprint annotations** written in red ink (your contracts, invariants, requires/ensures) are the *pre/postconditions*.
- The **inspector** is VCC. He doesn't lay a single brick; he reads the blueprint and asks: *"If the foundation (precondition) holds, will the roof (postcondition) hold after this sequence of steps?"*
- For each floor (procedure), the inspector walks room by room (**intraprocedural verification**), checking every wall (statement) and the load-bearing columns (loop invariants).
- The inspector is allowed to keep a *private notebook* (the **ghost state**) where he scribbles temporary measurements and intermediate calculations that are useful to him but never become part of the actual building. The construction crew never sees them, and they don't appear in the final structure.

This perfectly mirrors VCC: contracts annotate, the tool reasons, ghost code is verification-only.

## 1.3 Why VCC Matters in KTU Formal Methods

| Property | Classical Hoare Logic | VCC's Extension |
|----------|----------------------|-----------------|
| Target language | Tiny WHILE / toy languages | Full ISO C (with concurrency) |
| Concurrency | Not handled | **Two-state invariant / ownership model** |
| Memory model | Implicit | Explicit **typed, fractional ownership** |
| Ghost code | Absent | First-class via `/*@ ghost @*/` |
| Automation | Mostly manual | SMT-based (Z3 backend) |

> [!NOTE]
> VCC is often paired with the **Boogie intermediate verification language** and the **Z3 SMT solver**. The pipeline is: *Annotated C → VCC → Boogie → Z3 → SAT/UNSAT result*.

## 1.4 Three Pillars of the Module (as the KTU 2024 scheme expects)

1. **Hoare-Triple Foundation** — the logical inference rules that VCC encodes.
2. **Intraprocedural Verification** — the algorithmic generation of Verification Conditions (VCs) over a single procedure's control-flow graph using the **weakest precondition transformer** $wp(\,C,\,Q\,)$.
3. **Ghost Statements** — auxiliary constructs (`ghost variable`, `ghost statement`, `ghost call`) that carry purely logical information.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU answers, always begin a VCC question by reminding the examiner of the underlying Hoare triple. It is the *spine* of the whole module.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hoare-Logic Inference System Used by VCC

VCC's core reasoning engine encodes the *standard Hoare logic* (originally due to Floyd–Hoare, 1969) plus extensions for structured C. The relevant inference rules — every one of which is **directly testable** in the KTU 2024 scheme — are:

### 2.1.1 The Assignment Axiom
For an assignment statement $x := E$, with $E$ free of side effects in the postcondition $Q$:

$$\{\,Q[E/x]\,\}\ \ x := E\ \ \{\,Q\,\}$$

where $Q[E/x]$ means "$Q$ with every free occurrence of $x$ replaced by $E$".

**Why this is the 'right' backward rule:** It is the simplest way to *propagate* the postcondition backwards across an assignment, which is exactly what the *weakest precondition* transformer does.

### 2.1.2 The Precondition-Strengthening Rule
If $P \Rightarrow P'$ is provable, and $\{\,P'\,\} \ C\ \{\,Q\,\}$ is valid, then $\{\,P\,\} \ C\ \{\,Q\,\}$ is valid.

### 2.1.3 The Postcondition-Weakening Rule
If $\{\,P\,\} \ C\ \{\,Q'\,\}$ is valid and $Q' \Rightarrow Q$ is provable, then $\{\,P\,\} \ C\ \{\,Q\,\}$ is valid.

### 2.1.4 The Composition Rule
If $\{\,P\,\} \ C_1\ \{\,R\,\}$ and $\{\,R\,\} \ C_2\ \{\,Q\,\}$, then $\{\,P\,\} \ C_1;\ C_2\ \{\,Q\,\}$.

### 2.1.5 The Conditional Rule
If $\{\,P \wedge B\,\} \ C_1\ \{\,Q\,\}$ and $\{\,P \wedge \neg B\,\} \ C_2\ \{\,Q\,\}$, then $\{\,P\,\} \ \text{if}\ B\ \text{then}\ C_1\ \text{else}\ C_2\ \ \{\,Q\,\}$.

### 2.1.6 The While Rule (with invariant $I$)
If $\{\,I \wedge B\,\} \ C_{\text{body}}\ \{\,I\,\}$ holds, then $\{\,I\,\} \ \text{while}\ B\ \text{do}\ C_{\text{body}}\ \ \{\,I \wedge \neg B\,\}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The while-rule is the *only* rule in Hoare logic that requires **human-supplied loop invariants** $I$. This is precisely why VCC demands the user write `invariant(...)` annotations — it cannot invent invariants automatically in the general case.

### 2.1.7 The Consequence Rule
If $P \Rightarrow P'$ and $P' \Rightarrow P$ (i.e., $P \equiv P'$) — actually the *full* consequence rule combines strengthening and weakening:

$$\frac{P \Rightarrow P' \quad \{\,P'\,\}\ C\ \{\,Q'\,\} \quad Q' \Rightarrow Q}{\{\,P\,\}\ C\ \{\,Q\,\}}$$

## 2.2 Weakest Precondition (WP) Transformer

The **weakest precondition** $wp(\,C,\,Q\,)$ is the *loosest* (logically weakest) predicate such that $\{\,wp(\,C,\,Q\,)\} \ C\ \{Q\}$ is valid. It is the engine VCC uses to walk the control-flow graph backwards.

**Definition (Dijkstra, 1976).** $wp(\,C,\,Q\,)$ is the *weakest* predicate $P$ such that every execution of $C$ terminating in a state satisfying $Q$ begins in a state satisfying $P$.

| Statement $C$ | $wp(\,C,\,Q\,)$ |
|----------------|------------------|
| `x := E` (atomic) | $Q[E/x]$ |
| `skip` | $Q$ |
| `abort` | `false` |
| `assert(R)` | $R \wedge Q$ |
| `assume(R)` | $R \Rightarrow Q$ |
| `C1; C2` | $wp(\,C_1,\ wp(\,C_2,\,Q\,)\,)$ |
| `if (B) C1 else C2` | $(B \wedge wp(\,C_1,\,Q\,)) \ \vee\ (\neg B \wedge wp(\,C_2,\,Q\,))$ |
| `while (B) { I; C }` | $I$ (provided $\{I \wedge B\}\ C\ \{I\}$ and $I \wedge \neg B \Rightarrow Q$) |

> [!NOTE]
> For an `assert(R)`, the *weakest* precondition that guarantees both $R$ holds at runtime and $Q$ holds afterwards is $R \wedge Q$. VCC emits a *proof obligation* that the caller's state implies $R \wedge Q$ before the assert executes.

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Construct | Hoare Rule / WP | VCC Keyword |
|---|-----------|-----------------|-------------|
| 1 | Assignment | $wp(\,x{:=}E,\,Q\,) = Q[E/x]$ | (built-in) |
| 2 | Skip | $wp(\,\text{skip},\,Q\,) = Q$ | `;` |
| 3 | Assert | $wp(\,\text{assert}(R),\,Q\,) = R \wedge Q$ | `assert(R)` |
| 4 | Assume | $wp(\,\text{assume}(R),\,Q\,) = (R \Rightarrow Q)$ | `assume(R)` |
| 5 | Sequence | $wp(\,C_1;C_2,\,Q\,) = wp(\,C_1,\,wp(\,C_2,\,Q\,)\,)$ | (sequencing) |
| 6 | Conditional | $wp(\,\text{if }B\text{ then }C_1\text{ else }C_2,\,Q\,) = (B \wedge wp(\,C_1,Q\,)) \vee (\neg B \wedge wp(\,C_2,Q\,))$ | `if / else` |
| 7 | While (inv $I$) | require $\{I \wedge B\}\ C_{\text{body}}\ \{I\}$; conclude $I$ | `while(...) invariant(I)` |
| 8 | Function call | $\text{Requires}(P) \Rightarrow wp(\,\text{body},\,Q\,)$ | `requires(P) ensures(Q)` |
| 9 | Consequence | $\frac{P \Rightarrow P', \ Q' \Rightarrow Q}{\{P\}\ C\ \{Q\}}$ | automatic via SMT |
| 10 | Ghost update | $wp(\,\text{ghost }x{:=}E,\,Q\,) = Q[E/x]$ (verified, then erased) | `ghost(...)` |

> [!WARNING]
> In markdown tables, the disjunction symbol is rendered using $\vee$ so that no raw `|` appears inside the table. This avoids breaking the table column separator.

## 2.4 Intraprocedural Verification — Algorithmic Pipeline

The *intraprocedural* verifier in VCC performs the following six steps:

1. **Parse** the annotated C body of a single function.
2. **Build the Control-Flow Graph (CFG)** — nodes are program points; edges are statements.
3. **Cut the loops** by introducing a fresh predicate variable for each loop head — this is the loop invariant placeholder $I$.
4. **Apply the WP transformer** along every edge, propagating the user's `ensures(Q)` backwards to the function entry.
5. **Emit VCs** — first-order proof obligations of the form $P \Rightarrow wp(\,C,\,Q\,)$ at every cut.
6. **Dispatch** each VC to the **Z3 SMT solver** via the Boogie IVL; success = verified, counter-example = VCC prints a failed obligation.

> [!TIP]
> In a KTU 14-mark answer, the six steps above are worth **3 marks** by themselves. Always enumerate them — examiners reward structure.

## 2.5 Ghost Statements — What They Are and Why They Are Needed

A **ghost statement** is *C code* that is **type-checked and verified by VCC** but **erased before machine-code generation**. VCC recognises ghost contexts via the `ghost` keyword (and by typing — ghost data lives in a separate heap universe).

### 2.5.1 Three Primary Uses

| Use Case | KTU-Relevant Insight |
|----------|----------------------|
| **Loop-variant / measure** | Prove termination with `decreases(...)`. |
| **Inductive invariants** | A ghost variable that records a *history* of values (e.g., a counter of writes). |
| **Auxiliary lemma calls** | A `ghost_call` to a function whose only purpose is to discharge a proof obligation. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A ghost statement must be **erasable**: removing it must not change the semantics of any concrete (non-ghost) variable. VCC enforces this by a *non-interference* check.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of VCC in Engineering

- **Hyper-V / Windows kernel drivers**: Microsoft used VCC to verify the core of the Hyper-V hypervisor's device virtualization, including concurrent ring-buffer code. This is a flagship industrial deployment.
- **Avionics and railway signaling**: The pattern of "annotate C, prove it with VCC/Boogie" has influenced DO-178C supplement DO-333 (formal methods) workflows.
- **Cryptographic libraries**: Used to verify constant-time, side-channel-resistant implementations.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility of the Underlying Equations

The *weakest precondition calculus* underpins not just VCC but **AutoProof (Eiffel)**, **Frama-C / WP plugin**, **SPARK Ada**, **Dafny**, and **Spec#** (VCC's own close cousin). Mastering $wp(\,C,\,Q\,)$ for the 7 constructs above is therefore a **transferable skill** across the formal-methods industry.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The WP of an Assignment from the Hoare Assignment Axiom

We want to derive $wp(\,x := E,\,Q\,) = Q[E/x]$ formally.

**Step 1.** Recall the Hoare assignment axiom (Floyd–Hoare, 1969):

$$\{\,Q[E/x]\,\}\ \ x := E\ \ \{\,Q\,\}$$

**Step 2.** The axiom guarantees that *if* we start in a state satisfying $Q[E/x]$, *then* after the assignment $x := E$ we end in a state satisfying $Q$. This means $Q[E/x]$ is **a sufficient precondition**.

**Step 3.** We must show it is also the **weakest** sufficient precondition. Suppose some other predicate $P$ satisfies $\{P\}\ x := E\ \{Q\}$. Pick any state $\sigma$ satisfying $P$. After the assignment, the new state is $\sigma'$ with $\sigma'(x) = \llbracket E \rrbracket_\sigma$ and otherwise identical. We need $\sigma' \models Q$. The only way this can hold for *every* $\sigma$ satisfying $P$ is that the *initial* value of $x$ (call it $v$) satisfies $Q$ when $x$ is replaced by $\llbracket E \rrbracket_\sigma$. Since $E$ evaluates to $v' = \llbracket E \rrbracket_\sigma$, we need $P$ to imply that the value currently bound to $x$ (i.e., $v$) is such that $Q[v'/x]$ — which is exactly $Q[E/x]$.

**Step 4.** Therefore $P \Rightarrow Q[E/x]$, which is the definition of $P$ being *stronger* than $Q[E/x]$. Hence $Q[E/x]$ is the weakest such $P$, and:

$$wp(\,x := E,\,Q\,) = Q[E/x]$$

> [!NOTE]
> The notation $Q[E/x]$ is read "Q with E substituted for x" (also called *capture-avoiding substitution*). For composite $E$ it is defined recursively on the structure of $Q$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — WP of an Assert Statement

**Step 1.** An `assert(R)` is a statement that, at runtime, checks that the current state satisfies $R$. If the check fails the program aborts. Therefore, for any postcondition $Q$ to hold *after* the assert, the assert must not abort — i.e., $R$ must hold — *and* $Q$ must hold.

**Step 2.** Combining these two requirements conjunctively gives $R \wedge Q$.

**Step 3.** Formally, the Hoare rule is:

$$\frac{}{\{R \wedge Q\}\ \text{assert}(R)\ \{Q\}}$$

**Step 4.** By definition of *weakest* precondition, we cannot drop $R$ (the program would be allowed to abort) and we cannot drop $Q$ (the postcondition must follow). Hence $R \wedge Q$ is indeed the weakest.

$$wp(\,\text{assert}(R),\,Q\,) = R \wedge Q$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — WP of an Assume Statement

**Step 1.** An `assume(R)` does not check $R$. Instead, it *prunes* executions: any execution reaching the assume in a state where $R$ is false is *removed* from consideration. So the weakest precondition must make those pruned paths *impossible* to begin with, which is achieved by the implication $R \Rightarrow Q$ on the pre-state.

**Step 2.** Equivalently, after an assume, the only states that can continue are those where $R$ holds. If we want the post-state to satisfy $Q$ in every surviving execution, the pre-state must satisfy $R \Rightarrow Q$ — because if $R$ holds, $Q$ must hold, and if $R$ does not hold, the execution is gone, so the implication is trivially satisfied.

$$wp(\,\text{assume}(R),\,Q\,) = (R \Rightarrow Q)$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — WP of a Sequence

Let $C_1$ and $C_2$ be statements. We compute $wp(\,C_1; C_2,\,Q\,)$ by *composing* the two WPs.

**Step 1.** Let $R = wp(\,C_2,\,Q\,)$. Then by definition, $\{R\}\ C_2\ \{Q\}$ is valid with $R$ weakest.

**Step 2.** To find $wp(\,C_1; C_2,\,Q\,)$, we must find the weakest $P$ such that $\{P\}\ C_1\ \{R\}$ (because then after $C_1$ we are in an $R$-state, and from there $C_2$ gives $Q$).

**Step 3.** The weakest such $P$ is exactly $wp(\,C_1,\,R\,) = wp(\,C_1,\,wp(\,C_2,\,Q\,)\,)$.

$$wp(\,C_1; C_2,\,Q\,) = wp(\,\ C_1,\ \ wp(\,C_2,\,Q\,)\ \,)$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — WP of an If-Statement

**Step 1.** For `if (B) C1 else C2`, the runtime branch is *non-deterministic* with respect to the verifier unless $B$'s truth is known. To prove $\{P\}\ \text{if}\,(B)\,C_1\,\text{else}\,C_2\ \{Q\}$ for the weakest $P$, we need:

- $P$ must guarantee that *if* $B$ holds then $C_1$ leads to $Q$, *and*
- $P$ must guarantee that *if* $B$ does not hold then $C_2$ leads to $Q$.

**Step 2.** Therefore the weakest $P$ is the disjunction of the two cases:

$$wp(\,\text{if }B\text{ then }C_1\text{ else }C_2,\,Q\,) = (B \wedge wp(\,C_1,\,Q\,)) \ \vee\ (\neg B \wedge wp(\,C_2,\,Q\,))$$

## 3.6 Worked Example — WP Computation on a Tiny Snippet

Consider the following annotated C procedure (intraprocedural scope):

```c
int square(int n)
    requires(n >= 0)
    ensures(\result == n * n);
{
    int r = 0;
    int i = 0;
    while (i < n)
        invariant(0 <= i && i <= n && r == i * i)
        decreases(n - i)
    {
        r = r + 2 * i + 1;
        i = i + 1;
    }
    return r;
}
```

We compute the WP starting from the postcondition $\text{ensures}(\result == n \cdot n)$:

**Step 1.** `return r;` is, in Hoare terms, equivalent to `x := r` followed by treating the postcondition as $Q[\result / x]$. But for simplicity we work backwards through the loop invariant $I \equiv (0 \le i \le n) \wedge (r = i \cdot i)$.

**Step 2.** At loop exit, the predicate $I \wedge \neg B$ must imply $\result = n \cdot n$:

$$(0 \le i \le n) \wedge (r = i \cdot i) \wedge (i \ge n) \ \Rightarrow\ (r = n \cdot n)$$

**Step 3.** The body of the loop has $I \wedge B$ as its precondition (where $B \equiv i < n$). We compute the WP of the body, taken under the invariant $I$:

$$wp(\,r := r + 2i + 1,\ wp(\,i := i + 1,\,I\,)\,)$$

**Step 4.** Inner WP: $wp(\,i := i+1,\,I\,) = I[i+1/i]$. So:

$$I[i+1/i] \equiv (0 \le i+1 \le n) \wedge (r = (i+1) \cdot (i+1))$$

**Step 5.** Outer WP: substitute $r + 2i + 1$ for $r$:

$$(0 \le i+1 \le n) \wedge (r + 2i + 1 = (i+1) \cdot (i+1))$$

**Step 6.** Algebraic simplification of the right side:

$$(i+1) \cdot (i+1) = i \cdot i + 2i + 1$$

**Step 7.** Therefore the WP becomes:

$$(0 \le i+1 \le n) \wedge (r = i \cdot i)$$

**Step 8.** We must prove that $I \wedge B$ implies this WP. That is:

$$(0 \le i \le n) \wedge (r = i \cdot i) \wedge (i < n) \ \Rightarrow\ (0 \le i+1 \le n) \wedge (r = i \cdot i)$$

**Step 9.** The conjunct $r = i \cdot i$ matches directly. The conjunct $0 \le i+1 \le n$ follows because $i \ge 0$ and $i < n$ together give $i+1 \le n$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The above nine steps constitute a **full intraprocedural proof** that VCC would attempt. The proof obligations at steps 8 and 2 are precisely the VCs that VCC emits to Boogie/Z3. In the KTU exam, **write out the WP algebra explicitly** — the marks are for the *transformation*, not the answer.

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Verification Obligations Generated by VCC

| # | Statement Type | VC Shape | Notes |
|---|----------------|----------|-------|
| 1 | `requires(P)` at function entry | $P_{\text{caller}} \Rightarrow P_{\text{callee}}$ | Caller must establish callee's precondition. |
| 2 | `ensures(Q)` at function exit | $wp(\,\text{body},\,Q\,) \Rightarrow Q_{\text{exit}}$ | Postcondition must hold after body. |
| 3 | `assert(R)` | $P_{\text{pre-assert}} \Rightarrow (R \wedge Q_{\text{after}})$ | Pre-state must imply $R$. |
| 4 | `assume(R)` | $P_{\text{pre-assume}} \Rightarrow (R \Rightarrow Q_{\text{after}})$ | Implication form. |
| 5 | Loop invariant preservation | $I \wedge B \Rightarrow wp(\,C_{\text{body}},\,I\,)$ | Loop body must re-establish $I$. |
| 6 | Loop invariant establishment | $P_{\text{pre-loop}} \Rightarrow I$ | Invariant must hold at entry. |
| 7 | Use before definition | $P_{\text{use}} \Rightarrow \text{defined}(x)$ | No reading of uninitialised data. |
| 8 | Ghost purity | No write to a concrete variable in ghost code | VCC type system enforces. |
| 9 | Concurrency ownership | $O_1 \cap O_2 = \emptyset$ for two threads | Disjoint ownership of the shared region. |
| 10 | Decreases measure | $P \wedge B \Rightarrow (E > 0 \wedge E < E_0)$ | Termination via well-founded order. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 VCC Tool Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The following diagram shows the end-to-end VCC compilation-verification pipeline, from annotated C source down to either verified Boogie output or a counter-example trace.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SRC_C: Annotated C Source]:::in --> B[VCC Front End Parser]:::in
    B --> C[Abstract Syntax Tree]:::in
    C --> D[Contract Extractor]:::mid
    D --> E[CFG Builder per Procedure]:::mid
    E --> F[WP Transformer]:::core
    F --> G[VC Emitter]:::core
    G --> H[Boogie IVL Output]:::mid
    H --> I[Boogie Verifier]:::core
    I --> J{SMT Solver Z3}:::core
    J -->|UNSAT all VCs| K[VERIFIED]:::ok
    J -->|SAT some VC| L[FAILED with Counter Example]:::err
    L --> M[Source Mapping]:::err
    M --> N[Diagnostic to User]:::err
    K --> O[Compilable C Output Ghost Erased]:::out
    A -.-> O

    classDef in fill:#E8F1FB,stroke:#2A5DB0,color:#0B2545
    classDef mid fill:#FFF4D6,stroke:#B07A00,color:#3D2A00
    classDef core fill:#DDF2D8,stroke:#3A7A2A,color:#1B3D0B
    classDef out fill:#E0E5F5,stroke:#4B4F8B,color:#101246
    classDef ok fill:#C7F0C0,stroke:#0F6B0F,color:#0A2E0A
    classDef err fill:#F8D0CE,stroke:#8B1A14,color:#400B07
```

**Reading guide:** Front-end nodes (`in`) are *blue*, Boogie-side middle nodes (`mid`) are *amber*, the core deductive engine (`core`) is *green*. Success path ends in `K` (verified), failure in `L`/`N` (diagnostic). Note that the same annotated C source `A` feeds *both* the verification pipeline and the *separate* MSVC compilation path `O` (with ghost code stripped).

## 4.2 Intraprocedural Verification Flow Within One Function

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Entry Contract: requires P]:::in --> P2[Initialize Ghost State]:::mid
    P2 --> P3[Apply WP Backward through CFG]:::core
    P3 --> P4{At Loop Head?}:::core
    P4 -->|Yes| P5[Insert Invariant Predicate I]:::core
    P4 -->|No| P6[Continue WP Propagation]:::core
    P5 --> P7[Check Invariant Establishment VC]:::core
    P7 --> P8[Check Invariant Preservation VC]:::core
    P8 --> P9[Check Decreases Measure VC]:::core
    P9 --> P10[Check Loop Exit Implies Postcondition]:::core
    P6 --> P11[Check Branch VCs for If Else]:::core
    P11 --> P12[Check Assert Assume VCs]:::core
    P10 --> P13[All VCs Discharged by Z3]:::core
    P12 --> P13
    P13 --> P14[Function Body VERIFIED]:::ok

    classDef in fill:#E8F1FB,stroke:#2A5DB0,color:#0B2545
    classDef mid fill:#FFF4D6,stroke:#B07A00,color:#3D2A00
    classDef core fill:#DDF2D8,stroke:#3A7A2A,color:#1B3D0B
    classDef ok fill:#C7F0C0,stroke:#0F6B0F,color:#0A2E0A
```

## 4.3 Ghost Statement Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Programmer Writes Ghost Annotation]:::in --> Q2[VCC Type Checks Ghost]:::mid
    Q2 --> Q3[Ghost is Verified: Proof Obligations]:::core
    Q3 --> Q4{All Ghost Proof Obligations Pass?}:::core
    Q4 -->|Yes| Q5[Ghost Erased from C Source]:::mid
    Q4 -->|No| Q6[Verification Error Report]:::err
    Q5 --> Q7[Pure C Emitted to Compiler]:::out
    Q6 --> Q8[Source Locator and Hint]:::err

    classDef in fill:#E8F1FB,stroke:#2A5DB0,color:#0B2545
    classDef mid fill:#FFF4D6,stroke:#B07A00,color:#3D2A00
    classDef core fill:#DDF2D8,stroke:#3A7A2A,color:#1B3D0B
    classDef out fill:#E0E5F5,stroke:#4B4F8B,color:#101246
    classDef err fill:#F8D0CE,stroke:#8B1A14,color:#400B07
```

## 4.4 Relationship Between Hoare Logic, WP, and VCC's Three Pillars

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Hoare Logic Foundation 1969]:::root --> B1[Hoare Triple P C Q]:::mid
    A1 --> B2[Assignment Axiom Q E over x]:::mid
    A1 --> B3[While Rule with Invariant I]:::mid
    B1 --> C1[Dijkstra WP Transformer 1976]:::mid
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[VCC Intraprocedural VC Generation]:::core
    C1 --> D2[VCC Ghost Statement Reasoning]:::core
    C1 --> D3[VCC Concurrent Ownership via Boogie]:::core
    D1 --> E1[Microsoft Hyper V Kernel Verified]:::ok
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1

    classDef root fill:#F0E5FF,stroke:#5A2A8B,color:#1A0633
    classDef mid fill:#FFF4D6,stroke:#B07A00,color:#3D2A00
    classDef core fill:#DDF2D8,stroke:#3A7A2A,color:#1B3D0B
    classDef ok fill:#C7F0C0,stroke:#0F6B0F,color:#0A2E0A
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. Define the Hoare triple and explain its role in VCC. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECST741]`

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**
A Hoare triple is a logical statement of the form $\{P\}\ C\ \{Q\}$, where $P$ is a *precondition* (predicate on the state before execution), $C$ is a *command* (program statement), and $Q$ is a *postcondition* (predicate on the state after execution). The triple is **valid** iff every execution of $C$ starting in a state satisfying $P$ terminates in a state satisfying $Q$. In VCC, every annotated function carries a Hoare triple — `requires(P)` plays the role of $P$, and `ensures(Q)` plays the role of $Q$. The body of the function must then be proven correct w.r.t. this triple using the inference rules of Hoare logic. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. What is a "ghost statement" in VCC? Give one example. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PECST741]`

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**
A **ghost statement** is a piece of C code (declared with the `ghost` keyword) that is **type-checked and verified** by VCC but is **completely erased** before the program is handed to the C compiler. It is used purely as a *proof aid* — for example, to maintain an auxiliary counter, a decreasing measure for termination, or an inductive invariant. **Example:**

```c
/*@ ghost int write_count = 0; @*/

void increment()
    requires(write_count < 1000)
    ensures(write_count == \old(write_count) + 1);
{
    /*@ ghost write_count = write_count + 1; @*/
    /* concrete code */
}
```
Ghost code is non-interfering: it cannot influence concrete state. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split typically as (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. Internal choice is *between* Question A and Question B (not between sub-parts).

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### Q.A(a) `[7 marks]` — WP Computation on a Program Fragment

> *Consider the following C-like annotated code:*
>
> ```c
> { P }
> if (x > 0)
>     y = x + 1;
> else
>     y = -x;
> { Q : y = abs(x) }
> ```
>
> *(a) Compute the weakest precondition $wp(\,\text{if-then-else},\,Q\,)$ symbolically and hence derive a sufficient precondition $P$ that guarantees $Q$. (b) Explain how VCC would encode this verification obligation for the Z3 SMT solver.*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Symbolic computation of WP.** From the table in Section 2.2, for an if-statement:

$$wp(\,\text{if }B\text{ then }C_1\text{ else }C_2,\,Q\,) = (B \wedge wp(\,C_1,\,Q\,)) \ \vee\ (\neg B \wedge wp(\,C_2,\,Q\,))$$

Let $B \equiv (x > 0)$, $C_1 \equiv y := x + 1$, $C_2 \equiv y := -x$, and $Q \equiv (y = \text{abs}(x))$.

- $wp(\,y := x+1,\,y = \text{abs}(x)\,) = (x + 1 = \text{abs}(x))$
- $wp(\,y := -x,\,y = \text{abs}(x)\,) = (-x = \text{abs}(x))$

Hence:

$$wp(\,C,\,Q\,) = (x > 0 \wedge x + 1 = \text{abs}(x)) \ \vee\ (x \le 0 \wedge -x = \text{abs}(x))$$

A simple sufficient precondition is $P \equiv (x > 0 \Rightarrow x + 1 = x + 1) \wedge (x \le 0 \Rightarrow -x = -x)$, which is simply `true` — i.e., the program is correct for *every* integer $x$. Alternatively, take $P \equiv \text{true}$. **(7 marks — full WP derivation: 4, simplification: 2, final P: 1)**

**(b) VCC encoding for Z3.** VCC would:

1. Translate the predicate into a Boogie `assert` statement placed at the function entry.
2. Z3 then tries to find an integer assignment to $x$ that *violates* the predicate. Since the disjunction is logically valid, the SMT query returns **UNSAT** and the obligation is discharged.
3. The encoding uses uninterpreted functions for `abs` if not modelled, or an explicit axiomatisation $\forall x.\ (x \ge 0 \Rightarrow \text{abs}(x) = x) \wedge (x < 0 \Rightarrow \text{abs}(x) = -x)$.

---

#### Q.A(b) `[7 marks]` — Intraprocedural Verification Algorithm

> *Explain, with a labelled diagram, the six steps VCC performs for the intraprocedural verification of a single procedure's body. Highlight the role of the loop invariant and the weakest precondition transformer.*

**Model Solution:**

VCC performs the following six steps within a *single* procedure (no calls considered except atomically):

1. **Parse** the annotated C body.
2. **Build the Control-Flow Graph (CFG)** where nodes are program points and edges are statements.
3. **Cut loops** by introducing a fresh predicate variable $I$ at each loop head — this is the loop invariant.
4. **Apply the WP transformer** along every edge, propagating the `ensures(Q)` *backwards* to the function entry.
5. **Emit Verification Conditions (VCs)** of the form $P_{\text{pre}} \Rightarrow wp(\,C,\,P_{\text{post}}\,)$ at every cut.
6. **Dispatch** each VC to the **Z3 SMT solver** through the **Boogie IVL**; success = verified.

The **loop invariant** is the human-supplied predicate that must hold at the head of the loop, must be re-established by the loop body, and must imply the postcondition at loop exit (when $B$ is false). The **WP transformer** automates the *backward* propagation; the *invariant* is the single point where the verifier must ask the user for help, because in general the invariant is not computable. **(7 marks — 6 steps: 3, role of invariant: 2, role of WP: 2)**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Internal Alternative to Question A*

#### Q.B(a) `[7 marks]` — Ghost Statements: Purpose, Rules, and a Worked Example

> *(a) What problem do ghost statements solve in VCC? (b) State two syntactic forms of ghost statements supported by VCC. (c) Construct a small annotated C function that uses a ghost variable to prove that a loop executes exactly $n$ times.*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Problem solved (≈ 2 marks).** Some correctness arguments (induction, decreasing measures, complex relational invariants) cannot be stated in plain Hoare triples. Ghost statements allow the programmer to *introduce auxiliary state* that records intermediate proof information, without altering the *concrete* behaviour of the program.

**(b) Two syntactic forms (≈ 2 marks):**

- `/*@ ghost T x = init; @*/` — a ghost variable declaration.
- `/*@ ghost (T) = fn_call(args); @*/` — a ghost function call.
- `/*@ ghost x = E; @*/` — a ghost assignment.

**(c) Worked example (≈ 3 marks):**

```c
void run_n_times(int n)
    requires(n >= 0)
    ensures(true);
{
    int i = 0;
    /*@ ghost int g = 0; @*/
    while (i < n)
        invariant(0 <= i && i <= n && g == i)
        decreases(n - i)
    {
        i = i + 1;
        /*@ ghost g = g + 1; @*/
    }
}
```

The ghost variable `g` records how many iterations have been performed. The invariant $g = i$ is preserved by every iteration (the concrete update `i = i + 1` is mirrored by the ghost update `g = g + 1`). At loop exit, $g = n$, but this fact exists only in the verification world; it does not appear in the compiled binary. **(7 marks total)**

---

#### Q.B(b) `[7 marks]` — Soundness, Completeness, and Limitations of VCC

> *Discuss the **soundness** of VCC's Hoare-style verification, its **relative completeness** with respect to Hoare logic, and two practical limitations that an engineer must keep in mind.*

**Model Solution:**

**Soundness (≈ 2 marks).** VCC is *sound*: if it reports "verified", then for every execution of the program (concurrent or sequential), the contracts hold. This is guaranteed by construction because VCs are derived using only the *sound* Hoare inference rules and discharged by a sound SMT solver (Z3).

**Relative completeness (≈ 2 marks).** VCC is *relatively complete* in the sense of Cook: for any valid triple $\{P\}\ C\ \{Q\}$, there *exists* an annotation (loop invariant, intermediate assertion) that allows VCC to prove it. However, the annotation must be *supplied by the user* — VCC cannot invent invariants in the general case.

**Two practical limitations (≈ 3 marks):**

1. **Annotation burden.** Writing contracts and invariants is a non-trivial engineering effort, often comparable in size to the code itself. For large code bases this becomes the dominant cost.
2. **SMT non-termination.** Z3 may not terminate on complex VCs (e.g., nonlinear integer arithmetic, deep quantifier alternations). VCC may time out rather than return a definitive answer.

A third, often-mentioned limitation: VCC targets *C* (not C++ in full), and assumes a well-defined C semantics that some embedded / GNU-extension code violates. **(7 marks total)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — How Students Lose Marks on This Topic**
>
> 1. **Writing the Hoare triple without naming the inference rule** used — always say "by the assignment axiom" or "by the consequence rule". **(–2 marks)**
> 2. **Confusing weakest precondition (WP) with strongest postcondition (SP)** — VCC uses *WP* (backward), not *SP* (forward). Examiners will deduct for a forward-only derivation. **(–2 marks)**
> 3. **Forgetting that ghost code is type-checked and verified** — students often wrongly claim ghost code is "ignored" by VCC; in fact VCC *proves* ghost code correct and only then erases it. **(–1 mark)**
> 4. **Drawing the if-rule as a *conjunction* rather than a *disjunction*** of the two branch WPs. **(–1 mark)**
> 5. **Omitting the loop invariant entirely** when describing intraprocedural verification. Loop invariants are *non-negotiable*. **(–2 marks)**
> 6. **Citing "VCC" without a single Hoare-rule reference** — the question is from a *Formal Methods* paper, so examiners expect the *logic*, not just tool syntax. **(–2 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (last 5 minutes before the exam):**

- [ ] **Hoare triple** $\{P\}\ C\ \{Q\}$ — partial correctness semantics; $P$ precondition, $Q$ postcondition.
- [ ] **Assignment axiom** — $wp(\,x := E,\,Q\,) = Q[E/x]$; substitute *expression for variable* in postcondition.
- [ ] **Sequence** — $wp(\,C_1;C_2,\,Q\,) = wp(\,C_1,\,wp(\,C_2,\,Q\,)\,)$; right-to-left WP composition.
- [ ] **Conditional** — $wp(\,\text{if }B\text{ then }C_1\text{ else }C_2,\,Q\,) = (B \wedge wp(\,C_1,Q\,)) \vee (\neg B \wedge wp(\,C_2,Q\,))$ — *disjunction* of the two branches.
- [ ] **While (loop)** — requires loop invariant $I$, with $I \wedge B \Rightarrow wp(\,C_{\text{body}},\,I\,)$ as the preservation VC.
- [ ] **Assert** — $wp(\,\text{assert}(R),\,Q\,) = R \wedge Q$.
- [ ] **Assume** — $wp(\,\text{assume}(R),\,Q\,) = (R \Rightarrow Q)$.
- [ ] **VCC's 6 intraprocedural steps** — Parse → CFG → Cut loops with $I$ → WP propagate → Emit VCs → SMT via Boogie/Z3.
- [ ] **Ghost statement** — verified, then erased; non-interfering with concrete state; lives in a separate ghost heap universe.
- [ ] **Ghost uses** — loop-variant / decreases, inductive invariants, auxiliary lemma calls.
- [ ] **Soundness** — VCC never says "verified" falsely.
- [ ] **Relative completeness** — every valid triple is provable *if* the right invariant is given.
- [ ] **Real-world deployment** — Microsoft Hyper-V hypervisor device-virtualization core.
- [ ] **Tool chain** — VCC (front end) → Boogie (IVL) → Z3 (SMT solver).
- [ ] **KTU keyword associations** — `requires` ↔ $P$; `ensures` ↔ $Q$; `invariant` ↔ $I$; `decreases` ↔ termination measure; `assert` ↔ runtime check; `assume` ↔ environment assumption; `ghost` ↔ proof-only code.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inter-procedure Verification of Programs in VCC
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST741 - FORMAL METHODS IN SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Inter-procedure Verification of Programs in VCC

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Inter-procedure Verification** in **VCC (Verification Condition Calculator)** is the systematic process of reasoning about the correctness of a C program across function/procedure boundaries by composing individual procedure-level proof obligations into a single, global proof obligation. Each procedure is annotated with a **contract** comprising a *precondition* (`requires`), a *postcondition* (`ensures`), and a *modifies/assigns* clause that bounds the side effects a function may perform.

When a caller invokes a procedure, VCC must guarantee that the caller's state at the call site satisfies the callee's precondition. Upon return, VCC must re-establish the caller's post-state using the callee's postcondition, while ensuring that the **frame condition** (the set of locations the callee is permitted to modify) is respected.

Mathematically, for a procedure `P` with contract `{ requires Q; assigns A; ensures R }`, inter-procedural verification demands:

$$
\frac{\Gamma \vdash Q \quad \Gamma, R, \text{modifies}(A) \vdash S_{\text{rest}}}{\Gamma \vdash S \;\; \text{call } P(\vec{x})}
$$

This is the **Call Rule** of VCC's program logic, often called the **consequence rule** for procedure calls.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 4**
> Inter-procedure verification in VCC is rooted in the **Dijkstra-style weakest precondition calculus** combined with **separation logic** extensions. VCC specifically uses *two-state invariants* and *ownership* semantics to allow modular reasoning at the procedure level.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The "Contract between Engineers"**
>
> Imagine a construction project with multiple subcontractors. Each subcontractor signs a *contract* before starting:
> - **What they need to begin** (precondition — "The site must be cleared and permit issued")
> - **What they promise to deliver** (postcondition — "A finished wall painted blue")
> - **What they are allowed to touch** (assigns clause — "Only the wall in Sector B")
>
> The general contractor (the *caller*) doesn't need to know *how* the subcontractor builds the wall — only that the contract is honored. Inter-procedure verification in VCC works identically: each function is a "subcontractor" with a contract, and VCC verifies that *every caller* of that function abides by the contract at the point of invocation. This is **modular verification** — you don't need to look inside a function to trust it, just trust its contract.

## 1.3 Why Inter-procedure Verification is Hard

> [!WARNING]
> **Three Core Challenges in Inter-procedural Reasoning**
>
> 1. **State Coupling**: Two procedures share global/heap state — a callee might mutate objects the caller relies on.
> 2. **Aliasing**: The `assigns` clause must be precise, otherwise soundness breaks.
> 3. **Recursive Calls**: Reasoning must support cyclic call graphs, requiring **induction over call depth** or **k-induction**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 VCC's Logical Foundations

VCC is built on top of three pillars:
- **Boogie intermediate verification language (IVL)**
- **Z3 SMT solver** (developed at Microsoft Research)
- **A two-state separation logic with ownership**

When VCC encounters a C file, it translates each C procedure into a **Boogie procedure**. The body of every C function is encoded as a control-flow graph of Boogie `goto` statements. VCC then emits **verification conditions (VCs)** — first-order logic formulas that, if discharged by Z3, prove the program's correctness.

The translation pipeline is:

$$
\text{C Source} \xrightarrow{\text{Parse}} \text{Boogie AST} \xrightarrow{\text{WP Calc}} \text{VCs} \xrightarrow{\text{Z3}} \text{Valid / Invalid}
$$

## 2.2 Components of a VCC Procedure Contract

A VCC contract has six formal elements:

1. **`requires P`** — Boolean predicate that must hold on entry.
2. **`ensures Q`** — Boolean predicate that must hold on exit (post-state).
3. **`assigns A`** — The set of memory locations the procedure is allowed to modify.
4. **`decreases e`** — A ranking expression for proving termination (variant function).
5. **`reads A`** — Locations read but not modified (for read-only procedures).
6. **`writes A`** — A synonym for `assigns` used in VCC's spec language.

## 2.3 The Five Rules of Inter-procedure Reasoning

| **Rule** | **Purpose** | **Logical Form** |
|---|---|---|
| **Call Rule** | Validate a procedure call | $\frac{\text{Pre}(P)[\vec{a}/\vec{x}] \quad \text{Post}(P)[\vec{a}/\vec{x}] \Rightarrow C'}{\text{call } P(\vec{a}) \text{ is valid}}$ |
| **Frame Rule** | Restrict callee to assigned locations | $\text{Modifies}(P) \subseteq A$ |
| **Consequence Rule** | Strengthen pre, weaken post | $\frac{P \Rightarrow P' \quad Q' \Rightarrow Q}{\{P\} \; S \; \{Q\} \Rightarrow \{P'\} \; S \; \{Q'\}}$ |
| **Recursive Call Rule** | Use inductive hypothesis | Treat recursive call as if it's an opaque call satisfying the contract |
| **Disjointness Rule** | Prevent aliasing of `assigns` | $\text{Assigned}(P) \cap \text{Untouchable} = \emptyset$ |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in 14-mark KTU problems regularly.**

| **Symbol / Concept** | **Definition** | **Application in VCC** |
|---|---|---|
| $wp(S, Q)$ | Weakest precondition of statement $S$ w.r.t. postcondition $Q$ | $wp(\text{call } P(\vec{a}), Q) \equiv \text{Pre}(P)[\vec{a}/\vec{x}] \land \forall \vec{v}.\;\text{Post}(P)[\vec{a}/\vec{x}] \Rightarrow Q$ |
| $\text{Frame}(F, P)$ | Framing predicate — what $F$ protects | $\text{Frame}(F, P) \equiv \forall x.\; x \in F \Rightarrow P(x)$ |
| $\text{Owned}(o)$ | Ownership predicate for object $o$ | $o \mapsto v$ — exclusive ownership of $o$'s value $v$ |
| $\text{Valid}(p)$ | Pointer $p$ points to a valid (allocated) object | Required precondition of dereferences |
| $\text{Inv}(o, I)$ | Invariant $I$ holds for object $o$ | $I(o)$ checked on entry & maintained across all operations |
| $I_{\text{2-state}}(o)$ | Two-state invariant relating pre- and post-states | $\text{Old}(I) \Rightarrow I$ across procedure calls |
| $r(P)$ | `requires` of $P$ | Boolean formula |
| $e(P)$ | `ensures` of $P$ | Boolean formula in post-state |
| $a(P)$ | `assigns` set of $P$ | Set of addresses |
| $\mid$ (frame separator) | $\text{olds}(e) \mid e$ — old vs new value | Write $\text{old}(x)$ for pre-call value of $x$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Use `\vert` in LaTeX inside tables instead of the pipe character `|`** — this is the KTU 2024 standard to avoid markdown rendering bugs in `README` exports.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Inter-procedure verification in VCC is used in industry for:
- **Microsoft Hyper-V / Hyper-V VSPs** — verified drivers in the Windows kernel
- **Axiomatic verification of device drivers** (SDV — Static Driver Verifier)
- **Concurrency primitives** (locks, atomic operations) in OS code
- **Safety-critical embedded systems** (medical, avionics) where C is the lingua franca

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, this topic is examined under **CO4: Apply formal verification tools to construct and validate program correctness proofs**, typically carrying **14 marks** in Part B.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The VCC Call Rule from First Principles

Let $P$ be a procedure with formal parameters $\vec{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$, precondition $Q_P(\vec{x})$, and postcondition $R_P(\vec{x}, \text{result})$. A caller invokes $P$ with actual arguments $\vec{a} = (a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n)$.

**Step 1 — Substitution.** Replace formal parameters with actuals in the precondition:

$$
Q_P[\vec{a}/\vec{x}] = Q_P(a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitution in the postcondition.** Replace $\vec{x}$ by $\vec{a}$ and bind the return value:

$$
R_P[\vec{a}/\vec{x}, \text{result} \mapsto v] = R_P(a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n, v)
$$

**Step 3 — Universal quantification over return value.** Since the return value is chosen by the callee, the caller can only assume:

$$
\forall v.\; R_P(a_1, \ldots, a_n, v) \Rightarrow \text{caller's-post}(v)
$$

**Step 4 — The wp of a call is the conjunction of (a) the pre-substituted precondition, and (b) the universally-quantified implication**:

$$
\boxed{wp(\text{call } P(\vec{a}),\; Q) \;\equiv\; Q_P[\vec{a}/\vec{x}] \;\land\; \forall v.\; \big(R_P[\vec{a}/\vec{x}, \text{result}\mapsto v] \;\Rightarrow\; Q[v/\text{result}]\big)}
$$

**Step 5 — Frame preservation.** If the caller has an invariant $I$ over some set of locations $F$ that $P$ does not touch, then:

$$
I(F) \;\land\; F \cap a(P) = \emptyset \;\Rightarrow\; I(F) \text{ in post-state}
$$

This is the **frame rule** applied across procedures.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Verified `max` Function

Consider the following VCC-annotated C function:

```c
int max(int a, int b)
  __CPROVER_requires(INT_MIN <= a && a <= INT_MAX)
  __CPROVER_requires(INT_MIN <= b && b <= INT_MAX)
  __CPROVER_ensures(__CPROVER_result == a || __CPROVER_result == b)
  __CPROVER_ensures(__CPROVER_result >= a && __CPROVER_result >= b)
{
  return (a > b) ? a : b;
}
```

*(Note: VCC uses `requires`/`ensures`; the example above shows equivalent contract patterns.)*

**Derivation of the VCs:**

**VC1 — Precondition check at every call site.** For every `call max(x, y)`, VCC generates:

$$
\text{VC}_1 \equiv \text{INT\_MIN} \leq x \leq \text{INT\_MAX} \;\land\; \text{INT\_MIN} \leq y \leq \text{INT\_MAX}
$$

If `x` and `y` are 32-bit `int`, this VC is trivially true.

**VC2 — Postcondition at the return statement.** The `return (a > b) ? a : b;` statement splits into two cases:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VC}_{2a} &\equiv a > b \;\Rightarrow\; (\text{result} = a) \;\land\; (\text{result} \geq a) \;\land\; (\text{result} \geq b) \\
\text{VC}_{2b} &\equiv a \leq b \;\Rightarrow\; (\text{result} = b) \;\land\; (\text{result} \geq a) \;\land\; (\text{result} \geq b)
\end{aligned}
$$

**VC3 — Termination.** Since `max` is non-recursive, no `decreases` clause is needed. VCC inserts a synthetic `decreases 0` for non-recursive functions.

**Z3 result:** All VCs are `Valid` ⇒ function verified.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Inter-procedure Reasoning with Frame

Consider a caller that holds an invariant on a global counter `g`:

```c
volatile int g = 0;

void inc()
  __CPROVER_assigns(g)
  __CPROVER_ensures(g == \old(g) + 1);

void caller()
  __CPROVER_requires(g == 42)
  __CPROVER_assigns(g)
  __CPROVER_ensures(g == 43)
{
  inc();
  // VCC must verify: old(g)=42, post of inc gives g=43 ✓
}
```

**Step-by-step VC generation:**

**Step 1** — At the call `inc();`, VCC substitutes pre and post of `inc`:

$$
\text{Pre}_{\text{inc}}[\,] \equiv \text{true} \quad (\text{no preconditions on } \text{inc})
$$

$$
\text{Post}_{\text{inc}} \equiv g = \text{old}(g) + 1
$$

**Step 2** — Apply Call Rule to the caller's postcondition $g = 43$:

$$
\text{VC} \equiv \text{Pre}_{\text{inc}} \;\land\; \forall v.\; (\text{Post}_{\text{inc}} \Rightarrow g = 43)
$$

**Step 3** — Substitute `old(g) = 42` from the caller's precondition:

$$
g = 42 + 1 = 43 \;\Rightarrow\; g = 43 \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Step 4** — Frame check: `assigns(g)` of `inc` ⊆ `assigns(g)` of caller ✓

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Inlining vs. Modular Verification

| **Criterion** | **Inlining (Unrolling)** | **Modular (Contract-Based)** |
|---|---|---|
| **VC size** | Quadratic in call depth | Linear in code size |
| **Reusability** | None — each caller re-verifies callee | Callee verified once |
| **Requires** | Full source of callee | Only contract needed |
| **Scales to** | Small programs ($< 1$k LOC) | Large codebases ($> 100$k LOC) |
| **Soundness** | Trivial | Requires strict `assigns` discipline |
| **Used by VCC** | As fallback when no contract | Default mode |

> [!NOTE]
> VCC's default is **modular verification** — the practical scalability win. KTU problems often ask students to **compare** these two strategies.

## 3.5 Derivation: Recursive Procedure Verification

For a recursive function $P$ with parameter $n$, VCC generates an induction proof:

**Step 1** — The user annotates a `decreases` clause:

$$
\text{decreases } n
$$

**Step 2** — For each recursive call $P(n-1)$, VCC must verify:

$$
\text{decreases clause at call} \equiv n - 1 < n \;\; \checkmark \text{ (always true)}
$$

**Step 3** — The recursive call is treated as an opaque call satisfying the contract — this is the **inductive hypothesis**.

**Step 4** — Termination VC:

$$
\text{VC}_{\text{term}} \equiv n \geq 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{loop decreases} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{termination}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of VCC's Verification Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[C Source Code] --> B[VCC Frontend Parser]
    B --> C[Boogie Intermediate Verification Language]
    C --> D[Weakest Precondition Calculator]
    D --> E[First-Order VC Generation]
    E --> F[Z3 SMT Solver]
    F --> G{All VCs Valid?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Verified: Program Correct]
    G -- No --> I[Counterexample Trace]
    I --> J[User Debugging]
    J --> A
    H --> K[Trust Certificate]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style F fill:#ffe1e1
    style H fill:#d4f5d4
    style I fill:#ffd4d4
    style K fill:#fff5d4
```

## 4.2 Inter-procedure Call Flow in VCC

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Caller as Caller Procedure
    participant VCC1 as VCC Call-Site Checker
    participant Contract as Procedure Contract
    participant VCC2 as VCC Callee-Body Checker
    participant Callee as Callee Body
    participant Z3 as Z3 SMT Solver

    Caller->>VCC1: call P(a1, a2, ..., an)
    VCC1->>Contract: substitute actuals into Pre(P)
    Contract-->>VCC1: Pre(P)[a/x]
    VCC1->>Z3: VC1 - check Pre holds
    Z3-->>VCC1: valid / invalid

    VCC1->>VCC2: Verify callee body
    VCC2->>Callee: parse body into Boogie
    Callee->>VCC2: control-flow graph
    VCC2->>Z3: VC2 - check Post(P) at every return
    Z3-->>VCC2: valid / invalid

    VCC2-->>VCC1: contract established
    VCC1->>Contract: substitute into Post(P)
    Contract-->>Caller: Post(P)[a/x, result->v]
    VCC1->>Z3: VC3 - check caller's continues from Post(P)
    Z3-->>Caller: valid / invalid
```

## 4.3 Frame Condition Visualization

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph CallerState["Caller State (Heap)"]
        A1[Object x - caller owns]
        A2[Object y - caller owns]
        A3[Object z - shared invariant]
    end

    subgraph AssignsClause["Callee assigns"]
        B1[Object x only]
    end

    subgraph AfterCall["Post-Call State"]
        C1[Object x - mutated OK]
        C2[Object y - UNCHANGED via frame]
        C3[Object z - invariant preserved]
    end

    CallerState -->|call P| AssignsClause
    AssignsClause --> AfterCall

    style A1 fill:#ffd4d4
    style B1 fill:#fff5d4
    style A2 fill:#d4f5d4
    style A3 fill:#d4d4f5
    style C1 fill:#ffd4d4
    style C2 fill:#d4f5d4
    style C3 fill:#d4d4f5
```

## 4.4 The Five Steps of Inter-procedure Verification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Locate call site in caller] --> S2[Step 2: Substitute actuals into Pre P]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Generate VC1 - check Pre holds]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Substitute actuals into Post P]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Generate VC2 - frame check and continuation]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Send VCs to Z3]
    S6 --> S7{Valid?}
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Call Site Verified]
    S7 -- No --> S9[Counterexample / Error]

    style S1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style S7 fill:#ffe1e1
    style S8 fill:#d4f5d4
    style S9 fill:#ffd4d4
```

## 4.5 Modular vs. Inlining Comparison

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Inlining["Inlining Strategy"]
        I1[Caller Body] --> I2[Copy Callee Body]
        I2 --> I3[Single Monolithic CFG]
        I3 --> I4[Generate VCs for entire graph]
    end

    subgraph Modular["Modular Strategy - VCC Default"]
        M1[Caller Body] --> M2[Use Callee Contract Only]
        M2 --> M3[Substitute Pre and Post]
        M3 --> M4[Generate VCs for caller only]
    end

    style Inlining fill:#ffe1e1
    style Modular fill:#d4f5d4
```
