# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q1]**
> **Q1.** Define the three components of a VCC procedure contract and explain the role of the `assigns` clause in inter-procedure verification.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A VCC procedure contract consists of three components:

1. **`requires` (Precondition)** — A Boolean predicate $Q(\vec{x})$ over the formal parameters and global state that must hold at the call site. It defines the conditions under which the procedure promises to behave correctly.

2. **`ensures` (Postcondition)** — A Boolean predicate $R(\vec{x}, \text{result})$ that must hold when the procedure returns, possibly referring to pre-state values via $\text{old}(\cdot)$.

3. **`assigns` (Frame condition)** — A set $A$ of memory locations that the procedure is permitted to modify. Any location not in $A$ must remain unchanged across the call.

**Role of `assigns` in inter-procedure verification:**
The `assigns` clause enables the **frame rule** to be applied at call sites. When a caller invokes a procedure, VCC uses the `assigns` clause to determine which memory locations the callee is allowed to touch. Any location owned by the caller but **not** in the callee's `assigns` set is automatically guaranteed to be preserved, allowing the caller's local invariants to be maintained without re-deriving them. This is the foundation of **modular verification** in VCC.

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q2]**
> **Q2.** What is the difference between **inlining** and **modular (contract-based)** verification of procedures? Which one does VCC use by default, and why?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Inlining** | **Modular Verification** |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Callee body is inlined at each call site | Only callee's contract is used |
| **VC size** | Grows with call depth (quadratic) | Linear in code size |
| **Re-verification** | Required for every call | One-time verification per callee |
| **Source needed** | Full source of callee | Only the contract specification |
| **Default in VCC** | Used as fallback | **Default** (preferred) |

**VCC uses modular verification by default** because:
- It scales to large codebases (Microsoft uses it on $\geq 100$k LOC kernels).
- It separates concerns — each procedure is verified in isolation.
- It reduces VC explosion by abstracting callees via contracts.
- It supports **separate compilation** — a callee can be verified once and reused across many callers.

Inlining is used only as a **fallback** when no contract is provided, in which case VCC inlines the callee body into the caller's CFG and verifies monolithically.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Question]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of VCC's verification pipeline from C source to Z3 SMT solver. Describe each stage in detail, including the role of the Boogie intermediate verification language. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following C program with VCC contracts:

```c
int square(int x)
  __CPROVER_requires(x >= 0 && x <= 1000)
  __CPROVER_ensures(__CPROVER_result >= 0)
  __CPROVER_ensures(__CPROVER_result == x * x);

int compute(int a, int b)
  __CPROVER_requires(a >= 0 && b >= 0)
  __CPROVER_assigns(nothing)
  __CPROVER_ensures(__CPROVER_result == square(a) + square(b))
{
  int s1 = square(a);
  int s2 = square(b);
  return s1 + s2;
}
```

Demonstrate, using the VCC **Call Rule**, how the verification conditions are generated at the call sites `square(a)` and `square(b)`. Show how the frame condition and postcondition of `square` are used to discharge the VCs for `compute`. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) VCC Verification Pipeline (7 marks):**

The VCC pipeline consists of **five stages**:

1. **Parsing (C → AST):** VCC's frontend parses the annotated C source code into an abstract syntax tree. VCC-specific annotations (`requires`, `ensures`, `assigns`, `decreases`) are preserved as attributes on function declarations.

2. **Translation (AST → Boogie):** Each C procedure is translated into a Boogie procedure. C statements become Boogie `goto` statements forming a control-flow graph. Loops are unrolled into structured `while` constructs, and contracts are encoded as `requires`/`ensures` in Boogie's specification language.

3. **Weakest Precondition Computation (Boogie → VCs):** VCC applies Dijkstra's weakest precondition calculus over the control-flow graph, propagating postconditions backwards through each statement. This produces a set of first-order logic formulas — the **verification conditions (VCs)**.

4. **SMT Solving (VCs → Valid/Invalid):** The VCs are sent to the **Z3 SMT solver** (developed at Microsoft Research). Z3 checks the validity of each VC using decision procedures for arithmetic, arrays, bit-vectors, and uninterpreted functions.

5. **Result Reporting (Valid → Trust Certificate / Invalid → Counterexample):** If all VCs are valid, VCC emits a "verification succeeded" message. If any VC fails, VCC produces a **counterexample trace** showing the inputs and execution path that violates the contract, aiding debugging.

The **Boogie intermediate verification language** plays a crucial role:
- It decouples C-specific concerns from generic VC generation.
- It enables VCC to support multiple frontends (C, C++, and others).
- It provides a clean separation between *program logic* and *decision procedures*.

**(b) VCs at Call Sites in `compute` (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the call sites and substitute actuals into `square`'s contract.**

For `square(a)`:
- $\text{Pre}_{\text{square}}[a/x] \equiv a \geq 0 \;\land\; a \leq 1000$
- $\text{Post}_{\text{square}}[a/x, \text{result}\mapsto v] \equiv v \geq 0 \;\land\; v = a \cdot a$

For `square(b)`:
- $\text{Pre}_{\text{square}}[b/x] \equiv b \geq 0 \;\land\; b \leq 1000$
- $\text{Post}_{\text{square}}[b/x, \text{result}\mapsto v] \equiv v \geq 0 \;\land\; v = b \cdot b$

**Step 2 — Apply the VCC Call Rule for `square(a)`.**

The wp of the statement `int s1 = square(a);` with postcondition $Q \equiv s_1 + s_2 = a^2 + b^2$ is:

$$
wp(\text{call } \text{square}(a),\; Q) \equiv \text{Pre}_{\text{square}}[a/x] \;\land\; \forall v.\; \big(\text{Post}_{\text{square}}[a/x, \text{result}\mapsto v] \;\Rightarrow\; v + s_2 = a^2 + b^2\big)
$$

Substituting:

$$
\equiv a \geq 0 \;\land\; a \leq 1000 \;\land\; \forall v.\; \big(v \geq 0 \;\land\; v = a^2 \;\Rightarrow\; v + s_2 = a^2 + b^2\big)
$$

The implication reduces to $s_2 = b^2$, which becomes the new postcondition for the next statement.

**Step 3 — Apply the VCC Call Rule for `square(b)`.**

Similarly:

$$
\equiv b \geq 0 \;\land\; b \leq 1000 \;\land\; \forall v.\; \big(v \geq 0 \;\land\; v = b^2 \;\Rightarrow\; s_1 + v = a^2 + b^2\big)
$$

This is discharged by substituting $s_1 = a^2$ and $v = b^2$:

$$
s_1 + b^2 = a^2 + b^2 \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Step 4 — Frame Condition Check.**

`square` has no `assigns` clause beyond its local variables (which are stack-allocated and discarded). VCC verifies that `square`'s `assigns` set is disjoint from `compute`'s locals (`s1`, `s2`, `a`, `b`). Since `square` only writes to its own stack frame, the frame condition is trivially satisfied. No global state is modified, so the caller's invariants are preserved.

**Step 5 — Final Postcondition Discharge.**

The function returns $s_1 + s_2$, and VCC checks:

$$
s_1 + s_2 = a^2 + b^2 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{result} = a^2 + b^2 \;\; \checkmark
$$

All VCs are discharged by Z3, and `compute` is verified.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **two-state invariant** mechanism in VCC and describe how it is used to maintain consistency of object invariants across procedure boundaries. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following recursive factorial function in VCC:

```c
int factorial(int n)
  __CPROVER_requires(n >= 0 && n <= 12)
  __CPROVER_decreases(n)
  __CPROVER_ensures(__CPROVER_result >= 1)
  __CPROVER_ensures(__CPROVER_result == product_of_1_to_n(n));

int factorial_caller(int m)
  __CPROVER_requires(m >= 0 && m <= 12)
  __CPROVER_assigns(nothing)
  __CPROVER_ensures(__CPROVER_result == factorial(m));
{
  return factorial(m);
}
```

Explain how VCC handles the **recursive call** within `factorial`, the role of the `decreases` clause, and the **inductive hypothesis** used in the proof. Also show the VCs generated for `factorial_caller` using the Call Rule. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Two-State Invariants in VCC (7 marks):**

VCC's **two-state invariant** mechanism allows an object's invariant $I(o)$ to refer to **both** the pre-state (before a procedure call) and the post-state (after the call). This is essential for inter-procedure verification because:

- An invariant may depend on the *relationship* between fields that a callee is allowed to modify.
- Without two-state reasoning, a callee that touches one field of an object would force the caller to re-verify the entire invariant.

A two-state invariant has the form:

$$
I(\text{old}(x_1), \ldots, \text{old}(x_n), x_1, \ldots, x_n)
$$

VCC's **admissibility check** ensures that for any field $f$ the callee is allowed to modify, the invariant's truth value after the call does not depend on the pre-state value of $f$ — only on fields the callee does not touch.

For example, an invariant that a sorted array's elements sum to a value stored in a separate field:

$$
I(\text{arr}) \equiv \text{sorted}(\text{arr}) \;\land\; \text{sum}(\text{arr}) = \text{arr.sum}
$$

If a callee is allowed to modify individual elements but not `arr.sum`, then after the call, VCC must re-verify $\text{sorted}(\text{arr})$ in the new state, but `sum(arr) = arr.sum` is preserved by the frame rule. The two-state invariant makes this reasoning modular.

**(b) Recursive Procedure and Caller Verification (7 marks):**

**Handling the Recursive Call in `factorial`:**

The body of `factorial` (omitted for brevity) contains a recursive call `factorial(n-1)`. VCC handles this as follows:

1. **Inductive hypothesis:** VCC treats the recursive call as an opaque call that satisfies `factorial`'s contract. That is, the proof inside `factorial` may assume:

$$
\text{result} = \text{factorial}(n-1)
$$

without inspecting the recursive call's body again.

2. **`decreases` clause:** The user specifies `decreases(n)`. VCC generates a VC that, at every recursive call site, the argument strictly decreases:

$$
\text{VC}_{\text{decreases}} \equiv n - 1 < n \;\; \checkmark \text{ (trivially true for any } n \text{)}
$$

3. **Termination:** Since $n$ is bounded below by 0 (from the precondition) and strictly decreases at each call, the recursion must terminate after at most $n + 1$ calls. VCC's `decreases` mechanism guarantees termination.

4. **Postcondition on return:** On the base case (typically `n == 0` or `n == 1`), the function returns 1, satisfying `result >= 1` and `result == product_of_1_to_n(n)`. In the recursive case, the inductive hypothesis plus the multiplication step establish the postcondition.

**VCs for `factorial_caller`:**

**Step 1 — Substitute actuals into `factorial`'s contract:**

- $\text{Pre}_{\text{factorial}}[m/n] \equiv m \geq 0 \;\land\; m \leq 12$
- $\text{Post}_{\text{factorial}}[m/n, \text{result}\mapsto v] \equiv v \geq 1 \;\land\; v = \prod_{i=1}^{m} i$

**Step 2 — Apply the Call Rule:**

$$
wp(\text{return } \text{factorial}(m),\; \text{result} = \text{factorial}(m)) \equiv \text{Pre}_{\text{factorial}}[m/n] \;\land\; \forall v.\; \big(\text{Post}_{\text{factorial}}[m/n, \text{result}\mapsto v] \;\Rightarrow\; v = \text{factorial}(m)\big)
$$

**Step 3 — Discharge:**

The caller's precondition establishes $m \geq 0 \;\land\; m \leq 12$, satisfying the callee's precondition. The callee's postcondition directly matches the caller's postcondition under the substitution $v = \text{result}$. Z3 discharges this VC trivially.

**Step 4 — Frame Check:**

`factorial` has no global `assigns` (purely functional on stack). The caller's `assigns(nothing)` is respected. No frame violation.

**Result:** `factorial_caller` is verified with **3 VCs**, all discharged by Z3.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (and Where They Lose Marks)**
>
> 1. **Confusing `assigns` with `requires`/`ensures`** — `assigns` is the *frame* (what the callee may modify), not a precondition. Mixing these up costs 2-3 marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the `forall v` quantifier over the return value** in the Call Rule. Many students write:
>    $$wp(\text{call } P(\vec{a}), Q) \equiv \text{Pre}(P)[\vec{a}/\vec{x}] \land \text{Post}(P)[\vec{a}/\vec{x}] \Rightarrow Q$$
>    This is **wrong** — the return value must be universally quantified, not existentially or concretely substituted.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the frame condition** — The frame rule is what makes modular verification sound. Students who skip the `assigns` check typically lose 3-4 marks on 14-mark questions.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the `decreases` clause for recursive functions** — Without it, VCC will not prove termination, and the verification will fail. This is a frequent 2-mark deduction.
>
> 5. **Writing `old(x)` instead of `\old(x)` or `\text{old}(x)` in equations** — Examiners are strict about notation. Use the LaTeX `\text{old}(x)` form.
>
> 6. **Not mentioning Boogie or Z3** — VCC's pipeline involves both. A complete answer must reference the **Boogie intermediate language** and the **Z3 SMT solver** for full marks.
>
> 7. **Confusing modular verification with dynamic testing** — VCC is a *static* tool, not a runtime checker. Mentioning "test cases" instead of "verification conditions" is a fundamental error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Inter-procedure Verification in VCC**
>
> - **VCC** = Verification Condition Calculator, a static verifier for concurrent C code, built on **Boogie + Z3**.
> - **Procedure contract** = `requires` (pre) + `ensures` (post) + `assigns` (frame).
> - **Call Rule**: $wp(\text{call } P(\vec{a}), Q) \equiv \text{Pre}(P)[\vec{a}/\vec{x}] \land \forall v.\,(\text{Post}(P)[\vec{a}/\vec{x}, \text{result}\mapsto v] \Rightarrow Q[v/\text{result}])$.
> - **Frame Rule**: Locations not in `assigns(P)` are preserved across the call.
> - **Consequence Rule**: Strengthen pre, weaken post.
> - **Recursion**: Requires `decreases` clause + inductive hypothesis (recursive call treated as opaque).
> - **Two-state invariants**: Allow $I$ to reference both pre- and post-state; subject to admissibility check.
> - **Default mode** of VCC is **modular verification**; inlining is a fallback.
> - **Boogie** is the IVL; **Z3** is the SMT solver; both are essential pipeline components.
> - **Termination** is mandatory — VCC checks it via the `decreases` measure.
> - **Soundness** depends on strict `assigns` discipline — never omit it.
> - **Frame separator notation** in VCC: $\text{old}(x) \;\mid\; x$ distinguishes pre- and post-values of $x$.
> - **For KTU 14-mark answers**: Always structure as **(a) Theory + Diagram**, **(b) Worked Example + VC derivation**.
> - **Key trade-off**: Modular = scalable but requires contracts; Inlining = automatic but doesn't scale.
> - **Industry relevance**: Used in Microsoft Hyper-V, Windows kernel drivers, and static driver verifier (SDV).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Function Contracts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST741 - FORMAL METHODS IN SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Function Contracts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Function Contract** is a formal, mathematically precise specification attached to a function (or method, procedure, subroutine) that rigorously documents the *obligations* between the **caller (client)** and the **implementation (supplier)**. It is a central construct in *Design by Contract* (DbC), introduced by Bertrand Meyer, and is foundational to Hoare-style axiomatic semantics used in formal software verification.

A function contract is formally expressed as a **Hoare Triple** (also called a Floyd–Hoare triple) with a standard syntactic form:

$$\{P\}\; S \;\{Q\}$$

Where:
- $P$ is the **Precondition** — the predicate that the *caller* must guarantee *before* invoking the function $S$.
- $Q$ is the **Postcondition** — the predicate that the *supplier* must guarantee *upon* termination of $S$.
- $S$ is the function/statement whose behaviour is being constrained.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:** A function contract is a logical specification consisting of a precondition $P$ and a postcondition $Q$, optionally augmented with a *frame condition* (modifies clause) and an *invariant clause*. It defines a mutual, verifiable obligation between the calling context and the function body, enabling *proof obligations* during formal verification.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Legal Contract" of Software**

Think of a function contract as a **legally binding agreement** between two parties:

| Real-World Analogy | Software Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Terms & Conditions a buyer must satisfy to purchase | **Precondition** ($P$) — what the *caller* must ensure |
| Promises the seller must deliver after payment | **Postcondition** ($Q$) — what the *function* must deliver |
| Warranty void if the product is misused | **Frame Condition** — what the function is *not* allowed to touch |
| Arbitration clause for breach of terms | **Invariant** — global property that must never be violated |

If the **buyer (caller) violates** the precondition (e.g., asks to purchase without money), the seller is **released from all obligations** — the function may legally do *anything*, including crash, return garbage, or simply skip work. If the **seller (function) violates** the postcondition, the function is *incorrect*, irrespective of what input was given.

This is the famous Eiffel motto: **"No transaction without a contract."**

### 1.3 Why Function Contracts Matter in Formal Methods

- **Eliminate Ambiguity:** Natural-language specs like "the function should work for valid inputs" are replaced by rigorous first-order logic predicates.
- **Enable Mechanical Verification:** Tools (e.g., *KeY*, *Frama-C*, *SPARK Prover*, *Dafny*, *JML tools*) can discharge *Verification Conditions (VCs)* automatically.
- **Support Modular Reasoning:** A function can be verified *in isolation* using only its contract — the internals of the caller or callee do not need to be inspected.
- **Form the Basis of Weakest Precondition Calculus (Dijkstra):** Contracts enable automated computation of what must hold *before* a statement to guarantee a desired outcome *after*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Metric:** Contract strength is measured in terms of the **weakest precondition** $wp(S, Q)$, which represents the *least* restrictive condition under which $S$ is guaranteed to establish $Q$. A contract $\{P\}S\{Q\}$ is **valid** iff $P \Rightarrow wp(S, Q)$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of a Function Contract

A complete function contract in KTU 2024 formal methods is composed of the following four logical clauses:

#### 2.1.1 Precondition ($P$)
- The predicate that must hold *immediately before* function entry.
- **Responsibility:** Caller.
- **Logical Form:** A first-order predicate over the function's input parameters, global state, and any relevant class invariants.
- **Example:** $\text{Pre} \equiv x \neq 0 \land y \geq 0$

#### 2.1.2 Postcondition ($Q$)
- The predicate that must hold *immediately after* function termination (assuming precondition was satisfied).
- **Responsibility:** Supplier (function body).
- **Logical Form:** Describes the relationship between the post-state and the pre-state, using notations like $\backslash\text{old}(\cdot)$ to refer to the value of a variable *before* the call.
- **Example:** $\text{Post} \equiv \backslash\text{result} = x + y$

#### 2.1.3 Frame Condition / Modifies Clause
- A **set of variables or memory locations** the function is *permitted* to modify.
- Anything not listed in the frame is **forbidden** to be altered (an implicit postcondition).
- **Example:** $\text{modifies} \; \{ \text{balance} \}$

#### 2.1.4 Invariant Clause
- A global property of the data structure or class that must be preserved by *every* public function call (i.e., it is a postcondition of every public method, assuming it was a precondition).
- **Example:** $\text{Inv} \equiv \text{size} \geq 0 \land \text{capacity} \geq \text{size}$

### 2.2 The Hoare Triple — A Rigorous Treatment

A Hoare triple $\{P\}\;S\;\{Q\}$ has the following partial-correctness interpretation:

$$\models \{P\}\; S \;\{Q\} \iff \forall \sigma, \sigma'. \; (\sigma \models P \land \langle S, \sigma \rangle \to \sigma') \Rightarrow \sigma' \models Q$$

Where $\sigma$ and $\sigma'$ are program states (mappings from variables to values), and $\langle S, \sigma \rangle \to \sigma'$ denotes the execution of $S$ starting from $\sigma$ and terminating in $\sigma'$.

**Two flavours of correctness:**
- **Partial Correctness (Hoare):** If $P$ holds and the program terminates, then $Q$ holds. Termination is *not* guaranteed.
- **Total Correctness (Manna–Pnueli):** If $P$ holds, then the program *is guaranteed* to terminate and $Q$ will hold. Denoted as $[P]\;S\;[Q]$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the HIGH-YIELD reference for KTU 2024 board questions. Memorize every row.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hoare Triple (Partial) | $\{P\}\;S\;\{Q\}$ | Specifying partial correctness |
| 2 | Hoare Triple (Total) | $[P]\;S\;[Q]$ | Specifying total correctness (with termination) |
| 3 | Weakest Precondition | $wp(S, Q)$ | Foundation of Dijkstra's calculus |
| 4 | Strongest Postcondition | $sp(P, S)$ | Reverse-direction analysis |
| 5 | Skip / No-op | $wp(\text{skip}, Q) = Q$ | Identity element |
| 6 | Abort | $wp(\text{abort}, Q) = \text{false}$ | Proof of infeasibility |
| 7 | Assignment | $wp(x := E, Q) = Q[E/x]$ | Substitution rule |
| 8 | Sequence | $wp(S_1; S_2, Q) = wp(S_1, wp(S_2, Q))$ | Backward composition |
| 9 | Conditional | $wp(\text{if } B \text{ then } S_1 \text{ else } S_2, Q) = (B \land wp(S_1, Q)) \lor (\neg B \land wp(S_2, Q))$ | Branch reasoning |
| 10 | While Loop (Dijkstra) | $wp(\text{while } B \text{ do } S, Q) = \exists k. \; H_k(Q)$ where $H_0 = \neg B \land Q$ and $H_{k+1} = \neg B \land Q \lor B \land wp(S, H_k)$ | Loop invariant-based reasoning |
| 11 | Loop Invariant | $I \land \neg B \Rightarrow Q$ (to establish postcondition) | Termination measure $(t, \prec)$ must be supplied |
| 12 | Strength Rule | If $P \Rightarrow P'$ and $\{P'\}S\{Q'\}$, then $\{P\}S\{Q'\}$ | Precondition weakening |
| 13 | Consequence Rule | If $\{P\}S\{Q\}$ and $Q \Rightarrow Q'$, then $\{P\}S\{Q'\}$ | Postcondition strengthening |
| 14 | Composition | If $\{P\}S_1\{Q\}$ and $\{Q\}S_2\{R\}$, then $\{P\}S_1;S_2\{R\}$ | Stitching sequential code |
| 15 | Disjunction Rule | If $\{P_1\}S\{Q\}$ and $\{P_2\}S\{Q\}$, then $\{P_1 \lor P_2\}S\{Q\}$ | Case merging |
| 16 | Conjunction Rule | If $\{P_1\}S\{Q_1\}$ and $\{P_2\}S\{Q_2\}$, then $\{P_1 \land P_2\}S\{Q_1 \land Q_2\}$ | Multi-property proof |
| 17 | Validity Check | $P \Rightarrow wp(S, Q)$ | Contract verification |
| 18 | Conjunction (P) | $wp(S, Q_1) \land wp(S, Q_2) \Rightarrow wp(S, Q_1 \land Q_2)$ | Healthiness condition |
| 19 | Law of Excluded Miracle | $wp(S, \text{false}) = \text{false}$ | Strictly-impossible-state rejection |
| 20 | Monotonicity | $Q_1 \Rightarrow Q_2 \Rightarrow wp(S, Q_1) \Rightarrow wp(S, Q_2)$ | Order preservation |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value / set-cardinality notations in this table use `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\vert S \vert$) instead of the vertical pipe character `|` to preserve the markdown table structure.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Function contracts are the backbone of **safety-critical software verification** in:
- **Aerospace (DO-178C Level A):** Avionics software (Airbus A380, Boeing 787) uses SPARK Ada with formal contracts to mathematically prove the absence of runtime errors.
- **Railway Signalling (EN 50128 SIL 4):** Train control systems rely on contracts for verification of interlocking logic.
- **Medical Devices (FDA IEC 62304):** Infusion pumps and pacemakers use contract-based verification to demonstrate safety properties.
- **Automotive (ISO 26262 ASIL D):** Autonomous driving stacks employ contracts for ISO 26262 compliance.
- **Cryptographic Libraries:** Used to specify and verify functional correctness of primitives like RSA, AES, and TLS state machines.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Weakest Precondition for Sequential Composition

**Theorem (Sequence Rule):** For statements $S_1$ and $S_2$ and a postcondition $Q$:
$$wp(S_1; S_2, Q) = wp(S_1, wp(S_2, Q))$$

**Proof by Predicate Transformer Semantics:**

By definition, $wp(S_1; S_2, Q)$ is the *weakest* predicate $P$ such that executing $S_1; S_2$ from any state satisfying $P$ is guaranteed to terminate in a state satisfying $Q$.

Let $\sigma_0$ be the initial state, $\sigma_1$ the state after $S_1$, and $\sigma_2$ the state after $S_2$.

We require: $\sigma_0 \models wp(S_1; S_2, Q) \Rightarrow \sigma_2 \models Q$.

By the **Healthiness Condition of Monotonicity** (Formula #20), $S_2$ from $\sigma_1$ establishes $Q$ iff $\sigma_1 \models wp(S_2, Q)$. Therefore, the condition reduces to:
$$\sigma_0 \models wp(S_1; S_2, Q) \iff \sigma_1 \models wp(S_2, Q)$$

Now, the requirement that $S_1$ from $\sigma_0$ leads to $\sigma_1$ satisfying $wp(S_2, Q)$ is exactly the definition of $wp(S_1, wp(S_2, Q))$. Therefore:
$$wp(S_1; S_2, Q) = wp(S_1, wp(S_2, Q)) \qquad \blacksquare$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Weakest Precondition for Assignment

**Theorem (Assignment Rule):** $wp(x := E, Q) = Q[E/x]$.

**Proof:** $Q$ is a predicate over the post-state. The post-state is the same as the pre-state except that $x$ now has the value $E$. So $Q$ evaluated in the post-state is equivalent to $Q$ with every free occurrence of $x$ replaced by the expression $E$ that was assigned, evaluated in the pre-state. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Worked Example — Computing $wp$ for a Full Function Body

Consider the following function contract for safe integer division:

```
function divide(x: int, y: int) returns (r: int)
  Pre:  y ≠ 0
  Post: r * y = x   ∧   ∃q. (x = q * y)   (functional correctness)
```

**Goal:** Compute $wp(\text{body}, \text{Post})$ and confirm that $\text{Pre} \Rightarrow wp(\text{body}, \text{Post})$.

**Hypothetical body:**
```
r := x;
while (r ≥ y) loop
    r := r - y;
end loop;
```

**Step 1: Compute $wp$ of the loop.**

Let the loop invariant be:
$$I \;\equiv\; \exists q.\; (x = q \cdot y + r) \;\land\; 0 \leq r \;\land\; r < y \;\lor\; r \geq y$$

(More precisely, $I \equiv \exists q.\; (x = q \cdot y + r) \;\land\; r \geq 0$.)

**Step 2: Verify $I$ holds initially (before the loop).**

Initially, $r = x$. Choose $q = 0$. Then $x = 0 \cdot y + r = r$, so $x = q \cdot y + r$ holds trivially. Also $r = x \geq 0$ — wait, this requires $x \geq 0$. We must strengthen the precondition.

For full correctness, the precondition must include $x \geq 0$:
$$\text{Pre} \equiv y \neq 0 \;\land\; x \geq 0$$

**Step 3: Show $I \land \neg B \Rightarrow \text{Post}$.**

When the loop exits, $\neg B$ holds, i.e., $r < y$. Combined with invariant $x = q \cdot y + r$, this gives the full postcondition.

**Step 4: Show $\{I \land B\}\; r := r - y \;\{I\}$ (preservation).**

After $r := r - y$, the new $r'$ satisfies $r' = r - y$, so:
$$x = q \cdot y + r = q \cdot y + r' + y = (q+1) \cdot y + r'$$

Thus the invariant $I$ is preserved. $\blacksquare$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Contract Strength Ordering

A central KTU 2024 question theme is comparing contracts based on the *strength* of pre/postconditions:

| Contract | Precondition | Postcondition | Strength |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_1$ | $P_1$ | $Q_1$ | Baseline |
| $C_2$ | $P_2 \equiv P_1 \land R$ | $Q_1$ | **Stronger** precondition |
| $C_3$ | $P_1$ | $Q_2 \equiv Q_1 \land S$ | **Stronger** postcondition |
| $C_4$ | $P_1 \land R$ | $Q_1 \land S$ | **Strictly stronger** overall |

**Rule of Thumb (KTU Board Exam):**
- *Stronger* precondition ⇒ *easier* to call (fewer obligations), but *harder* to implement.
- *Stronger* postcondition ⇒ *harder* to call (caller relies on more), but *easier* to implement on restricted inputs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** In safety-critical avionics, contracts are deliberately *strengthened* with rich postconditions to enable deep property verification. In general-purpose API design, contracts are *weakened* to maximize usability.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Define a function contract in formal methods and list its four essential components.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **function contract** is a formal, mathematically precise specification of a function's behaviour expressed using first-order logic predicates, defining mutual obligations between the *caller* and the *implementation*.

Its **four essential components** are:
1. **Precondition ($P$):** A predicate that must hold before the function is called. The *caller* is responsible for ensuring this.
2. **Postcondition ($Q$):** A predicate that must hold after the function terminates (assuming the precondition held). The *implementation* is responsible for ensuring this.
3. **Frame Condition (modifies clause):** The set of variables or memory locations the function is permitted to alter. All other state must remain unchanged.
4. **Invariant:** A global property of the data structure that must be preserved across every public function invocation.

Together, these form the Hoare-style specification $\{P\}\;S\;\{Q\}$ augmented with frame and invariant clauses, which can be mechanically verified using weakest precondition calculus. **(3 Marks)**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Explain the difference between partial correctness and total correctness in Hoare triples, with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The difference lies in the **termination guarantee**:

- **Partial Correctness $\{P\}\;S\;\{Q\}$:** Asserts that *if* the program terminates starting from a state satisfying $P$, *then* the final state satisfies $Q$. It does **not** guarantee that $S$ will actually halt.

- **Total Correctness $[P]\;S\;[Q]$:** Asserts that starting from $P$, the program is **guaranteed to terminate** and the final state will satisfy $Q$. Denoted with square brackets and a **variant** (or *ranking function*) that strictly decreases with each loop iteration.

**Example:** For an integer division routine:
- Partial: $\{y \neq 0 \land x \geq 0\}\; \text{divide}(x,y) \;\{ \text{result} \cdot y = x \}$ — correct only if it terminates.
- Total: $[y \neq 0 \land x \geq 0]\; \text{divide}(x,y) \;[ \text{result} \cdot y = x ]$ — also asserts termination after a finite number of subtraction steps. **(3 Marks)**

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**Q. (a) [7 Marks] Compute the weakest precondition $wp(S, Q)$ for the following program segment $S$ and postcondition $Q$, and verify whether the precondition $P$ is sufficient.**
```
S:  y := x * x;
    z := y + x;
    if (z > 10) then
        w := z - 5;
    else
        w := z + 1;
    end if;
```
Given: $P \equiv x \geq 0$ and $Q \equiv w > 6$.

**(b) [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, explain the structure of a function contract. Discuss the role of *frame conditions* and *loop invariants* in contract-based verification.**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — Step by Step:**

**Step 1: Apply the assignment rule to the first statement $y := x \cdot x$.**

By Formula #7, $wp(y := x \cdot x, Q') = Q'[x \cdot x / y]$, where $Q'$ is the postcondition of the rest of the program.

**Step 2: Apply the assignment rule to the second statement $z := y + x$.**

$$wp(z := y + x, Q') = Q'[y + x / z]$$

**Step 3: For the conditional, apply Formula #9.**

Let $B \equiv (z > 10)$. We need the wp of both branches:
- $wp(w := z - 5, Q) = Q[z - 5 / w] \equiv (z - 5) > 6 \equiv z > 11$
- $wp(w := z + 1, Q) = Q[z + 1 / w] \equiv (z + 1) > 6 \equiv z > 5$

By the conditional rule:
$$wp(\text{if-then-else}, Q) = (z > 10 \land z > 11) \lor (z \leq 10 \land z > 5) = z > 11 \lor (5 < z \leq 10)$$

**Step 4: Substitute back to get $wp(S, Q)$.**

Replacing $z$ with $y + x$, and $y$ with $x \cdot x$:

$$wp(S, Q) = (x^2 + x > 11) \lor (5 < x^2 + x \leq 10)$$

$$\boxed{wp(S, Q) \equiv (x^2 + x > 5)}$$

(after simplifying the disjunction with $5 < x^2 + x$ as the inclusive lower bound).

**Step 5: Verify that $P \Rightarrow wp(S, Q)$.**

We need: $x \geq 0 \Rightarrow x^2 + x > 5$.

Test $x = 0$: $x^2 + x = 0 \not> 5$. **Fails!**

So $P$ is **insufficient**. The corrected precondition would be $P' \equiv x \geq 3$ (since $3^2 + 3 = 12 > 5$, and for $x \geq 3$, $x^2 + x$ is monotonically increasing). **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (b) — Step by Step:**

A function contract is a logical specification consisting of a precondition $P$, postcondition $Q$, frame condition $F$, and invariant $\mathcal{I}$, as illustrated in the diagram from Section 4.1.

**Frame Conditions:**
- The *modifies clause* $F$ explicitly enumerates the variables the function may change.
- By implicit postcondition, *all variables not in $F$* must be **unchanged** at function exit.
- This enables **local reasoning**: the verifier can assume non-listed variables are untouched, dramatically reducing the proof state.
- **Example:** `modifies \result, balance` — only the return value and the `balance` field may be modified; all other fields remain invariant.

**Loop Invariants in Verification:**
- A *loop invariant* $I$ is a predicate that holds **before** the loop, is **preserved** by every iteration, and when combined with the negation of the loop guard, **implies the postcondition**.
- For verification, three Verification Conditions (VCs) must be discharged:
  - **VC1 (Entry):** $P \Rightarrow I$ — the invariant holds initially.
  - **VC2 (Preservation):** $I \land B \Rightarrow wp(S, I)$ — the body $S$ preserves $I$ when the guard $B$ holds.
  - **VC3 (Exit):** $I \land \neg B \Rightarrow Q$ — the invariant plus loop exit implies the postcondition.
- For total correctness, an additional **variant** (a non-negative integer that strictly decreases) must be supplied to prove termination.

**Conclusion:** Frame conditions and loop invariants are *indispensable* for making contract verification tractable — they localize reasoning and allow the prover to handle arbitrarily long loops through a single invariant predicate. **(7 Marks)**

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Alternative)**

**Q. (a) [7 Marks] Explain Dijkstra's Weakest Precondition Calculus. Derive the wp rules for: (i) assignment, (ii) sequence, and (iii) the if-then-else statement.**

**(b) [7 Marks] Consider the following function contract for an integer square-root routine:**
```
function isqrt(n: int) returns (r: int)
  Pre:  n ≥ 0
  Post: r*r ≤ n < (r+1)*(r+1)
```
**Show how to discharge the verification conditions using a suitable loop invariant, and explain why this proves the function correct.**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — Step by Step:**

**Dijkstra's Weakest Precondition (wp) Calculus:**

Proposed by Edsger W. Dijkstra in 1975, $wp(S, Q)$ is defined as the *weakest* (least restrictive) predicate on the initial state that guarantees that executing $S$ terminates in a state satisfying $Q$. Formally:

$$wp(S, Q) \equiv \bigwedge \{P \mid \{P\}\;S\;\{Q\}\}$$

That is, $wp(S, Q)$ is the *meet* (greatest lower bound) of all valid preconditions for the same postcondition $Q$. It is a *predicate transformer* — it takes a postcondition $Q$ and transforms it into the weakest precondition that ensures $Q$.

**Property:** $P \Rightarrow wp(S, Q) \iff \{P\}\;S\;\{Q\}$ is a valid Hoare triple. This single equivalence is the foundation of all mechanical contract verification.

---

**(i) Derivation of Assignment Rule: $wp(x := E, Q) = Q[E/x]$**

We require the weakest $P$ such that $\{P\}\;x := E\;\{Q\}$ is valid. After assignment, $x$ holds the value $E$ (evaluated in the pre-state). So $Q$ must hold in the post-state, meaning that *in the pre-state* the predicate $Q$ with $x$ replaced by $E$ must hold:

$$\boxed{wp(x := E, Q) = Q[E/x]}$$

---

**(ii) Derivation of Sequence Rule: $wp(S_1; S_2, Q) = wp(S_1, wp(S_2, Q))$**

To establish $Q$ after $S_1; S_2$, we need (a) executing $S_2$ to lead to $Q$, i.e., the state after $S_1$ must satisfy $wp(S_2, Q)$; and (b) executing $S_1$ must lead to a state satisfying $wp(S_2, Q)$. Combining:

$$\boxed{wp(S_1; S_2, Q) = wp(S_1, wp(S_2, Q))}$$

---

**(iii) Derivation of If-Then-Else Rule:**

For `if B then S_1 else S_2 end`, the precondition must guarantee that *whichever branch is taken* leads to $Q$:
- If $B$ holds initially, then $S_1$ executes, and we need $wp(S_1, Q)$.
- If $\neg B$ holds initially, then $S_2$ executes, and we need $wp(S_2, Q)$.

Therefore:
$$\boxed{wp(\text{if } B \text{ then } S_1 \text{ else } S_2, Q) = (B \land wp(S_1, Q)) \lor (\neg B \land wp(S_2, Q))}$$

**(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (b) — Step by Step:**

**Hypothetical Implementation of `isqrt`:**
```
r := 0;
s := 1;
while (s ≤ n) loop
    r := r + 1;
    s := s + 2*r + 1;
end loop;
```

**Note on correctness of the body:** The identity $s_{\text{new}} = s_{\text{old}} + 2r + 1$ uses the fact that $(r+1)^2 = r^2 + 2r + 1$, so $s$ tracks $r^2$ at every step.

**Step 1: Propose a loop invariant $I$.**

$$I \;\equiv\; r \geq 0 \;\land\; s = r \cdot r \;\land\; s > n \;\lor\; s \leq n$$

More cleanly:
$$I \;\equiv\; r \geq 0 \;\land\; s = r^2 \;\land\; (s \leq n \;\lor\; s > n)$$

(But we need a more useful invariant that ties $r$, $s$, and $n$): 
$$\boxed{I \;\equiv\; 0 \leq r \;\land\; s = r^2 \;\land\; s \leq n \;\Rightarrow\; r^2 \leq n}$$

Actually, the precise invariant is:
$$I \;\equiv\; 0 \leq r \;\land\; s = r^2 \;\land\; (r = 0 \lor s \leq n)$$

**Step 2: Verify the three Verification Conditions (VCs).**

**VC1 (Entry — Invariant holds initially):**
- $r = 0, s = 1$ initially.
- $r \geq 0$ ✓
- $s = r^2 \Rightarrow 1 = 0$? **False!**

So this simple invariant fails at entry. We need a *different* invariant. The correct choice is:

$$I \;\equiv\; 0 \leq r \;\land\; s = (r+1)^2 \;\land\; (r = 0 \lor s \leq n + 1)$$

But a cleaner approach uses the postcondition directly. Let $I \equiv r \geq 0 \land s = (r+1)^2$.

Initially $r = 0, s = 1$: $s = (0+1)^2 = 1$ ✓.

**Induction through the loop body:** After one iteration, $r' = r+1$, $s' = s + 2r + 1 = (r+1)^2 + 2r + 1 = r^2 + 4r + 2$. Wait, recalculate:

$s' = s + 2r' + 1 = (r+1)^2 + 2(r+1) + 1 = r^2 + 2r + 1 + 2r + 2 + 1 = r^2 + 4r + 4 = (r+2)^2 = (r'+1)^2$ ✓

**VC2 (Preservation):** $I \land B \Rightarrow wp(\text{body}, I)$
- $B \equiv s \leq n$, $I$ holds, $s = (r+1)^2 \leq n$.
- After body: $r' = r+1$, $s' = (r'+1)^2$, so $I$ holds. ✓

**VC3 (Exit — Implies Postcondition):** $I \land \neg B \Rightarrow \text{Post}$
- $\neg B \equiv s > n$, so $(r+1)^2 > n$.
- $I$ gives $r \geq 0$, $s = (r+1)^2$, hence $r^2 \leq (r+1)^2 = s$ (since $r \geq 0$).
- Wait — we need $r^2 \leq n$ to satisfy `Post`. We have $s = (r+1)^2 > n$, so $(r+1)^2 > n$, which gives $n < (r+1)^2$ — this is the **upper bound** of Post.
- For the **lower bound** $r^2 \leq n$, we need to argue that $r^2 \leq (r+1)^2 = s$. But this doesn't give $r^2 \leq n$ directly.

The postcondition is $r^2 \leq n < (r+1)^2$. From $\neg B$: $(r+1)^2 > n$ ✓. We need $r^2 \leq n$, which requires showing $r^2$ was *always* $\leq n$ during the iterations when $s \leq n$ held.

This is why a stronger invariant is needed:
$$I \;\equiv\; 0 \leq r \;\land\; s = (r+1)^2 \;\land\; s \leq n + 1$$

Then at exit, $s > n$ gives $(r+1)^2 > n$ ✓, and $s \leq n+1$ gives $(r+1)^2 \leq n+1$, hence $r^2 \leq n$ (since $r^2 = (r+1)^2 - 2r - 1 \leq n+1 - 1 = n$ for $r \geq 0$). ✓

**Step 3: Total Correctness (Termination):** The variant $t \equiv n - r^2$ is non-negative (from invariant), and strictly decreases each iteration because $s = (r+1)^2 > r^2$, so $(r+1)^2 - r^2 = 2r+1 > 0$ for $r \geq 0$, meaning $n - (r+1)^2 < n - r^2$. Thus the loop terminates. ✓

**Conclusion:** All VCs are discharged, and the variant proves termination. Therefore, the function contract $\text{Pre} \equiv n \geq 0$ and $\text{Post} \equiv r^2 \leq n < (r+1)^2$ is **fully verified**. **(7 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting `\old()` in postconditions:** When a postcondition refers to the *initial* value of a variable, students often write just `balance` (current value) instead of `\old(balance)`. This is the **#1 mark-deduction error** in JML/ACSL-style contract questions.
> 2. **Confusing precondition weakening with strengthening:** The *Consequence Rule* (Formula #12) lets you **weaken** the precondition ($P \Rightarrow P'$) and **strengthen** the postcondition ($Q' \Rightarrow Q$). Students often invert these directions.
> 3. **Ignoring the frame condition:** A function that modifies an *unlisted* variable is **automatically incorrect**, even if the postcondition holds. Many students omit frame conditions entirely.
> 4. **Partial vs. total correctness confusion:** KTU questions often ask for *termination proofs* (variants). Writing only the loop invariant without the variant costs **at least 3 marks** in Part B.
> 5. **Forgetting the Law of Excluded Miracle (Formula #19):** $wp(S, \text{false}) = \text{false}$ for *all* statements $S$ — this healthiness condition is regularly tested as a 1-mark trick question.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **function contract** is the formal specification $\{P\}S\{Q\}$ augmented with frame and invariant clauses, encoding the **caller–supplier agreement**.
- ✅ **Precondition** = caller's responsibility; **Postcondition** = supplier's responsibility.
- ✅ **Hoare triple** = partial correctness (no termination guarantee); **square-bracket triple** = total correctness.
- ✅ **Weakest Precondition** $wp(S, Q)$ is the *least restrictive* $P$ such that $\{P\}S\{Q\}$ holds.
- ✅ **Validity check:** $\{P\}S\{Q\}$ is valid iff $P \Rightarrow wp(S, Q)$.
- ✅ **Assignment rule:** $wp(x := E, Q) = Q[E/x]$ — substitute the expression for the variable.
- ✅ **Sequence rule:** $wp(S_1; S_2, Q) = wp(S_1, wp(S_2, Q))$ — apply backwards.
- ✅ **Conditional rule:** $(B \land wp(S_1, Q)) \lor (\neg B \land wp(S_2, Q))$.
- ✅ **Loop rule** uses invariants $I$ that satisfy **Entry, Preservation, Exit VCs**.
- ✅ **Frame condition** = `modifies` clause; everything else is *implicitly unchanged*.
- ✅ **Law of Excluded Miracle:** $wp(S, \text{false}) = \text{false}$ always.
- ✅ **Consequence rule:** $P \Rightarrow P'$ and $\{P'\}S\{Q'\}$ and $Q' \Rightarrow Q$ implies $\{P\}S\{Q\}$.
- ✅ **Termination proof** requires a **variant** (well-founded decreasing expression), not just an invariant.
- ✅ **Old value notation:** Use $\backslash\text{old}(x)$ in postconditions to refer to pre-call value of $x$.
- ✅ **Tools:** Frama-C (ACSL), KeY (JML), SPARK Prover, Dafny, VerCors, Why3 — all rely on wp-calculus internally.
- ✅ **Industrial use:** Avionics (DO-178C), railways (EN 50128), automotive (ISO 26262), medical devices (IEC 62304).
- ✅ **Discharge of VCs** is done by SMT solvers (Z3, CVC5, Alt-Ergo) — they are *semi-decidable* for first-order logic fragments.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pure Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST741 - FORMAL METHODS IN SOFTWARE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Pure Functions ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Formal Methods in Software Engineering** (KTU 2024 Scheme, PECST741), a **Pure Function** is rigorously defined as a computational construct that satisfies two mathematically necessary and sufficient conditions: 1. **Determinism**: For every input value $x$ drawn from the function's domain $D$, the function $f$ produces exactly one output value $y$ in the codomain $C$, such that $y = f(x)$ is uniquely determined. 2. **Absence of Side Effects**: The function's evaluation alters **no observable state** outside of its own local scope. No external variable, data structure, file system, network resource, console output, or hardware state is modified as a consequence of evaluation. Mathematically expressed, a pure function is a total mapping: $$f: D \rightarrow C \quad \text{where} \quad \forall x \in D, \exists ! y \in C \mid y = f(x)$$ In formal specification languages such as **VDM-SL**, **Z notation**, **B-Method**, and **Alloy**, pure functions form the foundational primitive from which all larger behavioural specifications (operations, schemas, state machines) are constructed. They are the direct computer-science counterparts of mathematical functions as understood in classical set theory and lambda calculus ($\lambda$-calculus). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: Pure functions are the cornerstone of *referential transparency* — a property essential to *equational reasoning*, *symbolic execution*, and *model-based testing* in formal verification tools. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Deterministic Coffee Vending Machine" Imagine a vending machine where you press a single button labelled "Espresso". - The machine has a **fixed, pre-defined recipe** (input: 1 token; output: exactly 90 ml of espresso at 92°C). - Every time you insert the same token and press the same button, you receive an **identical cup of espresso** — same volume, same temperature, same taste. - The machine **does not change the world around it**: it does not alter the time of day, the weather, your bank balance (only the inserted token is consumed locally), the position of the moon, or the temperature of the room. - You can give this machine to a mathematician in a black box. Without looking inside, the mathematician can **predict the output with 100 % certainty** purely from the input and the machine's published specification. A **pure function is exactly such a machine**. Given the same input, it always returns the same output, and it never "leaks" any side effect into the surrounding computational environment. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in Formal Methods**: A pure function's behaviour can be captured by a simple, finite set of input-output pairs (or a closed-form expression). This makes it directly amenable to *exhaustive verification*, *proof by induction*, and *property-based testing* — three pillars of formal software engineering. ### 1.3 Contrast — Impure vs. Pure Functions | Aspect | Pure Function | Impure Function | |---|---|---| | **Determinism** | Always returns same output for same input | May return different outputs for same input | | **Side Effects** | None — no I/O, no mutation | May read/write global state, perform I/O | | **Referential Transparency** | Holds (expression == its value) | Fails (expression != its value) | | **Testability** | Trivial — given input, check output | Requires mocks, harnesses, ordering | | **Parallel Safety** | Inherently thread-safe | Requires locks, synchronization | | **Formal Verifiability** | Directly verifiable | Requires complex state machines | | **Example** | `square(x) = x * x` | `Date.now()`, `Math.random()` | > [!TIP] > **Key Insight for KTU Exams**: A student must always justify the *purity* of a function by demonstrating (a) it is a total mapping from a declared domain to a codomain, and (b) it produces no observable change in the program's external state. Mentioning only one of these two is **incomplete** and attracts partial mark loss. ### 1.4 Formal Role in Specification Languages | Language | Pure Function Construct | Notation Example | |---|---|---| | **VDM-SL** | Explicit function definition | `square(x: nat) r: nat`
`pre true`
`post r = x * x` | | **Z Notation** | Schema-based function | $\Delta$ schema-free function with decoration | | **B-Method** | Abstract Machine substitution | `res <-- square(x) = ...` | | **Alloy** | Pure relation (function symbol `->`) | `square: Int -> Int { x -> y: y = mul[x,x] }` | | **Haskell** | Function (all functions are pure by default) | `square x = x * x` | ### 1.5 Axiomatic Properties (Statements without Proof — to be cited as-is) - **Property 1 (Determinism)**: $\forall x \in D, \quad f(x) = f(x)$ (trivially unique). - **Property 2 (Idempotence of Substitution)**: If $e_1 \equiv e_2$ (syntactically/extensionally equal), then $f(e_1) \equiv f(e_2)$ in any context. - **Property 3 (Compositional Closure)**: The composition $g \circ f$ of two pure functions is itself a pure function. - **Property 4 (Referential Transparency)**: Any sub-expression of a pure function can be replaced by its value without changing the program's overall behaviour. > [!IMPORTANT] > These four properties collectively form the **theoretical backbone** of equational reasoning in functional programming and form-based specification — both are *direct exam questions* in KTU Module 4 of PECST741. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "Why" and "How" of Pure Functions The semantics of a pure function $f$ can be decomposed into a sequence of logical steps that are universally applied across formal specification frameworks: - **Step 1 — Domain Declaration**: The specifier explicitly states the type or set $D$ of legal input values. Example: `f: ℕ → ℕ` (a function from natural numbers to natural numbers). This is critical because *partial* functions (functions that are undefined on some inputs) are not considered pure in a strict sense — they require preconditions. - **Step 2 — Codomain Declaration**: The specifier declares the type or set $C$ of all possible output values. The codomain may be a strict superset of the actual *image* (range) of the function. - **Step 3 — Precondition Specification** (when required): If the function is partial, a precondition `pre` clause is added. For a *total* pure function, the precondition is implicitly `true` for the entire domain. - **Step 4 — Postcondition Specification**: A `post` clause or equivalent defines the output mathematically. The postcondition must reference only the function's arguments and the result variable — **never** any external state. - **Step 5 — Locality Enforcement**: The implementation must be verified (by proof, by type system, or by inspection) to access no global mutable state. In languages like Haskell, the type system itself prevents impurity at compile time. In VDM-SL, the specifier must justify purity through the `externals` clause being empty. - **Step 6 — Composition and Substitution**: Once purity is established, the function may be freely substituted into any other expression without altering the surrounding context. This is the *raison d'être* of pure functions in formal proof systems. ### 2.2 Referiential Transparency — The Deep Principle > [!NOTE] > **Referential Transparency (RT)** is a property of expressions in a program: an expression is referentially transparent if it can be replaced by its value without changing the program's behaviour. Formally, an expression $e$ is referentially transparent if and only if: $$ \forall \text{ context } C[\cdot], \quad C[e] \equiv C[\text{val}(e)] $$ where $\text{val}(e)$ denotes the value of $e$, and $\equiv$ denotes *observational equivalence* (i.e., the two programs produce the same output given the same input and have the same effect on the external world). **The Golden Link**: *A function is pure if and only if all of its applications are referentially transparent.* This equivalence is the fundamental theorem that bridges functional programming and formal specification. It is provable by mutual implication: - **Forward**: If $f$ is pure (no side effects, deterministic), then $f(x)$ depends only on $x$. Substituting $f(x)$ for its value cannot change anything outside $f$'s scope. Hence, $f(x)$ is RT. - **Reverse**: If $f$ is not pure, then either (a) it depends on hidden state (RT fails because the value depends on context), or (b) it modifies hidden state (RT fails because substitution would lose the modification). Hence, $f$ is impure. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examination Cheat Sheet — Pin This Table** | Symbol / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Meaning in PECST741 Context | |---|---|---| | Pure Function | $f: D \to C$ | Total mapping with no side effects | | Domain | $D$ | Set of legal input values | | Codomain | $C$ | Set of declared possible outputs | | Image / Range | $f(D) = \{f(x) \mid x \in D\}$ | Actual outputs produced | | Determinism | $\forall x \in D, \forall y_1, y_2 \in C, (f(x) = y_1 \land f(x) = y_2) \Rightarrow y_1 = y_2$ | Uniqueness of output | | Side-Effect Freedom | $\text{observable}(f, \text{world}) = \text{const}$ | World state unchanged after $f$ | | Referential Transparency | $C[e] \equiv C[\text{val}(e)]$ | Substitution invariance | | Composition | $(g \circ f)(x) = g(f(x))$ | Function chaining | | Precondition (VDM) | $\text{pre } P(x)$ | Condition on inputs for partiality | | Postcondition (VDM) | $\text{post } R(\vec{x}, \vec{r})$ | Relation between inputs and result | | Currying | $f(a, b) \leftrightarrow f'(a)(b)$ | Multi-arg as nested single-arg | | $\eta$-Reduction | $\lambda x. (f x) \to f$ | Equivalent expressions | | $\beta$-Reduction | $(\lambda x. e) \, a \to e[x := a]$ | Function application | | Fixed Point | $x = f(x)$ | Equational solution (FP) | | Map Fusion Law | $f \circ \text{map}(g) = \text{map}(f \circ g)$ | Composition through map | | Fold Law | $f(\text{fold} \, g \, a \, xs) = \text{fold} \, h \, f(a) \, \text{map}(f, xs)$ | Structural recursion rule | > [!TIP] > The $\beta$-reduction and $\eta$-reduction rules come directly from $\lambda$-calculus, the formal foundation of functional programming. KTU 2024 examiners frequently ask: *"Show that two Haskell expressions are equivalent using $\beta$- and $\eta$-reduction."* Practice these reductions carefully. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Pure functions are not a theoretical curiosity — they have tangible, industrial impact in: - **Cryptography**: SHA-256, AES round functions, and HMAC primitives are pure — given the same plaintext, they always produce the same ciphertext. This is essential for reproducibility and verification. - **Aerospace & Automotive (DO-178C, ISO 26262)**: Code at the highest safety integrity levels (DAL A / ASIL D) is required to be free of side effects on safety-critical state. Pure functions dominate these code bases. - **Distributed Systems (Apache Spark, Hadoop)**: All transformations in Spark RDDs are pure functions — this is what allows the framework to safely reorder, partition, and parallelize computations across thousands of nodes. - **Build Systems (Make, Bazel, Nix)**: Build rules are pure functions from source files to artifact hashes. A pure build is *reproducible* — given the same inputs, it produces the same outputs, byte-for-byte. - **Database Query Optimizers (SQL)**: Pure expressions in SELECT clauses can be evaluated in any order, on any node, with aggressive optimization — because order does not matter. - **Compilers and Symbolic Execution Tools**: Pure functions are the only construct that symbolic executors (KLEE, CBMC, Java Pathfinder) can reason about without state-space explosion. ### 2.5 Deep Pitfalls — Common Student Errors in KTU Exams > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall 1**: Confusing *partial* functions with *impure* functions. A partial function is one that is undefined for some inputs in its declared domain. A *pure* function must be **total** (or, if partial, must carry an explicit precondition). These are orthogonal concepts. > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall 2**: Treating a function as pure merely because it does not perform I/O. A function that reads a global mutable variable is impure, even if it never performs I/O. Purity requires *total locality* of state. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Equivalence of Purity and Referential Transparency We prove the central theoretical claim that anchors Module 4 of PECST741. **Theorem (Purity $\Leftrightarrow$ Referential Transparency)**: A function $f$ is pure if and only if every application $f(x)$ is a referentially transparent expression. **Proof (Forward Direction: Purity $\Rightarrow$ RT)**: Assume $f$ is pure. By definition, this means: 1. $\forall x \in D, f(x)$ is uniquely determined — i.e., $f$ is deterministic. 2. The evaluation of $f(x)$ has no observable effect on the external world. We must show that for any context $C[\cdot]$ and any expression $e \equiv f(x)$: $$C[e] \equiv C[\text{val}(e)]$$ By (1), $\text{val}(e) = \text{val}(f(x))$ is unique. By (2), neither $e$ nor $\text{val}(e)$ has any side effect on $C$. Therefore, replacing $e$ with its value inside $C$ changes nothing observable. Hence $C[e] \equiv C[\text{val}(e)]$. $\blacksquare$ **Proof (Reverse Direction: RT $\Rightarrow$ Purity)**: Assume every application of $f$ is referentially transparent. We prove purity by contradiction. Suppose $f$ is **not** pure. Then either: - (a) $f$ is non-deterministic: there exist $x \in D$ and $y_1 \neq y_2 \in C$ such that $f(x)$ may yield $y_1$ on one call and $y_2$ on another. - (b) $f$ has a side effect on external state. Consider (a). The expression $f(x)$ is non-deterministic. Construct the context: $$C[\cdot] = \text{let } y = [\cdot] \text{ in } y + y$$ If $f(x)$ were referentially transparent, $C[f(x)]$ would equal $C[\text{val}(f(x))]$. But since $f(x)$ may produce different values, the result is non-deterministic. By hypothesis, $f(x)$ is RT, so $C[f(x)]$ must be deterministic — contradiction. Consider (b). If $f$ mutates external state, then the context $C[\cdot]$ that reads that state after $f$ runs will observe the mutation. But if we substitute $\text{val}(f(x))$ for $f(x)$ and re-run, the side effect is lost. Hence $C[f(x)] \not\equiv C[\text{val}(f(x))]$, contradicting RT. $\blacksquare$ **Conclusion**: The two notions are logically equivalent. This theorem is the formal basis for using *substitution-based reasoning* (the workhorse of every equational proof in functional programming and formal specification). ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Purity | Observation about $f$ | Pure? | Justification | |---|---|---| | $f$ is total, returns the same value for the same input, never reads/writes globals, never does I/O | **Yes** | Satisfies both determinism and locality | | $f$ returns the same value but writes to a log file | **No** | Side effect (file I/O) violates locality | | $f$ reads a global counter to compute its result | **No** | Reads external state — non-local | | $f$ uses `Date.now()` internally | **No** | Reads non-deterministic time source | | $f$ is a partial function (undefined for $x < 0$) with a precondition `pre x >= 0` | **Yes** | Pure, but with restricted domain | | $f$ is undefined for $x < 0$ and has **no** precondition | **No** | Not a total mapping — not a proper function | | $f$ calls a pure function $g$ and uses the result | **Yes** | Compositional closure (Property 3) | | $f$ is recursive but well-founded (base case terminates) | **Yes** | Termination makes it total | | $f$ is recursive but may not terminate (e.g., `f(x) = f(x)`) | **No** | Not total — not a function in the mathematical sense | ### 3.3 Worked Example — VDM-SL Specification of a Pure Function **Specification**: Define a pure function `factorial` in VDM-SL that computes $n!$. **Step 1 — Identify the Domain and Codomain**: - Domain: $\mathbb{N}_1$ (positive natural numbers, since $0! = 1$ is the base case). - Codomain: $\mathbb{N}_1$. **Step 2 — Decide Total vs. Partial**: - We declare `factorial` as a total function on $\mathbb{N}$, with the convention that $0! = 1$. **Step 3 — Write the Explicit Function Definition**: ``` factorial: nat -> nat factorial(n) == if n = 0 then 1 else n * factorial(n - 1) pre true post RESULT = product {1, ..., n} ``` **Step 4 — Verify Purity**: - **Determinism**: Given any natural number $n$, the recursive descent is unique. The arithmetic operators are deterministic. Hence `factorial(n)` is unique. ✓ - **Side-Effect Freedom**: The function reads only its argument `n` and the constant `1`. It writes only to the local `RESULT` variable. No global state, no I/O. ✓ - **Compositional Closure**: We may freely substitute `factorial(5)` with `120` anywhere in a larger expression. ✓ **Step 5 — Construct the Postcondition Proof Obligation**: The postcondition `RESULT = product {1, ..., n}` must be proven by induction. This is precisely the kind of proof obligation a VDM-SL tool (e.g., the VDMTools, Overture) generates and discharges. ### 3.4 Worked Example — $\beta$-Reduction in Haskell Consider the Haskell expression: $$(\lambda x. x^2 + 1) \, 5$$ Apply $\beta$-reduction step by step: **Step 1 — Identify the $\lambda$-abstraction**: $\lambda x. x^2 + 1$. **Step 2 — Identify the argument**: $5$. **Step 3 — Substitute $5$ for $x$ in the body** $x^2 + 1$: $$5^2 + 1$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the arithmetic**: $$5^2 = 25 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 25 + 1 = 26$$ **Final result**: $(\lambda x. x^2 + 1) \, 5 \xrightarrow{\beta} 26$. This is a direct, mechanical application of the $\beta$-reduction rule $(\lambda x. e) \, a \to e[x := a]$. ### 3.5 Derivation of the Map Fusion Law **Claim**: $f \circ \text{map}(g) = \text{map}(f \circ g)$ over a list $[x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]$. **Proof by Structural Induction on the list**: **Base Case (empty list)**: $$f(\text{map}(g, [])) = f([]) = [] = \text{map}(f \circ g, [])$$ **Inductive Step**: Assume the law holds for a list of length $n$. Consider a list of length $n+1$: $[x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{n+1}] = (x_{n+1} : [x_1, \ldots, x_n])$. We apply the definition of `map` to the right-hand side: $$f(\text{map}(g, x_{n+1} : xs)) = f(g(x_{n+1}) : \text{map}(g, xs))$$ Since $f$ is pure, we may apply it element-wise without affecting external state: $$= (f \circ g)(x_{n+1}) : f(\text{map}(g, xs))$$ By the inductive hypothesis, $f(\text{map}(g, xs)) = \text{map}(f \circ g, xs)$: $$= (f \circ g)(x_{n+1}) : \text{map}(f \circ g, xs) = \text{map}(f \circ g, x_{n+1} : xs)$$ Hence the law holds for lists of length $n+1$. By induction, the law holds for all finite lists. $\blacksquare$ > [!NOTE] > The map fusion law is a direct consequence of purity and is one of the most frequently tested **equational reasoning** laws in KTU Module 4. Mastering its proof is a high-yield exam investment. ### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Reasoning Technique | Scenario | Recommended Technique | Justification | |---|---|---| | Verify a single property of a pure function | Equational reasoning / substitution | Direct RT | | Prove a property over a recursive data structure | Structural induction | Purity + well-foundedness | | Show two programs are equivalent | Denotational semantics + bisimulation | Functional equality | | Show a property holds for all inputs | Property-based testing (QuickCheck) | Randomized input generation | | Discover counter-examples | Bounded model checking (Alloy, Spin) | Finite exhaustive search | | Verify a concurrent / impure program | Refinement calculus, process algebras | State-based reasoning | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Anatomy of a Pure Function Call The following Mermaid diagram depicts the *observational architecture* of a pure function invocation. The arrows crossing the **System Boundary** are the critical locus of purity — a pure function must have **zero** arrows crossing this boundary that represent state mutation or external reads. ```mermaid flowchart LR Caller["Caller / Expression
C[ . ]"] ArgIn["Input Value
x in D"] ResultOut["Output Value
y = f(x)"] LocalState["Local State
Arguments, Local Vars"] Body["Function Body
Deterministic Computation"] PrePost["Pre / Post Conditions
pre P(x) post R(x, RESULT)"] ReturnArrow["Return Value
y"] Boundary1["System Boundary"] Boundary2["System Boundary"] Caller -->|"1. Call f(x)"| ArgIn ArgIn -->|"2. Enter function scope"| Boundary1 Boundary1 -->|"3. Bind x to local"| LocalState LocalState --> Body PrePost -.->|"Validates"| Body Body -->|"4. Compute y = f(x)"| ReturnArrow ReturnArrow -->|"5. Return y to caller"| Boundary2 Boundary2 --> ResultOut ResultOut -->|"6. Substitute"| Caller ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the Diagram**: Arrows numbered 1 through 6 trace the lifecycle of a single pure function call. The two **System Boundary** walls (Boundary1 on entry, Boundary2 on exit) are conceptual fences. The diagram shows that only the *input value* enters and only the *output value* exits. Crucially, **no arrows cross the boundaries for global state reads or writes**, which is the visual signature of purity. ### 4.2 State Machine — Compositional Closure of Pure Functions ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Define_Pure_f Define_Pure_f --> Verify_Determinism Verify_Determinism --> Verify_Locality Verify_Locality --> Purity_Established Purity_Established --> Allow_Substitution Allow_Substitution --> Allow_Composition Allow_Composition --> Allow_Equational_Reasoning Allow_Equational_Reasoning --> [*] Verify_Determinism --> Reject_NonDeterministic Reject_NonDeterministic --> [*] Verify_Locality --> Reject_SideEffect Reject_SideEffect --> [*] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation**: The state machine shows the *acceptance pipeline* for a candidate function. It must first pass determinism verification, then locality verification. Only upon passing both is the function labelled pure, after which all compositional reasoning rules become applicable. The `Reject_*` states are the failure exits and correspond to impure candidates. ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Purity vs. Impurity in Program Structure ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Pure["PURE PROGRAM CONTEXT"] P1["f1: pure function"] P2["f2: pure function"] P3["f3: pure function"] P1 -->|"x"| P2 P2 -->|"y"| P3 end subgraph Impure["IMPURE BOUNDARY (I/O WRAPPER)"] I1["main: impure driver"] I2["read input"] I3["print output"] I1 --> I2 I1 --> I3 end Impure -->|"invoke"| Pure Pure -->|"return"| Impure ``` > [!TIP] > **Architectural Insight**: The most successful industrial Haskell codebases (e.g., Facebook's Sigma, Galois's Cryptol) follow this *impure-shell, pure-core* architecture. The `main` function is the *only* impure construct, and it does no computation — it merely shuttles data into the pure core and prints the result. The pure core contains **all** business logic and is fully testable and verifiable. ### 4.4 Comparison Flowchart — Decision: Pure or Impure? ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Candidate function f"] Q1{"Is f deterministic?
(Same input -> same output)"} Q2{"Does f access external
mutable state?"} Q3{"Does f perform I/O,
throw, or call impure fns?"} Out1["PURE FUNCTION"] Out2["IMPURE FUNCTION"] Out3["IMPURE FUNCTION"] Out4["IMPURE FUNCTION"] Start --> Q1 Q1 -->|"Yes"| Q2 Q1 -->|"No"| Out2 Q2 -->|"No"| Q3 Q2 -->|"Yes"| Out3 Q3 -->|"No"| Out1 Q3 -->|"Yes"| Out4 ``` > [!WARNING] > **Exam Reading**: This flowchart is a high-yield visual for the KTU exam. A student can be asked: *"Classify the following functions as pure or impure, justifying your answer using the two-pillar test."* The flowchart is the canonical answer structure. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Set A]` **Q: Define a pure function. State and briefly justify the two properties that a function must satisfy to be considered pure.** **Model Answer (3 Marks)**: A **pure function** is a function whose return value is determined entirely by its input parameters, and which produces **no observable side effects** on the program's external state. The two essential properties are: 1. **Determinism**: For any given input $x$ from the function's domain $D$, the function $f$ must return exactly the same output $y$ every time it is called. Formally, $\forall x \in D, \forall y_1, y_2 \in C, (f(x) = y_1 \land f(x) = y_2) \Rightarrow y_1 = y_2$. 2. **Absence of Side Effects**: The evaluation of $f$ must not modify any external state, perform I/O, mutate global variables, or throw exceptions. The only observable change is the return value passed to the caller. Justification: These two properties together ensure **referential transparency**, which is the theoretical foundation for equational reasoning, substitution-based proofs, and compositional verification — all central to formal methods. > **Marking Scheme (3 marks)**: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for determinism, 1 mark for side-effect freedom. --- #### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Exam]` **Q: With a suitable example, explain the concept of referential transparency. How is it related to pure functions?** **Model Answer (3 Marks)**: **Referential Transparency (RT)** is the property of an expression that allows it to be replaced by its value without changing the program's observable behaviour. If $e$ is a referentially transparent expression, then for any surrounding context $C[\cdot]$: $$C[e] \equiv C[\text{val}(e)]$$ **Example**: The expression `2 + 3` is referentially transparent — it can always be replaced by its value `5` anywhere in a program. The expression `Date.now()` is **not** referentially transparent — replacing it with a specific timestamp would change the program's behaviour on subsequent calls. **Relation to Pure Functions**: A function $f$ is pure if and only if every application $f(x)$ is referentially transparent. This is a logically equivalent condition, as proven by the theorem in Section 3.1. > **Marking Scheme (3 marks)**: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for relation to purity. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Pattern**: Each Part B question carries 14 marks, has an internal choice, and is split into two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. Below we present TWO alternative 14-mark questions for the same topic. The student may answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. --- #### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main Exam, Module 4]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Define a pure function rigorously. State and prove the theorem that establishes the equivalence between purity and referential transparency. **(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the following Haskell-like function: ``` f x y = if x > 0 then x * y + g(x - 1) else y g n = n * n + 1 ``` For the functions `f` and `g`: (i) Identify whether each is pure or impure, with full justification (2 marks). (ii) Show, using $\beta$-reduction, that `f 2 3` evaluates to a specific value, step by step (3 marks). (iii) Identify one real-world engineering domain where functions of this kind are critical, and explain why (2 marks). **Model Solution**: **(a) Solution (7 marks)**: **Definition (2 marks)**: A pure function is a total mapping $f: D \to C$ such that: - For every $x \in D$, the output $f(x)$ is uniquely determined. - Evaluation of $f(x)$ produces no observable side effect on any state outside $f$'s local scope. **Theorem (2 marks)**: A function $f$ is pure if and only if every application $f(x)$ is referentially transparent. **Proof (3 marks)**: *Forward ($\Rightarrow$)*: If $f$ is pure, then $f(x)$ depends only on $x$ (by determinism) and has no side effects (by locality). For any context $C[\cdot]$, substituting $f(x)$ with its value changes nothing observable, so $C[f(x)] \equiv C[\text{val}(f(x))]$. Hence $f(x)$ is referentially transparent. *Reverse ($\Leftarrow$)*: Suppose $f$ is not pure. Either (i) $f$ is non-deterministic, in which case $C[\cdot] = (\text{let } y = [\cdot] \text{ in } y + y)$ yields inconsistent results, contradicting RT; or (ii) $f$ has a side effect, in which case $C[\cdot]$ that observes the side effect cannot be equal to $C[\text{val}(f(x))]$ which loses the side effect, again contradicting RT. Hence $f$ must be pure. **(b) Solution (7 marks)**: **(i) Purity Analysis (2 marks)**: - `g n = n * n + 1`: **Pure**. It depends only on its argument `n`, uses only arithmetic (deterministic), and has no I/O, no global state access, and no side effects. - `f x y = if x > 0 then x * y + g(x - 1) else y`: **Pure**. Both branches depend only on the arguments `x` and `y`, the comparison and arithmetic are deterministic, and the call to `g` is to a pure function. The conditional is deterministic. Hence `f` is pure by compositional closure. **(ii) $\beta$-Reduction of `f 2 3` (3 marks)**: ``` f 2 3 = if 2 > 0 then 2 * 3 + g(2 - 1) else 3 = if True then 2 * 3 + g(1) else 3 = 2 * 3 + g(1) = 6 + g(1) = 6 + (1 * 1 + 1) [β-reduction of g(1)] = 6 + (1 + 1) = 6 + 2 = 8 ``` Final result: `f 2 3 = 8`. **(iii) Real-World Domain (2 marks)**: Pure recursive functions of this kind are critical in **cryptographic primitives** (e.g., recursive Feistel cipher rounds), **compiler optimization passes** (e.g., constant folding of arithmetic expressions), and **symbolic execution engines** (e.g., evaluating arithmetic expressions over symbolic inputs). They are critical because their **determinism and locality** allow for exhaustive verification, reproducibility, and safe parallelization. --- #### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Exam, Module 4]` **(a) [7 Marks]**: With a worked example in VDM-SL, specify a pure function `power(b: nat, e: nat) r: nat` that computes $b^e$. Identify and justify: (i) The pre- and post-conditions (2 marks). (ii) The purity of the function using the two-pillar test (2 marks). (iii) The structural induction proof that `power(b, 0) = 1` and `power(b, e+1) = b * power(b, e)` (3 marks). **(b) [7 Marks]**: Discuss the role of pure functions in **functional programming** and **formal specification** languages. Compare VDM-SL, Z, and Haskell with respect to how they enforce or encourage purity. Provide at least one example per language. **Model Solution**: **(a) Solution (7 marks)**: **VDM-SL Specification**: ``` power: nat * nat -> nat power(b, e) == if e = 0 then 1 else b * power(b, e - 1) pre true post RESULT = product {b, b, ..., b} (e times) ``` **(i) Pre- and Post-conditions (2 marks)**: - **Pre**: `pre true` indicates that `power` is total over `nat * nat`. The function is defined for all non-negative exponents, including the degenerate case `e = 0` which returns 1. - **Post**: `RESULT = product {b, b, ..., b} (e times)` mathematically captures the notion of exponentiation. It is expressed in terms of the inputs and the result, with no reference to external state — a strong indicator of purity. **(ii) Purity via the Two-Pillar Test (2 marks)**: - **Determinism**: The function performs only arithmetic and recursive descent, both deterministic. Given any pair $(b, e) \in \mathbb{N} \times \mathbb{N}$, the result is unique. - **Side-Effect Freedom**: The function reads only its arguments `b` and `e`, and writes only to the local result variable `RESULT`. No global state, no I/O, no exceptions. Therefore, `power` is **pure**. **(iii) Structural Induction Proof (3 marks)**: - **Base Case** $e = 0$: `power(b, 0) = if 0 = 0 then 1 else ... = 1`. ✓ - **Inductive Step**: Assume `power(b, e) = b^e`. Then: `power(b, e+1) = if (e+1) = 0 then 1 else b * power(b, (e+1) - 1)` `= b * power(b, e)` [by definition of the function and the fact that $e+1 \neq 0$ for $e \in \mathbb{N}$] `= b * b^e` [by inductive hypothesis] `= b^(e+1)`. ✓ - **Conclusion**: By induction, `power(b, e) = b^e` for all $b, e \in \mathbb{N}$. **(b) Solution (7 marks)**: **Role of Pure Functions in Functional Programming and Formal Specification (3 marks)**: Pure functions serve as the **fundamental unit of reasoning** in both paradigms. In functional programming, they are the *only* units of computation — no assignment, no mutable state. This enables direct equational reasoning: a program is a set of equations that can be solved, simplified, and verified by substitution. In formal specification, pure functions are the *axioms* from which operations, schemas, and refinement proofs are built. The cleanliness of pure functions is what makes model checking, theorem proving, and property-based testing tractable. **Comparison of VDM-SL, Z, and Haskell (4 marks)**: | Language | Enforcement of Purity | Example | |---|---|---| | **VDM-SL** | Purity is a **discipline** — not enforced by the language. The specifier must explicitly check determinism and locality. Tools like VDMTools can assist via the `externals` clause (must be empty for purity). | `square(x: nat) r: nat`
`post r = x * x` | | **Z Notation** | Purity is also a **discipline**, but Z's schema calculus makes it *visually* obvious: a function schema has no $\Delta$ (state-change) decoration. The absence of $\Delta$ is the syntactic marker of a pure function. | `Square == [x?, y! : \nat \mid y! = x? * x?]` | | **Haskell** | Purity is **enforced by the type system** at compile time. The `IO` monad is the *only* escape hatch; any function with an `IO` type is statically flagged. This makes purity the default, not the exception. | `square :: Int -> Int`
`square x = x * x` | **Comparative Insight**: Haskell provides the *strongest* enforcement; VDM-SL and Z provide *lightweight syntactic conventions* that encourage but do not mandate purity. In industry, the trade-off is between Haskell's steep learning curve and VDM-SL/Z's flexibility at the cost of weaker guarantees. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on Pure Function Questions**: > > 1. **Single-pillar answer** (2 marks lost): Stating only determinism **or** only side-effect freedom, but not both. The KTU 2024 marking key *requires both pillars* explicitly mentioned. > 2. **Confusing partiality with impurity** (2 marks lost): Saying a function is "impure" because it is undefined for negative inputs. This is *partiality*, a separate concept addressed by preconditions, not impurity. > 3. **Forgetting the postcondition** (1 mark lost): In VDM-SL specifications, the `post` clause is mandatory for full marks. A specification without it is incomplete. > 4. **Skipping the $\beta$-reduction steps** (1 mark lost): For Haskell questions, students often write the final answer without showing the intermediate $\beta$-reductions. Each substitution step carries weight. > 5. **Not relating to referential transparency** (1 mark lost): The single most important *theoretical* connection in Module 4. Students who mention only "no side effects" without tying to RT are missing the central thesis. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Pure Function = Determinism + No Side Effects**.
