Both pillars are mandatory for full marks in KTU exams. - **Referential Transparency** is the *theoretical* counterpart of purity; they are logically equivalent by a constructive proof. - **Mathematical Form**: $f: D \to C$ — a total mapping from domain to codomain. - **Lambda Calculus Foundations**: $\beta$-reduction and $\eta$-reduction are the mechanical reasoning tools. Practice both. - **VDM-SL Purity Markers**: empty `externals` clause, `pre true`, and a `post` clause that references only inputs and `RESULT`. - **Z Notation Purity Marker**: absence of the $\Delta$ (state-change) decoration in the schema. - **Haskell Purity**: enforced by the type system; `IO` monad is the only impurity channel. - **Real-World Domains**: cryptography, aerospace DO-178C, distributed systems (Spark), build systems (Nix), database query optimizers, symbolic execution. - **Map Fusion Law**: $f \circ \text{map}(g) = \text{map}(f \circ g)$ — the canonical equational reasoning example. - **Compositional Closure**: The composition of two pure functions is pure. Use this in proofs. - **Architectural Pattern**: Impure-shell / pure-core. The `main` function is impure; everything else is pure. - **Common Confusions to Avoid**: Partial $\neq$ Impure. Total $\neq$ Pure. Determinism alone is not enough. Side-effect freedom alone is not enough. - **High-Yield Exam Topics** (from KTU 2024 model papers): purity definition, RT theorem, VDM-SL specification, $\beta$-reduction, map fusion, architectural role.

topics.title: Loop Invariants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST741 - FORMAL METHODS IN SOFTWARE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Loop Invariants # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Loop Invariant (Formal Definition)** > A **Loop Invariant** is a predicate (a logical assertion) $P$ that holds true **before** the loop begins (initialization) AND is **preserved** by every iteration of the loop body (consecution). Upon loop termination, the invariant $P$ (combined with the loop's negation condition) is used to formally derive the postcondition $Q$ of the entire loop construct. In Hoare Logic notation, a loop with invariant $P$ and condition $B$ satisfies the following rule: $$\frac{\{P \land B\}\ \text{loop body}\ \{P\}}{\{P\}\ \textbf{while}\ B\ \textbf{do}\ \text{loop body}\ \{P \land \neg B\}}$$ The invariant is, by definition, the **only thing that remains true throughout the entire execution** of the loop — from start to finish. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Analogy: The Elevator Floor Counter** > Imagine a person riding an elevator from the ground floor (0) to the 5th floor. The elevator is the loop. After each "iteration" (each floor passed), the floor counter changes. The **loop invariant** is the statement: *"At any moment, the number of floors climbed so far + the number of floors remaining = 5."* > > - It was true before the elevator started (0 climbed + 5 remaining = 5). > - It remains true after every floor change (1 climbed + 4 remaining = 5, 2 climbed + 3 remaining = 5, etc.). > - When the loop terminates (elevator reaches floor 5), the invariant combined with the termination condition (no floors remaining) gives the conclusion: *"All 5 floors have been climbed."* The **loop invariant is the property that links the loop's beginning to its end**, surviving every iteration unscathed. ## 1.3 The Three Fundamental Properties of a Loop Invariant A valid loop invariant $P$ must satisfy these three conditions: | Property | Description | Hoare Logic Notation | |---|---|---| | **Initialization** | $P$ is true just before the loop's first iteration. | $\{I \land B\} \Rightarrow \text{true}$ before entry | | **Maintenance (Consecution)** | If $P$ is true at the start of an iteration, it remains true at the end. | $\{P \land B\}\ S\ \{P\}$ | | **Termination** | When the loop exits, $P \land \neg B$ implies the desired postcondition $Q$. | $(P \land \neg B) \Rightarrow Q$ | > [!TIP] > **KTU Quick Memory Trick:** Think **I-M-T** = **I**nitialization, **M**aintenance, **T**ermination. These three are the cornerstone of any KTU question on loop invariants. ## 1.4 Loop Invariants in the Context of Formal Methods In the **KTU 2024 PECST741 syllabus**, loop invariants fall under *Program Verification using Axiomatic Semantics* (Hoare Logic). They form the bridge between: - **Precondition** (what must be true before the loop) - **Postcondition** (what must be true after the loop) The invariant is the **engine** that drives the deductive proof of partial and total correctness. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Theoretical Role of Loop Invariants in Hoare Logic A loop introduces **non-determinism in the number of iterations** — we do not always know in advance how many times the body will execute. The loop invariant abstracts away this uncertainty by expressing what is **common to all reachable states**, regardless of iteration count. ### 2.1.1 Operational Breakdown 1. **Initial State Setup:** Before the loop, an assignment or sequence of statements establishes the invariant $P$. 2. **Iterative Verification:** For each iteration where the guard $B$ holds, executing the loop body $S$ must transform $P \land B$ back into $P$. 3. **Exit Analysis:** When $B$ becomes false, the conjunction $P \land \neg B$ is used to logically derive the postcondition $Q$. ### 2.1.2 Partial vs. Total Correctness - **Partial Correctness:** $\{P\}\ S\ \{Q\}$ — assumes the program terminates; loop invariant is sufficient. - **Total Correctness:** $[P]\ S\ [Q]$ — requires proof of termination. This introduces the **Variant Function (Bound Function)** $t$ — a strictly decreasing natural number that proves the loop must eventually exit. ## 2.2 The Variant Function (Termination Metric) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Variant Function $t$** — A well-founded expression over the loop variables that strictly decreases (but stays $\geq 0$) with every iteration. Proves total correctness. For total correctness, the Hoare rule becomes: $$\frac{\{P \land B \land t = T\}\ S\ \{P \land t < T\}}{\{P\}\ \textbf{while}\ B\ \textbf{do}\ S\ \{P \land \neg B\}}$$ where $T$ is a fresh logical variable capturing the value of $t$ at the start of the iteration. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Invariant Preservation** | $\{P \land B\}\ S\ \{P\}$ | Maintains truth across iterations | | **Initialization** | $P$ must hold before first iteration | Establishes base case | | **Termination Postcondition** | $(P \land \neg B) \Rightarrow Q$ | Derives the postcondition | | **Variant Function** | $t : \text{State} \rightarrow \mathbb{N}$, $t$ strictly decreases | Proves termination | | **Loop Rule (Hoare)** | $\dfrac{\{P \land B\}\ S\ \{P\}}{\{P\}\ \textbf{while}\ B\ \textbf{do}\ S\ \{P \land \neg B\}}$ | Axiomatic semantics of while loop | | **Loop Rule (Dijkstra WPC)** | $wp(\textbf{while}\ B\ \textbf{do}\ S,\ Q) \equiv P$ where $P \equiv (B \land wp(S, P)) \lor (\neg B \land Q)$ | Weakest precondition calculus | | **Variant Bound** | $t \in \mathbb{N}$ and $t \geq 0$ always | Ensures finite decrease | | **Decrease Condition** | $t_{new} < t_{old}$ | Strictly decreasing metric | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse **strict decrease** with mere change. A variant function must *strictly* decrease in a well-founded order — this guarantees no infinite loops. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Loop invariants are indispensable in: - **Safety-Critical Systems** (Avionics, Medical Devices): Where code must be mathematically proven correct before deployment. - **Compiler Optimization Verification:** Proving that loop transformations (loop unrolling, fusion) preserve program semantics. - **Model Checking & Static Analysis Tools** (e.g., Dafny, Frama-C, SPARK Ada): Automated provers use invariants to verify code. - **Distributed Systems Verification:** Proving liveness and safety properties in concurrent algorithms. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Classic Example: Sum of First $n$ Natural Numbers ### 3.1.1 Program Under Verification $$ \begin{aligned} &\{\text{true}\} \\ &n \leftarrow N; \quad // N \text{ is a given non-negative integer} \\ &\text{sum} \leftarrow 0; \quad i \leftarrow 0; \\ &\textbf{while}\ i < n\ \textbf{do} \\ &\quad \text{sum} \leftarrow \text{sum} + i; \\ &\quad i \leftarrow i + 1; \\ &\textbf{end while} \\ &\{\text{sum} = 0 + 1 + 2 + \dots + (n-1) = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}\} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.1.2 Proposing the Loop Invariant > The invariant must capture the **partial progress** of the loop. After $i$ iterations, `sum` should equal the sum of integers from $0$ to $i-1$. $$ P:\ \text{sum} = \frac{i(i-1)}{2} \land 0 \leq i \leq n $$ ### 3.1.3 Proof of Initialization Before the loop: $i = 0$ and $\text{sum} = 0$. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Check } P: \quad \text{sum} &= \frac{i(i-1)}{2} \\ 0 &= \frac{0 \cdot (0-1)}{2} \\ 0 &= \frac{0 \cdot -1}{2} \\ 0 &= 0 \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ Also, $0 \leq 0 \leq n$ holds (since $N \geq 0$). **Initialization is verified.** ### 3.1.4 Proof of Maintenance (Consecution) **Assume:** $P$ holds at loop start, i.e., $\text{sum} = \frac{i(i-1)}{2} \land 0 \leq i < n$ (since guard $i < n$ is true). **Execute body:** $\text{sum}_{new} = \text{sum} + i$ and $i_{new} = i + 1$. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{sum}_{new} &= \text{sum} + i \\ &= \frac{i(i-1)}{2} + i \\ &= \frac{i(i-1) + 2i}{2} \\ &= \frac{i^2 - i + 2i}{2} \\ &= \frac{i^2 + i}{2} \\ &= \frac{i(i+1)}{2} \\ &= \frac{i_{new}(i_{new}-1)}{2} \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ Also, $0 \leq i_{new} = i+1 \leq n$ holds (since $i < n$ implies $i+1 \leq n$). **Maintenance is verified.** ### 3.1.5 Proof of Termination When the loop exits: $i \geq n$ (negation of guard $i < n$). From invariant: $0 \leq i \leq n$, and from exit condition: $i \geq n$. Therefore $i = n$. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{sum} &= \frac{n(n-1)}{2} \\ \text{sum} &= 0 + 1 + 2 + \dots + (n-1) \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.1.6 Proof of Total Correctness (Variant Function) **Variant function:** $t = n - i$. - Initially: $t = n - 0 = n \geq 0$. - After each iteration: $i_{new} = i + 1$, so $t_{new} = n - (i+1) = (n - i) - 1 < t$. - $t \in \mathbb{N}$ and strictly decreases, hence loop terminates. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.2 Example: Array Linear Search ### 3.2.1 Program Under Verification $$ \begin{aligned} &\{\text{true}\} \\ &i \leftarrow 0; \quad \text{found} \leftarrow \text{false}; \\ &\textbf{while}\ i < N \land \neg \text{found}\ \textbf{do} \\ &\quad \textbf{if}\ A[i] = \text{key}\ \textbf{then} \\ &\quad\quad \text{found} \leftarrow \text{true}; \\ &\quad \textbf{else} \\ &\quad\quad i \leftarrow i + 1; \\ &\quad \textbf{end if} \\ &\textbf{end while} \\ &\{(\text{found} \Rightarrow \exists j.\, 0 \leq j < N \land A[j] = \text{key}) \land (\neg \text{found} \Rightarrow \forall j.\, 0 \leq j < N \Rightarrow A[j] \neq \text{key})\} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2.2 Loop Invariant $$ P:\ 0 \leq i \leq N \land \text{found} \Rightarrow A[i] = \text{key} \land \forall j.\, 0 \leq j < i \Rightarrow A[j] \neq \text{key} $$ ### 3.2.3 Initialization Check $i = 0$, found = false. The condition $\forall j.\, 0 \leq j < 0 \Rightarrow A[j] \neq \text{key}$ is vacuously true. $0 \leq 0 \leq N$ holds. $\checkmark$ ### 3.2.4 Maintenance Check Two cases: - **Case A:** $A[i] = \text{key}$ → found becomes true. $A[i] = \text{key}$ and $\forall j < i,\ A[j] \neq \text{key}$ still holds (from invariant). - **Case B:** $A[i] \neq \text{key}$ → $i$ increments to $i+1$. Now $\forall j < i+1,\ A[j] \neq \text{key}$ includes the new $j = i$. $\checkmark$ ### 3.2.5 Termination Check Loop exits when $i \geq N$ or found = true. - If found = true: $A[i] = \text{key}$, postcondition satisfied. - If $i \geq N$: from invariant, $\forall j < N,\ A[j] \neq \text{key}$, postcondition satisfied. **Variant function:** $t = N - i$ (when not found). Strictly decreases. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.3 Example: Multiplication via Repeated Addition ### 3.3.1 Program and Invariant $$ \begin{aligned} &\{\text{true}\} \\ &\text{result} \leftarrow 0; \quad k \leftarrow 0; \\ &\textbf{while}\ k < m\ \textbf{do} \\ &\quad \text{result} \leftarrow \text{result} + n; \\ &\quad k \leftarrow k + 1; \\ &\textbf{end while} \\ &\{\text{result} = m \times n\} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.3.2 Invariant Proposal $$ P:\ \text{result} = k \times n \land 0 \leq k \leq m $$ ### 3.3.3 Initialization $k = 0$, $\text{result} = 0$. $0 = 0 \times n$ holds. $0 \leq 0 \leq m$ holds. $\checkmark$ ### 3.3.4 Maintenance $$ \begin{aligned} \text{result}_{new} &= \text{result} + n = k \times n + n = (k+1) \times n = k_{new} \times n \end{aligned} $$ $0 \leq k_{new} = k+1 \leq m$ holds (since $k < m$). $\checkmark$ ### 3.3.5 Termination Loop exits when $k \geq m$. Combined with $k \leq m$ (from invariant), we get $k = m$. So $\text{result} = m \times n$. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.4 Finding Loop Invariants — The Substitution Method A systematic technique taught in KTU: 1. **State the postcondition $Q$.** 2. **Replace constant terms with loop variables.** For example, if $Q$ says $\text{sum} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$, introduce $i$ (the loop counter) to get $\text{sum} = \frac{i(i-1)}{2}$. 3. **Add range constraints** based on the loop guard: $0 \leq i \leq n$. 4. **Verify** using initialization, maintenance, and termination. > [!TIP] > This is the **substitution heuristic** that examiners expect students to demonstrate in KTU Part B answers worth 14 marks. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Loop Invariant Verification Cycle The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the three-step verification cycle for a loop invariant $P$ with guard $B$ and body $S$. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Precondition holds] --> B[Step 1: Initialization
Establish P before first iteration] B --> C{Loop Guard B
holds?} C -- Yes --> D[Step 2: Maintenance
Execute body S] D --> E[P remains true after iteration] E --> C C -- No --> F[Step 3: Termination
Use P and NOT B] F --> G[Derive Postcondition Q] G --> H[End: Program verified] style A fill:#e1f5ff style B fill:#fff4e1 style D fill:#fff4e1 style F fill:#fff4e1 style G fill:#e8f5e9 style H fill:#e8f5e9 ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Loop Invariant Proof ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PROG[Program Segment] P1[Precondition
Initial assertions] --> L[While Loop] L --> P2[Postcondition
Final assertions] end subgraph INV[Invariant System] I1[Invariant P
sum = i*i-1/2
0 <= i <= n] --> I2[Maintenance Rule
P and B implies wp S P] I2 --> I3[Termination Rule
P and not B implies Q] end subgraph VAR[Termination Proof] V1[Variant Function t = n - i] --> V2[Strict Decrease
t_new < t] V2 --> V3[Well Founded
t in Natural Numbers] end PROG --> INV INV --> VAR VAR --> OUT[Total Correctness
Verified] style PROG fill:#e3f2fd style INV fill:#fff3e0 style VAR fill:#f3e5f5 style OUT fill:#c8e6c9 ``` ## 4.3 The I-M-T Verification Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD START([Propose Loop Invariant P]) --> I{Initialization
P true at entry?} I -- No --> FAIL1[Reject Invariant
Choose New P] I -- Yes --> M{Maintenance
P and B implies S preserves P?} M -- No --> FAIL2[Reject Invariant
Choose New P] M -- Yes --> T{Termination
P and NOT B implies Q?} T -- No --> FAIL3[Reject Invariant
Choose New P] T -- Yes --> VT{Variant Function t
strictly decreases?} VT -- No --> FAIL4[Partial Correctness Only] VT -- Yes --> DONE([Total Correctness Proved]) style START fill:#bbdefb style DONE fill:#a5d6a7 style FAIL1 fill:#ef9a9a style FAIL2 fill:#ef9a9a style FAIL3 fill:#ef9a9a style FAIL4 fill:#ffcc80 ``` ## 4.4 State Transition Diagram for Sum Loop Example ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> S0: i=0, sum=0 S0: i=0, sum=0
Invariant holds S0 --> S1: sum = sum + 0
i becomes 1 S1: i=1, sum=0 S1 --> S2: sum = sum + 1
i becomes 2 S2: i=2, sum=1 S2 --> S3: sum = sum + 2
i becomes 3 S3: i=3, sum=3 S3 --> Sn: continue until i=n Sn: i=n, sum = n*n-1/2 Sn --> [*]: Loop exits ``` ## 4.5 Loop Invariant Construction Heuristic Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Identify Postcondition Q] --> B[List Loop Variables x1 x2 ... xn] B --> C[Replace Constants in Q with Loop Variables] C --> D[Add Range Constraints from Loop Guard B] D --> E[Form Candidate Invariant P] E --> F{Verify IMT
Three Properties} F -- Pass --> G[Valid Invariant Found] F -- Fail --> H[Refine P
Weaken or Strengthen] H --> E style A fill:#e1f5ff style G fill:#c8e6c9 style H fill:#ffecb3 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define a loop invariant. State and briefly explain the three properties that a valid loop invariant must satisfy.** **Model Answer:** A **loop invariant** is an assertion $P$ that holds true at the start of every iteration of a loop — before the first iteration, and after every subsequent iteration. It is used in Hoare Logic to formally prove the correctness of iterative constructs. The three properties are: 1. **Initialization:** $P$ must be true just before the first iteration of the loop (when control first reaches the loop). 2. **Maintenance (Consecution):** If $P$ is true at the beginning of an iteration, and the loop guard $B$ holds, then executing the loop body $S$ must preserve $P$ — i.e., $\{P \land B\}\ S\ \{P\}$. 3. **Termination:** When the loop exits, the conjunction of $P$ and $\neg B$ must imply the desired postcondition $Q$. ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is a variant function in the context of total correctness of loops? Why is it necessary?** **Model Answer:** A **variant function** $t$ is an expression over the loop variables that maps program states to the natural numbers $\mathbb{N}$ and **strictly decreases** with every iteration of the loop. It is necessary to prove **total correctness** (i.e., that the loop must eventually terminate). Without a well-founded variant function, one can only claim **partial correctness** — that *if* the loop terminates, the postcondition holds. The strict decrease property, combined with $t \in \mathbb{N}$, prevents infinite loops by the well-ordering principle of natural numbers. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Consider the following program segment designed to compute the product of all elements in an array $A[0..N-1]$: $$ \begin{aligned} &\{\text{true}\} \\ &\text{prod} \leftarrow 1; \quad i \leftarrow 0; \\ &\textbf{while}\ i < N\ \textbf{do} \\ &\quad \text{prod} \leftarrow \text{prod} \times A[i]; \\ &\quad i \leftarrow i + 1; \\ &\textbf{end while} \\ &\{\text{prod} = \prod_{k=0}^{N-1} A[k]\} \end{aligned} $$ **Propose a suitable loop invariant and prove partial correctness** (initialization, maintenance, and termination). **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Explain how the **variant function** is used to prove total correctness of the above program. State the variant function and show that it strictly decreases. **[7 Marks]** --- #### Model Solution to Question A **Part (a) — Proposing the Invariant and Proving Partial Correctness [7 Marks]** **Proposed Invariant:** $$ P:\ \text{prod} = \prod_{k=0}^{i-1} A[k] \ \land\ 0 \leq i \leq N $$ **Step 1: Initialization [2 Marks]** Before the first iteration: $i = 0$, $\text{prod} = 1$. The product of zero terms is the empty product, which equals $1$. So: $$\text{prod} = \prod_{k=0}^{-1} A[k] = 1 \quad \checkmark$$ Range check: $0 \leq 0 \leq N$ holds. Initialization verified. **Step 2: Maintenance (Consecution) [3 Marks]** Assume $P$ holds at the start of an iteration, with guard $i < N$ true. So $0 \leq i < N$. After executing the body: $$\text{prod}_{new} = \text{prod} \times A[i]$$ Substituting from the invariant: $$\text{prod}_{new} = \left(\prod_{k=0}^{i-1} A[k]\right) \times A[i] = \prod_{k=0}^{i} A[k]$$ And $i_{new} = i + 1$, so: $$\text{prod}_{new} = \prod_{k=0}^{i_{new}-1} A[k]$$ Range check: $0 \leq i_{new} = i+1 \leq N$ holds (since $i < N$). Maintenance verified. **Step 3: Termination [2 Marks]** When the loop exits: $\neg (i < N)$, i.e., $i \geq N$. Combined with $i \leq N$ from $P$, we conclude $i = N$. Therefore: $$\text{prod} = \prod_{k=0}^{N-1} A[k] \quad \checkmark$$ Partial correctness established. $\blacksquare$ --- **Part (b) — Variant Function and Total Correctness [7 Marks]** **Variant Function:** $t = N - i$ **Properties to verify:** 1. **Non-negativity:** Since $0 \leq i \leq N$ (from invariant), $t = N - i \geq 0$. So $t \in \mathbb{N}$. [2 Marks] 2. **Strict Decrease:** After one iteration, $i_{new} = i + 1$. So: $$t_{new} = N - i_{new} = N - (i+1) = (N - i) - 1 = t - 1 < t \quad \checkmark$$ [3 Marks] 3. **Well-Foundedness:** Since $t$ is a natural number that strictly decreases, by the well-ordering principle of $\mathbb{N}$, $t$ cannot decrease indefinitely. Hence the loop must terminate after at most $t_0 = N - 0 = N$ iterations. [2 Marks] Total correctness is established. $\blacksquare$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Consider the following program that computes $y = x^3$ using only addition and multiplication by 2 (doubling): $$ \begin{aligned} &\{\text{true}\} \\ &y \leftarrow 0; \quad p \leftarrow x; \quad k \leftarrow 0; \\ &\textbf{while}\ k < 3\ \textbf{do} \\ &\quad y \leftarrow y + p; \\ &\quad p \leftarrow p + p; \quad // p \text{ doubles} \\ &\quad k \leftarrow k + 1; \\ &\textbf{end while} \\ &\{\,y = x^3\,\} \end{aligned} $$ **Identify a suitable loop invariant** and **prove partial correctness** by verifying all three properties. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Compare and contrast the use of loop invariants in **Hoare Logic** versus **Dijkstra's Weakest Precondition (wp) Calculus**. Use the while-loop rule in both frameworks. **[7 Marks]** --- #### Model Solution to Question B **Part (a) — Loop Invariant and Partial Correctness [7 Marks]** **Proposed Invariant:** After $k$ iterations, $y$ should equal $x \cdot 2^0 + x \cdot 2^1 + \dots + x \cdot 2^{k-1} = x(2^k - 1)$, and $p = x \cdot 2^k$. $$P:\ y = x \cdot (2^k - 1) \ \land\ p = x \cdot 2^k \ \land\ 0 \leq k \leq 3$$ **Step 1: Initialization [2 Marks]** $k = 0$, $y = 0$, $p = x$. - $y = x(2^0 - 1) = x(1-1) = 0$ $\checkmark$ - $p = x \cdot 2^0 = x$ $\checkmark$ - $0 \leq 0 \leq 3$ $\checkmark$ **Step 2: Maintenance [3 Marks]** Assume $P$ holds and guard $k < 3$ is true. After the body: $$y_{new} = y + p = x(2^k - 1) + x \cdot 2^k = x(2^k - 1 + 2^k) = x(2^{k+1} - 1)$$ $$p_{new} = p + p = 2 \cdot x \cdot 2^k = x \cdot 2^{k+1}$$ $$k_{new} = k + 1$$ So $P$ holds with $k_{new} = k+1$. Range: $0 \leq k+1 \leq 3$ (since $k < 3$). $\checkmark$ **Step 3: Termination [2 Marks]** Loop exits when $k \geq 3$. Combined with $k \leq 3$, we get $k = 3$. So: $$y = x(2^3 - 1) = x \cdot 7$$ Wait — this gives $y = 7x$, not $x^3$. **The invariant is incorrect.** Let's revise. **Revised Invariant:** $y = \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} x \cdot 2^i$ and $p = x \cdot 2^k$. Same as before: $y = x(2^k - 1)$. For $x^3$, the algorithm above is **incorrect** for general $x$. (It computes $x \cdot 7$ when $x$ itself is added, then double of $x$, then double of double of $x$.) A correct $x^3$ program needs a nested loop. We will assume the postcondition has been corrected to $\{y = x(2^k - 1) \text{ with } k = 3\}$, yielding $y = 7x$. **Final answer:** With $k = 3$, $y = x(2^3 - 1) = 7x$. $\blacksquare$ > [!NOTE] > *Examiner's note: Students who blindly wrote $y = x^3$ without verifying the algorithm would lose marks. The verification process itself surfaces such errors — this is the pedagogical value of loop invariants.* --- **Part (b) — Hoare Logic vs. wp Calculus [7 Marks]** | Aspect | Hoare Logic | Dijkstra's wp Calculus | |---|---|---| | **Notation** | Hoare triple $\{P\}\ S\ \{Q\}$ | $wp(S, Q)$ — the weakest precondition | | **Direction of Reasoning** | Forward: from $P$ derive $Q$ | Backward: from $Q$ compute minimal $P$ | | **Loop Rule** | $\dfrac{\{P \land B\}\ S\ \{P\}}{\{P\}\ \textbf{while}\ B\ \textbf{do}\ S\ \{P \land \neg B\}}$ | $wp(\textbf{while}\ B\ \textbf{do}\ S,\ Q) \equiv \exists k.\, P_k$ where $P_k$ is defined inductively, OR equivalently the invariant $P$ satisfies $P \equiv (B \land wp(S,P)) \lor (\neg B \land Q)$ | | **Role of Invariant** | User must *propose* a candidate invariant $P$ that works | The invariant emerges naturally as a fixed point of the equation $P = (B \land wp(S, P)) \lor (\neg B \land Q)$ | | **Discharge of Verification Condition** | Manual or semi-automated | Often fully automated via theorem provers | | **Strength** | Intuitive, matches human reasoning style | Algorithmic, mechanizable | **Key Insight [3 Marks]:** Both frameworks rely on the same logical core — the loop invariant $P$ that survives every iteration. The difference is purely methodological: Hoare Logic is *goal-oriented but requires user ingenuity* to find $P$; wp calculus is *prescription-oriented* and can in principle compute the strongest such $P$ automatically. In practice, both still need human insight to discover the right invariant, especially for complex loops. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Loop Invariant Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting Range Constraints** — Invariants like `sum = i(i-1)/2` alone are incomplete. You MUST also include the range `$0 \leq i \leq n$`. Examiners deduct **1-2 marks** for missing bounds. > > 2. **Conflating Strict Decrease with Non-Increase** — A variant function $t$ must satisfy $t_{new} < t$, NOT $t_{new} \leq t$. Writing $\leq$ instead of $<$ is a guaranteed 1-mark loss. > > 3. **Skipping the Termination Step** — Many students prove initialization and maintenance but forget to show $P \land \neg B \Rightarrow Q$. This is the most common **2-3 mark** deduction. > > 4. **Misidentifying the Invariant** — Writing `sum = 0 + 1 + ... + n` (the postcondition) as the invariant is **incorrect** — that is the postcondition, not the loop invariant. The invariant must involve the loop counter. > > 5. **Failing to State I-M-T Explicitly** — Examiners reward students who label each proof step as "Initialization," "Maintenance," and "Termination." Structure matters for partial credit. > > 6. **Confusing Partial and Total Correctness** — Partial correctness (no termination) is worth fewer marks. Always include the variant function for full 14-mark questions. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition (Must Memorize):** A loop invariant is a predicate $P$ true before and after every loop iteration. - **I-M-T Properties:** **I**nitialization, **M**aintenance, **T**ermination — these are the three pillars of any invariant proof. - **Hoare Triple for While:** $\{P\}\ \textbf{while}\ B\ \textbf{do}\ S\ \{P \land \neg B\}$ — remember the invariant is the loop's "soul." - **Variant Function $t$:** A well-founded, strictly decreasing natural number — required for **total correctness** proofs. - **Construction Heuristic:** Start from postcondition $Q$, replace constants with loop variables, add range constraints. - **Verification Conditions (VCs):** $P \Rightarrow wp(S, P)$ for maintenance, and $P \land \neg B \Rightarrow Q$ for termination. - **Empty Product / Empty Sum:** Useful initialization sanity checks ($\prod_{k=0}^{-1} = 1$, $\sum_{k=0}^{-1} = 0$). - **Algorithmic vs. Verification:** Loop invariants are the *backbone* of automated verifiers like Dafny, SPARK Ada, and Frama-C. - **Partial vs. Total:** Partial = Hoare triple (no termination proof). Total = includes variant function. - **Common Exam Traps:** Forgetting range bounds, miswriting strict decrease, confusing invariant with postcondition. - **Engineering Relevance:** Safety-critical systems (aerospace, medical), compiler optimization validation, distributed algorithm proofs.

topics.title: Proving Total Correctness of Programs in VCC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST741 - FORMAL METHODS IN SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Proving Total Correctness of Programs in VCC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Correctness of a Program** is a property asserting that **for every legal input, the program terminates** AND **produces an output satisfying the post-condition**. Formally, for a program $P$ with precondition $Q$ and postcondition $R$, total correctness is the Hoare triple $\vdash \{Q\}\ P\ \{\text{true}\}$ combined with the proof of termination, i.e., the *well-founded* ranking of the loop-variant.

In the context of **VCC (Verified C Compiler)** — a deductive verification tool developed at **Microsoft Research** for annotated ISO C code — total correctness is established by combining two verifications:

$$
\text{Total Correctness} \;\equiv\; \underbrace{\{Q\}\ P\ \{R\}}_{\text{Partial Correctness}} \;\;\land\;\; \underbrace{\Downarrow P}_{\text{Termination}}
$$

Where the termination arrow $\Downarrow P$ means *“program $P$ is guaranteed to terminate on all inputs satisfying $Q$.”*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Bakery Receipt System** 🧾
> Imagine a bakery where the cashier (the program) must give a customer a receipt (postcondition) AND eventually close the register (terminate).
> - **Partial Correctness** is the promise: *“If the cashier ever finishes, the receipt will be correct.”*
> - **Termination** is the proof: *“The cashier has a finite queue and a non-decreasing will to work — they will definitely finish.”*
> - **Total Correctness** is the union: *“The cashier will definitely finish, and when they do, the receipt will be correct.”*

**VCC** acts like an automated auditor that inspects every line of C code, checking both *“will the receipts ever be wrong?”* and *“will the cashier ever freeze at the register forever?”*

### 1.3 Key VCC Vocabulary (Bolded Core Metrics)

| Term | Symbol / Annotation | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Precondition | `requires P` | Contract that must hold on entry |
| Postcondition | `ensures Q` | Contract that must hold on exit |
| Loop invariant | `invariant I` | Holds at every iteration boundary |
| Termination measure | `decreases e` | Expression strictly decreasing in a well-founded order |
| Ownership | `owns(x)` | Thread exclusively owns mutable state |
| Admissibility | `admissibility(p)` | Predicate that *may* eventually become true |
| Volatility | `volatile` | Marked as observable by other threads |

> [!TIP]
> The `decreases` clause is VCC's **only mechanism** to encode termination — without it, VCC only proves *partial* correctness.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Decomposition of Total Correctness in VCC

A VCC program is a triple $\langle C, \text{Contracts}, \text{Ranking} \rangle$ where:

1. **C** — annotated ISO C99 source.
2. **Contracts** — a set of `requires`, `ensures`, `invariant` clauses forming a *Hoare-style proof outline*.
3. **Ranking** — a well-founded measure used in every cycle of the control-flow graph (loop / recursion).

The **Why** and **How** behind each step:

- **Why preconditions?** They model the environment's guarantee. Without them, the verifier must assume the worst-case input and may flag false positives.
- **How does VCC encode well-foundedness?** It accepts any tuple whose components are drawn from VCC's built-in well-founded lexicographic order $\prec_{\text{lex}}$ over signed integers, with the bottom element $-\infty$.
- **Why a *decreases* expression and not just a timeout?** A timeout proves *boundedness* but not termination. The `decreases` expression mathematically guarantees *eventual* termination under infinite time.

### 2.2 The Two Pillars of Total Correctness in VCC

#### Pillar 1: Partial Correctness (Established via Contracts)

$$
\frac{\{Q\}\ S\ \{R\}}{\text{Verified by Boogie/Z3 SMT solver}}
$$

- **Forward reasoning** at each statement using **weakest precondition** computation.
- VCC translates the C program into **Boogie** (an intermediate verification language), which generates verification conditions (VCs) discharged by the **Z3** SMT solver.

#### Pillar 2: Termination (Established via the `decreases` Clause)

$$
\frac{\forall \vec{v}\ \bigl(\text{pre}(S) \Rightarrow \text{decreases}(\vec{e}) \land \text{rank}_{\text{wf}}(\vec{e})\bigr)}{\Downarrow S}
$$

The verifier enforces:

$$
\text{rank}(e_{\text{new}}) \prec_{\text{wf}} \text{rank}(e_{\text{old}})
$$

at every loop head, with the well-founded order being the default $\mathbb{Z}$ order restricted to non-positive monotone descent (i.e., $e$ must strictly decrease in every iteration and remain bounded below).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Construct | Syntax | Verifies | Required For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Precondition | `requires P` | Entry obligation | Partial correctness |
| 2 | Postcondition | `ensures Q` | Exit obligation | Partial correctness |
| 3 | Loop Invariant | `invariant I` | Holds at head & exit of loop | Loop partial correctness |
| 4 | Termination | `decreases e` | Strictly decreasing tuple | Total correctness |
| 5 | Ownership | `_(requires, ensures) \owns(x)` | Exclusive write right | Concurrency |
| 6 | Admissibility | `_(ghost \text{ bool } p; \text{ admissibility}(p);)` | Non-blocking future read | Concurrent reads |
| 7 | Volatile frame | `volatile \vert x;` | Fresh value per read | Cross-thread observability |
| 8 | Ghost code | `_(ghost \text{ ... } \text{ )}` | Auxiliary state for proof | Decoupling proof & code |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A loop is **not** total-correct unless both `invariant` and `decreases` are present. Marks are split roughly **70% partial / 30% termination** by VCC's default VC generator.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | How VCC Total Correctness Helps |
|---|---|
| **Avionics (DO-178C Level A)** | Replaces costly model-checking by per-function proof |
| **Hypervisor / OS Kernels** | Microsoft Hyper-V's `xhv` interface was verified in VCC |
| **Cryptographic Libraries** | Proves absence of integer overflow side-channels |
| **Medical Device Firmware** | Establishes FDA-grade evidence of no infinite loop |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Canonical Worked Example — Integer Square Root

We now exhaustively prove total correctness of the integer square-root program. Each step is fully written out.

**The Program (annotated in VCC C):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Function} \; & \text{isqrt}(\text{int } n) \\
\text{Requires:} \quad & n \geq 0 \\
\text{Ensures:} \quad & \text{result}^2 \leq n \;<\; (\text{result}+1)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Annotated C:

```c
int isqrt(int n)
  _(requires n >= 0)
  _(ensures \result * \result <= n && n < (\result + 1) * (\result + 1))
{
  int r = 0;
  int s = 1;
  while (s <= n) {
    _(invariant r*r <= n)
    _(invariant s == (r+1)*(r+1))
    _(invariant s > 0)
    _(decreases n - r)
    r = r + 1;
    s = s + 2*r + 1;
  }
  return r;
}
```

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Total Correctness

We shall prove:

$$
\{n \geq 0\}\ \text{isqrt}(n)\ \{\text{result}^2 \leq n < (\text{result}+1)^2\}
\;\;\land\;\; \Downarrow \text{isqrt}(n)
$$

#### Step 1 — Precondition ingestion

At the function entry, the verifier assumes $n \geq 0$ (the precondition). It then instantiates the local variable axioms:
$r = 0$, $s = 1$.

**Logic row:** The Boogie translator emits the verification condition

$$
\text{VC}_0 \;:\; n \geq 0 \;\land\; r = 0 \;\land\; s = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; I_0
$$

where $I_0$ is the *loop invariant at entry*, namely the conjunction

$$
I_0 \;\equiv\; (r \cdot r \leq n) \;\land\; (s = (r+1)(r+1)) \;\land\; (s > 0)
$$

#### Step 2 — Invariant preservation

Assume the loop guard $s \leq n$ holds, and assume $I$ holds from the previous iteration. The body updates:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{\text{new}} & = r + 1 \\
s_{\text{new}} & = s + 2 r_{\text{new}} = s + 2(r+1)
\end{aligned}
$$

The verifier discharges the obligation

$$
\text{VC}_1 \;:\; I \;\land\; s \leq n \;\land\; r_{\text{new}} = r+1 \;\land\; s_{\text{new}} = s + 2 r_{\text{new}} \;\Rightarrow\; I_{\text{new}}
$$

Substituting $I_{\text{new}} = I$ (since invariants are preserved):

$$
r_{\text{new}} \cdot r_{\text{new}} \leq n \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad (r+1)^2 \leq n
$$

Using $s \leq n$ and $s = (r+1)^2$ from $I$, we obtain $(r+1)^2 = s \leq n$, hence preserved.

#### Step 3 — Decreases obligation (Termination)

The chosen ranking expression is $n - r$. At every iteration:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(n - r)_{\text{new}} & = n - (r + 1) = (n - r) - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence the strict descent:

$$
(n - r)_{\text{new}} \;<\; (n - r)_{\text{old}}
$$

Furthermore, the invariant $s = (r+1)^2$ combined with $s > 0$ and the precondition $n \geq 0$ ensures

$$
n - r \;\geq\; 0
$$

at every loop head. Therefore the well-founded order $\langle \mathbb{Z}, < \rangle$ is respected and the loop is **terminating** (no infinite descent possible below $0$).

The VCC compiler emits:

$$
\text{VC}_2 \;:\; I \;\land\; s \leq n \;\Rightarrow\; (n - (r+1)) < (n - r) \;\land\; 0 \leq (n - r)
$$

#### Step 4 — Postcondition on exit

When the loop exits, $\neg(s \leq n)$, i.e., $s > n$. Combined with invariant $s = (r+1)^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(r+1)^2 & = s > n
\end{aligned}
$$

And from $s = (r+1)^2$ plus the fact that before this iteration we had $s_{\text{old}} \leq n$, the prior invariant tells us $r^2 \leq n$. Hence

$$
r^2 \leq n < (r+1)^2
$$

which is exactly the postcondition with $\text{result} = r$. QED.

### 3.3 Analytical Verification Matrix

| Verification Condition | Hypothesis | Goal | Solver Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| VC₀ (entry invariant) | $n \geq 0$, $r=0$, $s=1$ | $I_0$ | ✅ Z3 (arithmetic) |
| VC₁ (preservation) | $I$, $s \leq n$ | $I_{\text{new}}$ | ✅ Z3 (nonlinear arith) |
| VC₂ (termination) | $I$, $s \leq n$ | $n - r' < n - r \land n - r \geq 0$ | ✅ Z3 (linear arith) |
| VC₃ (postcondition) | $I$, $s > n$ | $r^2 \leq n < (r+1)^2$ | ✅ Z3 (nonlinear arith) |

> [!TIP]
> Note the **nonlinear arithmetic** sub-problems — they are the *hardest* for SMT solvers. VCC handles them by quantifier instantiation and pattern matching; in KTU exam derivations, students should be able to show each polynomial inequality by hand.

### 3.4 Termination for Recursive Functions

For recursion, VCC requires a `decreases` clause on the function header itself. The obligation is:

$$
\forall \vec{x}_{\text{caller}} : \text{rank}(\vec{x}_{\text{recursive call}}) \prec_{\text{wf}} \text{rank}(\vec{x}_{\text{current}})
$$

**Example — Euclidean GCD:**

```c
int gcd(int a, int b)
  _(requires a > 0 && b > 0)
  _(ensures \result > 0 && a % \result == 0 && b % \result == 0)
  _(decreases a + b)
{
  if (b == 0) return a;
  return gcd(b, a % b);
}
```

The verifier must show $b + (a \bmod b) < a + b$ at the recursive call. Since $0 \leq a \bmod b < b \leq a + b$, the descent is immediate.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 VCC Total-Correctness Verification Pipeline (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[C Source with Annotations] --> B[Front-End Parser]
    B --> C[Boogie IR Generation]
    C --> D[VC Generation via Weakest Precondition]
    D --> E[Z3 SMT Solver]
    E --> F{All VCs Valid?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Total Correctness Certified]
    F -- No  --> H[Counterexample Trace]
    H --> I[Developer Fixes Annotations / Code]
    I --> A
    D --> D1[Contract VCs partial]
    D --> D2[Invariant VCs loop]
    D --> D3[Decreases VCs termination]
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
```

### 4.2 Loop-Level Decomposition (Per-Iteration Obligations)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L0[Entry: requires P] --> L1[Loop Head: check invariant I]
    L1 --> L2{Guard G true?}
    L2 -- Yes --> L3[Body Exec]
    L3 --> L4[decreases e check strict]
    L4 --> L5[invariant I re-established]
    L5 --> L1
    L2 -- No --> L6[Exit: ensures Q from I and not G]
    L6 --> L7[Postcondition Verified]
```

### 4.3 Ownership-Admissibility-Decreases Triangle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph TRI[Total Correctness Pillars]
        O[Ownership  owns x]
        A[Admissibility admissibility p]
        D[Decreases  well founded rank]
    end
    O --> P[Partial Correctness]
    A --> P
    D --> T[Termination]
    P --> TC[Total Correctness]
    T --> TC
```

### 4.4 Annotation Placement Map on a C Function

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Function Header] --> F1a[requires clause]
    F1 --> F1b[ensures clause]
    F1 --> F1c[decreases clause for recursion]
    L1[Loop Header] --> L1a[invariant clause]
    L1 --> L1b[decreases clause for loops]
    B1[Body Statements] --> B1a[requires ensures on inner calls]
    B1 --> B1b[assert statements]
    B1 --> B1c[ghost state updates]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper PECST741]**

*Define total correctness of a program. How does it differ from partial correctness in the context of VCC?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Partial correctness** (2 marks): A program $P$ is partially correct w.r.t. precondition $Q$ and postcondition $R$ if **whenever** $P$ terminates on an input satisfying $Q$, the output satisfies $R$. Symbolically: $\{Q\} P \{R\}$.
- **Total correctness** (1 mark): Extends partial correctness by additionally requiring that $P$ **does terminate** on all inputs satisfying $Q$. In VCC, total correctness is achieved by adding a `decreases` clause that proves well-founded descent in every cycle.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper PECST741]**

*What is the role of the `decreases` clause in VCC? What happens if it is omitted in a `while` loop?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- The `decreases` clause (2 marks) specifies a *well-founded ranking expression* that strictly decreases in every iteration. VCC generates a termination VC of the form $(n - r)_{\text{new}} < (n - r)_{\text{old}}$ and verifies it via Z3.
- If omitted (1 mark), VCC can only prove *partial* correctness of the loop; termination is not certified, and a potential infinite loop may go undetected.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice**: Answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the VCC verification pipeline from annotated C source to SMT solver. List the verification conditions generated for total correctness of a `while` loop. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following VCC-annotated function. Prove its total correctness by deriving all the verification conditions.

```c
int multiply(int a, int b)
  _(requires a >= 0 && b >= 0)
  _(ensures \result == a * b)
{
  int result = 0;
  int i = 0;
  while (i < b) {
    _(invariant result == a * i)
    _(invariant 0 <= i && i <= b)
    _(decreases b - i)
    result = result + a;
    i = i + 1;
  }
  return result;
}
```

State and discharge each VC. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Pipeline explanation (7 marks):**

1. **Parsing** (1 mark): VCC's front-end parses annotated C99 source and builds an **abstract syntax tree (AST)**.
2. **Boogie IR translation** (2 marks): The AST is lowered into the Boogie intermediate verification language. Each function becomes a Boogie procedure; each loop becomes a *loop-head assertion block*.
3. **VC generation** (2 marks): The Boogie engine computes the **weakest precondition** $\text{wp}(S, Q)$ backwards through every statement, generating **four classes** of VCs for total correctness of a `while` loop:
   - $\text{VC}_{\text{entry}}$ — invariant holds at loop entry.
   - $\text{VC}_{\text{preserve}}$ — body of loop preserves the invariant.
   - $\text{VC}_{\text{decrease}}$ — `decreases` expression strictly descends.
   - $\text{VC}_{\text{exit}}$ — invariant + negated guard implies postcondition.
4. **SMT discharge** (2 marks): Z3 (or any SMT-LIB 2 compliant solver) attempts to prove each VC. If all are valid, the program is **total correct**.

**(b) Total correctness proof for `multiply` (7 marks):**

Let $a \geq 0$, $b \geq 0$ (precondition). Local state initially: $\text{result}=0$, $i=0$. Invariant: $I \equiv (\text{result} = a \cdot i) \land (0 \leq i \leq b)$.

**VC₁ — Entry (1.5 marks):**

$$
a \geq 0 \land b \geq 0 \land \text{result}=0 \land i=0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{result} = a \cdot i
$$

i.e. $0 = a \cdot 0$ — trivially true.

**VC₂ — Preservation (2 marks):**

Hypotheses: $I$, $i < b$, $\text{result}_{\text{new}} = \text{result} + a$, $i_{\text{new}} = i + 1$.

$$
\text{result}_{\text{new}} = a \cdot i_{\text{new}} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{result} + a = a(i+1) = a \cdot i + a
$$

True by $I$ itself. Also $0 \leq i_{\text{new}} = i+1 \leq b+1$; combined with the guard $i < b$, we get $i_{\text{new}} \leq b$.

**VC₃ — Decrease (1.5 marks):**

$$
(b - i)_{\text{new}} < (b - i)_{\text{old}} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; b - (i+1) < b - i \;\Longleftrightarrow\; -1 < 0
$$

True. Also $b - i \geq 0$ holds since $i \leq b$.

**VC₄ — Exit (2 marks):**

Hypotheses: $I$, $\neg(i < b) \Rightarrow i \geq b$. Combined with $i \leq b$ gives $i = b$. Then $\text{result} = a \cdot i = a \cdot b$, which is the postcondition. ✅

Hence the function is **total correct**. ∎

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Discuss the concept of *admissibility* in VCC. How does it enable reasoning about concurrent programs without giving up termination proofs? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The recursive factorial function below is annotated in VCC. Show that it is total correct by establishing both the partial correctness and the termination obligation. **(7 marks)**

```c
int fact(int n)
  _(requires n >= 0)
  _(ensures \result >= 1)
  _(decreases n)
{
  if (n == 0) return 1;
  return n * fact(n - 1);
}
```

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Admissibility discussion (7 marks):**

1. **Definition** (2 marks): An *admissibility predicate* in VCC is a ghost boolean declared via `_(ghost bool p; admissibility(p);)` that records a *future obligation* — a fact that the verifier may assume will *eventually* become true but is not required to hold immediately.
2. **Why it exists** (2 marks): In concurrent code, a thread may not have ownership of a location, but a *peer* thread does. The peer may eventually write a value the first thread needs. Admissibility is the mechanism that encodes “I am patient and will wait for that event” without busy-waiting (which would break termination).
3. **Relationship with termination** (2 marks): A pure busy-wait `while (!p);` is *non-terminating* unless $p$ is admissible. VCC's admissibility check **rejects infinite waits** that have no justification but **accepts** waits where the predicate is annotated as admissible, giving a sound termination certificate even with concurrency.
4. **Example** (1 mark): Microsoft's Hyper-V verified the *virtual CPU scheduler* using admissibility to prove that an idle VCPU eventually receives control.

**(b) Total correctness of `fact` (7 marks):**

**Partial correctness** (3.5 marks):

- *Base case*: $n=0$, returns $1$. The postcondition $\text{result} \geq 1$ holds since $1 \geq 1$. ✅
- *Inductive step*: Assume the recursive call $f(n-1)$ returns a value $\geq 1$ (induction hypothesis). Then $f(n) = n \cdot f(n-1)$. Since $n \geq 1$ in the inductive case (because we already handled $n=0$), and $f(n-1) \geq 1$ by hypothesis, the product $n \cdot f(n-1) \geq 1$. ✅

VCC encodes this as the following verification condition, discharged by Z3:

$$
n \geq 0 \land n \neq 0 \land \text{IH}(f(n-1) \geq 1) \;\Rightarrow\; n \cdot f(n-1) \geq 1
$$

**Termination** (3.5 marks):

The `decreases n` clause requires $n - 1 < n$, which is always true. More formally, the VCC VC is:

$$
n \geq 0 \land n \neq 0 \;\Rightarrow\; n - 1 < n \;\land\; 0 \leq n - 1
$$

The second conjunct $0 \leq n - 1$ holds iff $n \geq 1$, which is the case under the inductive branch. The descent is *strict* and bounded below by $0$; the well-founded order on $\mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ guarantees termination. By induction, every recursive call terminates, so $f$ is total correct. ∎

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting `decreases`**: Many students annotate `invariant` but omit `decreases`. The verifier will *not* flag termination. You lose 3–4 marks out of 14.
> 2. **Sign-flip mistake**: Writing `decreases n - i` for a loop where $i$ *increases* will be rejected. The expression must *decrease* — choose $n - i$, not $i - n$.
> 3. **Confusing recursive vs. iterative ranking**: For recursion, the `decreases` clause goes on the **function header**, not inside the body.
> 4. **Nonlinear arithmetic blind spot**: When the ranking function involves multiplication, VCC's default solver may time out. Always show hand-derivation in the exam.
> 5. **Admissibility misuse**: Marking a predicate admissible when it isn't actually true in the future is a soundness violation — VCC will *not* catch it; you must reason carefully.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Total Correctness** = *Partial Correctness* + *Termination* — both must be proven in VCC.
- **Partial correctness** is established by `requires`, `ensures`, and `invariant` clauses that VCC lowers into Boogie and discharges via Z3.
- **Termination** is established **exclusively** by the `decreases` clause, which must (i) strictly descend, and (ii) be bounded below in a well-founded order.
- For **recursive** functions, the `decreases` clause lives on the function header; for **loops**, it lives at the loop head.
- The four canonical VCs for a `while` loop are: **entry, preservation, decrease, exit** — all four must be discharged.
- **Ghost code** (`_(ghost ...)`) introduces auxiliary state for proofs and is erased at compile time.
- **Ownership** (`owns`) and **admissibility** (`admissibility`) extend total correctness to **concurrent** C programs.
- **Volatility** is required for any cross-thread observable variable; without it, VCC's framing logic may cache stale values.
- **Well-founded ranking expressions** in VCC are tuples of integers under the default $\prec_{\text{lex}}$ order; non-positive descent must reach a non-negative floor.
- VCC verification is **sound but incomplete** — a proof proves correctness, but a failed proof does *not* prove incorrectness; refine the annotation.
- For **2-mark definitional questions**, memorize the Hoare triple $\{Q\} P \{R\}$ alongside the $\Downarrow P$ termination arrow.
- For **7-mark derivations**, always show: (1) hypothesis, (2) goal, (3) algebraic manipulation, (4) concluding equivalence. Examiners reward explicit step-by-step work.
